# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Define post-processing in CFD. List any four visualization techniques used during post-processing.**

**Model Answer:**
Post-processing is the final stage of the CFD workflow in which the raw numerical results produced by the solver (velocity, pressure, temperature, turbulence quantities, etc.) are visualized, analyzed, and interpreted to extract engineering-relevant information.

Four common visualization techniques are:
1. **Contour plots** — colored bands showing scalar field magnitude (pressure, temperature, Mach number).
2. **Vector plots** — arrows showing local velocity direction and magnitude on a 2-D plane.
3. **Streamlines** — instantaneous curves everywhere tangent to the velocity field, revealing flow topology.
4. **Iso-surfaces** — 3-D surfaces of constant scalar value (e.g., Q-criterion iso-surface to visualize vortices).

Other acceptable techniques: pathlines, streaklines, animations, XY plots, surface integrations.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State the Hadamard conditions for a well-posed mathematical problem.**

**Model Answer:**
According to Jacques Hadamard (1902), a mathematical problem is well-posed if it satisfies the following three conditions simultaneously:

1. **Existence:** For every admissible set of input data, there exists at least one solution to the problem.
2. **Uniqueness:** The solution, if it exists, is unique; i.e., no two different solutions can satisfy the same set of data.
3. **Continuous Dependence on Data (Stability):** Small perturbations in the input data (initial or boundary conditions, source terms) produce only small changes in the solution. Equivalently, the solution depends continuously on the data.

If any one of these conditions is violated, the problem is called **ill-posed**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - When classifying a PDE, students often forget to *rearrange into the standard form* before reading off *A, B, C*. The sign convention of the leading coefficient matters.
> - For continuity equation derivations, students confuse the **partial derivative** (local) with the **material derivative** (substantial). Always state which form you are deriving.
> - In Hadamard's stability, students write "small input ⇒ small output" without explaining *why* this matters (numerical round-off can otherwise grow unboundedly).
> - When listing BC types, students omit **initial conditions** for time-dependent problems — losing marks because well-posedness of parabolic/hyperbolic PDEs requires ICs *and* BCs.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Classify the following partial differential equations as elliptic, parabolic, or hyperbolic. Justify your answer using the discriminant $\Delta = B^{2} - 4AC$. Mention one physical situation in CFD where each typically arises. **(7 marks)**

- (i) $\quad \dfrac{\partial^{2} \phi}{\partial x^{2}} + \dfrac{\partial^{2} \phi}{\partial y^{2}} = 0$
- (ii) $\quad \dfrac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \nu \dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}}$
- (iii) $\quad \dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial t^{2}} = c^{2} \dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial x^{2}}$

**(b)** Derive the **differential form of the continuity equation** for a compressible fluid starting from the integral conservation statement. Clearly state the two vector identities you use. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

**General 2nd-order PDE form:** $A u_{xx} + B u_{xy} + C u_{yy} + \text{lower-order} = G$.

The discriminant is $\Delta = B^{2} - 4AC$.

**(i)** Equation: $\partial^{2} \phi / \partial x^{2} + \partial^{2} \phi / \partial y^{2} = 0$.

Comparing: $A = 1$, $B = 0$, $C = 1$.

$$\Delta = 0 - 4(1)(1) = -4 < 0 \Rightarrow \text{Elliptic}$$

CFD application: **Laplace's equation** governs 2-D incompressible irrotational (potential) flow around a body. Steady, no real characteristics; needs BCs on a closed contour.

**(ii)** Equation: $\partial u / \partial t = \nu \partial^{2} u / \partial x^{2}$.

Rearrange: $-\nu \partial^{2} u / \partial x^{2} + \partial u / \partial t = 0$. With *x* and *t* as independent variables:

$A = -\nu$, $B = 0$, $C = 0$ (no second derivative in $t$ — the $t$-derivative is *first-order*, hence not in *A, B, C*; only second derivatives count).

$$\Delta = 0 - 4(-\nu)(0) = 0 \Rightarrow \text{Parabolic}$$

CFD application: **Heat conduction** or **unsteady diffusion** in a boundary layer, or the parabolic velocity equation in a laminar boundary layer.

**(iii)** Equation: $\partial^{2} u / \partial t^{2} = c^{2} \partial^{2} u / \partial x^{2}$.

Rearrange: $c^{2} \partial^{2} u / \partial x^{2} - \partial^{2} u / \partial t^{2} = 0$. With *x* and *t*:

$A = c^{2}$, $B = 0$, $C = -1$.

$$\Delta = 0 - 4(c^{2})(-1) = 4c^{2} > 0 \Rightarrow \text{Hyperbolic}$$

CFD application: **Acoustic wave propagation**; **1-D unsteady Euler** (shock waves); pressure-pulse transmission.

---

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Integral Conservation Statement.**
For a fixed control volume (CV) with control surface (CS):

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = 0$$

This expresses: rate of mass accumulation inside CV + net efflux of mass through CS = 0.

**Step 2 — Convert the time derivative using the fact that the CV is fixed in space.**
For a *fixed* CV, the Reynolds Transport Theorem simplifies — the control volume does not deform, so we may move the time derivative inside:

$$\frac{d}{dt} \int_{CV} \rho \, dV = \int_{CV} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dV$$

**Step 3 — Convert the surface integral using the Divergence Theorem (Gauss's Theorem):**

$$\int_{CS} \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \, dV$$

**Step 4 — Substitute both identities into the integral equation:**

$$\int_{CV} \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} \, dV + \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \, dV = 0$$

Combine the integrals:

$$\int_{CV} \left[ \frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) \right] dV = 0$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Localization Theorem.**
Since the CV is arbitrary (any size, any location), the only way the volume integral can vanish for *every* CV is if the integrand itself is zero everywhere:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0\;}$$

The two vector identities used are:
1. **Reynolds Transport Theorem (fixed-volume limit):** $d/dt \int_{CV} \rho \, dV = \int_{CV} \partial \rho / \partial t \, dV$.
2. **Divergence (Gauss's) Theorem:** $\int_{CS} \vec{F} \cdot \hat{n} \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot \vec{F} \, dV$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** Define a **well-posed problem** in the sense of Hadamard. Explain each of the three conditions and discuss why well-posedness is essential for any numerical CFD simulation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the general 2nd-order linear PDE in two variables, derive the classification criteria into elliptic, parabolic, and hyperbolic forms. State one canonical example and the corresponding boundary-condition requirement for each class. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution to Part (a):**

A mathematical problem (PDE + boundary conditions + initial conditions) is called **well-posed** if it simultaneously satisfies the three Hadamard conditions:

**1. Existence:**
A solution $u(x, t)$ exists for every admissible choice of input data — the source terms, boundary values, and (if applicable) initial values. If the problem models a physical phenomenon (which has an observed reality), the mathematical model *must* admit at least one solution consistent with the data.

**2. Uniqueness:**
The solution, if it exists, is unique. No two different functions can satisfy the same set of governing equations and data. Without uniqueness, the simulation cannot answer the engineer's question: there would be multiple "valid" answers, and we would have no way to choose.

**3. Continuous Dependence on Data (Stability):**
A small perturbation in the input data produces only a small change in the solution. Formally, for every $\epsilon > 0$ there exists $\delta > 0$ such that if the data perturbation is less than $\delta$, the resulting change in solution is less than $\epsilon$. This is also called **stability**.

**Why It Matters in CFD:**
- Numerical solutions always carry **round-off error** (finite-precision arithmetic). Without stability, these errors amplify unboundedly, and the computed solution bears no resemblance to the true solution.
- Experimental inputs to CFD (boundary values, material properties) are measured quantities with small uncertainties. Stability guarantees that small measurement errors do not translate into large simulation errors.
- An ill-posed problem (e.g., backward heat equation, where one tries to recover the past temperature from present data) cannot be solved by *any* numerical scheme — the errors grow faster than the grid can be refined.

Thus, **well-posedness is a prerequisite for any meaningful CFD simulation.** A well-posed problem can still be solved by a bad scheme; but no scheme can rescue an ill-posed problem.

---

#### **Model Solution to Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Write the general second-order linear PDE in two independent variables** $x$ and $y$:

$$A u_{xx} + B u_{xy} + C u_{yy} + D u_x + E u_y + F u = G(x, y)$$

where $A, B, C, D, E, F$ may depend on $x, y$ (but not on $u$ or its derivatives for linearity).

**Step 2 — Classification depends only on the highest-order derivatives.** The lower-order terms ($D u_x + E u_y + F u - G$) do not affect the classification. Only $A, B, C$ matter.

**Step 3 — Compute the discriminant:**

$$\Delta = B^{2} - 4AC$$

The three cases are:

| Case | Sign | Class | Canonical Example | BC Requirement |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- |
| $\Delta < 0$ | Negative | **Elliptic** | $\nabla^{2} \phi = 0$ (Laplace) | Closed boundary with BC specified (Dirichlet, Neumann, or Robin) |
| $\Delta = 0$ | Zero | **Parabolic** | $\partial u / \partial t = \alpha \nabla^{2} u$ | BCs on open boundary + **initial condition** at $t = 0$ |
| $\Delta > 0$ | Positive | **Hyperbolic** | $\partial^{2} u / \partial t^{2} = c^{2} \partial^{2} u / \partial x^{2}$ | BCs on open boundary + **initial condition** at $t = 0$ |

**Step 4 — Why this classification matters.**
- **Elliptic** problems have **no real characteristics**; disturbances are felt everywhere simultaneously (steady-state). Solution is found by iterative relaxation over the entire closed domain.
- **Parabolic** problems have **one family of real characteristics** (parallel lines, e.g., $t = \text{const}$); solution can be marched forward in time from initial data, with boundary data required on an open boundary (e.g., inlet/outlet of a duct).
- **Hyperbolic** problems have **two families of real characteristics**; information propagates at finite speed along these characteristics, requiring initial conditions plus boundary data on appropriate inflow/outflow boundaries (e.g., characteristic-based BCs in supersonic flow).

**Step 5 — Numerical implication.**
| Class | Typical Numerical Approach |
| :---: | :--- |
| Elliptic | Iterative solvers (Jacobi, Gauss-Seidel, SOR, CG, multigrid) |
| Parabolic | Time-marching schemes (FTCS, Crank-Nicolson, fully implicit) |
| Hyperbolic | Method of characteristics, upwind schemes, Riemann solvers |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1 Core Topic**

**Post-Processing in CFD**
- ☐ Post-processing is the **final stage** of CFD; it visualizes and interprets solver output.
- ☐ Key visualizations: contour, vector, streamline, iso-surface, pathline, streakline.
- ☐ Key derived quantities: Cp, Cf, Nu, Re, Ma, y⁺.
- ☐ Validation against experimental/analytical data is part of post-processing.

**Classification of PDEs**
- ☐ General 2nd-order PDE in two variables: $A u_{xx} + B u_{xy} + C u_{yy} + \text{lower-order} = G$.
- ☐ Discriminant: $\Delta = B^{2} - 4AC$.
- ☐ $\Delta < 0$ ⇒ **Elliptic** (Laplace, Poisson): steady-state problems, BCs on **closed boundary**.
- ☐ $\Delta = 0$ ⇒ **Parabolic** (heat, diffusion): one characteristic family, needs IC + BC.
- ☐ $\Delta > 0$ ⇒ **Hyperbolic** (wave, Euler): two characteristic families, needs IC + BC.

**Boundary Conditions**
- ☐ **Dirichlet:** value specified ($u = u_{0}$).
- ☐ **Neumann:** normal derivative specified ($\partial u / \partial n = q_{0}$).
- ☐ **Robin/Cauchy:** linear combination of value and derivative.
- ☐ **Periodic/Cyclic:** $u(0) = u(L)$.
- ☐ **Initial conditions** are mandatory for unsteady (parabolic, hyperbolic) problems.

**Well-Posed Problems (Hadamard)**
- ☐ **Existence:** at least one solution.
- ☐ **Uniqueness:** at most one solution.
- ☐ **Stability:** continuous dependence on input data.
- ☐ Ill-posed if any one condition fails; cannot be solved reliably by any scheme.

**Continuity Equation (Mass Conservation)**
- ☐ **Integral form:** $\dfrac{d}{dt}\displaystyle\int_{CV}\rho\,dV + \displaystyle\int_{CS}\rho(\vec{V}\cdot\hat{n})\,dA = 0$.
- ☐ **Differential form (general/compressible):** $\partial \rho / \partial t + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$.
- ☐ **Incompressible form:** $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ (elliptic in space).
- ☐ **Material-derivative form:** $D\rho/Dt + \rho\,\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$.
- ☐ **1-D Cartesian:** $\partial \rho / \partial t + \partial (\rho u) / \partial x = 0$.
- ☐ **Two key identities used in derivation:** Reynolds Transport Theorem (fixed-volume limit) and Divergence (Gauss's) Theorem.
- ☐ **Localization Theorem:** if $\int_{CV} f\,dV = 0$ for *every* CV, then $f = 0$ everywhere.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Momentum Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Momentum Equation # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition The **Momentum Equation** in Computational Fluid Dynamics is the mathematical statement of **Newton's Second Law of Motion** applied to a fluid continuum. It expresses the principle of conservation of linear momentum for a fluid element or control volume, accounting for forces arising from pressure gradients, viscous stresses, body forces (e.g., gravity), and unsteady accelerations. In its most general vector form for a **Newtonian fluid**, the Navier-Stokes Momentum Equation is: $$\frac{\partial (\rho \vec{V})}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \vec{V}) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$ where the left-hand side represents the **inertial acceleration** terms, and the right-hand side comprises **pressure forces**, **viscous forces**, and **body forces**, respectively. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The momentum equation, together with the continuity equation and the energy equation, forms the governing system of partial differential equations (PDEs) that every CFD solver attempts to numerically approximate. Mastery of its derivation and physical meaning is the foundation of Module 1. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "River Raft" Intuition Imagine standing on the bank of a river, watching a **circular raft** floating downstream. Three things act on that raft simultaneously: 1. **Water pushes it from behind** (pressure force on its rear face) and **sucks it from the front** (negative pressure gradient) — this is the **pressure gradient force**. 2. **The raft spins and twists** because the water flowing faster at the top drags it one way while slower water near the bottom pulls it the other way — this is the **viscous shear force**. 3. **Gravity pulls it down the slope** of the riverbed — this is the **body force**. The raft's motion (its acceleration) is the **sum of all these forces divided by its mass**. That is precisely what the momentum equation says — only it is written for every infinitesimal fluid parcel at every point in the flow domain, and for **all three spatial directions (x, y, z)** simultaneously. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The momentum equation is essentially Newton's Second Law ($\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$) rewritten for a continuous fluid. The "force per unit volume" on the right-hand side replaces $\vec{F}$, while $\rho \vec{a}$ appears on the left-hand side. ## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The following constants and parameters appear repeatedly in the momentum equation and must be memorized: - **Dynamic viscosity ($\mu$)** — measured in **Pa·s** or **N·s/m²**; resistance of a fluid to deformation. - **Kinematic viscosity ($\nu = \mu / \rho$)** — measured in **m²/s**; useful for non-dimensionalization. - **Density ($\rho$)** — measured in **kg/m³**; for incompressible flow $\rho$ is **constant**. - **Pressure ($p$)** — measured in **Pa** or **N/m²**. - **Body force per unit mass ($\vec{g}$)** — measured in **m/s²**; commonly $\vec{g} = -9.81\,\hat{k}$ for gravity. # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — From Newton to Navier-Stokes The momentum equation is built layer-by-layer on three pillars of continuum mechanics. Understanding this hierarchy is essential for KTU exam questions. ### Pillar 1: System vs. Control Volume - **System:** A fixed mass of fluid moving through space. - **Control Volume (CV):** A fixed region in space through which fluid flows. - We apply **Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT)** to convert Newton's law from a system (Lagrangian) viewpoint to a CV (Eulerian) viewpoint. ### Pillar 2: Forces Acting on a Fluid Element Two categories of forces act on a fluid element of volume $\Delta V$: 1. **Surface Forces** — act on the element's boundary: - **Pressure forces** (normal stresses, isotropic). - **Viscous forces** (shear stresses, anisotropic, depend on velocity gradients). 2. **Body Forces** — act throughout the element's volume: - Gravity, electromagnetic, centrifugal forces. ### Pillar 3: The Stress Tensor A fluid element experiences **nine stress components** — three normal ($\sigma_{xx}, \sigma_{yy}, \sigma_{zz}$) and six shear ($\tau_{xy}, \tau_{yx}, \tau_{xz}, \tau_{zx}, \tau_{yz}, \tau_{zy}$). These are packaged into the **stress tensor** $\boldsymbol{\sigma}$: $$\boldsymbol{\sigma} = -p \mathbf{I} + \boldsymbol{\tau}$$ where $\mathbf{I}$ is the identity tensor and $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ is the **viscous stress tensor**. ## 2.2 Derivation Logic — Step-by-Step Breakdown 1. **Start with Newton's 2nd Law:** $\sum \vec{F} = \frac{d}{dt}(m\vec{V})$. 2. **Apply Reynolds Transport Theorem** to convert from system to CV: $$\sum \vec{F} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho \vec{V} \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho \vec{V} (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$ 3. **Decompose forces** into surface and body: $\sum \vec{F} = \vec{F}_{surface} + \vec{F}_{body}$. 4. **Express surface forces** via stress tensor: $\vec{F}_{surface} = \int_{CS} \boldsymbol{\sigma} \cdot \hat{n} \, dA$. 5. **Apply divergence theorem** to convert surface integrals to volume integrals. 6. **Assume Newtonian behavior:** $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mu \left[ \nabla \vec{V} + (\nabla \vec{V})^T \right] - \frac{2}{3}\mu (\nabla \cdot \vec{V}) \mathbf{I}$. 7. **Substitute and simplify** to arrive at the Navier-Stokes equations. > [!NOTE] > **Why This Matters for KTU:** Examiners frequently ask students to derive the momentum equation in one specific direction (often x-direction) starting from the integral form. Memorize the Reynolds Transport Theorem step — it is the most-tested transformation. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation Name | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Newton's Second Law (System) | $\sum \vec{F} = \frac{d}{dt}\int_{sys} \rho \vec{V} \, dV$ | Rate of change of system momentum | | 2 | Reynolds Transport Theorem | $\frac{D}{Dt}\int_{sys} \eta \, dV = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \eta \rho \, dV + \int_{CS} \eta \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$ | Converts system to control volume | | 3 | Continuity Equation (Compressible) | $\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$ | Conservation of mass | | 4 | Continuity Equation (Incompressible) | $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ | Constant density constraint | | 5 | General Momentum Equation (Vector) | $\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau} + \rho \vec{g}$ | Force balance per unit volume | | 6 | Stress Tensor Decomposition | $\boldsymbol{\sigma} = -p\mathbf{I} + \boldsymbol{\tau}$ | Pressure + viscous parts | | 7 | Newtonian Constitutive Relation | $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mu \left(\nabla \vec{V} + \nabla \vec{V}^T\right) - \frac{2}{3}\mu(\nabla \cdot \vec{V})\mathbf{I}$ | Linear stress-strain-rate law | | 8 | Navier-Stokes (Incompressible, Vector) | $\rho \left(\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V}\right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$ | Final working equation | | 9 | Navier-Stokes x-component | $\rho \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \left(\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2}\right) + \rho g_x$ | x-momentum equation | | 10 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \frac{\rho V L}{\mu} = \frac{VL}{\nu}$ | Ratio of inertial to viscous forces | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility The momentum equation is the workhorse behind virtually every engineering fluid flow simulation: - **Aerospace:** Predicting lift and drag on aircraft wings (NACA airfoils). - **Automotive:** Computing drag coefficients on car bodies for fuel efficiency. - **Biomedical:** Modeling blood flow through stenosed arteries and designing stents. - **Civil/Hydraulic:** Designing spillways, pipes, channels, and dams. - **Chemical:** Designing mixing tanks, reactors, and pipe networks. - **HVAC:** Computing airflow in ducts and around buildings. - **Marine:** Hydrodynamics of ship hulls and submarines. # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Continuity Equation in Differential Form The continuity equation is a prerequisite to the momentum equation — it enforces mass conservation. ### Step 1: Consider a fluid element (control volume) Let a differential fluid element have sides $dx$, $dy$, $dz$. Mass enters and leaves through each of the six faces. ### Step 2: Mass flow rate through the x-face at $x$ $$\dot{m}_x = \rho u \, dy \, dz$$ ### Step 3: Mass flow rate through the opposite x-face at $x + dx$ By Taylor series expansion: $$\dot{m}_{x+dx} = \left[\rho u + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x}dx\right] dy \, dz$$ ### Step 4: Net mass outflow in x-direction $$\Delta \dot{m}_x = \dot{m}_{x+dx} - \dot{m}_x = \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} dx \, dy \, dz$$ ### Step 5: Repeat for y- and z-directions Similar expansions give: $$\Delta \dot{m}_y = \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} dx \, dy \, dz, \qquad \Delta \dot{m}_z = \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} dx \, dy \, dz$$ ### Step 6: Apply conservation of mass The net outflow must equal the rate of decrease of mass inside the element: $$\Delta \dot{m}_x + \Delta \dot{m}_y + \Delta \dot{m}_z = -\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} dx \, dy \, dz$$ ### Step 7: Divide by the elemental volume $$\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = -\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}$$ ### Step 8: Final compact form $$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0}$$ For **incompressible flow** ($\rho = $ constant), this reduces to $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$. ## 3.2 Derivation of the x-Momentum Equation from Integral Form This is the **most heavily tested derivation** in KTU examinations. ### Step 1: Apply Newton's 2nd law to the control volume $$\sum F_x = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho u \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho u (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$ Here, $\sum F_x$ is the net x-component of all forces acting on the CV. ### Step 2: Identify the forces $$\sum F_x = F_{pressure,x} + F_{viscous,x} + F_{body,x}$$ ### Step 3: Express pressure force on a surface element The pressure force on a face with outward normal $\hat{n}$ is $-p \, dA \, \hat{n}$. The x-component over the entire control surface: $$F_{pressure,x} = -\int_{CS} p \, n_x \, dA = -\int_{CS} p \, dA_x$$ where $dA_x = \hat{i} \cdot \hat{n} \, dA$. ### Step 4: Apply divergence theorem to the surface integral By the divergence (Gauss) theorem: $$-\int_{CS} p \, n_x \, dA = -\int_{CV} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} \, dV$$ ### Step 5: Viscous force The viscous force in the x-direction arises from the x-component of the viscous stress tensor integrated over the surface: $$F_{viscous,x} = \int_{CS} \left( \tau_{xx} n_x + \tau_{yx} n_y + \tau_{zx} n_z \right) dA = \int_{CV} \left( \frac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z} \right) dV$$ ### Step 6: Body force For gravity acting in the $-z$ direction: $$F_{body,x} = \int_{CV} \rho g_x \, dV$$ ### Step 7: Substitute everything into the momentum balance $$\int_{CV} \left[ -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z} + \rho g_x \right] dV = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{CV} \rho u \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho u (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$ ### Step 8: Apply divergence theorem to the right-hand side $$\int_{CS} \rho u (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA = \int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\rho u \vec{V}) \, dV$$ ### Step 9: Combine terms inside a single volume integral $$\int_{CV} \left[ \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho u \vec{V}) + \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z} - \rho g_x \right] dV = 0$$ ### Step 10: Invoke the Arbitrary Control Volume Principle Since this must hold for *any* CV, the integrand itself must vanish identically: $$\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho u \vec{V}) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z} + \rho g_x$$ ### Step 11: Substitute Newtonian constitutive relations For a Newtonian, incompressible fluid: $$\tau_{xx} = 2\mu \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \qquad \tau_{yx} = \mu \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x} \right), \qquad \tau_{zx} = \mu \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial x} \right)$$ ### Step 12: Final x-momentum equation $$\boxed{\rho \left( \frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} \right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \left( \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2} \right) + \rho g_x}$$ This is the **x-component of the Navier-Stokes equation**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Insight:** The LHS is the **substantial derivative** $\rho D u/Dt$ — the total acceleration of a fluid particle as it moves. The three terms on the RHS are pressure gradient, viscous diffusion, and body force respectively. ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Significance of Each Term | Term | Mathematical Symbol | Physical Meaning | Magnitude Indicator | |---|---|---|---| | Local acceleration | $\partial \vec{V} / \partial t$ | Change of momentum at a fixed point | Important in unsteady flow | | Convective acceleration | $\vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V}$ | Momentum carried in/out of element | Dominant in high-Re flow | | Pressure gradient | $-\nabla p$ | Pressure-driven flow | Dominant in confined/ducted flow | | Viscous diffusion | $\mu \nabla^2 \vec{V}$ | Momentum diffusion by viscosity | Dominant in low-Re flow / boundary layers | | Body force | $\rho \vec{g}$ | External field force | Gravity, electromagnetic, etc. | # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Architecture of the Momentum Equation This schematic maps the conceptual hierarchy from fundamental physical laws to the final Navier-Stokes equation used in CFD solvers. ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Newton Second Law
F equals m times a"] --> B["Reynolds Transport Theorem
System to Control Volume"] B --> C["Force Decomposition
Surface plus Body Forces"] C --> D["Surface Force via
Stress Tensor Sigma"] D --> E["Newtonian Constitutive Law
Tau equals mu times Strain Rate"] E --> F["Divergence Theorem
Surface to Volume Integral"] F --> G["Arbitrary CV Principle
Integrand must vanish"] G --> H["Final Navier Stokes Equation
Vector Form for 3D Flow"] H --> I["Expand into x y z Components"] I --> J["Discretize via FVM or FDM
Module 2 and Module 3"] ``` ## 4.2 Process Flow — Term-by-Term Physical Origin ```mermaid flowchart LR P1["Inertia LHS
rho Du over Dt"] --> Merge["Final Equation"] P2["Pressure Force
minus grad p"] --> Merge P3["Viscous Force
mu Laplacian V"] --> Merge P4["Body Force
rho g"] --> Merge Merge --> Out["Navier Stokes PDE
Solved by CFD Solver"] ``` ## 4.3 Stress Element Functional Architecture A fluid element in motion experiences stress components on each of its six faces. The following block-level architecture maps the nine stress components and their decomposition. ```mermaid flowchart TD Sigma["Total Stress Tensor Sigma
3 by 3 matrix"] --> Decompose{Decompose} Decompose --> Pressure["Pressure Stress
Isotropic part
minus p times I"] Decompose --> Viscous["Viscous Stress Tensor Tau
Deviatoric part"] Viscous --> Norm["Normal Components
tau_xx tau_yy tau_zz"] Viscous --> Shear["Shear Components
tau_xy tau_xz tau_yx
tau_yz tau_zx tau_zy"] Norm --> NewLaw["Newtonian Law
tau_ii equals 2 mu du_i over dx_i"] Shear --> NewLaw2["Newtonian Law
tau_ij equals mu times du_i over dx_j
plus du_j over dx_i"] Pressure --> Final["Combined Stress State
for each face of element"] NewLaw --> Final NewLaw2 --> Final Final --> NS["Substitute into
Navier Stokes Equation"] ``` ## 4.4 Module-Level Roadmap for CFD Solution ```mermaid flowchart LR M1["Module 1
Governing Equations
Momentum Equation"] M2["Module 2
Discretization
FDM and FVM"] M3["Module 3
Pressure Velocity Coupling
SIMPLE Algorithm"] M4["Module 4
Turbulence Modeling
k epsilon and RANS"] M5["Module 5
Boundary Conditions
and Validation"] M1 --> M2 --> M3 --> M4 --> M5 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Note for Students:** The momentum equation derived in Module 1 is the *input* — a continuous PDE. Every subsequent module exists to *solve* this equation numerically. Always keep the link between physical term and numerical discretization clear. # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **State Newton's law of viscosity. Write the mathematical expression relating shear stress to velocity gradient for a Newtonian fluid and explain the physical significance of each term.** **Model Answer:** Newton's law of viscosity states that the **shear stress** in a Newtonian fluid is **directly proportional** to the **rate of angular deformation** (i.e., velocity gradient) perpendicular to the direction of flow. $$\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy}$$ where: - **$\tau$** = shear stress [Pa or N/m²] - **$\mu$** = dynamic viscosity [Pa·s] - **$du/dy$** = velocity gradient perpendicular to flow direction [s⁻¹] **Physical Significance:** The constant $\mu$ is a fluid property indicating its internal resistance to flow. Fluids obeying this linear relationship (water, air, oil) are called **Newtonian fluids**. The law implies that momentum diffuses from regions of high velocity to low velocity at a rate proportional to the local gradient — this is precisely the **viscous diffusion term** $\mu \nabla^2 \vec{V}$ in the momentum equation. > **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Statement — 1 mark; Equation — 1 mark; Physical significance — 1 mark. --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Write the Navier-Stokes equation in vector form for an incompressible Newtonian fluid. Identify and explain each term.** **Model Answer:** $$\rho \left( \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$ | Term | Name | Meaning | |---|---|---| | $\rho (\partial \vec{V} / \partial t)$ | Local acceleration | Unsteadiness at a fixed point | | $\rho (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V})$ | Convective acceleration | Momentum transport by flow | | $-\nabla p$ | Pressure force per unit volume | Drives flow from high to low pressure | | $\mu \nabla^2 \vec{V}$ | Viscous force per unit volume | Momentum diffusion due to viscosity | | $\rho \vec{g}$ | Body force per unit volume | Gravity or other body forces | **Mark Distribution (3 marks):** Equation — 1 mark; Three correct terms explained — 2 marks. --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Derive the continuity equation in differential form for a three-dimensional compressible flow. State the assumptions made.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** 1. Consider a differential rectangular fluid element with sides $dx$, $dy$, $dz$ centered at point $(x, y, z)$. 2. The mass flow rate entering through the face at $x$ (normal $\hat{i}$) is: $$\dot{m}_{x,in} = \rho u \, dy \, dz$$ 3. The mass flow rate leaving through the opposite face at $x + dx$ is (Taylor expansion): $$\dot{m}_{x,out} = \left[\rho u + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} dx\right] dy \, dz$$ 4. Net outflow in x-direction: $$\Delta \dot{m}_x = \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} dx \, dy \, dz$$ 5. Similarly for y and z directions. 6. Total net outflow: $$\Delta \dot{m} = \left[\frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z}\right] dx \, dy \, dz$$ 7. By conservation of mass, this must equal the rate of decrease of mass inside the element: $$\Delta \dot{m} = -\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} dx \, dy \, dz$$ 8. Equating and dividing by $dx \, dy \, dz$: $$\boxed{\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial (\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial (\rho w)}{\partial z} = 0}$$ **Assumptions:** (i) Continuous fluid (continuum hypothesis); (ii) No sources or sinks of mass within the element; (iii) Single-component fluid. **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Element setup — 1 mark; Face-by-face mass flow analysis — 3 marks; Conservation statement — 1 mark; Final equation + assumptions — 2 marks. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Starting from the integral momentum equation, derive the x-component of the Navier-Stokes equation for an incompressible Newtonian fluid.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** 1. Apply Newton's second law to a control volume in the x-direction: $$\sum F_x = \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{CV} \rho u \, dV + \int_{CS} \rho u (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}) \, dA$$ 2. Identify three contributions to $\sum F_x$: - Pressure force: $F_{p,x} = -\int_{CS} p \, n_x \, dA$ - Viscous force: $F_{\tau,x} = \int_{CS} (\tau_{xx} n_x + \tau_{yx} n_y + \tau_{zx} n_z) dA$ - Body force: $F_{b,x} = \int_{CV} \rho g_x \, dV$ 3. Apply the divergence theorem to surface integrals: $$\int_{CS} f \, n_i \, dA = \int_{CV} \frac{\partial f}{\partial x_i} dV$$ This converts surface integrals into volume integrals: $$F_{p,x} = -\int_{CV} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} dV, \qquad F_{\tau,x} = \int_{CV} \left(\frac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z}\right) dV$$ 4. Combine all terms into a single volume integral: $$\int_{CV}\left[\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial t} + \nabla\cdot(\rho u \vec{V}) + \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} - \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} - \frac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z} - \rho g_x\right] dV = 0$$ 5. Apply the **Arbitrary Control Volume Principle** — since the integrand vanishes for *any* CV, the integrand itself must equal zero: $$\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\rho u u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho u v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho u w)}{\partial z} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z} + \rho g_x$$ 6. For **incompressible** flow ($\rho$ = constant), divide by $\rho$: $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \frac{1}{\rho}\left(\frac{\partial \tau_{xx}}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial \tau_{yx}}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial \tau_{zx}}{\partial z}\right) + g_x$$ 7. Substitute Newtonian stress relations: $$\tau_{xx} = 2\mu\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \tau_{yx} = \mu\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right), \quad \tau_{zx} = \mu\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right)$$ 8. Use incompressibility $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ to simplify. The final x-momentum equation: $$\boxed{\rho\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu\left(\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2}\right) + \rho g_x}$$ **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** RTT application — 1 mark; Force decomposition — 1 mark; Divergence theorem — 1 mark; Arbitrary CV principle — 1 mark; Newtonian substitution — 2 marks; Final equation — 1 mark. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Question A:** > 1. Students frequently **forget to apply the divergence theorem** and leave surface integrals unconverted — examiners specifically look for this step (loss: 1–2 marks). > 2. Many students **omit the Arbitrary Control Volume Principle** justification when dropping the integral sign — this loses 1 mark. > 3. **Incompressibility assumption** must be **explicitly stated** when substituting Newtonian stress relations; failing to do so is a common deduction point. > 4. Skipping the assumption that viscosity $\mu$ is **constant** loses 1 mark in most valuation schemes. --- ### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Define the stress tensor for a fluid element. Derive the relationship between stress components and rate of strain for a Newtonian fluid.** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** 1. **Definition:** The stress tensor $\boldsymbol{\sigma}$ is a second-order tensor (3×3 matrix) that completely describes the state of stress at a point in a fluid. It has nine components: $$\boldsymbol{\sigma} = \begin{bmatrix} \sigma_{xx} & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{yx} & \sigma_{yy} & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{zy} & \sigma_{zz} \end{bmatrix}$$ where $\sigma_{ii}$ are **normal stresses** and $\tau_{ij}$ (with $i \neq j$) are **shear stresses**. 2. **Decomposition:** The total stress tensor is split into pressure (isotropic) and viscous (deviatoric) parts: $$\boldsymbol{\sigma} = -p \mathbf{I} + \boldsymbol{\tau}$$ where $\mathbf{I}$ is the 3×3 identity matrix and $\boldsymbol{\tau}$ is the viscous stress tensor. 3. **Rate of Strain Tensor:** Define the rate of deformation tensor: $$\dot{\boldsymbol{\epsilon}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[\nabla \vec{V} + (\nabla \vec{V})^T\right]$$ Components: $\dot{\epsilon}_{ij} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial u_j}{\partial x_i}\right)$ 4. **Newtonian Postulate:** For a Newtonian fluid, viscous stress is **linearly proportional** to rate of strain: $$\tau_{ij} = 2\mu \dot{\epsilon}_{ij} \quad \text{for } i = j$$ $$\tau_{ij} = 2\mu \dot{\epsilon}_{ij} \quad \text{for } i \neq j$$ 5. **Explicit form** (expanding): $$\tau_{xx} = 2\mu \frac{\partial u}{\partial x}, \quad \tau_{yy} = 2\mu \frac{\partial v}{\partial y}, \quad \tau_{zz} = 2\mu \frac{\partial w}{\partial z}$$ $$\tau_{xy} = \tau_{yx} = \mu\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)$$ $$\tau_{xz} = \tau_{zx} = \mu\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial x}\right)$$ $$\tau_{yz} = \tau_{zy} = \mu\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}\right)$$ 6. **Including bulk viscosity** (for compressible flows), Stokes' hypothesis gives: $$\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mu\left[\nabla\vec{V} + (\nabla\vec{V})^T\right] - \frac{2}{3}\mu(\nabla \cdot \vec{V})\mathbf{I}$$ **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Stress tensor definition — 1 mark; Decomposition — 1 mark; Rate of strain tensor — 2 marks; Newtonian relation derivation — 2 marks; Final explicit components — 1 mark. #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Write the momentum equation in (i) conservative form and (ii) non-conservative form. Explain the difference between the two forms. Why is the conservative form preferred for numerical solution?** **Step-by-Step Model Solution:** 1. **Conservative Form:** $$\frac{\partial (\rho \vec{V})}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \vec{V}) = -\nabla p + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau} + \rho \vec{g}$$ This expresses the equation in terms of the **fluxes** of momentum across control surfaces. 2. **Non-Conservative Form (Convective/Advective Form):** $$\rho \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla) \vec{V} = -\nabla p + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau} + \rho \vec{g}$$ Or equivalently: $$\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau} + \rho \vec{g}$$ This expresses the equation in terms of acceleration of a fluid particle. 3. **Algebraic relationship** between the two forms (using continuity): $$\frac{\partial (\rho \vec{V})}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \vec{V}) = \rho \frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \rho (\vec{V} \cdot \nabla) \vec{V} + \vec{V}\underbrace{\left[\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V})\right]}_{=0 \text{ by continuity}}$$ The two forms are **mathematically identical** when the continuity equation holds exactly. 4. **Why Conservative Form is Preferred Numerically:** - **Conservation guarantee:** Finite-volume discretization (FVM) automatically satisfies conservation of momentum across cell faces. - **Shock capture:** In flows with discontinuities (shocks, contact surfaces), the conservative form correctly captures jump conditions via Rankine-Hugoniot relations. - **Robustness:** Numerical fluxes can be defined at cell boundaries in a consistent manner. - **Non-conservative schemes** may converge to the wrong weak solution in discontinuous flows (Lax-Wendroff theorem). 5. **Example (x-direction, incompressible):** Conservative: $\quad \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial(\rho u^2)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho uv)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho uw)}{\partial z} = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu\nabla^2 u + \rho g_x$ Non-conservative: $\quad \rho\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + v\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + w\frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu\nabla^2 u + \rho g_x$ **Mark Distribution (7 marks):** Two forms written — 2 marks; Algebraic connection via continuity — 1 mark; Conservative form preferred — 3 marks; Numerical example — 1 mark. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning — Question B:** > 1. In **Part (a)**, students often **forget the symmetry property** of the stress tensor ($\tau_{ij} = \tau_{ji}$) — this is a 1-mark deduction. > 2. The **Stokes hypothesis** for bulk viscosity term is often completely omitted — examiners reward students who mention it even briefly. > 3. In **Part (b)**, students frequently write only the conservative form without explaining *why* it is numerically superior — failing to mention the **Lax-Wendroff theorem** or **shock capturing** costs 2 marks. > 4. Many students confuse the **substantial derivative** $D/Dt$ with the partial derivative $\partial/\partial t$. Be precise: $D/Dt = \partial/\partial t + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla$. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Momentum Equation** - **Core Statement:** Momentum equation is Newton's 2nd Law for a continuum: $\sum \vec{F} = d(m\vec{V})/dt$. - **Reynolds Transport Theorem** is the bridge from system (Lagrangian) to control volume (Eulerian) formulation. - **Three force types:** Surface pressure forces, surface viscous forces, body forces. - **Stress tensor** $\boldsymbol{\sigma} = -p\mathbf{I} + \boldsymbol{\tau}$ has 9 components (3 normal + 6 shear), symmetric for non-rotating fluids. - **Newtonian fluid:** $\tau_{ij} = \mu(\partial u_i/\partial x_j + \partial u_j/\partial x_i)$ for $i \neq j$. - **Final Navier-Stokes form (incompressible, vector):** $$\rho \left(\frac{\partial \vec{V}}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla \vec{V}\right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$ - **Five terms:** local acceleration, convective acceleration, pressure gradient, viscous diffusion, body force. - **Two forms of momentum equation:** Conservative (flux form, preferred for FVM) and non-conservative (advective form, useful for analysis). - **Continuity equation** must always be solved alongside the momentum equation — they form a coupled system. - **Three Navier-Stokes components** in 3D — one for each spatial direction (x, y, z). - **Divergence theorem** is essential to convert surface integrals to volume integrals during derivation. - **Arbitrary CV principle:** if an integral vanishes for every CV, the integrand itself must vanish. - **Incompressibility assumption** ($\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$) drastically simplifies the viscous stress term. - **Reynolds number** $Re = \rho V L / \mu$ governs the relative importance of inertial vs. viscous terms. - **Boundary conditions** required: velocity (Dirichlet) on no-slip walls, pressure (Neumann) on outlets, stress-free on slip walls and far-fields. - **Units check:** Every term in the momentum equation must have units of **N/m³** (force per unit volume). - **Physical constants to memorize:** $\rho$ (kg/m³), $\mu$ (Pa·s), $\nu = \mu/\rho$ (m²/s), $g = 9.81$ m/s².

topics.title: Energy Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Energy Equation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Energy Equation** (also called the **Total Energy Equation** or **First Law of Thermodynamics for a Fluid System**) is a fundamental conservation principle in Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) that mathematically expresses the balance between the rate of change of total energy within a fluid element and the net flux of energy across its boundaries, combined with the work done by surface and body forces and the heat added to the system.

For a Newtonian fluid undergoing three-dimensional, viscous, heat-conducting flow, the differential form of the energy equation is expressed as:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho E)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot \left[ \left(\rho E + p \right) \mathbf{V} \right] = \nabla \cdot \left( k_{\text{eff}} \nabla T \right) + \nabla \cdot \left( \boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{eff}} \cdot \mathbf{V} \right] + S_h
$$

where:
- $\rho$ = fluid density $(\text{kg/m}^3)$
- $E$ = total energy per unit mass $(\text{J/kg})$
- $p$ = static pressure $(\text{Pa})$
- $\mathbf{V}$ = velocity vector $(\text{m/s})$
- $k_{\text{eff}}$ = effective thermal conductivity $(\text{W/m·K})$
- $T$ = temperature $(\text{K})$
- $\boldsymbol{\tau}_{\text{eff}}$ = effective stress tensor $(\text{Pa})$
- $S_h$ = volumetric heat source term $(\text{W/m}^3)$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Energy Equation is the third fundamental conservation law (after mass and momentum) that must be solved in any CFD analysis. It governs the **temperature** and **energy distribution** field within the computational domain.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are managing a **bank account** for a fluid parcel:

- **Deposits (Inflow):** Heat added by conduction from neighboring hot regions, work done by pressure forces pushing the parcel, viscous heating from surrounding fluid.
- **Withdrawals (Outflow):** Heat lost by conduction to colder regions, work done by the parcel on its surroundings.
- **Net Balance:** The change in the parcel's "energy wealth" (kinetic + internal + potential) must equal the net deposit minus withdrawal.

Think of a flowing river with a **waterfall**:
- Water at the top has high **potential energy**.
- As it falls, potential energy converts to **kinetic energy** (speed).
- At the bottom, turbulent eddies and friction with rocks convert kinetic energy into **thermal (internal) energy** (slightly warmer water).
- The **Energy Equation** is the mathematical "ledger" that keeps track of all these conversions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The energy equation does not conserve each form of energy separately; instead, it conserves the **total energy** (kinetic + internal + potential) while allowing interchange between these forms.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Specific heat at constant pressure** ($c_p$) for air = **1005 J/(kg·K)**
- **Specific heat at constant volume** ($c_v$) for air = **718 J/(kg·K)**
- **Ratio of specific heats** ($\gamma$) for air = **1.4**
- **Universal gas constant** ($R$) = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- **Stefan-Boltzmann constant** ($\sigma$) = **5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)**
- **Gravitational acceleration** ($g$) = **9.81 m/s²**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Components of Total Energy

The total energy per unit mass $E$ of a fluid element is the sum of three contributions:

$$
E = e + \frac{1}{2} \vert \mathbf{V} \vert^2 + gz
$$

where:
- $e$ = specific internal energy $(\text{J/kg})$
- $\frac{1}{2} \vert \mathbf{V} \vert^2$ = specific kinetic energy $(\text{J/kg})$
- $gz$ = specific potential energy $(\text{J/kg})$

For an ideal gas, internal energy is directly related to temperature:

$$
e = c_v T
$$

The **specific enthalpy** is defined as:

$$
h = e + \frac{p}{\rho} = c_p T \quad \text{(for ideal gas)}
$$

### 2.2 Why the Energy Equation is Required

The continuity equation provides **mass conservation**, and the Navier-Stokes equations provide **momentum conservation**. However, neither of these directly gives us the **temperature field** or the **heat transfer** characteristics of the flow. The energy equation is essential for:

1. **Heat transfer analysis** (conduction, convection, radiation)
2. **Compressible flow** problems where temperature changes are significant
3. **Chemical reactions** and combustion modeling
4. **Phase change** problems
5. **Compressibility effects** (shock waves, expansions)

### 2.3 Physical Mechanisms in the Energy Equation

The energy equation accounts for the following physical processes:

- **Convection:** Transport of energy by bulk fluid motion $\nabla \cdot [(\rho E + p)\mathbf{V}]$
- **Conduction:** Heat transfer by molecular motion, governed by Fourier's law $q = -k\nabla T$
- **Viscous dissipation:** Conversion of mechanical energy to thermal energy by friction $\boldsymbol{\tau} : \nabla \mathbf{V}$
- **Pressure work:** Work done by pressure forces at the control surface
- **Body forces:** Work done by gravity and other body forces
- **Heat sources:** Volumetric or surface heat addition (e.g., chemical reactions, electrical heating)
- **Radiation:** Transport of energy by electromagnetic waves

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Applicable Flow Type |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------------|
| 1 | $E = e + \frac{1}{2} \vert \mathbf{V} \vert^2 + gz$ | Total specific energy | All flows |
| 2 | $e = c_v T$ | Internal energy (ideal gas) | Compressible flow |
| 3 | $h = c_p T$ | Specific enthalpy (ideal gas) | Compressible flow |
| 4 | $\rho \frac{DE}{Dt} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) - p \nabla \cdot \mathbf{V} + \Phi + S_h$ | Substantial derivative form | Viscous, heat conducting |
| 5 | $\rho c_p \frac{DT}{Dt} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \Phi + S_h$ | Temperature form (incompressible) | Incompressible, low Mach |
| 6 | $\rho \frac{Dh}{Dt} = \frac{Dp}{Dt} + \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \Phi + S_h$ | Enthalpy form | Compressible flow |
| 7 | $\Phi = \mu \left[ 2\left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\right)^2 + 2\left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial y}\right)^2 + 2\left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial z}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial u}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial x}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial v}{\partial z} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial y}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{\partial w}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial u}{\partial z}\right)^2 \right]$ | Viscous dissipation function | Viscous flow |
| 8 | $q = -k \nabla T$ | Fourier's law of heat conduction | Heat conducting |
| 9 | $a = \frac{k}{\rho c_p}$ | Thermal diffusivity $(\text{m}^2/\text{s})$ | All heat transfer |
| 10 | $\text{Pe} = \frac{\rho u L c_p}{k} = \text{Re} \cdot \text{Pr}$ | Peclet number | Convection analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols to remember:** $\Phi$ is the **viscous dissipation function** (always positive, dimensionless rate of energy conversion per unit mass). The Prandtl number $\text{Pr} = \mu c_p / k$ characterizes the relative thickness of thermal and velocity boundary layers.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The energy equation is the foundation of countless engineering applications:

- **Aerospace:** Re-entry vehicle thermal protection system (TPS) design, scramjet engine inlet analysis
- **Power Generation:** Boiler and steam turbine thermal analysis, nuclear reactor coolant design
- **HVAC:** Building ventilation, air conditioning duct design
- **Automotive:** Engine cooling jacket analysis, exhaust system design
- **Electronics:** CPU heat sink optimization, chip thermal management
- **Process Industries:** Heat exchanger design, chemical reactor thermal analysis
- **Meteorology:** Weather prediction models, atmospheric convection
- **Biomedical:** Blood flow heat transfer in hyperthermia treatment

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation from First Principles (Reynolds Transport Theorem Applied to Energy)

**Starting Point:** Apply the First Law of Thermodynamics to a fluid element of fixed mass (system approach).

For a system (Lagrangian view), the rate of change of total energy equals the rate of heat added plus the rate of work done:

$$
\frac{dE_{\text{system}}}{dt} = \dot{Q} + \dot{W}
$$

**Step 1:** Express the total energy of the fluid system. For a system of mass $M$ occupying volume $V(t)$:

$$
E_{\text{system}} = \int_{V(t)} \rho \left( e + \frac{1}{2} \vert \mathbf{V} \vert^2 + gz \right) dV
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Reynolds Transport Theorem (RTT) to convert from system (Lagrangian) to control volume (Eulerian):

$$
\frac{d}{dt} \int_{V(t)} \rho \phi \, dV = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{V_{cv}} \rho \phi \, dV + \oint_{S_{cv}} \rho \phi (\mathbf{V} \cdot \mathbf{n}) \, dA
$$

For $\phi = E = e + \frac{1}{2} \vert \mathbf{V} \vert^2 + gz$:

$$
\frac{dE_{\text{system}}}{dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{V} \rho E \, dV + \oint_{S} \rho E (\mathbf{V} \cdot \mathbf{n}) \, dA
$$

**Step 3:** Express the rate of heat addition $\dot{Q}$. This includes heat conduction across the surface and any volumetric heat sources:

$$
\dot{Q} = -\oint_{S} \mathbf{q} \cdot \mathbf{n} \, dA + \int_{V} S_h \, dV
$$

Using Fourier's law $\mathbf{q} = -k \nabla T$ and the divergence theorem:

$$
\dot{Q} = \int_{V} \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) \, dV + \int_{V} S_h \, dV
$$

**Step 4:** Express the rate of work done $\dot{W}$. This includes work by surface forces (pressure + viscous) and body forces (gravity):

$$
\dot{W} = -\oint_{S} p (\mathbf{V} \cdot \mathbf{n}) \, dA + \oint_{S} (\boldsymbol{\tau} \cdot \mathbf{V}) \cdot \mathbf{n} \, dA + \int_{V} \rho \mathbf{g} \cdot \mathbf{V} \, dV
$$

Applying the divergence theorem to the surface integrals:

$$
\dot{W} = -\int_{V} \nabla \cdot (p \mathbf{V}) \, dV + \int_{V} \nabla \cdot (\boldsymbol{\tau} \cdot \mathbf{V}) \, dV + \int_{V} \rho \mathbf{g} \cdot \mathbf{V} \, dV
$$

**Step 5:** Combine all the above into the integral energy equation. Equating RTT result to $\dot{Q} + \dot{W}$:

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_{V} \rho E \, dV + \oint_{S} \rho E (\mathbf{V} \cdot \mathbf{n}) \, dA = -\int_{V} \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) \, dV + \int_{V} S_h \, dV - \int_{V} \nabla \cdot (p \mathbf{V}) \, dV + \int_{V} \nabla \cdot (\boldsymbol{\tau} \cdot \mathbf{V}) \, dV + \int_{V} \rho \mathbf{g} \cdot \mathbf{V} \, dV
$$

**Step 6:** Move all terms to the left-hand side and combine:

$$
\int_{V} \left[ \frac{\partial (\rho E)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho E \mathbf{V}) + \nabla \cdot (p \mathbf{V}) - \nabla \cdot (\boldsymbol{\tau} \cdot \mathbf{V}) - \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) - \rho \mathbf{g} \cdot \mathbf{V} - S_h \right] dV = 0
$$

**Step 7:** Since the equation must hold for an arbitrary control volume, the integrand must be zero. This gives the **differential form of the energy equation**:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho E)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot \left[ (\rho E + p) \mathbf{V} \right] = \nabla \cdot (\boldsymbol{\tau} \cdot \mathbf{V}) + \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \rho \mathbf{g} \cdot \mathbf{V} + S_h
$$

This is the **general conservation form** of the energy equation used in CFD codes.

**Step 8:** Use the continuity equation to convert to the **non-conservative (substantial derivative) form**:

$$
\frac{\partial (\rho E)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho E \mathbf{V}) = \rho \frac{DE}{Dt}
$$

Therefore, the energy equation simplifies to:

$$
\rho \frac{DE}{Dt} = -p \nabla \cdot \mathbf{V} + \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \Phi + \rho \mathbf{g} \cdot \mathbf{V} + S_h
$$

where $\Phi = \boldsymbol{\tau} : \nabla \mathbf{V}$ is the viscous dissipation function.

### 3.2 Special Case: Incompressible Flow with Constant Properties

For **incompressible flow** ($\nabla \cdot \mathbf{V} = 0$) with constant $\rho$, $c_p$, and $k$, the energy equation simplifies dramatically.

Since $E = c_v T + \frac{1}{2} \vert \mathbf{V} \vert^2 + gz$ and $\rho \frac{DE}{Dt} = \rho c_v \frac{DT}{Dt} + \rho \frac{D}{Dt}\left( \frac{1}{2} \vert \mathbf{V} \vert^2 \right) + \rho g \frac{Dz}{Dt}$:

$$
\rho c_v \frac{DT}{Dt} + \rho \frac{D}{Dt}\left( \frac{1}{2} \vert \mathbf{V} \vert^2 \right) + \rho g \frac{Dz}{Dt} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \Phi + \rho \mathbf{g} \cdot \mathbf{V} + S_h
$$

Using the momentum equation to substitute for $\rho \frac{D\mathbf{V}}{Dt}$ and noting that $\mathbf{g} \cdot \mathbf{V} = g w$ for gravity in the $z$-direction, the kinetic and potential energy terms combine with the pressure work term to give:

$$
\rho c_p \frac{DT}{Dt} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \Phi + S_h
$$

This is the **standard form** used in most incompressible CFD codes (like SIMPLE algorithm in ANSYS Fluent).

### 3.3 Special Case: Compressible Flow — Enthalpy Form

For high-speed compressible flow, the **enthalpy form** is preferred. Defining $H = h + \frac{1}{2} \vert \mathbf{V} \vert^2 + gz$ (total enthalpy), we can show:

$$
\rho \frac{DH}{Dt} - \frac{\partial p}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \Phi + S_h
$$

For steady compressible flow, the **Crocco form** is often used:

$$
\rho \frac{D}{Dt}\left( h + \frac{1}{2} \vert \mathbf{V} \vert^2 \right) = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \Phi + \frac{\partial p}{\partial t}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Energy Equation Variations

| Flow Type | Equation Form | Key Assumptions | Typical Applications |
|-----------|---------------|-----------------|----------------------|
| Incompressible, inviscid | $\rho c_p \frac{DT}{Dt} = 0$ | Constant $\rho$, no dissipation | Idealized convection, Bernoulli |
| Incompressible, viscous | $\rho c_p \frac{DT}{Dt} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \Phi + S_h$ | Constant $\rho$ | Pipe flow, heat exchangers |
| Compressible, ideal gas | $\rho \frac{Dh}{Dt} = \frac{Dp}{Dt} + \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \Phi + S_h$ | Ideal gas law | Nozzles, shock waves |
| Compressible, adiabatic | $\rho \frac{Ds}{Dt} = \frac{\Phi}{T}$ | Adiabatic, no conduction | Supersonic aerodynamics |
| Compressible, isentropic | $\rho \frac{Ds}{Dt} = 0$ | Reversible, adiabatic | Ideal nozzle flow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Point:** The viscous dissipation function $\Phi$ is often neglected in low-speed flow (small Reynolds number gradients), but it becomes **critical** in high-speed aerodynamics where aerodynamic heating can cause structural damage (e.g., space shuttle re-entry).

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Energy Equation Components and Interactions

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the components of the energy equation and their interactions in a CFD solver:

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    C --> C5[Body Force Work rho g V]
    C --> C6[Heat Source Sh]

    A --> D[Governing Equation]
    D --> D1[Conservation Form]
    D --> D2[Substantial Derivative Form]

```

### 4.2 Process Flowchart: Derivation Sequence of the Energy Equation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: First Law of Thermodynamics dE_sys/dt = Q_dot + W_dot] --> S2[Step 2: Define Total Energy E = e + 0.5 V squared + gz]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Apply Reynolds Transport Theorem]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Express Heat Transfer by Fourier Law q = -k nabla T]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Express Work Done by Surface and Body Forces]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Apply Divergence Theorem]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Combine All Terms into Integral Form]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Use Arbitrary CV Argument to Get Differential Form]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Apply Continuity to Get Substantial Derivative Form]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Apply Assumptions for Simplified Cases]
    S10 --> S11[Final: rho cp DT/Dt = nabla dot k nabla T + Phi + Sh]
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Energy Conversion Pathways in a Flowing Fluid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PE[Potential Energy gz] -->|gravity| KE[Kinetic Energy 0.5 V squared]
    KE -->|viscous dissipation Phi| IE[Internal Energy cv T]
    PE -->|work| IE
    IE -->|expansion| PV[Pressure Volume Work p V]
    PV -->|flow work| KE
    IE -->|conduction| HEAT[Heat Transfer q]
    HEAT -->|Fourier law| IE
    HEAT -->|convection| TRANSPORT[Energy Transport rho E V]

    style PE fill:#ffcc99
    style KE fill:#99ccff
    style IE fill:#ff9999
    style HEAT fill:#99ff99
    style TRANSPORT fill:#cc99ff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The arrows represent energy conversion pathways. The energy equation is essentially a **balance sheet** that accounts for all these interchanges. Note that energy can neither be created nor destroyed (First Law), but it can be converted between forms.

### 4.4 Schematic: Physical Interpretation of the Energy Equation

The energy equation for a fluid element can be visualized as:

```
+----------------------+
|   INCOMING ENERGY    |  --> Convection: rho*V*E*A
|   - Convection       |  --> Conduction: -k*dT/dx*A
|   - Conduction       |  --> Pressure work: p*V*A
+----------------------+
         |
         v
+----------------------+
|  CHANGE IN STORAGE   |  --> Accumulation: d(rho*E*V)/dt
|  of Total Energy     |
+----------------------+
         |
         v
+----------------------+
|  OUTGOING ENERGY +   |  --> Same as incoming mechanisms
|  ENERGY SOURCES      |  --> Plus: S_h (heat source)
+----------------------+
```
