## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the generic form of the governing equations used in Computational Fluid Dynamics. Identify each physical term in the equation.**

**Model Answer:**

The generic form (conservation form) of the governing transport equation for a property $\phi$ in a fluid is given by:

$$\frac{\partial(\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi) + S_\phi$$

- **Transient term** $\partial(\rho \phi)/\partial t$: Local rate of change of $\phi$ per unit volume at a fixed point.
- **Convective term** $\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \phi)$: Transport of $\phi$ due to bulk fluid motion.
- **Diffusive term** $\nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi)$: Transport of $\phi$ due to molecular mixing, where $\Gamma_\phi$ is the diffusion coefficient.
- **Source term** $S_\phi$: Net volumetric generation rate of $\phi$.

This unified form allows a single numerical algorithm to solve all conservation laws by merely changing $\phi$, $\Gamma_\phi$, and $S_\phi$. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate between the conservation form and the non-conservation form of the governing equations. Which form is preferred in CFD and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Conservation Form | Non-Conservation Form |
|---|---|---|
| Mathematical structure | $\partial(\rho \phi)/\partial t + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}\phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla\phi) + S_\phi$ | $\rho D\phi/Dt = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla\phi) + S_\phi$ |
| Derivation | Direct from integral balance | Obtained by applying product rule + continuity |
| Physical meaning | Flux divergence form | Substantial-derivative form |
| Numerical property | Preserves integral conservation exactly | May not conserve across control volumes |

The **conservation form is preferred** in CFD (especially finite-volume method) because when discretized, it guarantees that the integral of $\phi$ over each cell equals the net flux across its faces plus sources — a property essential for accurate shock-capture and mass/momentum balance. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Starting from first principles, derive the continuity equation in differential form for a compressible fluid flowing through a fixed control volume. Express the result in the generic conservation form and identify $\phi$, $\Gamma_\phi$, and $S_\phi$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For the x-momentum equation of an incompressible Newtonian fluid with constant viscosity, write out all four terms of the generic equation explicitly. State the physical meaning of the source term.

**Model Solution for Q-A:**

**(a) Derivation of Continuity Equation:**

Consider a fixed rectangular control volume $d\forall = dx\,dy\,dz$. Let $\rho$ be the fluid density. The mass contained in the volume at time $t$ is:

$$m = \rho\, dx\,dy\,dz$$

By conservation of mass, the rate of accumulation of mass inside equals the negative of net mass efflux through all six faces.

**Net mass efflux in the x-direction:**

$$\left[\left(\rho u\right)_{x+dx/2} - \left(\rho u\right)_{x-dx/2}\right]dy\,dz$$

Taylor-expanding $\left(\rho u\right)_{x+dx/2} = \left(\rho u\right) + \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x}\frac{dx}{2}$ and similarly at $x - dx/2$:

$$\text{Net efflux in x} = \frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x}\,dx\,dy\,dz$$

Summing all three directions:

$$\text{Total efflux} = \left[\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z}\right]dx\,dy\,dz$$

The rate of mass accumulation is $\partial(\rho)/\partial t\, dx\,dy\,dz$. Setting accumulation = −efflux:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t}\,dx\,dy\,dz = -\left[\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w)}{\partial z}\right]dx\,dy\,dz$$

Dividing by $dx\,dy\,dz$ and rearranging:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

**Identification with generic form** ($\phi = 1$):

- $\phi = 1$
- $\Gamma_\phi = 0$
- $S_\phi = 0$
- Equation: $\frac{\partial(\rho \cdot 1)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \cdot 1) = 0$ ✓

**(b) X-Momentum Equation in Generic Form:**

For incompressible Newtonian flow with constant $\mu$:

$$\frac{\partial(\rho u)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} u) = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \nabla \cdot (\mu \nabla u) + \rho g_x$$

Explicit expansion of each term:

- **Transient:** $\partial(\rho u)/\partial t$
- **Convection:** $\frac{\partial(\rho u u)}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial(\rho v u)}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial(\rho w u)}{\partial z}$
- **Diffusion:** $\mu\left(\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial z^2}\right) = \mu \nabla^2 u$
- **Source:** $S_u = -\partial p/\partial x + \rho g_x$

The **physical meaning of the source term** is that it represents the **pressure-gradient force** (which drives the fluid from high to low pressure) plus the **body force per unit volume** (gravity in the x-direction).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the generic integral form of the governing transport equation starting from the differential form. State the Divergence Theorem explicitly.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a steady, one-dimensional, incompressible flow with constant $\Gamma_\phi$, simplify the generic equation and present the simplified form. Apply it to the case of fully-developed laminar flow between two parallel plates (plane Poiseuille flow) and obtain the velocity profile.

**Model Solution for Q-B:**

**(a) Derivation of Integral Form:**

Start with the generic differential form:

$$\frac{\partial(\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi) + S_\phi$$

**Step 1:** Integrate over a finite control volume $\forall$ bounded by surface $A$:

$$\int_{\forall}\frac{\partial(\rho \phi)}{\partial t}\,d\forall + \int_{\forall}\nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \phi)\,d\forall = \int_{\forall}\nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi)\,d\forall + \int_{\forall} S_\phi\,d\forall$$

**Step 2:** Apply the **Divergence Theorem (Gauss–Ostrogradsky Theorem):**

$$\int_{\forall}\nabla \cdot \vec{F}\,d\forall = \int_{A}\vec{F} \cdot \hat{n}\,dA$$

where $\hat{n}$ is the outward unit normal to surface $A$. Applying it to both divergence terms:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial t}\int_{\forall}\rho \phi\,d\forall + \int_{A}\rho \phi (\vec{V} \cdot \hat{n})\,dA = \int_{A}\Gamma_\phi (\nabla \phi \cdot \hat{n})\,dA + \int_{\forall} S_\phi\,d\forall$$

This is the **integral conservation form**, the direct basis of finite-volume discretization.

**(b) Steady 1-D Incompressible Flow Simplification:**

For steady ($\partial/\partial t = 0$), 1-D (only $x$-variation), incompressible ($\rho = $ const) flow:

- Continuity gives $du/dx = 0 \Rightarrow u = $ constant.
- The generic equation collapses to:

$$0 = \frac{d}{dx}\left(\Gamma_\phi \frac{d\phi}{dx}\right) + S_\phi$$

For plane Poiseuille flow, take $\phi = u$ (the velocity in the flow direction $x$), $\Gamma_\phi = \mu$, and $S_\phi = -dp/dx$ (pressure gradient driving force, constant).

$$0 = \mu \frac{d^2 u}{dy^2} - \frac{dp}{dx}$$

Integrate twice:

$$\frac{du}{dy} = \frac{1}{\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\,y + C_1$$

$$u(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\,y^2 + C_1 y + C_2$$

Apply no-slip boundary conditions at the plates $y = \pm h$: $u(\pm h) = 0$. By symmetry, $C_1 = 0$. Solving $u(h) = 0$:

$$C_2 = -\frac{1}{2\mu}\frac{dp}{dx}\,h^2$$

Final velocity profile (parabolic):

$$u(y) = \frac{1}{2\mu}\left(-\frac{dp}{dx}\right)\left(h^2 - y^2\right)$$

The maximum velocity at the centerline $y = 0$ is:

$$u_{\max} = \frac{h^2}{2\mu}\left(-\frac{dp}{dx}\right)$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake #1:** Students often write $\Gamma_\phi = \mu$ for energy as well, forgetting that for energy $\Gamma_h = k/c_p$, not $\mu$. Loss: **1 mark**.
> - **Common Mistake #2:** In the continuity derivation, students forget to apply the chain rule correctly when Taylor-expanding, leading to missing the density term inside the divergence. Loss: **2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake #3:** Confusing the substantial derivative $\frac{D\phi}{Dt}$ with the partial derivative $\partial \phi/\partial t$. The substantial derivative includes the convective part $\vec{V} \cdot \nabla \phi$. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> - **Common Mistake #4:** Forgetting that the generic form's source term $S_\phi$ is **per unit volume**, not the total source. Loss: **1 mark**.
> - **Time-Saver Tip:** Always write out all four generic terms explicitly in the answer — examiners award marks for explicit identification.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Generic equation (conservation form):** $\frac{\partial(\rho \phi)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} \phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma_\phi \nabla \phi) + S_\phi$.
- **Four canonical terms:** Transient, Convective, Diffusive, Source.
- **Substantial derivative:** $\frac{D}{Dt} = \frac{\partial}{\partial t} + \vec{V} \cdot \nabla$ converts Lagrangian to Eulerian view.
- **Continuity:** $\phi = 1$, $\Gamma_\phi = 0$, $S_\phi = 0$.
- **Momentum:** $\phi = u, v, w$, $\Gamma_\phi = \mu$, $S_\phi = -\nabla p + \rho \vec{g}$.
- **Energy:** $\phi = h$, $\Gamma_h = k/c_p$, $S_h$ = heat sources.
- **Conservation form preferred** in CFD because it preserves integral balance on discretized grids.
- **Divergence Theorem:** $\int_\forall \nabla \cdot \vec{F}\,d\forall = \int_A \vec{F} \cdot \hat{n}\,dA$ — used to obtain integral form.
- **Incompressible + steady simplification** reduces the equation to a 1-D ODE — useful for analytical benchmarking.
- **All conservation laws share the same generic structure** — only $\phi$, $\Gamma_\phi$, and $S_\phi$ change.
- **The generic form is the foundation** of finite-volume discretization (Module 2) — master it before proceeding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Discretization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Discretization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Discretization** is the process of transforming the continuous Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) governing fluid flow (mass, momentum, energy conservation) into a set of discrete algebraic equations that can be solved numerically on a digital computer using a finite number of algebraic operations.

In the context of **Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD)**, the Navier–Stokes equations are continuous, analytical statements valid at **every infinitesimally small point** in the flow domain. A digital computer, however, has a finite memory and finite processor speed, meaning it can only handle a **finite number of points** and a **finite number of equations**. Discretization is the mathematical bridge that converts the **infinite-dimensional continuous problem** into a **finite-dimensional numerical problem**.

Mathematically, this can be summarized as:

$$
\text{Continuous PDE (infinite unknowns)} \xrightarrow{\text{Discretization}} \text{Discrete Algebraic System (N unknowns)}
$$

where **N** is the finite number of grid points (nodes or cells) chosen by the CFD analyst.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PECHT635 – Module 2):**  
> The "Introduction to Discretization" topic establishes the **conceptual foundation** for the three principal numerical techniques used in CFD: the **Finite Difference Method (FDM)**, the **Finite Volume Method (FVM)**, and the **Finite Element Method (FEM)**. A clear grasp of the discretization philosophy is mandatory before any of these methods can be studied.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a beautiful painting printed on a very large canvas, and you want to reproduce it on a laptop screen. The original painting is **continuous** – it has an infinite number of color shades and an infinite number of brush strokes. But your laptop screen is made up of only a **finite number of pixels** – say, 1920 × 1080 dots.

To reproduce the painting, you must:
1. **Divide** the canvas into a finite grid of dots.
2. **Sample** the color at each dot.
3. **Approximate** the colors in between using interpolation.

That is exactly what **discretization** does to a fluid flow problem. The continuous fluid domain (the canvas) is replaced by a **finite mesh of cells or nodes** (the pixels), and the continuous PDEs are **sampled and approximated** at each of these discrete locations.

**Another simple analogy:** Think of recording a song. The actual sound wave is continuous in time. A digital recorder can only store sound as a series of snapshots taken at regular time intervals (e.g., 44,100 times per second). The higher the sampling rate, the closer the digital song is to the original – just as a finer mesh gives a more accurate CFD solution.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Idea:** The "mesh" or "grid" is the heart of discretization. It is the discrete set of points (and the connectivity between them) at which the governing equations are evaluated.

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of Discretization in CFD

Modern CFD is built on **three classical discretization techniques**, each approaching the approximation of the governing equations from a different angle:

1. **Finite Difference Method (FDM)** – The oldest and most intuitive. Derivatives in the PDE are directly replaced by **Taylor-series-based difference approximations** at grid nodes.
2. **Finite Volume Method (FVM)** – The workhorse of commercial CFD codes (ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM). The governing equations are integrated over **small control volumes**, naturally enforcing **conservation**.
3. **Finite Element Method (FEM)** – Originally developed for solid mechanics, widely used for complex geometries. The solution is expressed as a **weighted sum of basis (shape) functions** defined over the elements.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Definition – Grid / Mesh:**  
> A *grid* (or *mesh*) is a discrete set of points, lines, surfaces, and/or volumes that partition the continuous fluid domain into a finite number of sub-domains over which the governing equations are numerically solved.

## 1.4 The Discretization Pipeline (At a Glance)

The complete journey from a physical problem to a numerical solution is:

$$
\text{Physical Problem} \rightarrow \text{Mathematical Model (PDE)} \rightarrow \text{Discretization} \rightarrow \text{Algebraic System} \rightarrow \text{Solution}
$$

This Module 2 study material focuses exclusively on the **third arrow** – the **discretization step** – which is the heart of CFD.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Discretization is Necessary

A digital computer cannot:
- Represent an *infinity* of points.
- Perform an *infinite* number of operations.
- Store a *continuous* function in memory.

Therefore, any **continuous** mathematical model (e.g., the Navier–Stokes equation) must be converted into a **discrete** numerical model. This conversion is what we call **discretization**.

The motivations are:

- **Computational Feasibility:** A computer can only handle finite, countable unknowns.
- **Error Quantification:** Discretization produces algebraic equations whose truncation and round-off errors can be systematically analyzed.
- **Geometric Flexibility:** Real engineering domains (airfoils, pipes, heat exchangers) have curved boundaries that must be approximated by polygonal/polyhedral meshes.

## 2.2 The Core Steps of Discretization

The discretization process can be broken down into **five logical stages**:

1. **Pre-Processing (Domain Definition):** Define the geometry of the fluid domain and identify the boundary conditions.
2. **Grid Generation (Meshing):** Partition the continuous domain into a finite number of small sub-regions (cells/elements/nodes).
3. **Equation Discretization:** Replace the continuous differential operators (derivatives) with discrete algebraic approximations (difference quotients, volume integrals, weighted residuals, etc.).
4. **Assembly of Algebraic System:** Formulate the resulting algebraic equations into a system (linear or non-linear) of the form $A \mathbf{x} = \mathbf{b}$.
5. **Solution & Post-Processing:** Solve the algebraic system iteratively or directly, then visualize and interpret the results.

> [!NOTE]  
> The first two steps (pre-processing and grid generation) are often discussed separately. The "discretization" topic in this module emphasizes **Step 3 – the mathematical conversion of the PDE into algebraic form**.

## 2.3 Components of a Computational Grid

A typical CFD grid consists of:

- **Node (Grid Point):** A discrete point in the domain where the flow variables (velocity, pressure, temperature) are computed.
- **Cell / Element / Control Volume:** A small sub-region of the domain bounded by faces (in 2D) or surfaces (in 3D).
- **Face / Edge:** The boundary between two adjacent cells.
- **Vertex:** A corner point of a cell.
- **Neighbour Cells:** Cells that share a common face/edge.

## 2.4 Classification of Grids (Mesh Types)

Grids are broadly classified into two families:

| Grid Type | Description | Pros | Cons | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Structured Grid** | Grid lines have a regular, i–j–k indexing. Each node has a fixed number of neighbours identified by a logical index. | Easy to generate, efficient data storage, simple algorithms. | Difficult for complex geometries. | Simple ducts, channels, canonical flows. |
| **Unstructured Grid** | No regular indexing; cells/nodes are stored in arbitrary connectivity (typically using lists). | Handles very complex geometries. | More memory, more complex algorithms. | Aerodynamic bodies, biomedical flows. |
| **Hybrid Grid** | Combines structured grid (near walls) with unstructured grid (far field). | Best of both worlds. | More complex to generate. | Aerospace, turbomachinery. |

A sub-classification of structured grids includes:

- **O-grid** (e.g., around an airfoil)
- **H-grid** (e.g., simple Cartesian)
- **C-grid** (e.g., around an airfoil leading edge)

## 2.5 Grid Spacing and Uniformity

- **Uniform Grid:** The spacing $\Delta x$, $\Delta y$, $\Delta z$ between adjacent nodes is constant throughout the domain.
- **Non-Uniform Grid:** The spacing varies – typically **fine** (small $\Delta$) near regions of high gradients (walls, shocks) and **coarse** (large $\Delta$) elsewhere.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Engineering Best Practice:**  
> Always use a **non-uniform grid clustered near solid walls** to capture the steep velocity and temperature gradients in boundary layers. This is called **grid clustering** or **grid stretching**.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> The following table summarizes the foundational formulas and concepts that every KTU student must commit to memory for this topic.

| Concept / Symbol | Mathematical Expression / Definition | Engineering Meaning / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Continuous domain $\Omega$ | $\Omega \subset \mathbb{R}^d$, with $d = 1, 2, 3$ | The physical fluid region. |
| Discrete grid points $x_i$ | $x_i = i \cdot \Delta x$, $i = 0, 1, 2, \dots, N$ | Indexed node positions. |
| Grid spacing | $\Delta x, \Delta y, \Delta z$ (uniform) or $\Delta x_i$ (non-uniform) | Distance between adjacent nodes; units: **m**. |
| Number of intervals | $N$ | Integer. |
| Function value at node | $\phi_i = \phi(x_i)$ | Approximate solution. |
| **Order of accuracy** | A discretization is order $p$ if the truncation error is $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^p)$. | Higher $p \Rightarrow$ better accuracy for the same $\Delta x$. |
| Taylor series (forward) | $\phi_{i+1} = \phi_i + \Delta x \left.\dfrac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i + \dfrac{\Delta x^2}{2}\left.\dfrac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}\right\vert_i + \cdots$ | Foundation of finite difference formulas. |
| First derivative (forward) | $\left.\dfrac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i \approx \dfrac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i}{\Delta x} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ | First-order accurate. |
| First derivative (central) | $\left.\dfrac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i \approx \dfrac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2\Delta x} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ | **Second-order accurate** – the workhorse. |
| Second derivative (central) | $\left.\dfrac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}\right\vert_i \approx \dfrac{\phi_{i+1} - 2\phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ | Central second-order, used for diffusion terms. |
| Total truncation error | $\tau = \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^p)$ | Where $p$ is the formal order. |
| Reynolds number (reminder) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho U L}{\mu}$ | A non-dimensional number governing flow regime. |
| Mesh independence | Solution does not change appreciably upon further grid refinement. | **Mandatory validation step** in CFD. |

> [!WARNING]  
> **Pipe character prohibition:** In KTU answer scripts and in this formula sheet, absolute values are written using $\lvert \cdot \rvert$ or $\mathrm{abs}(\cdot)$. The raw pipe `|` would break the markdown table.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility of Discretization

Discretization is the foundation upon which engineers design:

- **Aircraft wings and turbomachinery blades** (aerodynamic optimization).
- **Pipelines and chemical reactors** (flow distribution analysis).
- **Heat exchangers and electronic cooling** (conjugate heat transfer).
- **Biomedical devices** (blood flow in stents, airways, arteries).
- **Weather prediction and ocean modelling** (geophysical flows).
- **Automotive aerodynamics** (drag reduction, fuel efficiency).

Without discretization, none of these analyses could be carried out on a computer.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]  
> This section provides the **exhaustive Taylor-series derivations** of the foundational finite-difference approximations. These are absolutely essential for KTU ESE questions in CFD.

## 3.1 Foundation: The Taylor Series Expansion

Let $\phi(x)$ be a smooth, continuously differentiable function. Its Taylor series expansion about a point $x_i$ is:

$$
\phi(x_i + \Delta x) = \phi(x_i) + \Delta x \left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2!} \left.\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}\right\vert_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{3!} \left.\frac{d^3\phi}{dx^3}\right\vert_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
$$

**Explanation of symbols:**
- $\phi(x_i)$ is the value of the function at node $i$.
- $\Delta x$ is the grid spacing.
- $\left.\dfrac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i$ is the first derivative at node $i$.
- The notation $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^n)$ means "terms of order $\Delta x^n$ and higher".

## 3.2 Derivation of the Forward Difference Approximation

We wish to approximate the first derivative $\left.\dfrac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i$ using the values $\phi_i$ and $\phi_{i+1}$.

**Step 1:** Write the Taylor expansion of $\phi_{i+1}$ about $x_i$:

$$
\phi_{i+1} = \phi_i + \Delta x \left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} \left.\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}\right\vert_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \left.\frac{d^3\phi}{dx^3}\right\vert_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the first derivative:

$$
\left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i = \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i}{\Delta x} - \frac{\Delta x}{2} \left.\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}\right\vert_i - \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} \left.\frac{d^3\phi}{dx^3}\right\vert_i - \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^3)
$$

**Step 3:** Truncate the higher-order terms. The leading term that is dropped is $\dfrac{\Delta x}{2} \left.\dfrac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}\right\vert_i$, which is $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$:

$$
\left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i \approx \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i}{\Delta x} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x)
$$

**Result – Forward Difference Formula:**

$$
\boxed{\left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i \approx \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i}{\Delta x}}
$$

**Order of accuracy:** $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ – **first-order accurate**.

## 3.3 Derivation of the Backward Difference Approximation

**Step 1:** Expand $\phi_{i-1}$ about $x_i$ (note: $\Delta x$ becomes $-\Delta x$):

$$
\phi_{i-1} = \phi_i - \Delta x \left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} \left.\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}\right\vert_i - \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \left.\frac{d^3\phi}{dx^3}\right\vert_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the first derivative:

$$
\left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i = \frac{\phi_i - \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x} + \frac{\Delta x}{2} \left.\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}\right\vert_i - \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} \left.\frac{d^3\phi}{dx^3}\right\vert_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^3)
$$

**Step 3:** Truncate to obtain:

$$
\boxed{\left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i \approx \frac{\phi_i - \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x)}
$$

**Order of accuracy:** $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ – **first-order accurate**.

## 3.4 Derivation of the Central Difference Approximation

**Step 1:** Write two Taylor expansions: one forward, one backward.

$$
\phi_{i+1} = \phi_i + \Delta x \left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} \left.\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}\right\vert_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \left.\frac{d^3\phi}{dx^3}\right\vert_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
$$

$$
\phi_{i-1} = \phi_i - \Delta x \left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} \left.\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}\right\vert_i - \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \left.\frac{d^3\phi}{dx^3}\right\vert_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
$$

**Step 2:** Subtract the second equation from the first:

$$
\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1} = 2 \Delta x \left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i + 2 \cdot \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \left.\frac{d^3\phi}{dx^3}\right\vert_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)
$$

**Step 3:** Divide by $2\Delta x$:

$$
\left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i = \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2 \Delta x} - \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} \left.\frac{d^3\phi}{dx^3}\right\vert_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
$$

**Step 4:** Truncate. The leading dropped term is $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$:

$$
\boxed{\left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i \approx \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2 \Delta x}}
$$

**Order of accuracy:** $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ – **second-order accurate**, superior to forward/backward.

## 3.5 Derivation of the Central Second-Derivative Approximation

**Step 1:** Add the two Taylor expansions of §3.4:

$$
\phi_{i+1} + \phi_{i-1} = 2 \phi_i + \Delta x^2 \left.\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}\right\vert_i + 2 \cdot \frac{\Delta x^4}{24} \left.\frac{d^4\phi}{dx^4}\right\vert_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^6)
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the second derivative:

$$
\left.\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}\right\vert_i = \frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2 \phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} - \frac{\Delta x^2}{12} \left.\frac{d^4\phi}{dx^4}\right\vert_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
$$

**Step 3:** Truncate. Leading dropped term is $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$:

$$
\boxed{\left.\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}\right\vert_i \approx \frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2 \phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2}}
$$

**Order of accuracy:** $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ – **second-order accurate**, used for diffusion terms.

## 3.6 Worked Example: Discretizing a 1-D Steady Convection-Diffusion Equation

**Problem:** Discretize the 1-D steady convection–diffusion equation using the central difference scheme for both first and second derivatives.

**PDE:**

$$
u \frac{d\phi}{dx} = \nu \frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}
$$

where $u$ is the constant velocity, $\nu$ is the constant diffusivity, and $\phi(x)$ is the transported scalar (e.g., temperature).

**Step 1 – Apply the central difference for the first derivative:**

$$
u \frac{d\phi}{dx} \approx u \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2 \Delta x}
$$

**Step 2 – Apply the central difference for the second derivative:**

$$
\nu \frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} \approx \nu \frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2 \phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2}
$$

**Step 3 – Equate the two sides:**

$$
u \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2 \Delta x} = \nu \frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2 \phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2}
$$

**Step 4 – Multiply through by $\Delta x^2$:**

$$
\frac{u \Delta x}{2} \left(\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}\right) = \nu \left(\phi_{i+1} - 2 \phi_i + \phi_{i-1}\right)
$$

**Step 5 – Define the (local) Péclet number, $Pe = \dfrac{u \Delta x}{\nu}$, and rearrange:**

$$
\left(\frac{Pe}{2} - 1\right) \phi_{i+1} + 2 \phi_i + \left(-\frac{Pe}{2} - 1\right) \phi_{i-1} = 0
$$

**Step 6 – Standard three-point stencil form $a_W \phi_W + a_P \phi_P + a_E \phi_E = 0$:**

$$
a_W = -\left(\frac{Pe}{2} + 1\right), \quad a_P = 2, \quad a_E = \left(\frac{Pe}{2} - 1\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Physical Insight:** The cell Péclet number $Pe = u \Delta x / \nu$ controls the **stability** and **accuracy** of the central difference scheme. If $Pe > 2$, the central difference scheme produces **unphysical oscillations** in the solution, and a **upwind scheme** is preferred.

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Discretization Schemes

| Scheme | Formula for $d\phi/dx$ | Order | Stability for Convection-Diffusion | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Forward Difference (FDS) | $\dfrac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i}{\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ | Stable but diffusive | Rare in modern CFD. |
| Backward Difference (BDS) | $\dfrac{\phi_i - \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ | Stable but diffusive | Upwind-like behaviour. |
| Central Difference (CDS) | $\dfrac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2 \Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ | Unstable for $Pe > 2$ | Low-Péclet-number flows. |
| Upwind Scheme | Uses upwind value based on flow direction. | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ | Always stable | High $Pe$, aerospace, MHD. |
| QUICK | Quadratic upstream interpolation. | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ or $3$ | Stable | High-quality production CFD. |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Discretization in CFD

The figure below depicts the **complete workflow of CFD discretization**, from the physical problem to the numerical solution. Each block represents a stage in the process, and the arrows represent the flow of information.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[PHYSICAL PROBLEM] --> A1[Mathematical Model PDE]
    A1 --> A2{Choose Numerical Method}
    A2 -->|Method 1| A3a[Finite Difference Method FDM]
    A2 -->|Method 2| A3b[Finite Volume Method FVM]
    A2 -->|Method 3| A3c[Finite Element Method FEM]
    A3a --> A4[Grid Generation]
    A3b --> A4[Grid Generation]
    A3c --> A4[Grid Generation]
    A4 --> A5{Mesh Type Decision}
    A5 -->|Structured| A6a[Uniform OR Stretched Grid]
    A5 -->|Unstructured| A6b[Triangular OR Tetrahedral]
    A5 -->|Hybrid| A6c[Prism Layers Plus Tetrahedral]
    A6a --> A7[Apply Discretization Formulas]
    A6b --> A7[Apply Discretization Formulas]
    A6c --> A7[Apply Discretization Formulas]
    A7 --> A8[Build Algebraic System]
    A8 --> A9[Solve Linear or Nonlinear System]
    A9 --> A10[Post Processing Visualization]
    A10 --> A11[Validate Using Mesh Independence Study]
    A11 -->|Not Converged| A4
    A11 -->|Converged| A12[FINAL NUMERICAL SOLUTION]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The flow begins at the **Physical Problem** (top left).
- A choice is made among **FDM, FVM, FEM** – all three share the same downstream steps.
- The **Mesh Type Decision** branch captures structured, unstructured, and hybrid grids.
- The **loop back** from "Validate" to "Grid Generation" reflects the iterative **mesh refinement** required for a mesh-independent solution.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: 1-D Grid and Stencil for Central Difference

The following diagram shows a one-dimensional domain divided into discrete nodes, highlighting the **three-point stencil** used by the central difference scheme.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Node i minus 1] -->|dx| N2[Node i]
    N2 -->|dx| N3[Node i plus 1]
    N2 --> S1[Stencil at Node i]
    N1 --> S1
    N3 --> S1
    S1 --> O1[Approximation: dphi/dx approx phi i plus 1 minus phi i minus 1 over 2 dx]
    S1 --> O2[Approximation: d2phi/dx2 approx phi i plus 1 minus 2 phi i plus phi i minus 1 over dx squared]
```

**Engineering meaning:**
- The **stencil** is the set of nodes involved in a discrete approximation.
- A **three-point stencil** is the simplest second-order accurate stencil in 1-D.
- Wider stencils (five-point, seven-point) yield higher-order accuracy at the cost of more neighbouring information.

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Trade-off Triangle in Discretization

Every CFD analyst must balance three competing demands. This is often depicted as a "triangle of trade-offs".

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[ACCURACY] --- T2[COMPUTATIONAL COST]
    T2 --- T3[STABILITY]
    T3 --- T1
    T1 -.->|Requires fine grid| T2
    T2 -.->|Restricts time step| T3
    T3 -.->|Drives scheme choice| T1
```

**Key insights:**
- **Finer grid** $\Rightarrow$ higher accuracy but higher computational cost.
- **Larger time step** $\Rightarrow$ cheaper but may violate stability limits (e.g., the CFL condition).
- **Higher-order scheme** $\Rightarrow$ more accurate per node but often less stable; a stable low-order scheme may be safer.

## 4.4 Schematic of a 2-D Structured Grid (Node Indexing)

The figure below sketches a small portion of a 2-D structured grid. Each node is identified by a pair of indices $(i, j)$.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph GR[Structured Grid 2D]
        direction TB
        R0[Node i minus 1 j plus 1] --- R1[Node i j plus 1] --- R2[Node i plus 1 j plus 1]
        R0 --- R3[Node i minus 1 j]
        R1 --- R4[Node i j]
        R2 --- R5[Node i plus 1 j]
        R3 --- R4
        R4 --- R5
        R3 --- R6[Node i minus 1 j minus 1]
        R4 --- R7[Node i j minus 1]
        R5 --- R8[Node i plus 1 j minus 1]
        R6 --- R7
        R7 --- R8
    end
    R4 --> STENCIL[Five Point Stencil around Node i j]
    R1 --> STENCIL
    R3 --> STENCIL
    R5 --> STENCIL
    R7 --> STENCIL
```

**Engineering meaning:**  
In 2-D, the central difference for the Laplacian $\nabla^2 \phi$ uses a **five-point stencil**: the centre node $(i, j)$ and its four immediate neighbours $(i\pm 1, j)$ and $(i, j\pm 1)$. This is the workhorse of 2-D finite difference / finite volume CFD codes.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define discretization. Why is it required in CFD?**

**Model Answer:**

**Discretization** is the process of converting the continuous governing partial differential equations (PDEs) of fluid flow into a set of discrete algebraic equations that can be solved numerically on a computer.

**Why it is required:**
1. Computers can only handle a finite number of unknowns – the continuous problem has an infinite number.
2. The continuous PDE must be sampled at a finite number of grid points.
3. Discretization enables systematic analysis of truncation and round-off errors.
4. It makes numerical solutions tractable for complex engineering geometries.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Differentiate between structured and unstructured grids.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Structured Grid | Unstructured Grid |
|---|---|---|
| **Indexing** | Regular i–j–k indexing. | Arbitrary connectivity. |
| **Neighbour Identification** | Trivial (index arithmetic). | Requires neighbour lists. |
| **Memory** | Low. | Higher. |
| **Geometry Flexibility** | Poor for complex shapes. | Excellent for complex shapes. |
| **Algorithm Complexity** | Simple. | More complex. |
| **Example** | H-grid, O-grid. | Triangular / tetrahedral mesh. |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) – Module Internal Choice

### Question B-A. `[KTU University Exam - Module Choice, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** Derive the **central difference approximation** for the first derivative $\dfrac{d\phi}{dx}$ using the Taylor series expansion. Show that the truncation error is $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Using the central difference scheme, **discretize the 1-D steady convection-diffusion equation** $u \dfrac{d\phi}{dx} = \nu \dfrac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}$ and write the resulting three-point stencil equation in terms of the cell Péclet number $Pe = \dfrac{u \Delta x}{\nu}$. Discuss the stability condition. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1:** Write the forward Taylor expansion of $\phi_{i+1}$ about $x_i$:

$$
\phi_{i+1} = \phi_i + \Delta x \left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} \left.\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}\right\vert_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \left.\frac{d^3\phi}{dx^3}\right\vert_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
$$

**Step 2:** Write the backward Taylor expansion of $\phi_{i-1}$ about $x_i$:

$$
\phi_{i-1} = \phi_i - \Delta x \left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i + \frac{\Delta x^2}{2} \left.\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}\right\vert_i - \frac{\Delta x^3}{6} \left.\frac{d^3\phi}{dx^3}\right\vert_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
$$

**Step 3:** Subtract Eq. (2) from Eq. (1):

$$
\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1} = 2 \Delta x \left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i + \frac{\Delta x^3}{3} \left.\frac{d^3\phi}{dx^3}\right\vert_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)
$$

**Step 4:** Divide by $2 \Delta x$:

$$
\left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i = \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2 \Delta x} - \frac{\Delta x^2}{6} \left.\frac{d^3\phi}{dx^3}\right\vert_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
$$

**Step 5:** Truncate:

$$
\boxed{\left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right\vert_i \approx \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2 \Delta x}}
$$

The leading dropped term is $\dfrac{\Delta x^2}{6} \left.\dfrac{d^3\phi}{dx^3}\right\vert_i$, which is $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$. Hence the **central difference scheme is second-order accurate**.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Discretize the first derivative using CDS:

$$
u \frac{d\phi}{dx} \approx u \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2 \Delta x}
$$

**Step 2:** Discretize the second derivative using CDS:

$$
\nu \frac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} \approx \nu \frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2 \phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2}
$$

**Step 3:** Equate:

$$
u \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2 \Delta x} = \nu \frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2 \phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2}
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply by $\Delta x^2$ and group:

$$
\left(\frac{u \Delta x}{2} - \nu\right) \phi_{i+1} + 2 \nu \, \phi_i + \left(-\frac{u \Delta x}{2} - \nu\right) \phi_{i-1} = 0
$$

**Step 5:** Introduce $Pe = \dfrac{u \Delta x}{\nu}$:

$$
\left(\frac{Pe}{2} - 1\right) \phi_{i+1} + 2 \phi_i - \left(\frac{Pe}{2} + 1\right) \phi_{i-1} = 0
$$

**Stability discussion:**  
The central difference scheme for convection–diffusion is **unstable (produces unphysical oscillations)** when the cell Péclet number satisfies $\lvert Pe \rvert > 2$. For high $Pe$ (convection-dominated flows), the **upwind scheme** or **exponential scheme** is preferred.

---

### Question B-B. `[KTU University Exam - Module Choice, 14 Marks]`

**(a)** What is a **computational grid (mesh)**? Classify grids with neat sketches (block diagrams) and explain the merits of structured and unstructured grids. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **second-order central difference approximation** for the second derivative $\dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2}$ and hence write the discrete form of the 1-D steady **pure diffusion equation** $\nu \dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = 0$ on a uniform grid. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A **computational grid (mesh)** is a finite, discrete partition of the continuous fluid domain into nodes, edges, faces, and cells/elements over which the governing PDEs are numerically solved.

**Classification of grids:**

1. **Structured Grid** – Regular i–j–k indexing. Each interior node has a fixed number of well-defined neighbours. Easy to generate, low memory, simple algorithms. Limitation: difficult to fit complex curved boundaries.

2. **Unstructured Grid** – No regular indexing. Cell connectivity is stored in a list. Highly flexible for complex geometries. Higher memory overhead.

3. **Hybrid Grid** – Combines structured near-wall regions (prism layers) with unstructured far-field cells.

**Merits comparison** (refer to Question A2 table above).

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1:** Add the two Taylor expansions from §3.4:

$$
\phi_{i+1} + \phi_{i-1} = 2 \phi_i + \Delta x^2 \left.\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}\right\vert_i + \frac{\Delta x^4}{12} \left.\frac{d^4\phi}{dx^4}\right\vert_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^6)
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for the second derivative:

$$
\left.\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}\right\vert_i = \frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2\phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} - \frac{\Delta x^2}{12} \left.\frac{d^4\phi}{dx^4}\right\vert_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)
$$

**Step 3:** Truncate:

$$
\boxed{\left.\frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}\right\vert_i \approx \frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2\phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2}}
$$

Truncation error: $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$.

**Step 4 – Discretize the 1-D diffusion equation $\nu \dfrac{d^2 \phi}{dx^2} = 0$:**

$$
\nu \frac{\phi_{i+1} - 2\phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2} = 0
$$

Since $\nu \neq 0$ and $\Delta x^2 \neq 0$:

$$
\boxed{\phi_{i+1} - 2 \phi_i + \phi_{i-1} = 0 \quad \text{for all interior nodes}}
$$

This is a **tridiagonal linear system**. For a domain of $N$ interior nodes, with Dirichlet boundary conditions $\phi_0 = \phi_A$ and $\phi_{N+1} = \phi_B$, the solution is the **exact linear profile** $\phi(x) = \phi_A + \dfrac{x - x_0}{x_{N+1} - x_0} (\phi_B - \phi_A)$, as expected from analytical theory.

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**  
> 1. **Forgetting the truncation term:** Many students write only the difference formula and forget to mention the **order of accuracy** ($\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ or $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$). This alone costs **1–2 marks**.  
> 2. **Sign errors in Taylor expansions:** When expanding backward, students often miss the alternating signs ($+\Delta x^2/2$ in both forward and backward, but $-\Delta x$ in the linear term of backward expansion).  
> 3. **Confusing forward with backward:** Forward uses $\phi_{i+1}$; backward uses $\phi_{i-1}$. Mis-labeling is a common error.  
> 4. **Forgetting the Péclet number stability condition** for convection–diffusion – this is a **favourite KTU 14-mark sub-question**.  
> 5. **Missing units and physical interpretation:** Always state the physical meaning (e.g., "second derivative represents diffusion flux gradient").

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]  
> The following is a **high-density, rapid-revision summary** of the entire "Introduction to Discretization" topic. KTU students should be able to reproduce this list from memory.

- **Discretization** converts continuous PDEs $\rightarrow$ discrete algebraic equations.
- The three core CFD discretization methods are **FDM, FVM, FEM**.
- The **finite difference method** is the simplest; it operates on Taylor series.
- **Forward difference** for $d\phi/dx$: $\dfrac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i}{\Delta x}$ – first-order, $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$.
- **Backward difference** for $d\phi/dx$: $\dfrac{\phi_i - \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x}$ – first-order, $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$.
- **Central difference** for $d\phi/dx$: $\dfrac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_{i-1}}{2\Delta x}$ – second-order, $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$.
- **Central difference** for $d^2\phi/dx^2$: $\dfrac{\phi_{i+1} - 2\phi_i + \phi_{i-1}}{\Delta x^2}$ – second-order, $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$.
- The **stencil** is the set of nodes involved in a discrete approximation.
- In 1-D, a second-order stencil uses **3 points**; in 2-D, the Laplacian uses a **5-point stencil**.
- **Structured grid**: regular i–j–k indexing. Best for simple geometries.
- **Unstructured grid**: arbitrary connectivity. Best for complex geometries.
- **Hybrid grid**: combines structured near-wall with unstructured far-field.
- **Grid clustering** near walls is essential to capture boundary-layer gradients.
- **Mesh independence** is the validation test that ensures the solution does not change upon further refinement.
- The **cell Péclet number** $Pe = u \Delta x / \nu$ governs the **stability** of the central difference scheme for convection–diffusion.
- **Central difference is unstable** for convection-dominated flows ($\lvert Pe \rvert > 2$).
- The **upwind scheme** is preferred for high $Pe$ – it is unconditionally stable but only first-order accurate.
- **QUICK** and other higher-order schemes (e.g., MUSCL) bridge the gap between accuracy and stability.
- The complete CFD workflow: **Pre-processing $\rightarrow$ Meshing $\rightarrow$ Discretization $\rightarrow$ Solution $\rightarrow$ Post-processing**.
- **Order of accuracy** $p$: a discretization is order $p$ if its truncation error is $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^p)$.
- Always **state the order** of accuracy explicitly in any KTU answer.
- The **discretization step** is the heart of CFD – without it, no numerical solution is possible.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Finite Difference Approximation of a Derivative
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Finite Difference Approximation of a Derivative

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Finite Difference Approximation (FDA)** is the foundational numerical technique in Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) used to approximate derivatives of continuous functions using discrete data points sampled from a computational grid. It replaces the analytical (infinitesimal) definition of a derivative with a finite algebraic expression constructed from neighboring nodal values.

The analytical derivative of a function $f(x)$ at a point $x$ is defined by the limit:

$$\frac{df}{dx} = \lim_{\Delta x \to 0} \frac{f(x+\Delta x) - f(x)}{\Delta x}$$

In **CFD discretization**, we cannot evaluate this limit. Instead, we choose a finite spacing $\Delta x$ (the grid step) and approximate the derivative using a **difference quotient**. This process is the first step in converting a partial differential equation (PDE) governing fluid flow into a system of algebraic equations solvable on a computer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Module 2 Outcome:**
> After this topic, students must be able to **derive and apply forward, backward, and central difference approximations**, identify their **order of accuracy**, and compute the **truncation error** using Taylor series expansion. This is a direct, high-weightage KTU exam topic that appears almost every semester in Part A (3 marks) and frequently in Part B (14 marks).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are driving a car along a winding hill road, and your **speedometer** (derivative of position w.r.t. time) is broken. The only information you have is the **odometer reading at every kilometer marker**. 

- **Forward Difference**: You estimate your speed by looking at *how far you will travel* in the next kilometer — like predicting tomorrow's weather from today's data. You are "looking ahead."
- **Backward Difference**: You estimate your speed from the *previous* kilometer — like reviewing yesterday's travel. You are "looking behind."
- **Central Difference**: You estimate speed from the **average** of the previous and next kilometer readings — this is the most accurate odometer trick because it cancels out symmetrical errors, just as a balanced weighing scale gives a more precise reading.

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Intuition:** The **grid spacing $\Delta x$** is like the "ruler length" with which you measure the slope. A *smaller* ruler (finer grid) gives a *more accurate* slope estimate, but requires more computing power. CFD is fundamentally the art of choosing the right ruler length.

---

## 1.3 Significance in CFD

In CFD, the governing equations (Navier-Stokes, continuity, energy) are **partial differential equations (PDEs)** containing derivatives like $\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}$, $\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial y^2}$, etc. To solve them numerically:

1. The **continuous domain** is replaced by a **discrete grid of nodes**.
2. Each derivative is replaced by a **finite difference formula**.
3. The PDE becomes a system of **linear/non-linear algebraic equations**.
4. These equations are solved iteratively or directly.

The choice of finite difference scheme directly controls:
- **Accuracy** (how close the numerical solution is to the exact solution).
- **Stability** (whether errors grow or decay as the solution evolves).
- **Conservation** (whether mass, momentum, and energy are preserved).

---

## 1.4 Types of Derivatives Approximated

| Derivative Type | Physical Meaning | CFD Example |
|:---|:---|:---|
| First-order spatial $\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}$ | Velocity gradient | Boundary layer shear |
| Second-order spatial $\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$ | Curvature / diffusion | Viscous diffusion term |
| First-order time $\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}$ | Temporal rate of change | Unsteady flow evolution |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical constants/Metrics used in this topic:** None of the universal constants (Reynolds number $Re$, Mach number $Ma$, etc.) appear in the **derivation** of FDA formulas. The only **key metric** is the **truncation error order** $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^n)$, which tells us how fast the approximation error decreases as the grid is refined.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation: Taylor Series Expansion

The entire theory of finite differences rests on the **Taylor Series Theorem**, which expresses any smooth function $f(x)$ in the neighborhood of a point $x_i$ as:

$$f(x_i + \Delta x) = f(x_i) + \Delta x \, f'(x_i) + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2!} f''(x_i) + \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{3!} f'''(x_i) + \frac{(\Delta x)^4}{4!} f^{(4)}(x_i) + \cdots$$

By rearranging this series, we can isolate $f'(x_i)$ and obtain various approximations with different accuracy.

---

## 2.2 The Three Primary FDA Schemes (Step-by-Step Logic)

### 2.2.1 Forward Difference Approximation (FDA)

**Step 1:** Write the Taylor expansion for $f(x_i + \Delta x)$, i.e., the point *ahead* of $x_i$:

$$f_{i+1} = f_i + \Delta x \, f'_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2} f''_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{6} f'''_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)$$

**Step 2:** Solve algebraically for $f'_i$:

$$f'_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x} - \frac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i - \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{6} f'''_i - \cdots$$

**Step 3:** Drop the higher-order derivative terms. The remaining leading error term is $-\frac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i$, which is proportional to $\Delta x$:

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{df}{dx}\right)_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x)}$$

**Why it works:** It uses only the current and the *next* node — useful for marching problems (e.g., parabolic PDEs solved along time).

---

### 2.2.2 Backward Difference Approximation (BDA)

**Step 1:** Write the Taylor expansion for $f(x_i - \Delta x)$, i.e., the point *behind* $x_i$:

$$f_{i-1} = f_i - \Delta x \, f'_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2} f''_i - \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{6} f'''_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $f'_i$:

$$f'_i = \frac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x} + \frac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i - \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{6} f'''_i + \cdots$$

**Step 3:** Truncate:

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{df}{dx}\right)_i \approx \frac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x)}$$

**Why it works:** Useful when boundary data is given "upstream" and we propagate downstream.

---

### 2.2.3 Central Difference Approximation (CDA)

**Step 1:** Subtract the backward Taylor expansion from the forward Taylor expansion:

$$f_{i+1} - f_{i-1} = 2 \Delta x \, f'_i + \frac{2(\Delta x)^3}{6} f'''_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^5)$$

**Step 2:** Solve for $f'_i$:

$$\boxed{\left(\frac{df}{dx}\right)_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)}$$

**Why it works:** All even-order error terms cancel out. The leading error is now proportional to $\Delta x^2$, making this **second-order accurate** — twice as accurate as forward or backward for the same $\Delta x$.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Scheme Name | Formula for $f'(x_i)$ | Order of Accuracy | Truncation Error (Leading Term) |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| 1 | Forward Difference | $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ | $-\dfrac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i$ |
| 2 | Backward Difference | $\dfrac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ | $+\dfrac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i$ |
| 3 | Central Difference | $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ | $-\dfrac{(\Delta x)^2}{6} f'''_i$ |
| 4 | 2nd-order Forward | $\dfrac{-3f_i + 4f_{i+1} - f_{i+2}}{2\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ | $-\dfrac{(\Delta x)^2}{3} f'''_i$ |
| 5 | 3rd-order Backward | $\dfrac{11f_i - 18f_{i-1} + 9f_{i-2} - 2f_{i-3}}{6\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^3)$ | $-\dfrac{(\Delta x)^3}{4} f^{(4)}_i$ |
| 6 | 4th-order Central | $\dfrac{-f_{i+2} + 8f_{i+1} - 8f_{i-1} + f_{i-2}}{12\Delta x}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)$ | $+\dfrac{(\Delta x)^4}{30} f^{(5)}_i$ |
| 7 | 2nd Derivative Central | $\dfrac{f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}}{(\Delta x)^2}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ | $-\dfrac{(\Delta x)^2}{12} f^{(4)}_i$ |
| 8 | 3rd Derivative Central | $\dfrac{-f_{i+2} + 2f_{i+1} - 2f_{i-1} + f_{i-2}}{2(\Delta x)^3}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ | — |
| 9 | 4th Derivative Central | $\dfrac{f_{i+2} - 4f_{i+1} + 6f_i - 4f_{i-1} + f_{i-2}}{(\Delta x)^4}$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** The most frequently tested formulas are **#1, #2, #3, and #7**. Be ready to derive any one of them from the Taylor series.

---

## 2.4 Higher-Order Derivative Approximations

### 2.4.1 Second Derivative (Used in Diffusion/Conduction Problems)

Add the forward and backward Taylor expansions:

$$f_{i+1} + f_{i-1} = 2f_i + (\Delta x)^2 f''_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^4}{12} f^{(4)}_i + \cdots$$

Solving for $f''_i$:

$$\boxed{f''_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}}{(\Delta x)^2} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)}$$

This is the **backbone** of the explicit finite difference solution to the 1-D heat equation.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Engineering Field | Application of FDA |
|:---|:---|
| **Aerospace CFD** | Solving Euler/Navier-Stokes equations over airfoils using central differences |
| **Automotive** | Boundary layer analysis on car bodies for drag estimation |
| **Heat Transfer** | 1-D heat conduction in nuclear reactor fuel rods |
| **Hydraulics** | Open channel flow simulations (Saint-Venant equations) |
| **Geophysical Flows** | Atmospheric and ocean modeling (weather prediction) |
| **Biomedical** | Blood flow through stenosed arteries |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Forward Difference

**Objective:** Derive the forward difference approximation for $f'(x_i)$ and find the truncation error.

**Given:** $f(x)$ is a smooth, differentiable function. We know $f_i = f(x_i)$ and $f_{i+1} = f(x_i + \Delta x)$.

**Step 1: Write Taylor Series expansion of $f(x_i + \Delta x)$ about $x_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x_i + \Delta x) = f(x_i) &+ \Delta x \cdot f'(x_i) + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2!} f''(x_i) + \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{3!} f'''(x_i) \\
&+ \frac{(\Delta x)^4}{4!} f^{(4)}(x_i) + \frac{(\Delta x)^5}{5!} f^{(5)}(x_i) + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute compact notation $f_i = f(x_i)$ and $f_{i+1} = f(x_i + \Delta x)$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} = f_i + \Delta x \cdot f'_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2} f''_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{6} f'''_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^4}{24} f^{(4)}_i + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Subtract $f_i$ from both sides:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} - f_i = \Delta x \cdot f'_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2} f''_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{6} f'''_i + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Divide by $\Delta x$ to isolate $f'_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x} = f'_i + \frac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{6} f'''_i + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Move $f'_i$ to the left-hand side:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x} - \frac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i - \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{6} f'''_i - \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Drop all terms involving $\Delta x$ and higher. The first neglected term is $-\frac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i$, which is $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$:**

$$
\boxed{f'_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - f_i}{\Delta x} \quad \text{with truncation error} \quad \tau = -\frac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)}
$$

**Conclusion:** Forward difference is **first-order accurate**, $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Backward Difference

**Objective:** Derive backward difference approximation for $f'(x_i)$.

**Step 1: Taylor expansion of $f(x_i - \Delta x)$ about $x_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f(x_i - \Delta x) = f(x_i) &- \Delta x \cdot f'(x_i) + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2!} f''(x_i) - \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{3!} f'''(x_i) \\
&+ \frac{(\Delta x)^4}{4!} f^{(4)}(x_i) - \frac{(\Delta x)^5}{5!} f^{(5)}(x_i) + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

*Note: All odd-powered terms have a minus sign because we are evaluating $f$ at a point to the left of $x_i$.*

**Step 2: Substitute compact notation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i-1} = f_i - \Delta x \cdot f'_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2} f''_i - \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{6} f'''_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^4}{24} f^{(4)}_i - \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Subtract $f_i$ from both sides:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i-1} - f_i = -\Delta x \cdot f'_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2} f''_i - \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{6} f'''_i + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Multiply by $-1$ to simplify:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_i - f_{i-1} = \Delta x \cdot f'_i - \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2} f''_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{6} f'''_i - \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Divide by $\Delta x$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x} = f'_i - \frac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{6} f'''_i - \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Isolate $f'_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'_i = \frac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x} + \frac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i - \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{6} f'''_i + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7: Truncate after the leading term:**

$$
\boxed{f'_i \approx \frac{f_i - f_{i-1}}{\Delta x} \quad \text{with truncation error} \quad \tau = +\frac{\Delta x}{2} f''_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)}
$$

**Conclusion:** Backward difference is also **first-order accurate**, $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Central Difference (Second-Order)

**Objective:** Derive the central difference formula which achieves $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ accuracy.

**Step 1: Subtract the backward expansion from the forward expansion:**

Forward:
$$f_{i+1} = f_i + \Delta x \, f'_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2} f''_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{6} f'''_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^4}{24} f^{(4)}_i + \cdots$$

Backward:
$$f_{i-1} = f_i - \Delta x \, f'_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2} f''_i - \frac{(\Delta x)^3}{6} f'''_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^4}{24} f^{(4)}_i - \cdots$$

**Step 2: Subtract term by term:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} - f_{i-1} = (f_i - f_i) &+ (\Delta x \, f'_i - (-\Delta x \, f'_i)) + \left(\frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2} f''_i - \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2} f''_i\right) \\
&+ \left(\frac{(\Delta x)^3}{6} f'''_i - \left(-\frac{(\Delta x)^3}{6} f'''_i\right)\right) + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Simplify. Notice all even-order terms cancel (e.g., $f''$ terms):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} - f_{i-1} = 2 \Delta x \, f'_i + \frac{2 (\Delta x)^3}{6} f'''_i + \frac{2 (\Delta x)^5}{120} f^{(5)}_i + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Divide by $2\Delta x$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x} = f'_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{6} f'''_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^4}{120} f^{(5)}_i + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Isolate $f'_i$ and truncate. Leading error is $\frac{(\Delta x)^2}{6} f'''_i$:**

$$
\boxed{f'_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - f_{i-1}}{2\Delta x} \quad \text{with truncation error} \quad \tau = -\frac{(\Delta x)^2}{6} f'''_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)}
$$

**Conclusion:** Central difference is **second-order accurate**, $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$. The factor **1/6** vs **1/2** in the leading error term confirms central difference is roughly **3 times more accurate** than forward/backward for the same $\Delta x$.

---

## 3.4 Exhaustive Derivation: Second-Order Central Difference for $f''(x_i)$

**Step 1: Add the forward and backward Taylor expansions:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_{i+1} + f_{i-1} = 2 f_i + (\Delta x)^2 f''_i + \frac{(\Delta x)^4}{12} f^{(4)}_i + \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Solve for $f''_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
f''_i = \frac{f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}}{(\Delta x)^2} - \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{12} f^{(4)}_i - \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Truncate:**

$$
\boxed{f''_i \approx \frac{f_{i+1} - 2f_i + f_{i-1}}{(\Delta x)^2} \quad \text{with truncation error} \quad \tau = -\frac{(\Delta x)^2}{12} f^{(4)}_i + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^4)}
$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Forward | Backward | Central |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Uses $f_{i+1}$? | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Uses $f_{i-1}$? | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Uses $f_i$? | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ |
| Order of accuracy | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ |
| Number of nodes required | 2 | 2 | 2 |
| Best for | Initial value (parabolic) | Boundary value | Elliptic, smooth data |
| Common usage in CFD | Explicit time marching | Boundary condition | Spatial derivatives in Navier-Stokes |
| Stability (for parabolic PDEs) | Conditionally stable | Conditionally stable | Unstable (used with special schemes) |

---

## 3.6 Numerical Demonstration (Worked Example)

**Problem:** Given $f(x) = e^x$ at grid points $x = 0, 0.1, 0.2$, estimate $f'(0.1)$ using all three schemes. Compare with the exact value.

**Step 1: Compute exact values:**
- $f(0) = e^0 = 1.000000$
- $f(0.1) = e^{0.1} = 1.105171$
- $f(0.2) = e^{0.2} = 1.221403$
- Exact: $f'(0.1) = e^{0.1} = 1.105171$

**Step 2: Apply Forward Difference** (using $f(0.2)$ and $f(0.1)$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'(0.1) &\approx \frac{f(0.2) - f(0.1)}{0.1} = \frac{1.221403 - 1.105171}{0.1} = 1.16232
\end{aligned}
$$

Error = $|1.16232 - 1.105171| = 0.05715$

**Step 3: Apply Backward Difference** (using $f(0.1)$ and $f(0)$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'(0.1) &\approx \frac{f(0.1) - f(0)}{0.1} = \frac{1.105171 - 1.000000}{0.1} = 1.05171
\end{aligned}
$$

Error = $|1.05171 - 1.105171| = 0.05346$

**Step 4: Apply Central Difference** (using $f(0.2)$ and $f(0)$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
f'(0.1) &\approx \frac{f(0.2) - f(0)}{0.2} = \frac{1.221403 - 1.000000}{0.2} = 1.10701
\end{aligned}
$$

Error = $|1.10701 - 1.105171| = 0.00184$

**Conclusion:** Central difference error ($\approx 1.84 \times 10^{-3}$) is roughly **30 times smaller** than forward or backward difference — directly validating the $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$ vs $\mathcal{O}(\Delta x)$ order of accuracy.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Stencil Diagram (Mermaid Block Diagram)

The following block-level architecture flow illustrates the **stencil** (node connectivity pattern) for each finite difference scheme on a 1-D uniform grid.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Node i-2] --> B[Node i-1]
    B --> C[Node i - Central Node]
    C --> D[Node i+1]
    D --> E[Node i+2]

    F[Forward Difference Stencil] -.uses.-> C
    F -.uses.-> D

    G[Backward Difference Stencil] -.uses.-> B
    G -.uses.-> C

    H[Central Difference Stencil] -.uses.-> B
    H -.uses.-> D

    style A fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px
    style D fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#dc3545,stroke-width:2px
    style H fill:#d1ecf1,stroke:#17a2b8,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Red node (Node i)**: The point where the derivative is being approximated.
- **Yellow nodes (i-1, i+1)**: First-neighbor nodes used by most schemes.
- **Blue nodes (i-2, i+2)**: Required only for higher-order (4th-order central) schemes.
- **Green block (Forward)**: Uses only the red and right-yellow node.
- **Red block (Backward)**: Uses only the red and left-yellow node.
- **Cyan block (Central)**: Uses both yellow nodes but skips the central red node.

---

## 4.2 Accuracy vs Grid Size Relationship Diagram

```mermaid
graph TB
    P[Grid refinement - decrease dx] --> Q[Error magnitude]
    Q --> R1[Forward: error proportional to dx - linear decay]
    Q --> R2[Backward: error proportional to dx - linear decay]
    Q --> R3[Central: error proportional to dx squared - quadratic decay]

    R1 --> S1[Halving dx - halves error]
    R2 --> S2[Halving dx - halves error]
    R3 --> S3[Halving dx - quarters error]

    S3 --> T[Central difference is preferred for smooth flows]

    style P fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:2px

    style S2 fill:#ffe6e6,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:2px
    style S3 fill:#e6ffe6,stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px

```

## 4.3 Numerical Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Continuous PDE - e.g. Navier Stokes] --> B[Domain Discretization - generate grid]
    B --> C[Replace derivatives with FDA formulas]
```

## 4.4 Higher-Domain Grid Architecture (Block-Level Functional Map)

For 2-D problems (typical in CFD), the FDA stencil extends to a rectangular neighborhood:

```mermaid
graph LR
    SW[i-1,j+1] --> S[i,j+1]
    S --> SE[i+1,j+1]
    W[i-1,j] --> C[i,j - Center Node]
    C --> E[i+1,j]
    NW[i-1,j-1] --> N[i,j-1]
    N --> NE[i+1,j-1]

    META[2D Central Stencil for Laplacian] -.uses.-> S
    META -.uses.-> N
    META -.uses.-> W
    META -.uses.-> C
    META -.uses.-> E

    style SW fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px
    style S fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px
    style SE fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px
    style W fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,stroke-width:3px
    style E fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px
    style NW fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px
    style N fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#cc9900,stroke-width:2px
    style NE fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:1px
    style META fill:#d4edda,stroke:#28a745,stroke-width:2px
```

The corresponding 2-D Laplacian (used in Poisson pressure equation) is:

$$\nabla^2 \phi = \frac{\phi_{i+1,j} + \phi_{i-1,j} + \phi_{i,j+1} + \phi_{i,j-1} - 4\phi_{i,j}}{(\Delta x)^2} + \mathcal{O}(\Delta x^2)$$
