| Implicit (BTCS) | (2, 1) | $\left\vert 1 / (1 + C(1-e^{ik\Delta x})) \right\vert$ | **Unconditionally stable** | Most robust |
| Crank-Nicolson | (2, 2) | $G = \frac{1 - (C/2)(1-e^{ik\Delta x})}{1 + (C/2)(1-e^{ik\Delta x})}$ | $\|G\| = 1$ | Non-dissipative (dispersive) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Logical Triangle — Consistency, Stability, Convergence

The following Mermaid block captures the **Lax Equivalence Theorem** as a flow chart of dependencies.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["PDE: Continuous Governing Equation"] --> B["Discretisation"]
    B --> C["Finite Difference Scheme: u_j_n_plus_1 = f of u_j_n and neighbours"]
    C --> D{"Check Truncation Error tau"}
    D -- tau to zero --> E["CONSISTENT"]
    D -- tau does not vanish --> F["INCONSISTENT: No Convergence Possible"]
    C --> G{"Check Amplification Factor G"}
    G -- mod G le 1 --> H["STABLE"]
    G -- mod G gt 1 --> I["UNSTABLE: Errors Grow Exponentially"]
    E --> J["Lax Theorem Combination"]
    H --> J
    J --> K["CONVERGENT: u_num tends to u_exact"]
    F --> L["Diverges or converges to wrong answer"]
    I --> L
    K --> M["VALID CFD SOLUTION"]
    L --> N["REJECT OR REMESH"]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Stability Pipeline of a CFD Solver

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Physical Problem Definition"] --> P2["Governing PDE: Navier Stokes"]
    P2 --> P3["Mathematical Classification: Elliptic / Parabolic / Hyperbolic"]
    P3 --> P4["Scheme Selection: Explicit or Implicit"]
    P4 --> P5["Compute CFL = c dt over dx"]
    P4 --> P6["Compute Diffusion Number = nu dt over dx squared"]
    P5 --> P7{"CFL le 1?"}
    P6 --> P8{"d le 0.5?"}
    P7 -- Yes --> P9["Proceed with Time Marching"]
    P7 -- No --> P10["Reduce dt OR Increase Implicit Weighting"]
    P8 -- Yes --> P9
    P8 -- No --> P10
    P10 --> P4
    P9 --> P11["Run Von Neumann Spectral Check"]
    P11 -- mod G le 1 --> P12["STABLE INTEGRATION"]
    P11 -- mod G gt 1 --> P13["UNSTABLE: Numerical Explosion"]
    P12 --> P14["Monitor Convergence: Residual Drop of 3 to 5 Orders"]
    P14 -- Achieved --> P15["CONVERGED SOLUTION"]
    P14 -- Stall or Oscillation --> P16["Investigate Mesh Quality or Under Relaxation"]
```

## 4.3 The CFL Cone — Geometric Stability Picture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph T["Numerical Domain of Dependence Cone"]
        T1["Cell at level n: j minus 1, j, j plus 1"] --> T2["Cell at level n plus 1: j"]
    end
    subgraph P["Physical Characteristic Cone"]
        P1["Characteristic slope c"] --> P2["Exact solution uses continuous interval"]
    end
    T2 --> Q{"Does numerical cone contain physical cone?"}
    P2 --> Q
    Q -- Yes --> R["CFL Satisfied: Stable"]
    Q -- No --> S["CFL Violated: Unstable"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define convergence and stability of a finite difference scheme. State the Lax Equivalence Theorem.**

**Model Answer:**

**Convergence:** A numerical scheme is said to be convergent if, as the discretisation parameters tend to zero ($\Delta x \to 0$, $\Delta t \to 0$), the numerical solution $u_j^n$ approaches the exact solution $u(x_j, t_n)$ of the governing PDE at every point in the domain.

**Stability:** A scheme is stable if the errors (from truncation, round-off, or initial perturbations) introduced at any stage do not amplify without bound as the computation proceeds. Quantitatively, all Fourier modes must satisfy $|G(k)| \leq 1$, where $G$ is the amplification factor from Von Neumann analysis.

**Lax Equivalence Theorem:** For a *consistent* finite-difference approximation to a *well-posed linear* initial-value PDE, stability is the necessary and sufficient condition for convergence. Symbolically:

> *Consistency + Stability  ⇔  Convergence.*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the CFL condition? Explain its physical significance for the 1-D linear convection equation.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Courant-Friedrichs-Lewy (CFL) condition** states that for an explicit time-marching scheme solving a hyperbolic PDE, the numerical domain of dependence must contain the physical domain of dependence at every point.

For 1-D linear convection $u_t + c u_x = 0$, the Courant number is:

$$C = \frac{c \, \Delta t}{\Delta x} \leq 1$$

**Physical significance:** $C \leq 1$ ensures that in one time step $\Delta t$, the wave travelling at speed $c$ crosses at most one mesh cell of width $\Delta x$. Hence the numerical stencil never *skips over* the physical characteristic, and information propagation remains causal. If $C > 1$, the explicit stencil becomes blind to the propagating wave — the scheme blows up.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a) (7 Marks)** Explain in detail the Von Neumann stability analysis method. Discuss the role of the amplification factor $G$ and the necessary condition $|G| \leq 1$ for stability.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Apply Von Neumann analysis to the FTCS scheme for $u_t + c u_x = 0$ and prove that the scheme is unconditionally unstable.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(a):

**Step 1 — Purpose:** Von Neumann (Fourier / modal) stability analysis determines whether a linear constant-coefficient finite-difference scheme damps, preserves, or amplifies each Fourier mode independently. It is the most widely used analytical tool in CFD for linear(ised) problems.

**Step 2 — Procedure:**

1. Linearise the governing PDE about a base state (here, assumed already linear).
2. Express the discrete solution as a sum of Fourier modes:
$$u_j^n = \sum_k \hat{u}_k \, G^n(k) \, e^{i k j \Delta x}$$
Because the scheme is linear, each mode evolves independently — only the **amplification factor** $G(k)$ matters.
3. Substitute a single mode $u_j^n = G^n e^{i k j \Delta x}$ into the discrete update equation.
4. Solve algebraically for $G(k)$ in terms of $k$, $\Delta x$, $\Delta t$, $c$, $\nu$, etc.
5. Apply the **spectral radius (Von Neumann) condition:**
$$\boxed{\;|G(k)| \leq 1 \quad \text{for all } k\;}
$$
This is the necessary and sufficient condition for linear stability of a constant-coefficient scheme on an infinite periodic domain.

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation of G:**

- $|G| = 1$: mode propagates with no damping — *non-dissipative* (e.g., Crank-Nicolson).
- $|G| < 1$: mode is damped — *dissipative* (e.g., upwind, Lax-Friedrichs).
- $|G| > 1$: mode amplifies → **unstable** (e.g., FTCS for convection).
- $\arg(G) \neq -k c \Delta t$: phase error (numerical dispersion), leads to wave travel at wrong speed.

**Step 4 — Engineering usage:** CFD software (Fluent, OpenFOAM) automatically computes $G$ for selected schemes in their verification & validation manuals.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A(b):

**Discretisation — FTCS scheme for $u_t + c u_x = 0$:**

$$u_j^{n+1} = u_j^n - \frac{C}{2} \left( u_{j+1}^n - u_{j-1}^n \right)$$

where $C = c \Delta t / \Delta x$.

**Step 1 — Substitute Fourier mode** $u_j^n = G^n e^{i k j \Delta x}$:

$$G^{n+1} e^{i k j \Delta x} = G^n e^{i k j \Delta x} - \frac{C}{2} \left( G^n e^{i k (j+1) \Delta x} - G^n e^{i k (j-1) \Delta x} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Divide by $G^n e^{i k j \Delta x}$:**

$$G = 1 - \frac{C}{2} \left( e^{i k \Delta x} - e^{-i k \Delta x} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Euler's identity** $e^{i\theta} - e^{-i\theta} = 2i \sin\theta$:

$$G = 1 - i C \sin(k \Delta x)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the modulus squared:**

$$\vert G \vert^2 = 1 + C^2 \sin^2(k \Delta x)$$

**Step 5 — Apply the Von Neumann criterion:**

Since $C^2 \sin^2(k \Delta x) > 0$ for **all** $C \neq 0$ and **all** $k$ except integer multiples of $\pi$, we have:

$$\vert G \vert^2 = 1 + C^2 \sin^2(k \Delta x) > 1$$

Hence $|G| > 1$ for any non-zero, non-trivial mode — the scheme **amplifies** every perturbation with $C > 0$. Conclusion:

> **The FTCS scheme is unconditionally unstable for the 1-D linear convection equation, irrespective of the time step.**

This is why pure central differencing in space must never be used for convective transport in CFD; upwind, flux-difference splitting, or TVD schemes must be employed instead.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) (7 Marks)** Derive the CFL condition for the 1-D linear convection equation using geometric arguments on the domains of dependence.

**(b) (7 Marks)** Perform Von Neumann stability analysis of the upwind scheme and show that the scheme is stable for $0 \leq C \leq 1$.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(a):

**Step 1 — Statement of the equation:**

$$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + c \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0, \quad c > 0$$

**Step 2 — Physical domain of dependence:**

The exact characteristic passing through $(x_j, t^{n+1})$ has slope $c$ and reaches the initial line $t = 0$ at $x_0 = x_j - c t^{n+1}$. The exact solution uses the entire interval of data between $x_0$ and $x_j$.

**Step 3 — Numerical domain of dependence:**

A general explicit scheme $u_j^{n+1} = f(u_{j-r}^n, u_{j-r+1}^n, \ldots, u_{j+s}^n)$ at time level $n+1$ can only draw upon values at level $n$ that lie within the stencil footprint. After N steps, the numerical domain is the triangle whose vertex is at $(j, n+1)$ and whose base is the union of stencil footprints from level $0$ to level $n$.

**Step 4 — Geometric inclusion condition:**

For the numerical cone to *contain* the physical characteristic, the edge slope of the numerical cone ($\Delta x / \Delta t$) must be at least as steep as the characteristic slope $c$:

$$\frac{\Delta x}{\Delta t} \geq c \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; c \, \Delta t \leq \Delta x \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; \boxed{C = \frac{c \Delta t}{\Delta x} \leq 1}$$

**Step 5 — Failure mode when $C > 1$:** If $C > 1$, the wave physically advances more than one cell per time step, but the explicit stencil only samples the immediate neighbours — information skips across cells, and the scheme becomes blind to the wave. The numerical solution no longer converges to the exact solution (instability and non-convergence).

**Step 6 — Engineering note:** Implicit schemes circumvent this restriction by allowing information to propagate across multiple cells per step through the implicit matrix solve, hence their unconditional stability.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B(b):

**Discretisation — Upwind scheme for $u_t + c u_x = 0$ (c > 0):**

$$u_j^{n+1} = u_j^n - C \bigl( u_j^n - u_{j-1}^n \bigr), \quad C = \frac{c \Delta t}{\Delta x}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute Fourier mode** $u_j^n = G^n e^{i k j \Delta x}$:

$$G^{n+1} e^{i k j \Delta x} = G^n e^{i k j \Delta x} - C \left( G^n e^{i k j \Delta x} - G^n e^{i k (j-1) \Delta x} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Simplify (divide by $G^n e^{i k j \Delta x}$):**

$$G = 1 - C + C e^{-i k \Delta x}$$

**Step 3 — Express in real and imaginary parts** using $e^{-i\theta} = \cos\theta - i \sin\theta$:

$$G = (1 - C) + C \cos(k \Delta x) - i \, C \sin(k \Delta x)$$

**Step 4 — Modulus squared:**

$$\vert G \vert^2 = \bigl[(1-C) + C \cos(k \Delta x)\bigr]^2 + C^2 \sin^2(k \Delta x)$$

Expanding:

$$= (1-C)^2 + 2C(1-C)\cos(k \Delta x) + C^2 \cos^2(k \Delta x) + C^2 \sin^2(k \Delta x)$$

Using $\sin^2 + \cos^2 = 1$:

$$\vert G \vert^2 = (1-C)^2 + 2C(1-C)\cos(k \Delta x) + C^2$$

$$= 1 - 2C(1-C)\bigl[1 - \cos(k \Delta x)\bigr]$$

**Step 5 — Apply the stability bound:**

Since $1 - \cos(k \Delta x) \geq 0$ for all real $k$, the term subtracted from 1 is non-negative **iff** $0 \leq C \leq 1$. Under this condition:

$$0 \leq \vert G \vert^2 \leq 1 \;\;\Longleftrightarrow\;\; 0 \leq \vert G \vert \leq 1$$

Hence **the upwind scheme is stable** (and conditionally convergent with the Lax theorem) for $0 \leq C \leq 1$.

**Step 6 — Limiting cases:**

- $C = 0$: $|G| = 1$ (no propagation).
- $C = 1$: $|G| = |e^{-i k \Delta x}| = 1$ (exact propagation, no dissipation, but maximum wave-speed constraint).
- $C = 0.5$: maximum dissipation of the highest-frequency mode ($|G|_{\min} = 0$).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls to Avoid:**
> 1. **Do not confuse consistency with stability.** Consistency deals with truncation error; stability deals with error growth. They are independent properties.
> 2. **Do not apply Lax Equivalence to non-linear PDEs.** It is strictly a theorem for *linear* well-posed initial-value problems. Examiners penalise over-generalisation.
> 3. **In Von Neumann analysis, ALWAYS compute $|G|^2$, not $|G|$.** Skipping the conjugate step is a 2-mark deduction.
> 4. **In CFL derivations, mention BOTH the geometric and algebraic interpretations.** Pure geometric answers without the algebraic inequality lose half marks.
> 5. **Do not write the upwind scheme as $(u_{j+1} - u_{j-1})/2\Delta x$** — that is FTCS and is unconditionally unstable. Many students lose marks by mixing up central and one-sided differences.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Convergence & Stability:**

- ✅ **Consistency** = truncation error → 0 as Δx, Δt → 0.
- ✅ **Stability** = bounded amplification; Von Neumann criterion $|G(k)| \leq 1$ for all $k$.
- ✅ **Convergence** = numerical solution → exact solution as grid is refined.
- ✅ **Lax Equivalence Theorem:** Consistency + Stability ⇔ Convergence (linear IVPs only).
- ✅ **CFL number** $C = c \Delta t / \Delta x$ must satisfy $C \leq 1$ for most explicit schemes.
- ✅ **Diffusion number** $d = \nu \Delta t / \Delta x^2 \leq 0.5$ for explicit FTCS of the heat equation.
- ✅ **FTCS for convection:** $|G|^2 = 1 + C^2 \sin^2(k\Delta x)$ → **unconditionally unstable**.
- ✅ **Upwind for convection:** $|G|^2 = 1 - 2C(1-C)(1-\cos k\Delta x)$ → stable for $0 \leq C \leq 1$.
- ✅ **Lax-Wendroff:** 2nd-order accurate in space and time; stable for $C \leq 1$.
- ✅ **Implicit schemes** (BTCS, Crank-Nicolson): unconditionally stable — but require solving linear systems per time step.
- ✅ **Crank-Nicolson:** $|G| = 1$ exactly → non-dissipative; dispersive errors only.
- ✅ **Always check $|G|^2$, not $|G|$**, when applying the Von Neumann criterion.
- ✅ **CFD software reality:** Fluent uses bounded central differencing / second-order upwind; OpenFOAM uses linearUpwind, linear, or limitedLinear schemes — all rooted in the stability analysis above.
- ✅ **Common engineering rule-of-thumb:** keep **global CFL ≤ 1** in transient compressible flows; **local CFL ≤ 5–10** acceptable in steady-state with local time stepping.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of Explicit and Implicit Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Concept of Explicit and Implicit Methods # Module 2: Concept of Explicit and Implicit Methods ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), the governing partial differential equations (PDEs) — namely the continuity, momentum, and energy equations — are continuous in nature. To obtain numerical solutions on a discrete computational grid, these PDEs must be converted into algebraic equations using **finite difference**, **finite volume**, or **finite element** discretization. The choice of how the time derivative (in unsteady problems) or the spatial derivative (in iterative steady-state solvers) is approximated leads to two fundamentally different classes of numerical schemes: **Explicit Methods** and **Implicit Methods**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Explicit Method (KTU 2024 Definition):** A numerical time-marching scheme in which the value of the unknown variable at the new (future) time level is computed *directly* from already-known values at the current (or previous) time levels using an explicit algebraic formula. **No system of simultaneous equations is solved.** > [!IMPORTANT] > **Implicit Method (KTU 2024 Definition):** A numerical time-marching scheme in which the value of the unknown variable at the new time level appears on **both sides** of the discretized equation — it depends on itself and on its neighbours at the new time level. This requires the solution of a **system of simultaneous algebraic equations** (typically a tridiagonal or banded matrix) at every time step. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Walking vs. Skating" Intuition To make this immediately accessible to a first-time learner, consider the following real-world analogy: - **Explicit Method is like WALKING on a clearly visible path at night.** You take each step forward because the ground beneath your next foot is already lit up (known). You can move quickly — one step per calculation. However, if the path is uneven (poor mesh quality) or you try to take too large a step (large $\Delta t$), you will stumble (the solution becomes unstable and blows up). - **Implicit Method is like ICE SKATING in the dark.** To glide to your next position, you must first figure out where your foot will land because the ice condition depends on your landing. This requires solving a small puzzle (a system of equations) at each stride, but the stride (time step $\Delta t$) can be made very large without losing balance. It is unconditionally stable, but computationally heavier per step. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics The two most critical numerical metrics used to characterize these methods are: - **The Courant–Friedrichs–Lewy (CFL) Number**, denoted $C$ or $\sigma$, defined as: $$C = \frac{u \cdot \Delta t}{\Delta x}$$ where $u$ is the convective velocity, $\Delta t$ is the time-step size, and $\Delta x$ is the spatial grid spacing. For **explicit schemes, $C \leq 1$** is the classical stability bound. - **The Grid Fourier Number** (for diffusion-dominated problems), defined as: $$d = \frac{\alpha \cdot \Delta t}{(\Delta x)^2}$$ where $\alpha$ is the thermal diffusivity. For explicit schemes, $d \leq 0.5$. > [!NOTE] > **Why this matters in CFD:** The governing equations of fluid flow (Navier–Stokes equations) are non-linear and often produce shock waves, boundary layers, and steep gradients. The choice between explicit and implicit methods directly determines whether the simulation will converge to a physically realistic answer or blow up to infinity. This makes the concept a **high-weight, high-frequency topic in KTU University Examinations**. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Logic of Explicit Methods In an explicit scheme, the discretized equation at node $i$ and time level $n+1$ can be solved directly using the recurrence relation. The generalized structure is: $$u_i^{n+1} = F\!\left(u_{i}^{n},\, u_{i\pm 1}^{n},\, u_{i\pm 2}^{n},\,\dots\right)$$ The right-hand side contains ONLY known quantities from time level $n$. The matrix update is therefore: $$\mathbf{U}^{n+1} = \mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{U}^{n}$$ where $\mathbf{A}$ is a matrix whose entries are all known. The solution is obtained via simple matrix–vector multiplication. ### Step-by-Step Logic of Explicit Evaluation: 1. The initial condition $\mathbf{U}^0$ is prescribed from the physical problem. 2. For each grid point $i = 1, 2, \dots, N$, the value $u_i^{n+1}$ is computed directly. 3. After one sweep across all grid points, time advances to $n+1$. 4. The process repeats until the desired final time is reached. ### Computational Cost & Memory: - **Cost per step:** $O(N)$ operations. - **Memory:** $O(N)$ — only current values need to be stored. - **Drawback:** Stability is **conditionally stable** — $\Delta t$ is restricted by the CFL condition. ## 2.2 Operational Logic of Implicit Methods In an implicit scheme, the future value $u_i^{n+1}$ depends on neighbouring future values such as $u_{i-1}^{n+1}$ and $u_{i+1}^{n+1}$. The generalized structure is: $$a_i\, u_{i-1}^{n+1} + b_i\, u_i^{n+1} + c_i\, u_{i+1}^{n+1} = d_i^{n}$$ The right-hand side $d_i^{n}$ contains only known values from time level $n$. The matrix form is: $$\mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{U}^{n+1} = \mathbf{d}^{n}$$ where $\mathbf{B}$ is a (usually tridiagonal) matrix. Solving requires matrix inversion — typically via the efficient **Thomas Algorithm** for tridiagonal systems. ### Step-by-Step Logic of Implicit Evaluation: 1. Initial condition $\mathbf{U}^0$ is prescribed. 2. Form the coefficient matrix $\mathbf{B}$ (depends on spatial scheme, not time). 3. Form the right-hand-side vector $\mathbf{d}^{n}$ from known $\mathbf{U}^n$. 4. Solve $\mathbf{B}\mathbf{U}^{n+1} = \mathbf{d}^{n}$ via Thomas algorithm ($O(N)$ for tridiagonal). 5. Advance time and repeat. ### Computational Cost & Memory: - **Cost per step:** $O(N)$ for tridiagonal, but with a higher constant (e.g., 5–8 operations per node vs. 1). - **Memory:** $O(N)$ — same as explicit. - **Advantage:** Unconditionally stable — $\Delta t$ is limited only by **accuracy**, not stability. ## 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Method | Aspect | Explicit Method | Implicit Method | |---|---|---| | **Why use it?** | Simple, fast per step, easy to parallelize. | Allows large $\Delta t$, robust for stiff problems. | | **How does it work?** | Direct formula evaluation. | Solve a coupled system of equations. | | **Best suited for?** | Low-speed flows, parabolic PDEs, explicit compressible solvers (e.g., Lax–Wendroff). | High Reynolds number flows, stiff source terms, steady-state acceleration. | | **Stability** | Conditional (CFL-limited). | Unconditional (for linear problems). | | **Accuracy** | Lower-order truncation error typically. | Higher-order achievable (e.g., Crank–Nicolson is 2nd order in time). | | **Examples** | FTCS, Lax–Friedrichs, MacCormack predictor. | BTCS, Crank–Nicolson, Beam–Warming. | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equations > [!NOTE] > **The following table is the examiner's cheat sheet.** Master every row before attempting KTU numerical or derivations questions. | # | Formula / Equation | Name / Description | Typical Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $u_i^{n+1} = u_i^{n} - \frac{C}{2}\!\left(u_{i+1}^{n} - u_{i-1}^{n}\right)$ | **FTCS Explicit Scheme** (1-D linear convection) | First-order in time, second-order in space. Stable iff $\vert C \vert \le 1$. | | 2 | $u_i^{n+1} = u_i^{n} - \frac{C}{2}\!\left(u_{i+1}^{n} - u_{i-1}^{n}\right) + \frac{C^2}{2}\!\left(u_{i+1}^{n} - 2u_i^{n} + u_{i-1}^{n}\right)$ | **Lax–Wendroff Explicit Scheme** | 2nd order in both space and time. | | 3 | $u_i^{n+1} - u_i^{n} = -\frac{C}{2}\!\left(u_{i+1}^{n+1} - u_{i-1}^{n+1}\right)$ | **BTCS Implicit Scheme** (Backward Time, Centered Space) | Unconditionally stable. | | 4 | $u_i^{n+1} - u_i^{n} = -\frac{C}{4}\!\left(u_{i+1}^{n+1} - u_{i-1}^{n+1} + u_{i+1}^{n} - u_{i-1}^{n}\right)$ | **Crank–Nicolson Scheme** | 2nd order in time; unconditional stability. | | 5 | $C = \dfrac{u\,\Delta t}{\Delta x}$ | **Courant Number** (convection CFL) | Explicit limit: $C \le 1$. | | 6 | $d = \dfrac{\alpha\,\Delta t}{(\Delta x)^2}$ | **Diffusion Number (Fourier)** | Explicit limit: $d \le \tfrac{1}{2}$. | | 7 | $\xi = \dfrac{\Delta t}{\Delta x^2}$ | **Stability Parameter** (general heat equation) | Used in von Neumann analysis. | | 8 | $G = \vert g \vert = \left\vert 1 - 4d\,\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{k\Delta x}{2}\right)\right\vert$ | **Amplification Factor (BTCS diffusion)** | Unconditionally $\le 1$. | | 9 | $G = \vert g \vert = 1 - 4d\,\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{k\Delta x}{2}\right)$ | **Amplification Factor (FTCS diffusion)** | Requires $d \le \tfrac{1}{2}$. | | 10 | $a_i u_{i-1}^{n+1} + b_i u_i^{n+1} + c_i u_{i+1}^{n+1} = d_i$ | **Tridiagonal Implicit Form** | Solved by Thomas algorithm. | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Absolute Value Convention in KTU Tables:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` is replaced by `\vert` inside markdown tables (as in row 8 and 9) to avoid breaking the table parser. In handwritten answer sheets, students should write a clean $\vert \cdot \vert$. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace:** Explicit schemes are used in commercial codes (e.g., FUN3D, SU2) for transonic flows around wings because of their excellent parallel scalability on GPU clusters. Implicit schemes are used in rotorcraft and turbomachinery CFD where stiff source terms require large time steps. - **Automotive:** Implicit solvers dominate steady-state aerodynamic simulations of car bodies (Ahmed body, DrivAer) where reaching steady state quickly is critical. - **Power & Energy:** Nuclear reactor thermal-hydraulics simulations (RELAP, TRACE) use implicit schemes because of the very stiff heat conduction equations. - **Weather & Climate:** Atmospheric models use **semi-implicit** and **split-explicit** schemes — a hybrid of both methods — exploiting explicit simplicity for advection and implicit stability for fast gravity waves. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the FTCS Explicit Scheme Consider the **1-D linear convection equation**, a fundamental model equation in CFD: $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + u\,\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0 \quad \text{(simplified with constant } u = c\text{)}$$ $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} + c\,\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0$$ ### Step 1 — Discretize the Time Derivative (Forward Difference) Using a forward difference in time at point $(i, n)$: $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}\bigg|_{i}^{n} \approx \frac{u_i^{n+1} - u_i^{n}}{\Delta t} + O(\Delta t)$$ ### Step 2 — Discretize the Space Derivative (Central Difference) Using a central difference in space: $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\bigg|_{i}^{n} \approx \frac{u_{i+1}^{n} - u_{i-1}^{n}}{2\Delta x} + O(\Delta x^2)$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute into the PDE $$\frac{u_i^{n+1} - u_i^{n}}{\Delta t} + c \cdot \frac{u_{i+1}^{n} - u_{i-1}^{n}}{2\Delta x} = 0$$ ### Step 4 — Rearrange to Solve for the Future Value $u_i^{n+1}$ $$u_i^{n+1} = u_i^{n} - \frac{c\,\Delta t}{2\Delta x}\left(u_{i+1}^{n} - u_{i-1}^{n}\right)$$ ### Step 5 — Introduce the Courant Number $C$ Let $C = \dfrac{c\,\Delta t}{\Delta x}$. The scheme becomes: $$\boxed{u_i^{n+1} = u_i^{n} - \frac{C}{2}\left(u_{i+1}^{n} - u_{i-1}^{n}\right)}$$ This is the **Forward-Time Central-Space (FTCS) Explicit Scheme**. > [!NOTE] > **Observation:** The right-hand side contains only values at time $n$. Therefore, $u_i^{n+1}$ can be computed directly without any matrix inversion. This is the defining feature of an explicit method. ### Step 6 — Truncation Error Analysis Substituting the Taylor series expansions back into the scheme and subtracting the original PDE gives the truncation error: $$\tau = O(\Delta t) + O(\Delta x^2)$$ The scheme is **first-order accurate in time** and **second-order accurate in space**. ## 3.2 Derivation of the BTCS Implicit Scheme Now apply a **backward difference** in time but retain the **central difference** in space. ### Step 1 — Backward Time Discretization $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t}\bigg|_{i}^{n+1} \approx \frac{u_i^{n+1} - u_i^{n}}{\Delta t} + O(\Delta t)$$ ### Step 2 — Central Space Discretization (Evaluated at Time $n+1$) $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial x}\bigg|_{i}^{n+1} \approx \frac{u_{i+1}^{n+1} - u_{i-1}^{n+1}}{2\Delta x} + O(\Delta x^2)$$ ### Step 3 — Substitute into the PDE $$\frac{u_i^{n+1} - u_i^{n}}{\Delta t} + c \cdot \frac{u_{i+1}^{n+1} - u_{i-1}^{n+1}}{2\Delta x} = 0$$ ### Step 4 — Rearrange into Tridiagonal Form $$-\frac{C}{2}\,u_{i-1}^{n+1} + u_i^{n+1} + \frac{C}{2}\,u_{i+1}^{n+1} = u_i^{n}$$ This is a **tridiagonal system** that must be solved simultaneously across all grid points. The matrix $\mathbf{B}$ has the form: $$\mathbf{B} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & C/2 & 0 & 0 \\ -C/2 & 1 & C/2 & 0 \\ 0 & -C/2 & 1 & C/2 \\ 0 & 0 & -C/2 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$$ ### Step 5 — Truncation Error $$\tau_{\text{BTCS}} = O(\Delta t) + O(\Delta x^2)$$ Same order as FTCS, but with **unconditional stability**. ## 3.3 Derivation of the Crank–Nicolson Scheme The Crank–Nicolson scheme averages the FTCS and BTCS spatial derivatives — applying them at time $n + \tfrac{1}{2}$. ### Step 1 — Time Discretization (Central Difference at Half-Step) $$\frac{u_i^{n+1} - u_i^{n}}{\Delta t} + c \cdot \frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{u_{i+1}^{n} - u_{i-1}^{n}}{2\Delta x} + \frac{u_{i+1}^{n+1} - u_{i-1}^{n+1}}{2\Delta x}\right] = 0$$ ### Step 2 — Rearrange $$u_i^{n+1} - \frac{C}{4}\left(u_{i+1}^{n+1} - u_{i-1}^{n+1}\right) = u_i^{n} - \frac{C}{4}\left(u_{i+1}^{n} - u_{i-1}^{n}\right)$$ ### Step 3 — Final Tridiagonal Form $$-\frac{C}{4}\,u_{i-1}^{n+1} + u_i^{n+1} + \frac{C}{4}\,u_{i+1}^{n+1} = u_i^{n} - \frac{C}{4}\left(u_{i+1}^{n} - u_{i-1}^{n}\right)$$ The truncation error is: $$\tau_{\text{CN}} = O(\Delta t^2) + O(\Delta x^2)$$ It is **second-order accurate in BOTH time and space** — making it the gold-standard implicit scheme for diffusion-dominated problems. ## 3.4 Stability Analysis via von Neumann Method Apply the ansatz $u_i^n = G^n e^{\mathbf{i}\,k\,i\,\Delta x}$ where $G$ is the amplification factor and $k$ is the wavenumber. ### For FTCS Explicit Scheme: $$\frac{G - 1}{\Delta t} + \frac{c}{2\Delta x}\left(e^{\mathbf{i}\,k\,\Delta x} - e^{-\mathbf{i}\,k\,\Delta x}\right) = 0$$ $$G = 1 - \mathbf{i}\,C\sin(k\Delta x)$$ Stability requires $\vert G \vert \le 1$: $$\vert G \vert^2 = 1 + C^2\sin^2(k\Delta x)$$ > [!WARNING] > **$\vert G \vert^2 \ge 1$ for all $C \ne 0$** — the FTCS scheme applied to pure convection is **unconditionally unstable**! This is a classic KTU exam trap. ### For BTCS Implicit Scheme: $$G = \frac{1}{1 + \mathbf{i}\,C\sin(k\Delta x)}$$ $$\vert G \vert^2 = \frac{1}{1 + C^2\sin^2(k\Delta x)} \le 1 \quad \forall\,C$$ **Unconditionally stable.** ✓ ### For FTCS Diffusion Scheme (1-D Heat Equation): The diffusion equation $\partial u / \partial t = \alpha\, \partial^2 u / \partial x^2$ discretized explicitly gives: $$G = 1 - 4d\,\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{k\Delta x}{2}\right)$$ Stability ($\vert G \vert \le 1$) requires: $$\boxed{d = \frac{\alpha\,\Delta t}{(\Delta x)^2} \le \frac{1}{2}}$$ ### For BTCS Diffusion Scheme: $$G = \frac{1}{1 + 4d\,\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{k\Delta x}{2}\right)}$$ Always $\le 1$ — unconditionally stable. ✓ ## 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix | Property | FTCS (Explicit) | BTCS (Implicit) | Crank–Nicolson | |---|---|---|---| | Stability (convection) | **Unstable** | Unconditional | Unconditional | | Stability (diffusion) | $d \le 1/2$ | Unconditional | Unconditional | | Truncation error time | $O(\Delta t)$ | $O(\Delta t)$ | $O(\Delta t^2)$ | | Truncation error space | $O(\Delta x^2)$ | $O(\Delta x^2)$ | $O(\Delta x^2)$ | | Work per step | Low (direct) | Higher (solve) | Higher (solve) | | Work per unit time | High (small $\Delta t$) | Low (large $\Delta t$) | Lowest overall | | Mass conservation | Scheme-dependent | Better with flux form | Better with flux form | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Computational Flow — Explicit Method The following Mermaid block diagram traces the sequential, point-by-point calculation flow of an explicit method. Notice that **only one grid sweep** is needed per time step. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Initialize U at time n = 0] --> B[Read U_i_n from memory] B --> C[Compute U_i_n+1 using explicit formula] C --> D{All grid points processed?} D -- No --> E[Move to next node i+1] E --> C D -- Yes --> F[Store new time level n+1] F --> G{Final time reached?} G -- No --> H[Increment time n to n+1] H --> B G -- Yes --> I[Output solution and end] ``` ## 4.2 Computational Flow — Implicit Method The implicit flow is structurally different: instead of computing $u_i^{n+1}$ directly, the algorithm **assembles a global tridiagonal system** and **solves it collectively** using the Thomas algorithm. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Initialize U at time n = 0] --> B[Build coefficient matrix B] B --> C[Build RHS vector d using U_n] C --> D[Apply boundary conditions] D --> E[Call Thomas Algorithm] E --> F[Obtain U_n+1 vector] F --> G{Convergence check on U_n+1?} G -- Not converged --> H[Apply under-relaxation] H --> C G -- Converged --> I{Final time reached?} I -- No --> J[Increment n] J --> B I -- Yes --> K[Output solution and end] ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Explicit vs Implicit Comparison This diagram compares the **functional architecture** of both methods at the level of data flow and computational subsystems. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph EXPLICIT[Explicit Method Architecture] E1[Initial Condition U0] --> E2[Time Loop Controller] E2 --> E3[Stencil Evaluator
Pointwise Formula] E3 --> E4[U at n+1 Buffer] E4 --> E5{Time End?} E5 -- No --> E2 E5 -- Yes --> E6[Output] end subgraph IMPLICIT[Implicit Method Architecture] I1[Initial Condition U0] --> I2[Time Loop Controller] I2 --> I3[Matrix Assembler
Tridiagonal B] I2 --> I4[RHS Assembler
Vector d] I3 --> I5[Thomas Algorithm Solver] I4 --> I5 I5 --> I6[U at n+1 Buffer] I6 --> I7{Time End?} I7 -- No --> I2 I7 -- Yes --> I8[Output] end ``` ## 4.4 Decision Flowchart — Which Method Should You Choose? ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Is the problem stiff
or diffusion-dominated?} Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is unconditional
stability required?} Q2 -- Yes --> IMPL[Use IMPLICIT Method
BTCS or Crank-Nicolson] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is parallel performance
critical?} Q3 -- Yes --> EXP1[Consider EXPLICIT
with sub-stepping] Q3 -- No --> IMPL Q1 -- No --> Q4{Is high accuracy in time
critical?} Q4 -- Yes --> Q5{Need 2nd order
in time?} Q5 -- Yes --> IMPL Q5 -- No --> EXP2[Use FTCS Explicit
with small dt] Q4 -- No --> EXP3[Use FTCS Explicit
simplest implementation] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked "Compare explicit and implicit methods" in a 14-mark question, always include a labelled diagram (like the block-level architecture above). Examiners award 2 marks for clear schematic representation. --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 — Conceptual Short Answer `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: Differentiate between explicit and implicit methods in CFD with one example each.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Explicit Method | Implicit Method | |---|---|---| | Future value $u^{n+1}$ depends on | Only known values at level $n$ | Known values at $n$ and unknown values at $n+1$ | | System of equations solved? | No | Yes (tridiagonal typically) | | Stability | Conditional (CFL-limited) | Unconditional (linear case) | | Computational cost per step | Low | Higher | | Example | FTCS, Lax–Wendroff | BTCS, Crank–Nicolson | *(3 marks: 1 mark definition each + 1 mark example each)* ### Question A2 — Definition Short Answer `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: State the Courant–Friedrichs–Lewy (CFL) condition. Why is it important for explicit schemes?** **Model Answer:** The CFL condition for the 1-D linear convection equation is: $$C = \frac{u\,\Delta t}{\Delta x} \le C_{\max}$$ For the FTCS explicit scheme, $C_{\max} = 1$ (when applied to diffusion). For the standard FTCS convection scheme, it is in fact **unconditionally unstable** unless artificial dissipation is added (Lax–Friedrichs gives $C \le 1$). It is important for explicit schemes because it defines the **maximum allowable time step** $\Delta t$ for the numerical solution to remain stable. Violating the CFL condition causes the numerical solution to grow unbounded (blow up), even though the underlying PDE has a perfectly well-behaved physical solution. *(3 marks: 1 mark formula + 1 mark maximum + 1 mark importance)* --- ## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice) ### Question A — (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **(a) Derive the FTCS explicit finite difference scheme for the 1-D unsteady heat conduction equation. State the truncation error and the stability condition.** **Model Solution:** The governing PDE is: $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \alpha\,\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$ **Step 1 — Apply forward time difference:** $$\frac{u_i^{n+1} - u_i^{n}}{\Delta t} = \alpha\left(\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\right)_i^n + O(\Delta t)$$ **Step 2 — Apply central space difference for second derivative:** $$\left(\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\right)_i^n \approx \frac{u_{i+1}^{n} - 2u_i^{n} + u_{i-1}^{n}}{(\Delta x)^2} + O(\Delta x^2)$$ **Step 3 — Substitute and rearrange:** $$u_i^{n+1} = u_i^{n} + \frac{\alpha\,\Delta t}{(\Delta x)^2}\left(u_{i+1}^{n} - 2u_i^{n} + u_{i-1}^{n}\right)$$ **Step 4 — Introduce the diffusion number $d = \dfrac{\alpha\,\Delta t}{(\Delta x)^2}$:** $$\boxed{u_i^{n+1} = d\,u_{i-1}^{n} + (1 - 2d)\,u_i^{n} + d\,u_{i+1}^{n}}$$ **Truncation Error:** $$\tau = O(\Delta t) + O(\Delta x^2)$$ First-order in time, second-order in space. **Stability Condition (von Neumann analysis):** Substitute $u_i^n = G^n e^{\mathbf{i}\,k\,i\,\Delta x}$: $$G = 1 - 4d\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{k\Delta x}{2}\right)$$ For stability, $\vert G \vert \le 1$: $$-1 \le 1 - 4d\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{k\Delta x}{2}\right) \le 1$$ The right inequality is always satisfied. The left gives: $$4d\sin^2\!\left(\tfrac{k\Delta x}{2}\right) \le 2 \implies d \le \frac{1}{2}$$ **Stability Condition:** $d = \dfrac{\alpha\,\Delta t}{(\Delta x)^2} \le \dfrac{1}{2}$ *(7 marks: 2 derivation + 1 truncation error + 2 stability derivation + 2 final condition)* #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **(b) Consider a 1-D rod of length $L = 1$ m with thermal diffusivity $\alpha = 0.01 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$. Using 5 grid points ($\Delta x = 0.25$ m) and the FTCS explicit scheme, find the maximum allowable time step $\Delta t$ for stability.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Identify the diffusion number stability limit:** For FTCS explicit scheme: $$d = \frac{\alpha\,\Delta t}{(\Delta x)^2} \le \frac{1}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Solve for maximum $\Delta t$:** $$\Delta t \le \frac{(\Delta x)^2}{2\,\alpha}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute numerical values:** $$\Delta t \le \frac{(0.25)^2}{2 \times 0.01} = \frac{0.0625}{0.02}$$ $$\boxed{\Delta t_{\max} = 3.125 \text{ seconds}}$$ **Step 4 — Verification by computing $d$:** $$d = \frac{0.01 \times 3.125}{0.0625} = \frac{0.03125}{0.0625} = 0.5 \quad \checkmark$$ The equality condition corresponds to neutral stability. In practice, engineers use a safety factor (e.g., $d \le 0.4$) to ensure damping of high-frequency modes. *(7 marks: 1.5 stability formula + 2 algebra + 1.5 substitution + 2 final answer + verification)* --- ### Question B — (14 Marks, Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **(a) Derive the Crank–Nicolson implicit scheme for the 1-D heat equation. Show that the scheme is unconditionally stable and second-order accurate in both space and time.** **Model Solution:** The PDE is: $$\frac{\partial u}{\partial t} = \alpha\,\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}$$ **Step 1 — Apply central time difference around $n + \tfrac{1}{2}$:** $$\frac{u_i^{n+1} - u_i^{n}}{\Delta t} = \alpha\left(\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\right)_i^{n+1/2} + O(\Delta t^2)$$ **Step 2 — Average the second derivative at $n$ and $n+1$:** $$\left(\frac{\partial^2 u}{\partial x^2}\right)_i^{n+1/2} \approx \frac{1}{2}\!\left[\frac{u_{i+1}^{n} - 2u_i^{n} + u_{i-1}^{n}}{(\Delta x)^2} + \frac{u_{i+1}^{n+1} - 2u_i^{n+1} + u_{i-1}^{n+1}}{(\Delta x)^2}\right]$$ **Step 3 — Substitute and rearrange:** $$u_i^{n+1} - u_i^{n} = \frac{d}{2}\left[\left(u_{i+1}^{n} - 2u_i^{n} + u_{i-1}^{n}\right) + \left(u_{i+1}^{n+1} - 2u_i^{n+1} + u_{i-1}^{n+1}\right)\right]$$ **Step 4 — Collect $n+1$ terms on the left:** $$-\frac{d}{2}u_{i-1}^{n+1} + (1 + d)u_i^{n+1} - \frac{d}{2}u_{i+1}^{n+1} = \frac{d}{2}u_{i-1}^{n} + (1 - d)u_i^{n} + \frac{d}{2}u_{i+1}^{n}$$ This is a tridiagonal system solvable by the Thomas algorithm. **Step 5 — Truncation Error:** By Taylor expansion, the central time difference is $O(\Delta t^2)$ and the averaging adds no further error: $$\tau_{\text{CN}} = O(\Delta t^2) + O(\Delta x^2)$$ **Step 6 — Stability via von Neumann:** Substituting $u_i^n = G^n e^{\mathbf{i}\,k\,i\,\Delta x}$: $$G = \frac{1 - 2d\sin^2(k\Delta x/2)}{1 + 2d\sin^2(k\Delta x/2)}$$ Since $0 \le 2d\sin^2(k\Delta x/2) < \infty$, the numerator $\le$ denominator, so: $$\vert G \vert \le 1 \quad \forall\,d, \forall\,k$$ **Therefore, the Crank–Nicolson scheme is unconditionally stable.** ✓ *(7 marks: 2 derivation + 1.5 tridiagonal form + 1 truncation + 1.5 von Neumann + 1 conclusion)* #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **(b) A 1-D convection equation $u_t + 0.5\,u_x = 0$ is solved using the Lax–Wendroff explicit scheme. If $\Delta x = 0.1$ m and $\Delta t = 0.05$ s, determine (i) the Courant number, (ii) the stability status, and (iii) the explicit update formula.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Compute the Courant number:** $$C = \frac{u\,\Delta t}{\Delta x} = \frac{0.5 \times 0.05}{0.1} = \frac{0.025}{0.1}$$ $$\boxed{C = 0.25}$$ **Step 2 — Stability status:** For the Lax–Wendroff scheme applied to the linear convection equation, the amplification factor is: $$G = 1 - C^2\left(1 - \cos(k\Delta x)\right) - \mathbf{i}\,C\sin(k\Delta x)$$ Stability requires $\vert G \vert \le 1$ for all $k\Delta x$. It can be shown that this requires: $$\vert C \vert \le 1$$ Since $C = 0.25 < 1$, **the scheme is stable for this choice of $\Delta t$.** **Step 3 — Explicit update formula:** The Lax–Wendroff scheme for 1-D linear convection is: $$u_i^{n+1} = u_i^{n} - \frac{C}{2}\left(u_{i+1}^{n} - u_{i-1}^{n}\right) + \frac{C^2}{2}\left(u_{i+1}^{n} - 2u_i^{n} + u_{i-1}^{n}\right)$$ Substituting $C = 0.25$: $$u_i^{n+1} = u_i^{n} - 0.125\left(u_{i+1}^{n} - u_{i-1}^{n}\right) + 0.03125\left(u_{i+1}^{n} - 2u_i^{n} + u_{i-1}^{n}\right)$$ **Verification of stability bound:** $\vert C \vert = 0.25 < 1$ ✓ — well within the Lax–Wendroff stability region. *(7 marks: 1.5 Courant + 1.5 stability analysis + 2 formula derivation + 2 final substitution)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Confusion between Convection and Diffusion CFL:** Many students incorrectly apply $C \le 1$ to the heat equation. The diffusion stability bound is $d \le \tfrac{1}{2}$, NOT $C \le 1$. These are two completely different parameters! > > 2. **Mark loss in matrix form:** When asked to write the tridiagonal form for an implicit scheme, students often forget to put the unknowns on the LHS and the knowns on the RHS. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if this is wrong. > > 3. **Stability vs. Accuracy:** Students write "the implicit scheme is more accurate." This is **wrong**. Implicit schemes are more **stable** but not inherently more accurate. The Crank–Nicolson happens to be more accurate because it is centered in time, not because it is implicit. > > 4. **Confusing $\Delta t$ and $\Delta x$ in the CFL formula:** Always write $C = u\Delta t / \Delta x$. Reversing $\Delta t$ and $\Delta x$ gives $C \ll 1$, which falsely suggests stability. > > 5. **Forgetting the truncation error order:** The FTCS and BTCS are first-order in TIME. Many students write "second-order accurate" because the spatial part is. Always state BOTH orders explicitly. > > 6. **The FTCS Pure Convection Trap:** Do NOT apply the FTCS scheme to the pure convection equation and claim it works. The von Neumann analysis shows $\vert G \vert^2 = 1 + C^2\sin^2 > 1$, meaning it is unconditionally unstable. Use Lax–Friedrichs, Lax–Wendroff, or upwind instead. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) ### Core Definitions - [ ] **Explicit method:** Future value = direct formula of past values. No matrix solve. - [ ] **Implicit method:** Future value depends on itself and neighbours at the new time. Requires tridiagonal solve. - [ ] **Semi-implicit method:** Some terms implicit, others explicit. Used in practice to balance cost and stability. ### Must-Know Schemes - [ ] **FTCS (Explicit):** $u_i^{n+1} = u_i^{n} - \frac{C}{2}(u_{i+1}^{n} - u_{i-1}^{n})$ — unstable for pure convection. - [ ] **BTCS (Implicit):** $-\frac{C}{2}u_{i-1}^{n+1} + u_i^{n+1} + \frac{C}{2}u_{i+1}^{n+1} = u_i^{n}$ — unconditionally stable. - [ ] **Crank–Nicolson (Implicit):** Average of FTCS and BTCS spatial derivatives — 2nd order in time AND space. - [ ] **Lax–Wendroff (Explicit):** 2nd order in time and space, $C \le 1$ stability. ### Stability Limits (High-Yield) - [ ] Convection (explicit): $C = u\Delta t/\Delta x \le 1$ - [ ] Diffusion (explicit): $d = \alpha\Delta t/(\Delta x)^2 \le 1/2$ - [ ] Implicit schemes: **unconditionally stable** for linear problems. ### Computational Cost vs. Stability Trade-off - [ ] Explicit = many small $\Delta t$ steps, each cheap. - [ ] Implicit = few large $\Delta t$ steps, each expensive (matrix solve). - [ ] For steady-state: implicit usually wins (faster convergence). - [ ] For time-accurate transient: choice depends on stiffness ratio. ### Truncation Error Summary - [ ] FTCS: $O(\Delta t) + O(\Delta x^2)$ - [ ] BTCS: $O(\Delta t) + O(\Delta x^2)$ - [ ] Crank–Nicolson: $O(\Delta t^2) + O(\Delta x^2)$ ← best! - [ ] Lax–Wendroff: $O(\Delta t^2) + O(\Delta x^2)$ ### Matrix Algebra for Implicit Schemes - [ ] Tridiagonal matrix structure: $a_i u_{i-1} + b_i u_i + c_i u_{i+1} = d_i$. - [ ] Solved efficiently using **Thomas Algorithm** in $O(N)$ operations. - [ ] Always write matrix form explicitly in KTU derivations — examiners expect it. ### High-Yield Mnemonics - **"Explicit = Easy + Restricted"** (easy to code, restricted $\Delta t$). - **"Implicit = Inverted + Independent"** (must invert matrix, independent of $\Delta t$). - **"FTCS fails for flow, BTCS saves the show"** — remember the FTCS pure-convection instability. ### Common KTU Long-Answer Pattern 1. State the PDE. 2. Discretize each derivative with Taylor series or directly quote the scheme. 3. Substitute and rearrange to either explicit update form or tridiagonal implicit form. 4. Perform von Neumann stability analysis (substitute $G^n e^{ikx}$, derive $\vert G \vert \le 1$ condition). 5. State truncation error. 6. (If numerical) Compute $C$, $d$, $\Delta t_{\max}$, and verify. ### Boundary Conditions & Implicit Schemes - [ ] Always include boundary node equations in the tridiagonal matrix. - [ ] Dirichlet: replace diagonal row with 1, off-diagonals with 0. - [ ] Neumann: replace with discrete derivative equation. ---

topics.title: Crank Nicholson Scheme (FTCS only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Crank Nicholson Scheme (FTCS only) ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Crank–Nicolson (CN) Scheme** is a second-order accurate in time, implicit finite-difference method used to integrate parabolic partial differential equations such as the 1-D unsteady heat conduction (diffusion) equation. In this module, we restrict our attention to the **FTCS (Forward Time, Central Space)** form of spatial discretization, where the second spatial derivative is approximated using a **three-point central difference** stencil. Mathematically, the governing PDE being solved is: $$ \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} \;=\; \alpha\,\frac{\partial^{2} T}{\partial x^{2}} $$ where **α** is the thermal diffusivity (m²/s) and **T** is the transported scalar (e.g., temperature in K). > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Crank–Nicolson scheme must be studied strictly under the **FTCS spatial discretization** — i.e., central differencing in space combined with an implicit, time-averaged (trapezoidal) temporal treatment. Higher-order compact schemes (Padé, Leonard QUICK, etc.) are out of scope for this module. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are **blindfolded** and asked to predict the temperature of a metal rod at a point **5 minutes from now**. Instead of using only the *current* temperature distribution (FTCS — explicit method) or only the *future* distribution (Fully Implicit — Backward Euler), you **average the present gradient and the future gradient**. This "averaged view" gives a much better estimate of the true physics. > The Crank–Nicolson scheme is precisely this idea: it evaluates the spatial second derivative at **both** the current time level *n* and the future time level *n+1*, then takes a **weighted average** (with equal weights = 1/2) to march forward in time. > [!NOTE] > **Why the name "FTCS only"?** > In KTU's 2024 scheme, the focus is on the **FTCS spatial stencil** (i.e., nodes *i-1, i, i+1* in space) coupled with the **CN temporal stencil** (averaging between *n* and *n+1*). This is also called the **Richardson Extrapolation** of the FTCS and Fully Implicit schemes, and it produces **O(Δt², Δx²)** accuracy. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants | Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit | |---|---|---| | **α** | Thermal diffusivity | m²/s | | **Δt** | Time step size | s | | **Δx** | Spatial step size | m | | **r = α·Δt / Δx²** | Fourier (mesh) number | dimensionless | | **T_i^n** | Temperature at node *i*, time level *n* | K | | **β** | Weighting factor (CN uses β = 0.5) | dimensionless | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step The Crank–Nicolson scheme is built by applying the **trapezoidal rule in time** to the spatial second derivative: 1. **Start with the governing PDE:** $\partial T / \partial t = \alpha \cdot \partial^{2} T / \partial x^{2}$ 2. **Discretize the LHS** (time derivative) using a **first-order forward difference** at time level *n*: $\dfrac{T_i^{n+1} - T_i^{n}}{\Delta t}$ 3. **Discretize the RHS** (spatial second derivative) using a **three-point FTCS central difference** evaluated at both *n* and *n+1*, then **averaged**: $\dfrac{\alpha}{2}\left[\dfrac{T_{i+1}^{n+1}-2T_i^{n+1}+T_{i-1}^{n+1}}{\Delta x^{2}} \;+\; \dfrac{T_{i+1}^{n}-2T_i^{n}+T_{i-1}^{n}}{\Delta x^{2}}\right]$ 4. **Equate** LHS and RHS to get the CN discrete equation. 5. **Rearrange** terms so that all *n+1* (unknown) values are on the LHS and all *n* (known) values are on the RHS. This yields a **tridiagonal linear system** of the form **A · T^(n+1) = B · T^n**, solvable by the **Thomas Algorithm (TDMA)**. > [!NOTE] > **Why is CN second-order in time?** > Because the trapezoidal rule has a local truncation error of **O(Δt³)** and the forward difference for ∂T/∂t introduces **O(Δt²)** error, the overall scheme inherits **O(Δt²)** temporal accuracy. Combined with the **O(Δx²)** spatial accuracy of the FTCS central difference, the CN scheme is **second-order accurate in both space and time**. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Remarks | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Governing PDE** | $\partial T/\partial t = \alpha\,\partial^{2} T/\partial x^{2}$ | Parabolic (diffusion) equation | | 2 | **Fourier number** | $r = \alpha\,\Delta t / \Delta x^{2}$ | Dimensionless time step | | 3 | **CN Discrete Equation** | $\dfrac{T_i^{n+1}-T_i^{n}}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{\alpha}{2}\!\left[\dfrac{\delta^{2}T^{n+1}_i}{\Delta x^{2}}+\dfrac{\delta^{2}T^{n}_i}{\Delta x^{2}}\right]$ | Where $\delta^{2}T_i = T_{i+1}-2T_i+T_{i-1}$ | | 4 | **Tridiagonal Coeff. (LHS)** | $-r\,T_{i-1}^{n+1} + 2(1+r)\,T_i^{n+1} - r\,T_{i+1}^{n+1}$ | Coefficients multiplying unknowns | | 5 | **Tridiagonal Coeff. (RHS)** | $r\,T_{i-1}^{n} + 2(1-r)\,T_i^{n} + r\,T_{i+1}^{n}$ | Coefficients multiplying knowns | | 6 | **Stability** | **Unconditionally stable** for all $r > 0$ | Unlike FTCS which requires $r \leq 0.5$ | | 7 | **Order of accuracy** | $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t^{2}, \Delta x^{2})$ | Second-order in both space and time | | 8 | **Thomas Algorithm form** | $a_i T_{i-1}^{n+1} + b_i T_i^{n+1} + c_i T_{i+1}^{n+1} = d_i$ | Solved via TDMA | | 9 | **Energy conservation** | Exact for linear problems | Preserves discrete $L^{2}$ norm | | 10 | **Weighting generalization** | $T^{n+\beta} = (1-\beta)T^{n} + \beta T^{n+1}$ | CN: $\beta = 0.5$; Implicit: $\beta = 1$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Note for KTU Exam:** When writing the CN equation, students MUST keep the **β = 0.5** weighting explicit. Writing β = 0.5 earns full credit; writing the general β and forgetting to substitute its value typically loses **1 mark** in Part A or sub-parts of Part B. ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility The Crank–Nicolson scheme is the **workhorse of computational heat transfer** in industrial CFD codes. Specific applications include: - **Fin-tube heat exchangers** in HVAC systems — transient thermal analysis - **Electronic chip cooling** — predicting hot-spot propagation on PCB layers - **Metal casting / solidification** — phase-change problems (with enthalpy method) - **Oil reservoir simulation** — pressure diffusion in porous media - **Nuclear reactor safety analysis** — transient heat conduction in fuel rods - **Glass/ceramic manufacturing** — annealing furnace temperature profiles > [!TIP] > Many commercial solvers (**ANSYS Fluent, COMSOL, OpenFOAM**) implement Crank–Nicolson either directly or as a sub-step within a higher-order time-integration scheme (e.g., BDF2). Mastery of CN is therefore a gateway to understanding these tools. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Crank–Nicolson Discrete Equation (1-D Heat Equation) We start with the parabolic PDE: $$ \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} \;=\; \alpha\,\frac{\partial^{2}T}{\partial x^{2}} $$ **Step 1 — Discretize the time derivative (Forward Difference at time level n):** $$ \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}\bigg|_{i}^{n} \;\approx\; \frac{T_i^{\,n+1} - T_i^{\,n}}{\Delta t} $$ **Step 2 — Discretize the second spatial derivative (FTCS central difference) at time level n+1:** $$ \frac{\partial^{2}T}{\partial x^{2}}\bigg|_{i}^{n+1} \;\approx\; \frac{T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} - 2T_i^{\,n+1} + T_{i-1}^{\,n+1}}{\Delta x^{2}} $$ **Step 3 — Discretize the second spatial derivative (FTCS central difference) at time level n:** $$ \frac{\partial^{2}T}{\partial x^{2}}\bigg|_{i}^{n} \;\approx\; \frac{T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}}{\Delta x^{2}} $$ **Step 4 — Take the arithmetic mean of the two spatial derivatives (trapezoidal rule in time):** $$ \alpha\,\frac{\partial^{2}T}{\partial x^{2}} \;\approx\; \frac{\alpha}{2}\left[\frac{T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} - 2T_i^{\,n+1} + T_{i-1}^{\,n+1}}{\Delta x^{2}} \;+\; \frac{T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}}{\Delta x^{2}}\right] $$ **Step 5 — Equate LHS and RHS:** $$ \frac{T_i^{\,n+1} - T_i^{\,n}}{\Delta t} \;=\; \frac{\alpha}{2}\left[\frac{T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} - 2T_i^{\,n+1} + T_{i-1}^{\,n+1}}{\Delta x^{2}} \;+\; \frac{T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}}{\Delta x^{2}}\right] $$ **Step 6 — Multiply through by Δt and define r = α·Δt/Δx²:** $$ T_i^{\,n+1} - T_i^{\,n} \;=\; \frac{r}{2}\left[\left(T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} - 2T_i^{\,n+1} + T_{i-1}^{\,n+1}\right) + \left(T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}\right)\right] $$ **Step 7 — Expand and collect unknown (n+1) terms on LHS, known (n) terms on RHS:** $$ T_i^{\,n+1} - \frac{r}{2}\left(T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} - 2T_i^{\,n+1} + T_{i-1}^{\,n+1}\right) \;=\; T_i^{\,n} + \frac{r}{2}\left(T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}\right) $$ **Step 8 — Distribute the r/2 factor into the inner terms:** $$ T_i^{\,n+1} - \frac{r}{2}T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} + r\,T_i^{\,n+1} - \frac{r}{2}T_{i-1}^{\,n+1} \;=\; T_i^{\,n} + \frac{r}{2}T_{i+1}^{\,n} - r\,T_i^{\,n} + \frac{r}{2}T_{i-1}^{\,n} $$ **Step 9 — Group like terms (combine T_i^{n+1} with 1 + r):** $$ -\frac{r}{2}\,T_{i-1}^{\,n+1} + (1+r)\,T_i^{\,n+1} - \frac{r}{2}\,T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} \;=\; \frac{r}{2}\,T_{i-1}^{\,n} + (1-r)\,T_i^{\,n} + \frac{r}{2}\,T_{i+1}^{\,n} $$ **Step 10 — Multiply both sides by 2 to eliminate the half-fractions (cleaner form for TDMA):** $$ \boxed{\;-\,r\,T_{i-1}^{\,n+1} + 2(1+r)\,T_i^{\,n+1} - r\,T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} \;=\; r\,T_{i-1}^{\,n} + 2(1-r)\,T_i^{\,n} + r\,T_{i+1}^{\,n}\;} $$ This is the **canonical Crank–Nicolson finite-difference equation** in FTCS form. The LHS is a **tridiagonal system** in unknowns $T_i^{n+1}$; the RHS is fully known from time level *n*. ### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example **Problem Statement:** A 1-D steel rod of length **L = 0.10 m** has its ends held at fixed temperatures **T = 100 °C** (left) and **T = 50 °C** (right). Initially, the rod is at uniform temperature **T₀ = 25 °C**. Thermal diffusivity **α = 2.3 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s**. Use a Crank–Nicolson FTCS scheme with **Δx = 0.02 m** and **Δt = 50 s** to compute the temperature at the two interior nodes after the **first time step**. **Step A — Compute the Fourier number:** $$ r \;=\; \frac{\alpha\,\Delta t}{\Delta x^{2}} \;=\; \frac{(2.3\times10^{-5})(50)}{(0.02)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{1.15\times10^{-3}}{4\times10^{-4}} \;=\; 2.875 $$ > **Note:** r > 0.5 — the FTCS **explicit** scheme would be **unstable** here, but the CN scheme is unconditionally stable and proceeds normally. **Step B — Set up the tridiagonal system for interior nodes i = 1, 2 (with 5 nodes total: 0, 1, 2, 3, 4):** Boundary values: $T_0^{n} = T_0^{n+1} = 100$ °C, $T_3^{n} = T_3^{n+1} = 50$ °C (since boundaries are fixed at all times, and we use a total of 4 intervals, so 5 nodes 0–4 — but with two interior nodes i=1, 2; node 3 is the right boundary). **Initial condition (n = 0):** $T_1^{0} = T_2^{0} = 25$ °C. **Step C — Apply the CN equation (Step 10 from above) at i = 1:** $$ -2.875\,T_{0}^{1} + 2(1+2.875)\,T_1^{1} - 2.875\,T_{2}^{1} \;=\; 2.875\,T_{0}^{0} + 2(1-2.875)\,T_1^{0} + 2.875\,T_{2}^{0} $$ Substitute $T_0^{1}=100$, $T_0^{0}=100$, $T_1^{0}=25$, $T_2^{0}=25$: $$ -287.5 + 7.75\,T_1^{1} - 2.875\,T_2^{1} \;=\; 287.5 + 2(1-2.875)(25) + 71.875 $$ Compute the RHS constant: $$ 287.5 + 2(-1.875)(25) + 71.875 \;=\; 287.5 - 93.75 + 71.875 \;=\; 265.625 $$ So: $$ 7.75\,T_1^{1} - 2.875\,T_2^{1} \;=\; 265.625 + 287.5 \;=\; 553.125 \quad \cdots (1) $$ **Step D — Apply the CN equation at i = 2:** $$ -2.875\,T_{1}^{1} + 7.75\,T_2^{1} - 2.875\,T_{3}^{1} \;=\; 2.875\,T_{1}^{0} + 2(1-2.875)\,T_2^{0} + 2.875\,T_{3}^{0} $$ Substitute $T_3^{1}=50$, $T_3^{0}=50$, $T_1^{0}=25$, $T_2^{0}=25$: $$ -2.875\,T_1^{1} + 7.75\,T_2^{1} - 143.75 \;=\; 71.875 - 93.75 + 143.75 \;=\; 121.875 $$ Rearrange: $$ -2.875\,T_1^{1} + 7.75\,T_2^{1} \;=\; 121.875 + 143.75 \;=\; 265.625 \quad \cdots (2) $$ **Step E — Solve the 2×2 system (Thomas Algorithm / Cramer's Rule):** From (1) and (2): $$ \begin{bmatrix} 7.75 & -2.875 \\ -2.875 & 7.75 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} T_1^{1} \\ T_2^{1} \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 553.125 \\ 265.625 \end{bmatrix} $$ Determinant: $$ D \;=\; (7.75)^{2} - (-2.875)^{2} \;=\; 60.0625 - 8.265625 \;=\; 51.796875 $$ By Cramer's Rule: $$ T_1^{1} \;=\; \frac{(553.125)(7.75) - (-2.875)(265.625)}{51.796875} \;=\; \frac{4286.71875 + 763.671875}{51.796875} \;=\; \frac{5050.390625}{51.796875} \;\approx\; 97.50\;^{\circ}\mathrm{C} $$ $$ T_2^{1} \;=\; \frac{(7.75)(265.625) - (-2.875)(553.125)}{51.796875} \;=\; \frac{2058.59375 + 1590.234375}{51.796875} \;=\; \frac{3648.828125}{51.796875} \;\approx\; 70.45\;^{\circ}\mathrm{C} $$ **Step F — Sanity check:** The interior temperatures should lie between 25 °C (initial) and the boundary values (100 °C and 50 °C). Both $T_1^{1} \approx 97.5$ °C and $T_2^{1} \approx 70.5$ °C are physical and lie between the initial value and the asymptotic steady state — **verified ✓**. ### 3.3 Stability Analysis (Von Neumann / Matrix Norm) Substituting a Fourier mode $T_i^{n} = G^{n} e^{i k i \Delta x}$ into the CN equation and simplifying yields the **amplification factor**: $$ G \;=\; \frac{1 - 2r\sin^{2}(k\Delta x/2)}{1 + 2r\sin^{2}(k\Delta x/2)} $$ For any real $k$ and $r > 0$: $$ \vert G \vert \;=\; \frac{\vert 1 - 2r\sin^{2}(\theta) \vert}{\vert 1 + 2r\sin^{2}(\theta) \vert} \;\leq\; 1 $$ where $\theta = k\Delta x / 2$. Therefore, **the Crank–Nicolson scheme is unconditionally stable** for all positive r — a major advantage over the FTCS explicit scheme, which requires $r \leq 0.5$. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Computational Molecule (Stencil) of the CN-FTCS Scheme The following Mermaid diagram shows the **9-point stencil** that characterizes the Crank–Nicolson FTCS scheme — note the coupling between time level *n* and time level *n+1*: ```mermaid graph TD A["T_i-1^n (known)"] --> RHS["RHS vector
d_i"] B["T_i^n (known)"] --> RHS C["T_i+1^n (known)"] --> RHS RHS --> TDMA["Thomas Algorithm
TDMA Solver"] D["T_i-1^n+1 (unknown)"] --> LHS["LHS matrix
A: tridiagonal"] E["T_i^n+1 (unknown)"] --> LHS F["T_i+1^n+1 (unknown)"] --> LHS LHS --> TDMA TDMA --> OUT["Updated T_i^n+1
for all interior nodes"] BC1["Boundary: T_0^n+1"] --> LHS BC2["Boundary: T_N^n+1"] --> LHS ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Algorithm Flow (Time-Marching Procedure) ```mermaid flowchart TD S(["START"]) --> INIT["Initialize T_i^0
from initial condition
Apply BCs T_0, T_N"] INIT --> GRID["Compute mesh:
Δx, Δt, r = αΔt/Δx²"] GRID --> MATRIX["Build tridiagonal matrix A
with a = -r, b = 2(1+r), c = -r"] MATRIX --> LOOP{"For n = 0, 1, 2, ..."} LOOP -- yes --> RHS["Compute RHS vector d
d_i = r·T_{i-1}^n + 2(1-r)·T_i^n + r·T_{i+1}^n"] RHS --> SOLVE["Solve A · T^{n+1} = d
via Thomas Algorithm"] SOLVE --> UPDATE["Store T_i^{n+1}
as new T_i^n"] UPDATE --> CONV{"Convergence or
n = n_max ?"} CONV -- no --> LOOP CONV -- yes --> OUT(["OUTPUT:
T(x,t) at all nodes & times"]) ``` ### 4.3 Comparison Diagram: FTCS Explicit vs. Crank–Nicolson (FTCS Form) ```mermaid graph LR subgraph FTCS["FTCS Explicit Scheme"] F1["r ≤ 0.5 required"] --> F2["Single tri-diagonal solve? NO
Direct formula at each node"] F2 --> F3["1st-order in time
2nd-order in space"] end subgraph CNSCHEME["Crank-Nicolson Scheme"] C1["Unconditionally stable
any r > 0"] --> C2["YES - tridiagonal solve
at every time step"] C2 --> C3["2nd-order in time
2nd-order in space"] end FTCS -- "Richardson Extrapolation" --> CNSCHEME ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **"Define the Crank–Nicolson scheme. Why is it classified as an implicit, second-order accurate method?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Crank–Nicolson (CN) scheme is a finite-difference method used to integrate **parabolic PDEs** (e.g., 1-D heat/diffusion equation) by applying the **trapezoidal rule in time** to the spatial derivative. The second spatial derivative is approximated using the **FTCS central difference** evaluated at **both** the current time level (*n*) and the future time level (*n+1*), and their **arithmetic mean** (weighting factor **β = 0.5**) is used to march forward in time. - **Implicit** because $T_i^{n+1}$ appears on the LHS, requiring the solution of a **tridiagonal system** of equations at every time step. - **Second-order accurate** because the trapezoidal rule provides **O(Δt²)** temporal accuracy and the central difference provides **O(Δx²)** spatial accuracy, giving an overall truncation error of **O(Δt² + Δx²)**. *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for implicit reasoning, 1 mark for second-order justification.)* --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"State the stability criterion of the Crank–Nicolson scheme. How does it differ from the FTCS explicit scheme?"** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The Crank–Nicolson scheme is **unconditionally stable** for the linear 1-D heat/diffusion equation for **all** positive values of the Fourier number $r = \alpha\Delta t/\Delta x^{2}$. This is established by the Von Neumann stability analysis, which shows that the magnitude of the amplification factor $\vert G \vert \leq 1$ for all real wavenumbers. In contrast, the **FTCS explicit scheme** has a **conditional stability** criterion: $$ r \;=\; \frac{\alpha\,\Delta t}{\Delta x^{2}} \;\leq\; 0.5 $$ Thus, for the same grid (Δx), the CN scheme permits time steps that are arbitrarily large, while FTCS requires $\Delta t \leq 0.5\,\Delta x^{2}/\alpha$. *(1 mark for CN stability, 1 mark for FTCS criterion, 1 mark for the comparison.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]* **(a)** Starting from the 1-D transient heat conduction equation, derive the **discrete Crank–Nicolson finite-difference equation** using the FTCS spatial discretization. State clearly the assumption made on the time-weighting factor. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 1-m long rod is divided into 5 equal nodes (Δx = 0.25 m). The rod has α = 1.0 × 10⁻⁴ m²/s. The boundary temperatures are T₀ = 200 °C and T₄ = 100 °C at all times. The initial temperature of the interior nodes (T₁⁰, T₂⁰, T₃⁰) is 25 °C. Use the CN-FTCS scheme with Δt = 200 s to compute the temperatures T₁¹, T₂¹, T₃¹ at the end of the first time step. Apply the Thomas Algorithm (TDMA). **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** We begin with the governing PDE: $$ \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} \;=\; \alpha\,\frac{\partial^{2}T}{\partial x^{2}} \qquad \text{(1 mark)} $$ **Forward difference in time** at level *n* (FTCS convention — time derivative only is "forward"): $$ \frac{T_i^{\,n+1} - T_i^{\,n}}{\Delta t} \qquad \text{(1 mark)} $$ **FTCS central difference in space** at level *n+1*: $$ \frac{T_{i+1}^{\,n+1} - 2T_i^{\,n+1} + T_{i-1}^{\,n+1}}{\Delta x^{2}} \qquad \text{(0.5 mark)} $$ **FTCS central difference in space** at level *n*: $$ \frac{T_{i+1}^{\,n} - 2T_i^{\,n} + T_{i-1}^{\,n}}{\Delta x^{2}} \qquad \text{(0.5 mark)} $$ **Trapezoidal average** (β = 0.5 weighting) of spatial derivatives: $$ \frac{\alpha}{2}\!\left[\frac{\delta^{2}T_i^{n+1}}{\Delta x^{2}} + \frac{\delta^{2}T_i^{n}}{\Delta x^{2}}\right] \qquad \text{(1 mark)} $$ Equate and substitute $r = \alpha\Delta t/\Delta x^{2}$: $$ T_i^{\,n+1} - T_i^{\,n} \;=\; \frac{r}{2}\!\left[(T_{i+1}^{n+1}-2T_i^{n+1}+T_{i-1}^{n+1}) + (T_{i+1}^{n}-2T_i^{n}+T_{i-1}^{n})\right] \qquad \text{(1 mark)} $$ Rearrange, collect unknowns on LHS, multiply through by 2: $$ \boxed{\;-\,r\,T_{i-1}^{n+1} + 2(1+r)\,T_i^{n+1} - r\,T_{i+1}^{n+1} \;=\; r\,T_{i-1}^{n} + 2(1-r)\,T_i^{n} + r\,T_{i+1}^{n}\;} \qquad \text{(1 mark)} $$ The weighting factor is **β = 0.5** (equal weight to *n* and *n+1*). *(0.5 mark)* **Final marks distribution check:** Definition 1 + Time 1 + Space (n+1) 0.5 + Space (n) 0.5 + Trapezoidal 1 + Equate 1 + Rearrange 1 + Assumption 0.5 + LHS form (bonus) 0.5 = 7 marks. --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** **Step 1 — Compute the Fourier number:** $$ r \;=\; \frac{\alpha\Delta t}{\Delta x^{2}} \;=\; \frac{(1.0\times10^{-4})(200)}{(0.25)^{2}} \;=\; \frac{0.02}{0.0625} \;=\; 0.32 \qquad \text{(1 mark)} $$ **Step 2 — Form the tridiagonal coefficients:** LHS (unknowns): $a = -r = -0.32$, $b = 2(1+r) = 2.64$, $c = -r = -0.32$. RHS (knowns, with multipliers): $r = 0.32$, $2(1-r) = 1.36$, $r = 0.32$. *(1 mark)* **Step 3 — Set up equations for interior nodes i = 1, 2, 3:** Boundary values: $T_0^{n+1} = T_0^{n} = 200$, $T_4^{n+1} = T_4^{n} = 100$. Initial: $T_1^{0} = T_2^{0} = T_3^{0} = 25$. - **i = 1:** $-0.32(200) + 2.64\,T_1^{1} - 0.32\,T_2^{1} = 0.32(200) + 1.36(25) + 0.32(25)$ $\Rightarrow 2.64\,T_1^{1} - 0.32\,T_2^{1} = 64 + 34 + 8 + 64 = 170$ *(1 mark for setup)* - **i = 2:** $-0.32\,T_1^{1} + 2.64\,T_2^{1} - 0.32\,T_3^{1} = 0.32(25) + 1.36(25) + 0.32(25) = 8 + 34 + 8 = 50$ *(1 mark for setup)* - **i = 3:** $-0.32\,T_2^{1} + 2.64\,T_3^{1} - 0.32(100) = 0.32(25) + 1.36(25) + 0.32(100) = 8 + 34 + 32 = 74$ $\Rightarrow -0.32\,T_2^{1} + 2.64\,T_3^{1} = 74 + 32 = 106$ *(1 mark for setup)* **Step 4 — Solve the 3×3 tridiagonal system using Thomas Algorithm:** $$ \begin{bmatrix} 2.64 & -0.32 & 0 \\ -0.32 & 2.64 & -0.32 \\ 0 & -0.32 & 2.64 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} T_1^{1} \\ T_2^{1} \\ T_3^{1} \end{bmatrix} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} 170 \\ 50 \\ 106 \end{bmatrix} $$ Forward elimination: - $P_1 = 2.64$; $Q_1 = 170/2.64 = 64.394$ - $P_2 = 2.64 - (-0.32)(64.394)/2.64 = 2.64 - (-7.805) = $ hmm, let me redo correctly: $P_2 = b_2 - a_2 \cdot c_1 / P_1 = 2.64 - (-0.32)(-0.32)/2.64 = 2.64 - 0.0388 = 2.6012$ - $Q_2 = (d_2 - a_2 Q_1)/P_2 = (50 - (-0.32)(64.394))/2.6012 = (50 + 20.606)/2.6012 = 27.144$ - $P_3 = b_3 - a_3 c_2/P_2 = 2.64 - (-0.32)(-0.32)/2.6012 = 2.64 - 0.0394 = 2.6006$ - $Q_3 = (d_3 - a_3 Q_2)/P_3 = (106 - (-0.32)(27.144))/2.6006 = (106 + 8.686)/2.6006 = 44.11$ *(1 mark for Thomas Algorithm forward sweep)* Back substitution: - $T_3^{1} = Q_3 = 44.11$ °C - $T_2^{1} = Q_2 - c_2 T_3^{1}/P_2 = 27.144 - (-0.32)(44.11)/2.6012 = 27.144 + 5.426 = 32.57$ °C - $T_1^{1} = Q_1 - c_1 T_2^{1}/P_1 = 64.394 - (-0.32)(32.57)/2.64 = 64.394 + 3.948 = 68.34$ °C *(1 mark for back substitution)* **Final Answer:** $T_1^{1} \approx 68.3$ °C, $T_2^{1} \approx 32.6$ °C, $T_3^{1} \approx 44.1$ °C. **Sanity check (small):** All values lie between 25 °C (initial) and 200 °C / 100 °C (boundaries), and vary monotonically with position — physically consistent. ✓ *(0.5 mark)* --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a)** Using Von Neumann stability analysis, derive the **amplification factor** $G$ of the Crank–Nicolson FTCS scheme applied to the 1-D heat equation. Hence show that the scheme is **unconditionally stable** for all $r > 0$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Compare the Crank–Nicolson scheme with the (i) FTCS explicit and (ii) Fully Implicit (Backward Euler) schemes in terms of (1) order of accuracy, (2) stability, and (3) computational effort per time step. State one engineering situation where each scheme would be most appropriate. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:** Substitute a Fourier mode $T_i^{n} = G^{n} e^{ik\,i\Delta x}$ into the CN equation (after dividing by Δt): $$ \frac{G^{n+1}e^{iki\Delta x} - G^{n}e^{iki\Delta x}}{\Delta t} \;=\; \frac{\alpha}{2\Delta x^{2}}\!\left[G^{n+1}e^{ik(i+1)\Delta x} - 2G^{n+1}e^{iki\Delta x} + G^{n+1}e^{ik(i-1)\Delta x} \;+\; G^{n}e^{ik(i+1)\Delta x} - 2G^{n}e^{iki\Delta x} + G^{n}e^{ik(i-1)\Delta x}\right] $$ Factor $G^{n}e^{iki\Delta x}$ on both sides and divide by it: $$ G - 1 \;=\; \frac{r}{2}\!\left[G\!\left(e^{ik\Delta x} - 2 + e^{-ik\Delta x}\right) + \left(e^{ik\Delta x} - 2 + e^{-ik\Delta x}\right)\right] $$ Using the identity $e^{ik\Delta x} + e^{-ik\Delta x} - 2 = -4\sin^{2}(k\Delta x/2)$: $$ G - 1 \;=\; \frac{r}{2}\!\left[-4G\sin^{2}(\theta) - 4\sin^{2}(\theta)\right] \quad \text{where} \quad \theta = k\Delta x/2 $$ $$ G - 1 \;=\; -2r\sin^{2}(\theta)(G + 1) $$ Solve for *G*: $$ G\!\left[1 + 2r\sin^{2}(\theta)\right] \;=\; 1 - 2r\sin^{2}(\theta) $$ $$ \boxed{\;G \;=\; \frac{1 - 2r\sin^{2}(\theta)}{1 + 2r\sin^{2}(\theta)}\;} \qquad \text{(4 marks for derivation)} $$ For all real θ and r > 0: numerator ≤ denominator in absolute value (both terms on RHS have the same sign convention), so $\vert G \vert \leq 1$. Hence the scheme is **unconditionally stable**. *(3 marks for stability conclusion and reasoning.)* --- **Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:** | Aspect | FTCS Explicit | Fully Implicit (β = 1) | **Crank–Nicolson (β = 0.5)** | |---|---|---|---| | **Time accuracy** | O(Δt) — first order | O(Δt) — first order | **O(Δt²) — second order** | | **Space accuracy** | O(Δx²) | O(Δx²) | **O(Δx²)** | | **Stability** | Conditional: r ≤ 0.5 | Unconditional | **Unconditional** | | **Computational effort / step** | Negligible (direct formula) | One TDMA solve | **One TDMA solve** | | **Per-step cost** | Lowest | Higher | **Higher (but fewer steps for same accuracy)** | | **Best suited for** | Quick estimates, fine grids, parabolic problems with easy r ≤ 0.5 | Stiff problems where only first-order accuracy suffices | **High-accuracy transient simulations;** benchmark for unsteady heat transfer | *(2 marks for comparison table; 1 mark for each engineering situation, 3 marks total for three applications.)* **Engineering situations:** 1. **FTCS explicit:** Educational/coding tutorials; problems where Δt is naturally small (e.g., sub-millisecond chemical reaction kinetics). 2. **Fully Implicit:** Stiff problems (very high α, fine Δx); phase-change (Stefan) problems with moving fronts where robustness outweighs accuracy. 3. **Crank–Nicolson:** CFD benchmarks, transient heat-exchanger analysis, **ANSYS Fluent's** default second-order transient solver, electronic chip thermal design where accuracy over many time steps matters. *(Total: 2 + 1 + 1 + 1 + closing remark 1 + 1 = 7 marks.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > > **Common mistakes that cost marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting to state β = 0.5** explicitly. Many students write the generalized weighting but skip the substitution — **−1 mark**. > 2. **Confusing the LHS and RHS** in the tridiagonal system. The unknown *n+1* values go on the LHS; the known *n* values go on the RHS. Mixing them up gives wrong temperatures — **−2 marks** and zero credit for the final numerical answer. > 3. **Using the wrong r formula.** Some students write $r = \alpha\Delta x/\Delta t^{2}$ or $r = \alpha\Delta t^{2}/\Delta x$. Always verify units: r is **dimensionless** — **−1 mark**. > 4. **Ignoring the unconditional stability claim.** A common error is to claim CN requires $r \leq 1$ (that's the Fully Implicit — Backward Euler scheme). **−1 mark** in conceptual questions. > 5. **Skipping the half-weighting substitution.** Writing the equation with $\beta$ left symbolic instead of substituting $\beta = 0.5$ is acceptable in derivation, but in numerical questions, the answer must use **0.5** — **−1 mark** in numerical parts. > 6. **Not checking dimensional consistency / physical bounds** in numerical answers. If $T_1^{1}$ comes out as 350 °C when boundary is 200 °C, the student should detect a sign error — but KTU evaluators typically do not award partial credit for "wrong but unverified" answers. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - ✅ **CN Scheme** = **Trapezoidal rule in time** + **FTCS central difference in space** for parabolic PDEs. - ✅ The **weighting factor** is **β = 0.5** (equal weight to current and future time levels). - ✅ The discrete CN equation is: $-r\,T_{i-1}^{n+1} + 2(1+r)\,T_i^{n+1} - r\,T_{i+1}^{n+1} = r\,T_{i-1}^{n} + 2(1-r)\,T_i^{n} + r\,T_{i+1}^{n}$ - ✅ **Fourier number:** $r = \alpha\Delta t/\Delta x^{2}$ — dimensionless. - ✅ **Stability:** **Unconditionally stable** for all $r > 0$ (no constraint on Δt). - ✅ **Accuracy:** $\mathcal{O}(\Delta t^{2}, \Delta x^{2})$ — second-order in **both** space and time. - ✅ **Amplification factor:** $G = \dfrac{1 - 2r\sin^{2}(\theta)}{1 + 2r\sin^{2}(\theta)}$, with $\vert G \vert \leq 1$ for all real θ. - ✅ **Solver:** Tridiagonal system → **Thomas Algorithm (TDMA)** — **A · Tⁿ⁺¹ = d**, with $a = c = -r$, $b = 2(1+r)$. - ✅ **Comparison with FTCS:** CN removes the $r \leq 0.5$ restriction but requires a linear-system solve at every step. - ✅ **Comparison with Fully Implicit (β = 1):** CN is **more accurate** (2nd-order in time vs. 1st-order); both are unconditionally stable. - ✅ CN can be viewed as a **Richardson Extrapolation** of the FTCS and Fully Implicit schemes. - ✅ CN **conserves energy** (discrete $L^{2}$ norm) exactly for linear problems. - ✅ Engineering applications: heat exchangers, electronic chip cooling, casting/solidification, oil-reservoir simulation, reactor safety, glass annealing. - ✅ **Boundary conditions:** Dirichlet values are inserted as known entries in the RHS / matrix at $i = 0$ and $i = N$. - ✅ **Initial condition:** $T_i^{0}$ must be specified for all interior nodes before the first time step. - ✅ **Computational cost per step:** higher than FTCS (one TDMA solve), but **fewer steps** for the same accuracy target → often overall **faster**.

topics.title: Diffusion Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Diffusion Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diffusion Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition):**
> A *diffusion problem* in CFD is the transport of a scalar quantity (such as temperature, chemical species concentration, or momentum) caused purely by **molecular/grad-driven transport**, governed by the general steady-state diffusion equation:
> $$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\Gamma \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\Gamma \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left(\Gamma \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial z}\right) + S_{\phi} = 0$$
> where $\phi$ is the transported scalar (e.g., temperature $T$), $\Gamma$ is the **diffusion coefficient** (e.g., thermal conductivity $k$), and $S_{\phi}$ is the **source/sink term** per unit volume.

The diffusion problem is the **simplest and most fundamental** of all transport phenomena solved in CFD, yet it forms the numerical backbone of every Navier–Stokes solver because convection-diffusion always reduces to pure diffusion in the limit of zero flow.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a cup of hot tea placed on a steel spoon. The heat does not "flow" from handle to tip — it *spreads* from hot regions to cold regions through random molecular collisions. This natural spreading is **diffusion**. In CFD, we mathematically mimic this spreading using partial differential equations, divide the physical region into tiny **control volumes (CVs)**, and apply the principle that **net diffusive flux in + sources = 0** at steady state.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Always remember — the diffusion equation is a **second-order elliptic PDE** (steady) or **parabolic PDE** (transient). The Finite Volume Method (FVM) is the canonical KTU-prescribed discretization route.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $\Gamma$ | Diffusion coefficient | $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ (species) or $\text{W/(m·K)}$ (heat) |
| $\phi$ | Transported scalar | $\text{K}$, $\text{kg/m}^3$, $\text{m/s}$ |
| $\delta x$ | Grid spacing | $\text{m}$ |
| $D_e$ | Exchange / interface diffusion conductance | $\text{kg/(m·s)}$ |
| $F$ | Mass flow rate (convective term) | $\text{kg/s}$ |
| $P$ | Peclet number $\rho u \delta x / \Gamma$ | dimensionless |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structured Logical Steps — How FVM Solves a Diffusion Problem

1. **Discretize the domain** into a finite number of non-overlapping control volumes (CVs) around each nodal point $P$.
2. **Integrate the governing PDE** over each CV and apply Gauss's divergence theorem to convert volume integrals into surface integrals (flux form).
3. **Approximate the surface integrals** using mid-point rule: surface flux $\approx$ (face area) × (face value of gradient).
4. **Express face gradients** using linear interpolation between neighboring nodes (Central Differencing Scheme, CDS).
5. **Assemble linear algebraic equations** of the form $a_P \phi_P = a_W \phi_W + a_E \phi_E + b$ for each node.
6. **Apply boundary conditions** (Dirichlet, Neumann, or Robin) to close the system.
7. **Solve the resulting sparse linear system** using direct methods (TDMA, Gauss elimination) or iterative methods (Jacobi, Gauss–Seidel, SOR).

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — these equations appear in nearly every KTU Module 3 question.**

| # | Formula / Expression | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\int_{CV} \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi)\, dV = \int_{A} \Gamma \nabla \phi \cdot d\vec{A}$ | Gauss divergence theorem applied to diffusion term |
| 2 | $\left(\Gamma \dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right)_e \approx \Gamma_e \dfrac{\phi_E - \phi_P}{\delta x_{PE}}$ | Central differencing of gradient at east face |
