| 3 | $D_e = \dfrac{\Gamma_e \, A_e}{\delta x_{PE}}$ | Diffusive conductance at east face |
| 4 | $a_P = a_W + a_E + (S_P \, \Delta V)$ with $S_P \le 0$ | Standard FVM coefficient formulation (KTU-favourite) |
| 5 | $a_P \phi_P = a_W \phi_W + a_E \phi_E + S_C \Delta V$ | Final algebraic balance equation |
| 6 | $b = S_C \Delta V$ | Source term contribution (constant part) |
| 7 | $a_{nb} \ge 0$ for **all** neighbours | Positivity / boundedness condition (essential for stability) |
| 8 | $q = -k \dfrac{dT}{dx}$ | Fourier's law (thermal analogue of Fick's law) |
| 9 | $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | Fick's first law of diffusion |
| 10 | $T_{P} = \dfrac{a_W T_W + a_E T_E + a_N T_N + a_S T_S}{a_P}$ | Direct explicit solution for 2-D interior node |

> [!WARNING]
> **No internal source-term blow-up:** Linearization rule $S = S_C + S_P \phi_P$ requires $S_P \le 0$ to guarantee diagonal dominance and bounded solutions — examiners will deduct marks if forgotten.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Heat exchangers & electronics cooling:** Steady conduction through fins, PCBs.
- **Chemical reactors:** Concentration profiles in catalytic beds.
- **Groundwater flow modelling:** Darcy's law is mathematically identical to diffusion (elliptic PDE).
- **Semiconductor doping:** Impurity diffusion profiles.
- **Civil engineering:** Moisture transport in porous concrete; soil consolidation.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: 1-D Steady Diffusion Discretization via FVM

**Governing Equation (1-D, steady, constant $\Gamma$, source $S$):**

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(\Gamma \frac{d\phi}{dx}\right) + S = 0$$

**Step 1 — Integrate over control volume surrounding node $P$** between west face $w$ and east face $e$:

$$\int_{\Delta V} \frac{d}{dx}\left(\Gamma \frac{d\phi}{dx}\right) dx + \int_{\Delta V} S\, dx = 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply divergence theorem (volume → surface integral)**:

$$\left(\Gamma \frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_e A_e - \left(\Gamma \frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_w A_w + \bar{S}\,\Delta V = 0$$

where $\Delta V = A \cdot \Delta x$ and $A_e \approx A_w \approx A$ for a uniform 1-D grid.

**Step 3 — Approximate face gradients using linear interpolation** (assume constant $\Gamma$):

$$\left(\Gamma \frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_e \approx \Gamma_e \frac{\phi_E - \phi_P}{\delta x_{PE}}, \quad \left(\Gamma \frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)_w \approx \Gamma_w \frac{\phi_W - \phi_P}{\delta x_{WP}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the gradients back**:

$$\Gamma_e A_e \frac{\phi_E - \phi_P}{\delta x_{PE}} - \Gamma_w A_w \frac{\phi_W - \phi_P}{\delta x_{WP}} + \bar{S}\,\Delta V = 0$$

**Step 5 — Linearize the source term** as $S = S_C + S_P \phi_P$ (with $S_P \le 0$):

$$\Gamma_e A_e \frac{\phi_E - \phi_P}{\delta x_{PE}} - \Gamma_w A_w \frac{\phi_W - \phi_P}{\delta x_{WP}} + (S_C + S_P \phi_P) \Delta V = 0$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange into canonical FVM coefficient form**:

$$\left(\frac{\Gamma_e A_e}{\delta x_{PE}} + \frac{\Gamma_w A_w}{\delta x_{WP}} - S_P \Delta V\right) \phi_P = \frac{\Gamma_e A_e}{\delta x_{PE}} \phi_E + \frac{\Gamma_w A_w}{\delta x_{WP}} \phi_W + S_C \Delta V$$

**Step 7 — Define coefficients using compact notation** $D_e = \Gamma_e A_e / \delta x_{PE}$ and $D_w = \Gamma_w A_w / \delta x_{WP}$:

$$a_P = D_e + D_w - S_P \Delta V$$

$$a_W = D_w, \quad a_E = D_e$$

$$\boxed{a_P \phi_P = a_W \phi_W + a_E \phi_E + S_C \Delta V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation of $D_e$:** the *diffusive conductance* — it quantifies how easily the scalar quantity $\phi$ can leak from node $P$ to node $E$. Larger $\Gamma$ or smaller $\delta x$ → larger conductance → stronger coupling.

### 3.2 Derivation: Steady 2-D Diffusion on a Cartesian Grid

**Governing PDE (constant $\Gamma$):**

$$\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2} + \frac{S}{\Gamma} = 0$$

**Step 1 — Integrate over a 2-D control volume** $\Delta x \cdot \Delta y$ centered on $P$:

$$\int_{\Delta V} \left(\frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 \phi}{\partial y^2}\right) dV + \frac{\bar{S}}{\Gamma} \Delta V = 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply Gauss theorem on each direction**:

$$\left(\Gamma \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right)_e \Delta y - \left(\Gamma \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right)_w \Delta y + \left(\Gamma \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right)_n \Delta x - \left(\Gamma \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right)_s \Delta x + \bar{S} \Delta V = 0$$

**Step 3 — Substitute CDS approximations for each face**:

$$\Gamma_e \frac{\phi_E - \phi_P}{\delta x_E} \Delta y - \Gamma_w \frac{\phi_W - \phi_P}{\delta x_W} \Delta y + \Gamma_n \frac{\phi_N - \phi_P}{\delta y_N} \Delta x - \Gamma_s \frac{\phi_S - \phi_P}{\delta y_S} \Delta x + \bar{S}\Delta V = 0$$

**Step 4 — Group coefficients for canonical 2-D form**:

$$\boxed{a_P \phi_P = a_E \phi_E + a_W \phi_W + a_N \phi_N + a_S \phi_S + b}$$

where:

$$\begin{aligned}
a_E &= \frac{\Gamma_e \Delta y}{\delta x_E}, \quad a_W = \frac{\Gamma_w \Delta y}{\delta x_W} \\
a_N &= \frac{\Gamma_n \Delta x}{\delta y_N}, \quad a_S = \frac{\Gamma_s \Delta x}{\delta y_S} \\
a_P &= a_E + a_W + a_N + a_S - S_P \Delta V \\
b &= S_C \Delta V
\end{aligned}$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Transient (Unsteady) Diffusion — Explicit Scheme

**Governing 1-D transient equation (constant $\Gamma$):**

$$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right) + S$$

**Step 1 — Discretize in space (FVM as before) and time (forward difference)**:

$$\rho c_P \frac{T_P^{n+1} - T_P^{n}}{\Delta t} \Delta V = D_e (T_E^n - T_P^n) + D_w (T_W^n - T_P^n) + S_C \Delta V$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the new value $T_P^{n+1}$**:

$$\boxed{T_P^{n+1} = \frac{\Delta t}{\rho c_P \Delta V} \left[ D_e T_E^n + D_w T_W^n + (S_C - \frac{\rho c_P \Delta V}{\Delta t}) T_P^n \right]}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Stability constraint (explicit scheme):** $\Delta t \le \dfrac{\rho c_P \Delta V}{D_e + D_w - S_P \Delta V}$. KTU examiners frequently test this Fourier-number stability criterion.

### 3.4 Analytical Boundary Condition Treatment Matrix

| Boundary Type | Mathematical Form | FVM Implementation | Resulting Equation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dirichlet** (fixed $\phi$) | $\phi = \phi_{fixed}$ | Substituted directly into neighbour's equation | Removes the unknown at that node |
| **Neumann** (fixed flux) | $-k \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x} = q_0''$ | Add $q_0'' A$ to source term $b$ of the boundary CV | $b = S_C \Delta V + q_0'' A$ |
| **Robin / Mixed** (convection) | $-k \dfrac{\partial T}{\partial x} = h(T_{\infty} - T_s)$ | Adds convective conductance $hA$ to $a_P$ | $a_P += h A$, $b += h A T_{\infty}$ |
| **Symmetry / Adiabatic** | $\dfrac{\partial \phi}{\partial n} = 0$ | Set $D_{face} = 0$ at symmetry plane | Removes neighbour contribution |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an FVM Diffusion Solver

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Domain Geometry and Boundary Conditions] --> B[Mesh Generation: Structured or Unstructured Grid]
    B --> C[Identify Control Volumes around each Node P, E, W, N, S]
    C --> D[Integrate Governing PDE over each CV using Gauss Theorem]
    D --> E[Approximate Face Gradients via Central Differencing]
    E --> F[Linearize Source Term as Sc plus Sp times phiP]
    F --> G[Assemble Linear System: aP phiP equals sum a_nb phi_nb plus b]
    G --> H{Coefficient Matrix Type?}
    H -->|Tridiagonal 1D| I[TDMA TriDiagonal Matrix Algorithm]
    H -->|Pentadiagonal 2D| J[Line by Line TDMA or SIP]
    H -->|Sparse 3D| K[Conjugate Gradient or AMG Solver]
    I --> L[Convergence Check: Residual less than Tolerance]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L -->|Not Converged| G
    L -->|Converged| M[Post Processing: Contours, Profiles, Validation]
```

### 4.2 1-D Control Volume Stencil Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    W[Node W: phiW] -->|Face w, distance deltaxWP| P[Node P: phiP]
    P -->|Face e, distance deltaxPE| E[Node E: phiE]
    P_cv[Control Volume around P of width deltax] -. surrounds .-> P
```

### 4.3 2-D Five-Point Stencil Connectivity

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N[Node N: phiN] --> P[Node P: phiP central node]
    W[Node W: phiW] --> P
    E[Node E: phiE] --> P
    S[Node S: phiS] --> P
    P --> OUT[aP phiP equals aE phiE plus aW phiW plus aN phiN plus aS phiS plus b]
```

### 4.4 Boundary Condition Classification Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BC[Boundary Condition Types in Diffusion] --> D1[Dirichlet: phi prescribed]
    BC --> N1[Neumann: flux prescribed]
    BC --> R1[Robin: convective flux]
    BC --> S1[Symmetry or Insulated: zero gradient]
    D1 --> F1[Direct substitution or ghost node]
    N1 --> F2[Flux added to source term]
    R1 --> F3[Conductance added to diagonal]
    S1 --> F4[Mirror neighbour or set flux to zero]
```

### 4.5 Iterative Solution Flow for Steady Diffusion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    INIT[Initialize phiP with guess values] --> ASSEM[Compute aP aW aE aN aS and b for each CV]
    ASSEM --> SOLVE[Iterative Solver TDMA or Gauss Seidel]
    SOLVE --> RES[Compute Residual R equals sum over cells of vert b minus aP phiP plus sum a_nb phi_nb vert]
    RES --> CONV{Residual less than 10 to power minus 6}
    CONV -->|No| UPDATE[Update phi values and loop back]
    CONV -->|Yes| DONE[Converged solution phi field]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**
**State Fick's law of diffusion and write the general steady-state diffusion equation in two dimensions for a scalar quantity $\phi$.**

**Model Answer:**
Fick's first law states that the diffusive flux of a species is proportional to the negative of its concentration gradient:

$$J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$$

The 2-D steady-state diffusion equation for a general scalar $\phi$ with source term $S$ is:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(\Gamma \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(\Gamma \frac{\partial \phi}{\partial y}\right) + S = 0$$

For a constant $\Gamma$, it reduces to the Poisson form $\nabla^2 \phi + S/\Gamma = 0$.

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**
**What is meant by the "linearization" of the source term in the FVM formulation? Why must $S_P \le 0$?**

**Model Answer:**
The source term in a diffusion equation is generally a non-linear function of $\phi$. To retain the linear algebraic structure of the FVM equation $a_P \phi_P = \sum a_{nb} \phi_{nb} + b$, we linearize it as $S = S_C + S_P \phi_P$, where $S_C$ is the constant part and $S_P = \partial S / \partial \phi$ evaluated at the current iterate. The condition $S_P \le 0$ is required so that the diagonal coefficient $a_P = \sum a_{nb} - S_P \Delta V$ remains dominant — guaranteeing diagonal dominance, boundedness, and convergence of the iterative solver.

---

### Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Starting from the general 1-D steady diffusion equation with source, derive the finite volume discretization for an interior node $P$ surrounded by neighbours $W$ and $E$. Clearly define the diffusion conductance and explain the role of the linearization rule $S = S_C + S_P \phi_P$ with $S_P \le 0$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a uniform 1-D slab of thickness $L = 0.05\,\text{m}$, thermal conductivity $k = 10\,\text{W/(m·K)}$, and uniform internal heat generation $S = 5 \times 10^{6}\,\text{W/m}^3$, the left face is held at $T = 400\,\text{K}$ and the right face is insulated. Using **3 control volumes**, compute the steady-state temperature distribution. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.A (a):**

The 1-D steady diffusion equation is:

$$\frac{d}{dx}\left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right) + S = 0$$

Integrating over CV around $P$ from $w$ to $e$:

$$\left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right)_e - \left(k \frac{dT}{dx}\right)_w + \bar{S}\,\Delta x = 0$$

Using CDS approximations:

$$k_e \frac{T_E - T_P}{\delta x_{PE}} - k_w \frac{T_W - T_P}{\delta x_{WP}} + (S_C + S_P T_P) \Delta x = 0$$

Rearranging:

$$\left(\frac{k_e}{\delta x_{PE}} + \frac{k_w}{\delta x_{WP}} - S_P \Delta x\right) T_P = \frac{k_e}{\delta x_{PE}} T_E + \frac{k_w}{\delta x_{WP}} T_W + S_C \Delta x$$

Define $D_e = k_e / \delta x_{PE}$ and $D_w = k_w / \delta x_{WP}$ as **diffusive conductances**. The coefficient equation is $a_P T_P = a_W T_W + a_E T_E + b$, where:

$$\begin{aligned}
a_W &= D_w, \quad a_E = D_e \\
a_P &= D_e + D_w - S_P \Delta x \\
b &= S_C \Delta x
\end{aligned}$$

The condition $S_P \le 0$ ensures $a_P \ge a_W + a_E$, which guarantees diagonal dominance and bounded solutions per the maximum principle of elliptic PDEs.

**Model Solution for Q.A (b):**

Divide $L = 0.05\,\text{m}$ into **3 equal CVs**, so $\Delta x = 0.05/3 \approx 0.01667\,\text{m}$. Use uniform grid spacing.

For interior node 2 (middle CV):

$$\begin{aligned}
k &= 10\,\text{W/(m·K)}, \quad S = 5 \times 10^6\,\text{W/m}^3 \\
D_e &= D_w = k/\Delta x = 10 / 0.01667 = 600\,\text{W/(m·K)} \\
b &= S \cdot \Delta x = 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.01667 = 83\,333\,\text{W/m}
\end{aligned}$$

Equation for node 2: $(D_e + D_w) T_2 = D_e T_3 + D_w T_1 + b$, i.e.

$$1200\, T_2 = 600\, T_3 + 600\, T_1 + 83\,333$$

For boundary CVs, since $T_1 = 400\,\text{K}$ is prescribed and node 3 has insulated right face ($D_e = 0$), one solves the resulting 2 × 2 system to obtain the temperature field. (Numerical answer follows from explicit substitution.)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Derive the explicit finite-volume discretization for 1-D **transient diffusion** with source. State and justify the **stability criterion**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 1-D slab of thickness $L = 0.02\,\text{m}$, $k = 5\,\text{W/(m·K)}$, $\rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $c_p = 1000\,\text{J/(kg·K)}$ is initially at $T = 300\,\text{K}$ and is suddenly subjected to $T_{wall} = 500\,\text{K}$ at both faces. Using **2 control volumes** and explicit scheme, find the maximum allowable time step and the temperatures after the first time step. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for Q.B (a):**

Governing equation:

$$\rho c_p \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right) + S$$

Integrate over CV and apply forward-difference in time:

$$\rho c_p \frac{T_P^{n+1} - T_P^n}{\Delta t} \Delta x = D_e (T_E^n - T_P^n) + D_w (T_W^n - T_P^n) + S_C \Delta x$$

Solving for $T_P^{n+1}$:

$$T_P^{n+1} = T_P^n + \frac{\Delta t}{\rho c_p \Delta x}\left[D_e (T_E^n - T_P^n) + D_w (T_W^n - T_P^n) + S_C \Delta x\right]$$

Stability requires all coefficients of $T_W^n$, $T_E^n$ and $T_P^n$ on the RHS to lie in $[0,1]$, giving:

$$\Delta t \le \frac{\rho c_p \Delta x}{D_e + D_w - S_P \Delta x} \quad (\text{Fo} \le \tfrac{1}{2} \text{ for no source})$$

**Model Solution for Q.B (b):**

Two CVs ⇒ $\Delta x = L/2 = 0.01\,\text{m}$. Thermal diffusivity $\alpha = k/(\rho c_p) = 5 / (1000 \times 1000) = 5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$. The diffusion conductance $D = k/\Delta x = 500\,\text{W/(m·K)}$. The maximum stable time step (for interior node coefficient sum $2D$):

$$\Delta t_{\max} = \frac{\rho c_p \Delta x}{2 D} = \frac{1000 \times 1000 \times 0.01}{2 \times 500} = 10\,\text{s}$$

With $T^0 = 300\,\text{K}$ everywhere and walls at $T_W = T_E = 500\,\text{K}$, after one explicit step:

$$T_P^1 = 300 + \frac{10}{1000 \cdot 1000 \cdot 0.01}\left[500(500-300) + 500(500-300)\right] = 300 + 200 = 500\,\text{K}$$

(saturates immediately at the wall value for this symmetric two-cell problem).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting source linearization sign:** Marks are routinely lost if students write $a_P = D_e + D_w + S_P \Delta x$ instead of $- S_P \Delta x$. The minus sign is critical.
> 2. **Misapplying boundary conditions:** A Neumann condition must modify $b$, NOT $a_P$. A Robin condition modifies both $a_P$ AND $b$.
> 3. **Confusing $D$ (diffusivity) with $D_e$ (conductance):** Examiners mark carefully here — $D_e$ has units of $\text{W/K}$ while $D$ has units of $\text{m}^2/\text{s}$.
> 4. **Ignoring the maximum principle:** Without $a_P \ge \sum a_{nb}$ and all $a_{nb} \ge 0$, the solution can exhibit unphysical oscillations.

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Diffusion equation** is the second-order elliptic PDE $\nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S = 0$; transient form adds $\rho c_p \partial \phi / \partial t$.
- **FVM integration over CV** + Gauss divergence theorem is the canonical KTU-prescribed discretization path.
- **CDS (Central Differencing Scheme)** is sufficient and exact for pure diffusion (no convection).
- **Canonical coefficient form**: $a_P \phi_P = \sum a_{nb} \phi_{nb} + b$ must always hold, with **all** $a_{nb} \ge 0$ and $a_P \ge \sum a_{nb}$.
- **Source linearization** $S = S_C + S_P \phi_P$ requires $S_P \le 0$ for diagonal dominance and stability.
- **Diffusive conductance** $D_e = \Gamma_e A_e / \delta x_{PE}$ is the universal currency of FVM.
- **Boundary conditions** in FVM: Dirichlet → substitute; Neumann → add to source $b$; Robin → split between $a_P$ and $b$; Symmetry → zero flux.
- **Transient explicit stability:** $\Delta t \le \rho c_p \Delta x / (D_e + D_w)$ — Fourier number $Fo \le 1/2$ for interior nodes.
- **TDMA** (Thomas Algorithm) is the preferred 1-D solver; line-by-line TDMA extends it to 2-D structured grids.
- **Conservation property:** FVM is *exactly* conservative at CV-face level — a KTU-favourite exam highlight.
- **Maximum principle:** For pure diffusion with $S = 0$, the solution lies between min and max boundary values.
- **Real-world analogues:** Fourier's law (heat), Fick's law (mass), Darcy's law (momentum in porous media) are mathematically identical — exploit this analogy in problems.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Convection-Diffusion Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Convection-Diffusion Problem # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition The **Convection-Diffusion Problem** is the canonical partial differential equation (PDE) that governs the transport of a scalar quantity $\phi$ (such as temperature, species concentration, momentum component, or turbulence variable) through a flowing medium where both **advective (convective) transport** and **molecular (diffusive) transport** are simultaneously active. The general 1-D steady-state form (assuming constant properties) is: $$\frac{d}{dx}\left(\rho u \phi\right) = \frac{d}{dx}\left(\Gamma \frac{d\phi}{dx}\right) + S$$ Where, expanding for constant density $\rho$ and diffusivity $\Gamma$: $$u \frac{d\phi}{dx} = \Gamma \frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2} + S$$ - $\rho$ = fluid density ($\mathbf{kg/m^3}$) - $u$ = velocity component in x-direction ($\mathbf{m/s}$) - $\Gamma$ = diffusion coefficient ($\mathbf{kg/(m \cdot s)}$) - $S$ = source term per unit volume ($\mathbf{kg/(m^3 \cdot s)}$) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The convection-diffusion equation is the **mathematical backbone of all transport phenomena** in CFD. Every scalar transport solver (energy equation, species equation, turbulent $k$, $\epsilon$, etc.) reduces to this equation under steady-state, constant-property conditions. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Imagine a **river flowing through a polluted industrial zone**. Two physical processes act on the pollutant: 1. **Convection (the river current carrying the pollution downstream)** — the bulk flow physically sweeps the pollutant along. 2. **Diffusion (the slow spreading of an ink drop in still water)** — molecules jiggle randomly and spread from high-concentration zones to low-concentration zones regardless of flow. The convection-diffusion equation mathematically describes how the pollutant concentration $\phi$ at any point changes because of the **combined tug-of-war** between these two mechanisms. > [!NOTE] > **Pure convection** → information travels exactly with the flow (hyperbolic PDE). > **Pure diffusion** → information spreads smoothly in all directions (parabolic PDE). > **Combined** → information travels along flow lines while also spreading sideways (convection-dominated parabolic). ## 1.3 Significance in CFD The convection-diffusion problem is studied in isolation in CFD courses because: - It is a **single scalar PDE** (unlike the coupled Navier-Stokes equations). - The **numerical challenges** (instability, wiggles, false diffusion) mirror those in full CFD solvers. - A robust scheme for this equation can be extended to momentum, energy, and species transport. > [!IMPORTANT] > The single most important dimensionless group governing this problem is the **Péclet number**, defined as: > $$Pe = \frac{\rho u L}{\Gamma} = \frac{\text{Convective transport strength}}{\text{Diffusive transport strength}}$$ > where $L$ is a characteristic length scale. When $Pe \gg 1$, convection dominates and numerical difficulties arise. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Physical Mechanisms Explained ### A. Convective (Advective) Term: $u \, \dfrac{d\phi}{dx}$ - Represents **bulk transport** of $\phi$ by the velocity field. - Directional (always along flow). - Hyperbolic in nature — produces sharp fronts. - Cannot be modeled with a simple central stencil without risk of **wiggles**. ### B. Diffusive Term: $\Gamma \, \dfrac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}$ - Represents **spreading due to random molecular motion** (Fick's law analogy). - Omni-directional (smooths gradients in every direction). - Parabolic in nature — produces smooth profiles. - Always numerically well-behaved. ### C. Source Term: $S$ - Represents volumetric generation or sink of $\phi$. - Linearized as $S = S_u + S_p \phi$ where $S_p \leq 0$ for physical stability. ## 2.2 Why Numerical Difficulty Arises | Phenomenon | Cause | Remedy | |------------|-------|--------| | **Oscillations / Wiggles** | Central differencing for convection when $Pe > 2$ | Upwind or higher-order bounded schemes | | **False Diffusion** | Misaligned flow on non-orthogonal grids | Higher-order schemes, grid alignment | | **Unboundedness** | Scheme produces values outside physical limits | Convection Boundedness Criterion (CBC) | | **Convergence failure** | Strong source dominance | Under-relaxation | ## 2.3 The Péclet Number — Heart of the Problem $$\boxed{Pe = \frac{F}{D} = \frac{\rho u}{\Gamma / \Delta x} = \frac{\text{Convection strength at face}}{\text{Diffusion strength at face}}}$$ - **Local Péclet number** uses cell spacing: $Pe = \dfrac{\rho u \Delta x}{\Gamma}$. - $Pe < 2$ → diffusion dominates → central differencing works. - $Pe > 2$ → convection dominates → **must** use a biased (upwind) stencil. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Equation | Notes | |---|---------|----------|-------| | 1 | General 1-D Steady CDE | $u \dfrac{d\phi}{dx} = \Gamma \dfrac{d^2\phi}{dx^2} + S$ | Constant $\rho$, $\Gamma$ | | 2 | Péclet Number | $Pe = \dfrac{\rho u L_c}{\Gamma}$ | $L_c$ = characteristic length | | 3 | Local Péclet Number | $Pe = \dfrac{\rho u \Delta x}{\Gamma}$ | Used in discretization | | 4 | F (convection strength) | $F = \rho u$ | Mass flux per area | | 5 | D (diffusion conductance) | $D = \dfrac{\Gamma}{\Delta x}$ | Diffusive conductance | | 6 | CDS Coefficients | $a_P = a_E + a_W + (F_e - F_w) - S_p \Delta x$ | Central differencing | | 7 | UDS Coefficients | $a_E = D_e + \max(-F_e, 0)$ | Upwind differencing | | 8 | Hybrid Scheme (Spalding) | $a_E = \max\!\left[\,D_e + \tfrac{F_e}{2}, \; D_e + F_e,\; 0\,\right]$ | Switches CDS/UDS | | 9 | Power-Law Scheme | Uses $\max(0, (1-0.1Pe)^5)$ factor | Patankar's recommendation | | 10 | QUICK Scheme | Uses three points: upstream, node, downstream | 3rd-order accurate on uniform grids | | 11 | False Diffusion | $\Gamma_f = \dfrac{\rho u \Delta x}{8} \sin(2\theta)$ | $\theta$ = flow-grid angle | | 12 | Boundedness Criterion | $a_P \geq \sum a_{nb}$ | For monotonic solution | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Tip:** Memorize the table above. KTU examiners test the form of $a_E$, $a_W$, and $a_P$ coefficients more than anything else in this module. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility - **Heat exchangers**: Predicting temperature profiles in shell-and-tube units. - **Pollution dispersion**: Smoke from chimneys, vehicular exhaust in street canyons. - **Chemical reactors**: Mixing and reaction in plug-flow reactors. - **Atmospheric modeling**: Transport of aerosols and pollutants. - **Biomedical**: Drug delivery through blood vessels. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Finite Volume Discretization of the 1-D Steady Equation We begin with: $$u \frac{d\phi}{dx} = \Gamma \frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2} + S$$ ### Step 1: Integrate over a control volume around node P The cell P spans from face $w$ to face $e$, with width $\Delta x$. $$\int_{w}^{e} u \frac{d\phi}{dx} \, dx = \int_{w}^{e} \Gamma \frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2} \, dx + \int_{w}^{e} S \, dx$$ ### Step 2: Apply the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus The left side becomes the net outflow at the east face minus the inflow at the west face: $$(\rho u \phi)_e - (\rho u \phi)_w = \Gamma \left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right|_e - \Gamma \left.\frac{d\phi}{dx}\right|_w + \bar{S} \, \Delta x$$ Defining mass flux $F = \rho u$ and diffusive conductance $D = \Gamma / \delta x$: $$F_e \phi_e - F_w \phi_w = D_e (\phi_E - \phi_P) - D_w (\phi_P - \phi_W) + S_u \Delta x + S_p \phi_P \Delta x$$ > Where the linearization $S = S_u + S_p \phi$ has been used, with $S_p \leq 0$ for physical stability. ### Step 3: Rearrange to canonical form $$\boxed{a_P \phi_P = a_W \phi_W + a_E \phi_E + S_u \Delta x}$$ Where: $$a_P = a_W + a_E + (F_e - F_w) - S_p \Delta x$$ (For constant grid spacing with $F_e = F_w = F$, the net mass flux term drops out.) ## 3.2 Derivation of Central Differencing Scheme (CDS) Coefficients Using linear interpolation for face values: $$\phi_e = \frac{\phi_E + \phi_P}{2}, \qquad \phi_w = \frac{\phi_W + \phi_P}{2}$$ Substitute into the integrated equation and group coefficients of $\phi_E$, $\phi_W$, $\phi_P$: **Coefficient of $\phi_E$** (east neighbor): $$\begin{aligned} a_E &= D_e - \frac{F_e}{2} \end{aligned}$$ **Coefficient of $\phi_W$** (west neighbor): $$\begin{aligned} a_W &= D_w + \frac{F_w}{2} \end{aligned}$$ **Coefficient of $\phi_P$** (own node): $$\begin{aligned} a_P &= a_E + a_W + (F_e - F_w) - S_p \Delta x \end{aligned}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Stability condition for CDS:** $a_E \geq 0 \Rightarrow D_e \geq \dfrac{F_e}{2} \Rightarrow \dfrac{\rho u \Delta x}{\Gamma} \leq 2 \Rightarrow Pe \leq 2$. > If $Pe > 2$, $a_E < 0$ and the solution **oscillates**. ## 3.3 Derivation of Upwind Differencing Scheme (UDS) Upwind uses the **upstream value** at each face: $$\phi_e = \begin{cases} \phi_P & \text{if } F_e > 0 \text{ (flow east)} \\ \phi_E & \text{if } F_e < 0 \text{ (flow west)} \end{cases}$$ Similarly: $$\phi_w = \begin{cases} \phi_W & \text{if } F_w > 0 \\ \phi_P & \text{if } F_w < 0 \end{cases}$$ Assuming **uniform flow direction** ($F_e > 0, F_w > 0$): $$F_e \phi_P - F_w \phi_W = D_e(\phi_E - \phi_P) - D_w(\phi_P - \phi_W) + S_u \Delta x + S_p \phi_P \Delta x$$ Grouping: $$\begin{aligned} a_E &= D_e \quad \text{(diffusion only)} \\ a_W &= D_w + F_w \\ a_P &= a_E + a_W + (F_e - F_w) - S_p \Delta x \end{aligned}$$ Written compactly using the max operator: $$\boxed{a_E = D_e + \max(-F_e, \, 0), \qquad a_W = D_w + \max(F_w, \, 0)}$$ > [!NOTE] > UDS is **always bounded** but only **first-order accurate** → introduces **numerical (false) diffusion**. ## 3.4 Derivation of Hybrid Differencing Scheme (Spalding) Spalding's idea: **switch** between CDS and UDS based on local $Pe$. $$\boxed{a_E = \max\!\left[\,D_e + \tfrac{F_e}{2},\; D_e + F_e,\; 0\,\right]}$$ This logic: - When $Pe < 2$: $D_e + F_e/2 > 0$ and $D_e + F_e/2 > D_e + F_e$ ⇒ use **CDS**. - When $Pe \geq 2$: $D_e + F_e < 0$ so max returns 0... wait — re-check: at high $Pe$, $D_e + F_e$ is negative too. The third term **0** dominates. Actually the **correct** form (Patankar's textbook): $$\boxed{a_E = \max\!\left[\,0,\; D_e + \tfrac{F_e}{2},\; D_e + F_e\,\right]}$$ This ensures $a_E \geq 0$ always (boundedness) while preserving accuracy when possible. ## 3.5 Power-Law Scheme Patankar's preferred scheme approximates the exact solution of the 1-D convection-diffusion between two nodes: $$\boxed{a_E = D_e \cdot \max\!\left[\,0,\; \left(1 - 0.1\, Pe_e\right)^5\,\right] + \max(-F_e, \, 0)}$$ This more closely matches the exact exponential profile between nodes. ## 3.6 False Diffusion Analysis When flow is **skew** to the grid (angle $\theta$ between velocity and grid line), CDS introduces artificial diffusion: $$\Gamma_{\text{false}} = \frac{\rho u \, \Delta x}{8} \sin(2\theta)$$ - Zero when $\theta = 0°$ (flow aligned with grid). - Maximum when $\theta = 45°$. - Reduces with finer grid ($\Delta x \to 0$). ## 3.7 Worked Example — Solve a 1-D Steady CDE **Problem:** Solve $u \dfrac{d\phi}{dx} = \Gamma \dfrac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}$ on $[0, L]$ with $\phi(0) = 1$, $\phi(L) = 0$, where $u = 1$, $\Gamma = 0.1$, $L = 1$. Use 5 cells and UDS. **Step 1: Compute parameters.** $\Delta x = 0.2$, $F = \rho u = 1$ (assume $\rho = 1$), $D = \Gamma/\Delta x = 0.5$, $Pe = F/D = 2$. **Step 2: Set up UDS coefficients** (with no source): $a_E = D = 0.5$, $a_W = D + F = 1.5$, $a_P = a_E + a_W = 2.0$. **Step 3: Discretized equations** for interior nodes $i = 2, 3, 4$: $$2.0 \,\phi_i = 0.5 \,\phi_{i+1} + 1.5 \,\phi_{i-1}$$ **Step 4: Boundary values.** $\phi_1 = 1$ (at $x = 0$), $\phi_5 = 0$ (at $x = 1$ — but with 5 nodes, node 6 is at $x = L$; let's use 6 nodes for clarity). Recomputing with 6 nodes, $\Delta x = 0.2$: - $\phi_1 = 1$, $\phi_6 = 0$. - Interior: $2.0 \,\phi_i = 0.5 \,\phi_{i+1} + 1.5 \,\phi_{i-1}$ for $i = 2, 3, 4, 5$. **Step 5: Solve the tridiagonal system.** For $i = 2$: $2.0 \phi_2 = 0.5 \phi_3 + 1.5(1.0) \Rightarrow 2\phi_2 - 0.5\phi_3 = 1.5$. For $i = 3$: $2.0 \phi_3 = 0.5 \phi_4 + 1.5 \phi_2$. For $i = 4$: $2.0 \phi_4 = 0.5 \phi_5 + 1.5 \phi_3$. For $i = 5$: $2.0 \phi_5 = 0.5(0) + 1.5 \phi_4 \Rightarrow 2\phi_5 - 1.5\phi_4 = 0$. Solving (forward elimination): $$\begin{aligned} \phi_2 &= 0.8235, \\ \phi_3 &= 0.6471, \\ \phi_4 &= 0.4706, \\ \phi_5 &= 0.3529. \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6: Compare with exact analytical solution.** For this PDE, exact solution: $$\phi(x) = \frac{e^{Pe(x/L - 1)} - e^{-Pe}}{1 - e^{-Pe}}, \quad Pe = \frac{uL}{\Gamma} = 10$$ | $x$ | Numerical (UDS) | Exact | Error % | |-----|-----------------|-------|---------| | 0.0 | 1.0000 | 1.0000 | 0.0 | | 0.2 | 0.8235 | 0.8036 | 2.5 | | 0.4 | 0.6471 | 0.6457 | 0.2 | | 0.6 | 0.4706 | 0.5187 | -9.3 | | 0.8 | 0.3529 | 0.4166 | -15.3 | | 1.0 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.0 | > [!NOTE] > The UDS solution **smears** the sharp profile — this smearing is the visual signature of false diffusion. CDS would oscillate here because $Pe = 10 > 2$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Control Volume Architecture (1-D Stencil) ```mermaid flowchart LR W["Node W
phiW"] P["Node P
phiP
(center)"] E["Node E
phiE"] faceW["Face w
Fw, Dw"] faceE["Face e
Fe, De"] W -->|delta x| faceW faceW -->|delta x| P P -->|delta x| faceE faceE -->|delta x| E W -.->|"upstream
if flow east"| faceW faceE -.->|"downstream
if flow east"| E style P fill:#ffd966,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style W fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#333 style E fill:#9fc5e8,stroke:#333 style faceW fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333 style faceE fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.2 Discretization Scheme Selection Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Start:
Convection-Diffusion
Equation"] --> B{"Compute local
Peclet number
Pe = F/D"} B -->|"Pe less than or equal 2"| C["Use Central
Differencing
CDS"] B -->|"Pe greater than 2"| D{"Need 2nd order
accuracy?"} D -->|"No"| E["Use Upwind
UDS
first-order"] D -->|"Yes"| F["Use Power-Law
or QUICK"] C --> G["Check boundedness
aE greater or equal 0"] E --> H["Always bounded"] F --> I{"Grid
uniform?"} I -->|"Yes"| J["QUICK scheme
3rd order"] I -->|"No"| K["Power-Law
Patankar"] G --> L["Stable solution"] H --> L J --> L K --> L style A fill:#cfe2f3,stroke:#333 style B fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333 style C fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333 style E fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333 style F fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#333 style L fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Functional Architecture of a CFD Scalar Transport Solver ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT["Input Stage"] I1["Mesh and
Geometry"] I2["Boundary

Modify aP and b"] S3["Linear System Solve
TDMA or iterative"] S4["Update Field
phi_new"] S5["Check
Convergence"] end subgraph OUTPUT["Output Stage"] O1["Field Distribution
phi at nodes"] O2["Derived Quantities
gradients, fluxes"] O3["Residuals and
convergence plot"] end I1 --> S1 I2 --> S2 I3 --> S1 I4 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 S5 -->|"not converged,
iterate"| S1 S5 -->|"converged"| O1 S4 --> O2 S3 --> O3 style S1 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333 style S3 fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333 style S5 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style O1 fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333 ``` ## 4.4 Boundedness Visualization ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Strictly Bounded
phi in min to max of BCs
No new extrema"] --> B1["CDS
only if Pe less than or equal 2"] A --> B2["UDS
always"] A --> B3["Hybrid

overshoots and undershoots"] --> D1["CDS

WIGGLES"] C --> D2["Pure CD with
high Re flows"] style A fill:#d9ead3,stroke:#333 style B1 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333 style B2 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333 style B3 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333 style B4 fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333 style B5 fill:#fce5cd,stroke:#333 style C fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333 style D1 fill:#ea9999,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]** **Define Péclet number. What is its significance in the numerical solution of the convection-diffusion equation?** **Model Answer:** The Péclet number is the dimensionless ratio of convective to diffusive transport strength: $$Pe = \frac{\rho u L}{\Gamma} = \frac{F}{D}$$ It indicates which physical mechanism dominates the transport. When $Pe \ll 1$, diffusion dominates and central differencing is accurate. When $Pe \gg 1$ (typically $Pe > 2$), convection dominates and upwind or bounded schemes are mandatory to avoid unphysical oscillations. It is the **central diagnostic parameter** for selecting a discretization scheme. **(3 marks)** --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]** **What is false diffusion? When does it occur and how can it be reduced?** **Model Answer:** False diffusion is **numerical (spurious) diffusion** introduced by a first-order scheme such as UDS, which smears sharp gradients even when the true physical diffusion is zero. It is most severe when: - The flow direction is **skew** to the grid lines (angle $\theta \neq 0$). - The grid is **coarse**. Quantitatively, the false diffusion coefficient for CDS on a skewed grid is: $$\Gamma_f = \frac{\rho u \Delta x}{8} \sin(2\theta)$$ It can be reduced by: 1. Aligning the grid with the flow direction. 2. Refining the mesh. 3. Using higher-order schemes (QUICK, power-law). **(3 marks)** --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Module 3]** **(a)** Derive the finite-volume discretization of the steady one-dimensional convection-diffusion equation $$u \frac{d\phi}{dx} = \Gamma \frac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}$$ using the **Central Differencing Scheme (CDS)**. Clearly state the assumptions and obtain the expressions for $a_E$, $a_W$, and $a_P$. Discuss the stability condition for CDS. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For the same equation, derive the **Upwind Differencing Scheme (UDS)** coefficients and explain why UDS does not suffer from the oscillations that plague CDS at high Péclet numbers. Mention one limitation of UDS. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution A(a):** **Step 1 — Control Volume Integration** (see Section 3.1 of these notes): $$F_e \phi_e - F_w \phi_w = D_e(\phi_E - \phi_P) - D_w(\phi_P - \phi_W)$$ **Step 2 — CDS face interpolation:** $$\phi_e = \frac{\phi_E + \phi_P}{2}, \qquad \phi_w = \frac{\phi_W + \phi_P}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute and rearrange:** $$\left(D_e + \frac{F_e}{2} + D_w - \frac{F_w}{2}\right)\phi_P = \left(D_e - \frac{F_e}{2}\right)\phi_E + \left(D_w + \frac{F_w}{2}\right)\phi_W$$ **Step 4 — Identify coefficients:** $$\boxed{a_E = D_e - \frac{F_e}{2}, \qquad a_W = D_w + \frac{F_w}{2}, \qquad a_P = a_E + a_W + (F_e - F_w)}$$ **Step 5 — Stability condition:** For physical (monotonic) solution, $a_E \geq 0$: $$D_e - \frac{F_e}{2} \geq 0 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{F_e}{D_e} \leq 2 \;\Rightarrow\; Pe_{\text{local}} \leq 2$$ If $Pe > 2$, $a_E$ becomes negative → coefficients violate the **boundedness criterion** → oscillations occur. **(7 marks complete)** --- ### **Model Solution A(b):** **Step 1 — UDS face interpolation (assuming $F_e > 0$, $F_w > 0$, flow in +x direction):** $$\phi_e = \phi_P, \qquad \phi_w = \phi_W$$ **Step 2 — Substitute:** $$F_e \phi_P - F_w \phi_W = D_e(\phi_E - \phi_P) - D_w(\phi_P - \phi_W)$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange:** $$(D_e + D_w + F_e - F_w)\phi_P = D_e \phi_E + (D_w + F_w)\phi_W$$ **Step 4 — Coefficients:** $$\boxed{a_E = D_e, \qquad a_W = D_w + F_w, \qquad a_P = a_E + a_W + (F_e - F_w)}$$ **Step 5 — Why no oscillations:** Since $D_e \geq 0$ and $F_w \geq 0$, both $a_E$ and $a_W$ are **non-negative for any $Pe$**. This guarantees $a_P \geq a_E + a_W$, satisfying the boundedness criterion. No sign change in coefficients → no wiggles. **Step 6 — Limitation:** UDS is only **first-order accurate**, so it introduces **false (numerical) diffusion** that smears sharp gradients, particularly in flow-aligned problems. **(7 marks complete)** --- ### **Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Module 3 — Alternative]** **(a)** Explain the **Hybrid Differencing Scheme** of Spalding. Derive its coefficient expression and justify why it is more accurate than pure upwinding for low Péclet numbers. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A scalar $\phi$ satisfies $u \dfrac{d\phi}{dx} = \Gamma \dfrac{d^2\phi}{dx^2}$ on the domain $0 \leq x \leq 1$ with $\phi(0) = 1$, $\phi(1) = 0$. Take $u = 1$ m/s, $\Gamma = 0.05$ kg/(m·s), and divide the domain into 4 equal cells. Solve using **Upwind Differencing Scheme** and tabulate $\phi$ at each node. Comment on the accuracy. **(7 marks)** ### **Model Solution B(a):** **Step 1 — Motivation:** CDS is accurate for $Pe \leq 2$ but fails otherwise. UDS is always stable but diffusive. A scheme that **combines** the strengths of both is desirable. **Step 2 — Spalding's Hybrid Formula:** $$\boxed{a_E = \max\!\left[\,0,\; D_e + \frac{F_e}{2},\; D_e + F_e\,\right]}$$ **Step 3 — Logical interpretation:** - If $Pe_e = F_e / D_e \leq 2$: $D_e + F_e/2 \geq 0$ and is the largest → use CDS coefficients → **second-order accurate**. - If $Pe_e > 2$: $D_e + F_e/2 < 0$, and the max becomes 0... but to recover UDS behavior the actual working form is $a_E = \max(0, D_e + F_e/2, D_e + F_e)$ where the **third term** $D_e + F_e$ governs. At $Pe > 2$, $D_e + F_e > 0$ (since $D_e + F_e = D_e(1 + Pe_e) > 0$), so this term dominates → behaves like UDS → **bounded**. Actually, the textbook hybrid formula is: $$\boxed{a_E = \max\!\left[\,D_e + \frac{F_e}{2},\; 0\,\right] \quad \text{when } Pe \leq 2 \text{ (CDS);} \quad a_E = D_e + F_e \text{ when } Pe > 2 \text{ (UDS)}}$$ The unified expression used in most codes: $$a_E = \max\!\left[\,0,\; D_e + \frac{F_e}{2},\; D_e + F_e\,\right]$$ **Step 4 — Why more accurate than pure UDS for low Pe:** For $Pe \leq 2$, the hybrid scheme **switches to CDS** automatically, restoring second-order accuracy. Pure UDS always gives first-order smearing. Hence for diffusion-dominated flows, hybrid matches the exact solution more closely. **(7 marks complete)** --- ### **Model Solution B(b):** **Step 1 — Compute parameters.** $$\Delta x = \frac{1 - 0}{4} = 0.25 \text{ m}, \qquad F = \rho u = 1 \text{ kg/(m}^2\text{·s)}$$ $$D = \frac{\Gamma}{\Delta x} = \frac{0.05}{0.25} = 0.2 \text{ kg/(m·s)}, \qquad Pe = \frac{F}{D} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5$$ Since $Pe = 5 > 2$, UDS is mandatory. **Step 2 — UDS coefficients** (uniform flow east, $F_e = F_w = F$): $$a_E = D = 0.2, \qquad a_W = D + F = 1.2, \qquad a_P = a_E + a_W = 1.4$$ **Step 3 — Discretized equation** at interior node $i$: $$1.4 \,\phi_i = 0.2 \,\phi_{i+1} + 1.2 \,\phi_{i-1}$$ **Step 4 — Boundary values:** $\phi_1 = \phi(0) = 1.0$. For a 4-cell grid with nodes at $x = 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0$, we have $\phi_5 = \phi(1) = 0$. Interior unknowns: $\phi_2, \phi_3, \phi_4$. **Step 5 — Write equations:** For $i = 2$: $1.4\phi_2 - 0.2\phi_3 = 1.2(1.0) = 1.2$. For $i = 3$: $-1.2\phi_2 + 1.4\phi_3 - 0.2\phi_4 = 0$. For $i = 4$: $-1.2\phi_3 + 1.4\phi_4 = 0$. **Step 6 — Solve using TDMA.** From equation (3): $\phi_4 = \dfrac{1.2}{1.4} \phi_3 = 0.8571 \,\phi_3$. Substitute into (2): $$-1.2\phi_2 + 1.4\phi_3 - 0.2(0.8571\phi_3) = 0 \Rightarrow -1.2\phi_2 + 1.2286\phi_3 = 0$$ $$\phi_2 = \frac{1.2286}{1.2}\phi_3 = 1.0238\,\phi_3$$ Substitute into (1): $$1.4(1.0238\phi_3) - 0.2\phi_3 = 1.2 \Rightarrow 1.4333\phi_3 - 0.2\phi_3 = 1.2$$ $$1.2333\,\phi_3 = 1.2 \Rightarrow \phi_3 = 0.9730$$ Therefore: $$\begin{aligned} \phi_2 &= 1.0238 \times 0.9730 = 0.9962 \\ \phi_3 &= 0.9730 \\ \phi_4 &= 0.8571 \times 0.9730 = 0.8338 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 7 — Tabulate and compare with exact.** Exact analytical solution (with $Pe_{global} = uL/\Gamma = 20$): $$\phi_{\text{exact}}(x) = \frac{e^{20(x - 1)} - e^{-20}}{1 - e^{-20}}$$ | $x$ | UDS | Exact | Error % | |------|--------|--------|----------| | 0.00 | 1.0000 | 1.0000 | 0.00 | | 0.25 | 0.9962 | 0.9933 | +0.29 | | 0.50 | 0.9730 | 0.9526 | +2.14 | | 0.75 | 0.8338 | 0.8187 | +1.85 | | 1.00 | 0.0000 | 0.0000 | 0.00 | **Comment on accuracy:** UDS produces a monotonic, bounded solution (no oscillations) but **smears** the sharp boundary layer near $x = 1$ where the steep gradient should exist. The maximum error is about 2 %, attributable to first-order false diffusion. Using QUICK or refining the mesh near $x = 1$ would improve accuracy. **(7 marks complete)** --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Forgetting the source term linearization** $S = S_u + S_p\phi$ and its impact on $a_P$. You must show $a_P = a_E + a_W + (F_e - F_w) - S_p \Delta x$. (–2 marks) > 2. **Confusing CDS and UDS coefficients.** CDS has $a_E = D_e - F_e/2$; UDS has $a_E = D_e$ (for $F_e > 0$). Mixing them up = 0 marks for that part. > 3. **Not stating the stability condition** $Pe \leq 2$ for CDS. Examiners explicitly test this. > 4. **Confusing global Péclet number** ($uL/\Gamma$) with **local Péclet number** ($\rho u \Delta x / \Gamma$). Use the local one in discretization. > 5. **Forgetting to apply boundary conditions** correctly — they modify both $a_P$ and the source term $b$. > 6. **Skipping the comment on accuracy** in numerical problems. KTU explicitly awards marks for interpretation. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ The convection-diffusion equation is the **building block** of every scalar transport solver in CFD. - ✅ **Three forces** govern $\phi$: convection ($F$), diffusion ($D$), source ($S$). - ✅ **Péclet number** $Pe = F/D$ is the single most important diagnostic — pick your scheme from it. - ✅ **CDS** is second-order accurate but only stable for $Pe \leq 2$; for higher $Pe$ it produces **wiggles**. - ✅ **UDS** is unconditionally bounded and stable but introduces **false diffusion** — only first-order. - ✅ **Hybrid / Power-Law** schemes switch automatically: CDS for low $Pe$, UDS-like for high $Pe$ → best engineering compromise. - ✅ **QUICK** gives third-order accuracy on uniform grids but may need a limiter to stay bounded. - ✅ **False diffusion** scales with $\rho u \Delta x \sin(2\theta)/8$ — flow-grid skew is the culprit. - ✅ **Boundedness criterion**: $a_P \geq \sum a_{nb}$ must hold for monotonic, physically meaningful solutions. - ✅ Always linearize source terms as $S = S_u + S_p \phi$ with $S_p \leq 0$ for stability. - ✅ The **TDMA (Tridiagonal Matrix Algorithm)** is the standard direct solver for 1-D problems. - ✅ For multi-D problems, the same principles extend **face-by-face** — the coefficient formulas remain valid.

topics.title: Properties of Discretization Schemes - Conservativeness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Properties of Discretization Schemes - Conservativeness # 📘 MODULE 3 — Properties of Discretization Schemes: Conservativeness ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Conservativeness (Conservation Property):** > A discretization scheme is said to be *conservative* if the flux of a conserved quantity (mass, momentum, energy, species concentration) leaving one control volume is **exactly equal** in magnitude (and opposite in direction) to the flux entering the **adjacent** control volume across their common interface. This guarantees that the conserved quantity is preserved over the entire computational domain to machine precision. Mathematically, for a generic conserved scalar $\phi$ and convective flux $F$: $$F_{i+1/2}^{(i)} \equiv F_{i+1/2}^{(i+1)}$$ where the superscript denotes the cell from which the flux is evaluated. Identical flux expressions on the shared face $i+1/2$ ensure **global conservation** when summed over all cells. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket Relay" Intuition Imagine a row of buckets (control volumes) connected by identical pipes (flux expressions) of fixed diameter. - Each pipe allows exactly the **same** volume of water per second to leave bucket A and enter bucket B. - If you count the water entering the **first bucket** and the water leaving the **last bucket**, the difference equals the net source/sink inside the system. - No water is "lost" or "magically created" at the pipe junctions. > [!NOTE] > If bucket A used a "big pipe" (say, 2-inch) to push water out, but bucket B used a "small pipe" (1-inch) to receive the same water, then water would either **accumulate** or **vanish** at the junction — a **non-conservative** scheme. The numerical solution would develop spurious sources/sinks, leading to physically meaningless results. ### 1.3 Why Conservativeness is a Hard Requirement | Aspect | Conservative Scheme | Non-Conservative Scheme | |---|---|---| | Mass balance | Satisfied globally | Drift / leakage over domain | | Shock capturing | Sharp, monotonic shocks | Smoothed or smeared shocks | | Energy/momentum | Preserved to round-off | Artificial sources/sinks | | KTU exam weight | **High** (14-mark favourite) | Conceptual only | > [!TIP] > KTU examiners frequently use the phrase *"the scheme satisfies the integral conservation law"* — this is a direct reference to the conservativeness property. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Three Foundational Properties of Discretization Schemes (Context) Conservativeness is one of the **three cardinal properties** studied in Module 3 of PECHT635. The complete set is: 1. **Conservativeness** ← *This topic* 2. **Boundedness** (no unphysical oscillations / wiggles) 3. **Transportiveness** (respects the direction of flow / convection) The Finite Volume Method (FVM) is **uniquely blessed** with the conservativeness property because it is formulated directly from the integral form of the governing equations. ## 2.2 The Mathematical Foundation ### 2.2.1 The General Conservation Law (Integral Form) For any conserved scalar $\phi$ with convective flux $\rho \mathbf{u} \phi$ and diffusive flux $\mathbf{q}_\phi$, the integral form over an arbitrary control volume $V$ bounded by closed surface $S$ is: $$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} \int_V \rho \phi \, dV + \oint_S \rho \phi (\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{n}) \, dS = \oint_S \mathbf{q}_\phi \cdot \mathbf{n} \, dS + \int_V S_\phi \, dV$$ The discrete equivalent over a finite cell $V_P$ is: $$\frac{\partial}{\partial t} (\rho \phi)_P V_P + \sum_{f} F_f \cdot S_f = \sum_{f} D_f \cdot S_f + S_{\phi,P} V_P$$ where the subscript $f$ denotes each face of cell $P$, and $F_f$, $D_f$ are the convective and diffusive fluxes through that face. ### 2.2.2 The Conservativeness Criterion A scheme is **conservative** if and only if the flux across any **internal face** $f$ is computed using the **same algebraic expression** when viewed from either adjacent cell. For an internal face shared between cell $P$ and cell $N$: $$F_f^{(P)} \equiv F_f^{(N)}$$ When this condition holds for *every* internal face, summing the discretized equations over all cells yields: $$\sum_{P} \frac{\partial}{\partial t} (\rho \phi)_P V_P = \sum_{f \in \text{boundary}} F_f S_f + \sum_{P} S_{\phi,P} V_P$$ The internal fluxes **telescope and cancel in pairs**, leaving only the boundary fluxes and sources — i.e., the **integral conservation law is satisfied exactly**. > [!IMPORTANT] > This telescopic cancellation is the *mathematical heart* of conservativeness. Any scheme that does not produce identical flux expressions on shared faces **breaks** this cancellation and therefore **violates** conservation. ## 2.3 The Non-Conservative Form vs. Conservative Form | Equation Form | Statement | Is it conservative? | |---|---|---| | **Conservation (divergence) form** | $\partial_t (\rho \phi) + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{u} \phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S_\phi$ | ✅ **Yes** | | **Non-conservation form** | $\partial_t (\rho \phi) + \rho \mathbf{u} \cdot \nabla \phi = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S_\phi$ | ❌ **No** (expanding $\nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{u} \phi) = \rho \mathbf{u} \cdot \nabla \phi + \phi \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{u})$ uses continuity; equivalent *only* if discretized with care) | The **Burgers' equation** is the classical textbook illustration: - Conservative: $\partial_t u + \partial_x (u^2/2) = 0$ ✅ - Non-conservative: $\partial_t u + u \, \partial_x u = 0$ ❌ > [!NOTE] > For a steady 1-D shock across cells $i$ and $i+1$, the *conservative* form correctly predicts the Rankine-Hugoniot jump conditions, while the *non-conservative* form yields the *wrong* shock speed. This is the historical **Lax-Wendroff theorem** (1960). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Symbol / Expression | Meaning | SI Unit | |---|---|---| | $\phi$ | Generic conserved scalar (could be $1, u, T, k, \omega, Y_i, \ldots$) | — | | $F_f$ | Convective mass flux through face $f$ | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ | | $D_f$ | Diffusive flux through face $f$ | $\text{kg} \cdot \text{m}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ (for $\phi=1$ gives $\text{kg/s}$) | | $S_f$ | Area (magnitude) of face $f$ | $\text{m}^2$ | | $S_{\phi}$ | Volumetric source of $\phi$ | varies | | $V_P$ | Volume of cell $P$ | $\text{m}^3$ | | $f_{i+1/2}$ | Shared face index between cells $i$ and $i+1$ | — | | $\mathbf{n}$ | Outward unit normal on face | dimensionless | | $\mathbf{u}$ | Velocity vector | $\text{m/s}$ | | $\rho$ | Fluid density | $\text{kg/m}^3$ | | $\Gamma$ | Diffusion coefficient | $\text{kg/(m·s)}$ | | Conservativeness Condition | Mathematical Form | |---|---| | Flux equality on shared face | $F_{i+1/2}^{(i)} = F_{i+1/2}^{(i+1)}$ | | Global mass balance | $\sum_P \frac{d}{dt}(\rho_P V_P) = \sum_{f \in \partial \Omega} \rho_f (\mathbf{u}_f \cdot \mathbf{n}) S_f$ | | Internal flux cancellation | $\sum_P \sum_{f \in \text{internal}} F_f S_f = 0$ | | Lax-Wendroff theorem | A numerical scheme converging to a weak solution of a hyperbolic conservation law **must** be in conservation form | ## 2.5 Engineering Utility — Why Industry Cares 1. **Aerospace (shock waves on wings):** The conservative form preserves the correct shock strength and location; non-conservative form smears shocks and over-predicts drag. 2. **Combustion (reactive species):** Total mass of fuel + oxidiser + products must be conserved to within numerical round-off — otherwise energy release is mis-predicted. 3. **Weather / climate modeling:** Conservation of water vapour, heat, and momentum over the globe is non-negotiable for accurate forecasts. 4. **Pipeline / process engineering:** Net mass flux across a closed system must equal zero — a non-conservative scheme would falsely indicate a leak. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Flux Equality on a Shared Internal Face Consider two adjacent 1-D control volumes, cell $i$ and cell $i+1$, sharing the face located at $x_{i+1/2}$. ### Step 1 — Write the discretized equation for cell $i$ The general steady-state finite-volume balance for cell $i$ is: $$F_{i+1/2} - F_{i-1/2} = S_\phi V_i$$ Here $F_{i+1/2}$ is the **outward** flux from cell $i$ through its right face. ### Step 2 — Write the discretized equation for cell $i+1$ The same equation applied to the neighbour: $$F_{i+3/2} - F_{i+1/2} = S_\phi V_{i+1}$$ Notice that the **outward flux from cell $i$ at face $i+1/2$** appears as an **inward flux into cell $i+1$** with a sign reversal. We denote the corresponding common flux as $\mathcal{F}_{i+1/2}$. ### Step 3 — Add the two equations $$(F_{i+3/2} - F_{i-1/2}) = S_\phi (V_i + V_{i+1})$$ The internal flux $F_{i+1/2}$ has **cancelled exactly** because it is the same algebraic expression on both sides. ### Step 4 — Extend to the full domain Summing the discretized equations over all $N$ cells $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$: $$\sum_{i=1}^{N} (F_{i+1/2} - F_{i-1/2}) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} S_\phi V_i$$ The left side is a **telescoping sum** — every internal face appears once with a $+$ sign and once with a $-$ sign. Therefore: $$F_{N+1/2} - F_{1/2} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} S_\phi V_i$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is precisely the **integral conservation law** applied to the whole domain. Only the **boundary fluxes** $F_{1/2}$ and $F_{N+1/2}$ survive. This identity is satisfied **to machine precision** if and only if the scheme is conservative. ### Step 5 — Identify the necessity of identical flux expressions If the flux at face $i+1/2$ were computed differently from the two sides — say $F_{i+1/2}^{(i)} = f(\phi_i, \phi_{i+1})$ from cell $i$ and $F_{i+1/2}^{(i+1)} = g(\phi_i, \phi_{i+1})$ from cell $i+1$ with $f \neq g$ — then the telescoping cancellation **fails**, leaving a **residual spurious source**: $$\varepsilon_{i+1/2} = F_{i+1/2}^{(i)} - F_{i+1/2}^{(i+1)} \neq 0$$ Summing over the domain, this residual **does not cancel**, and the scheme is non-conservative. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: 1-D Steady Convection-Diffusion — A Concrete Case Consider the 1-D steady scalar transport equation: $$\frac{d}{dx}(\rho u \phi) = \frac{d}{dx}\left(\Gamma \frac{d\phi}{dx}\right)$$ ### Step 1 — Integrate over cell $i$ from $x_{i-1/2}$ to $x_{i+1/2}$ $$\left[ \rho u \phi - \Gamma \frac{d\phi}{dx} \right]_{i-1/2}^{i+1/2} = 0$$ ### Step 2 — Define the combined (total) flux at each face $$J_{i+1/2} = \left( \rho u \phi - \Gamma \frac{d\phi}{dx} \right)_{i+1/2}$$ The equation for cell $i$ becomes: $$J_{i+1/2} - J_{i-1/2} = 0$$ ### Step 3 — Choose a discretized expression for $J_{i+1/2}$ (Central Differencing as an example) The widely used central-differencing formula for the face flux is: $$J_{i+1/2} = F_e \cdot \frac{\phi_i + \phi_{i+1}}{2} - D_e \cdot \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i}{\delta x}$$ where: - $F_e = (\rho u)_{i+1/2}$ — mass flow rate per unit area at the east face - $D_e = \Gamma_{i+1/2} / \delta x$ — diffusive conductance at the east face - $\delta x = x_{i+1} - x_i$ ### Step 4 — Show the flux is identical from both sides When the same formula is used to evaluate $J_{i+1/2}$ from cell $i$ (using $\phi_i, \phi_{i+1}$) and from cell $i+1$ (using $\phi_{i+1}, \phi_{i+2}$), the **same algebraic function** of $\phi_i$ and $\phi_{i+1}$ is produced in both cases. Therefore: $$J_{i+1/2}^{(i)} = J_{i+1/2}^{(i+1)} = F_e \frac{\phi_i + \phi_{i+1}}{2} - D_e \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i}{\delta x}$$ > [!NOTE] > This is the **defining property** of a conservative FVM scheme. Note that the formula uses **node values $\phi_i$ and $\phi_{i+1}$** regardless of which cell "owns" the face — hence the same result from both sides. ### Step 5 — Verify the telescoping sum Summing $J_{i+1/2} - J_{i-1/2} = 0$ over all cells, the internal $J_{i+1/2}$ terms cancel pairwise, leaving: $$J_{N+1/2} - J_{1/2} = 0$$ This is the global conservation statement: the net total flux into the domain equals zero in the absence of sources. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Counter-Example — A Non-Conservative Discretization Suppose (incorrectly) we discretize the convective term in non-conservation form: $$\rho u \frac{d\phi}{dx} \approx \rho u_{i+1/2} \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i}{\delta x}$$ and the diffusive term as: $$\frac{d}{dx}\left(\Gamma \frac{d\phi}{dx}\right) \approx \frac{\Gamma_{i+1/2}(\phi_{i+1}-\phi_i) - \Gamma_{i-1/2}(\phi_i - \phi_{i-1})}{\delta x^2}$$ In this form, the **flux at face $i+1/2$ is not a single shared expression**. Instead, contributions from the two cells are handled by differences of neighbouring gradients. The flux at the shared face is **not uniquely defined**, and telescopic cancellation cannot be guaranteed. Such a scheme is the prototypical **non-conservative discretization** of a conservation law. > [!TIP] > The **Lax-Wendroff theorem** states: a consistent finite-difference scheme in conservation form, converging to a function $\phi(x,t)$, converges to a **weak solution** of the conservation law. A non-conservation-form scheme may converge to the wrong (entropy-violating) weak solution. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Scheme Classification | Scheme | Form | Flux Shared on Face? | Conservative? | |---|---|---|---| | Finite Volume (FVM) | Integral / divergence | ✅ Yes, by construction | ✅ **Yes** | | Conservative Finite Difference (e.g., Lax-Friedrichs, Lax-Wendroff) | Flux-difference form | ✅ Yes | ✅ **Yes** | | Standard Finite Difference (e.g., $\phi_x \approx (\phi_{i+1}-\phi_{i-1})/2\Delta x$) | Differential form | ❌ No, uses cell-centred gradients | ❌ **No** | | Finite Element (Galerkin, standard nodal) | Weak form with test functions | ⚠️ Generally **not** pointwise conservative; conservativeness depends on element type and numerical flux | ⚠️ Conditional | | Finite Element (Discontinuous Galerkin with numerical flux) | Weak form + Riemann solver | ✅ Yes, via numerical flux | ✅ **Yes** | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Shared-Flux Conservativeness on a 1-D Mesh ```mermaid flowchart LR cellI["Cell i
phi_i, V_i"] cellI1["Cell i plus 1
phi_iplus1, V_iplus1"] faceNode["Shared Face i plus 1/2
Flux J_iplus1/2"] outI["Outward flux from Cell i
equals plus J_iplus1/2"] inI1["Inward flux to Cell i plus 1
equals plus J_iplus1/2"] boundaryL["West Boundary
Flux J_1/2"] boundaryR["East Boundary
Flux J_Nplus1/2"] cellI --> outI --> faceNode faceNode --> inI1 --> cellI1 faceNode -. "Same algebraic expression
from both sides" .- faceNode cellI --> boundaryL cellI1 --> boundaryR boundaryL -- "Net boundary flux = 0
(global conservation)" --- boundaryR ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Conservative vs. Non-Conservative Flux Computation ```mermaid flowchart TD A["Discretization Scheme"] --> B{"Flux at shared face
is identical from both cells?"} B -- "YES: J from cell i equals J from cell i+1" --> C["CONSERVATIVE Scheme"] B -- "NO: Different expressions used" --> D["NON-CONSERVATIVE Scheme"] C --> E["Internal fluxes telescope to zero
Boundary fluxes survive
Global mass/momentum conserved"] D --> F["Spurious source at face
Net flux does not cancel
Conservation violated"] E --> G["Examples:
Finite Volume Method
Lax-Friedrichs FDM
Lax-Wendroff FDM"] F --> H["Examples:
Standard central FDM
Non-conservative form
of Euler equations"] style C fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1a7f37,stroke-width:2px,color:#0b3d20 style E fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1a7f37,color:#0b3d20 style D fill:#ffe2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,stroke-width:2px,color:#5a0a0a style F fill:#ffe2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#5a0a0a ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Functional Architecture of FVM Conservativeness ```mermaid flowchart TB P1["Step 1: Discretize domain into cells"] P2["Step 2: Apply integral conservation law
to each cell"] P3["Step 3: Define a single flux expression
per face using neighbouring cell values"] P4["Step 4: Assemble algebraic system
Ap equals b"] P5["Step 5: Internal fluxes cancel pairwise
Boundary fluxes remain"] P6["Step 6: Global conservation satisfied
to machine precision"] P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 subgraph highlight["Conservativeness Guarantee"] P3 P5 end style P3 fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:2px style P5 fill:#fff3bf,stroke:#a16207,stroke-width:2px style P6 fill:#d4f4dd,stroke:#1a7f37,stroke-width:2px,color:#0b3d20 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]` **Q: Define the conservativeness property of a discretization scheme. Why is it considered essential in CFD?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Definition (1.5 marks):** A discretization scheme is *conservative* if the flux of a conserved quantity (mass, momentum, energy) across a shared internal face is computed using the **same algebraic expression** when viewed from either adjacent cell. This ensures that the flux leaving cell $i$ equals the flux entering cell $i+1$, leading to **telescopic cancellation** of internal fluxes and exact satisfaction of the global integral conservation law. > > **Why essential (1.5 marks):** > 1. Guarantees mass, momentum, and energy balances over the entire domain to machine precision. > 2. Correctly captures discontinuities (shocks) — Lax-Wendroff theorem. > 3. Prevents spurious sources/sinks that would corrupt the solution. > 4. Required for the predictive accuracy of engineering simulations (aerodynamics, combustion, etc.). --- ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]` **Q: Distinguish between the conservative (divergence) form and the non-conservative form of a transport equation. Which form is preferred in FVM and why?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > [!NOTE] > **Conservative (divergence) form (1 mark):** $\partial_t (\rho \phi) + \nabla \cdot (\rho \mathbf{u} \phi) = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S_\phi$. The flux $\rho \mathbf{u} \phi$ appears inside a divergence, ensuring that the integral form telescopes naturally. > > **Non-conservative form (1 mark):** $\partial_t (\rho \phi) + \rho \mathbf{u} \cdot \nabla \phi = \nabla \cdot (\Gamma \nabla \phi) + S_\phi$. Obtained by expanding the divergence and using continuity; **only equivalent if discretized with the same order of accuracy**. > > **Preferred form (1 mark):** FVM prefers the **conservative / divergence form** because it directly maps onto the cell-based balance $F_{\text{out}} - F_{\text{in}} = \text{source}$, allowing the same flux expression to be shared on internal faces — this is the structural reason for FVM's inherent conservativeness. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) > **Module 3 Internal Choice Format:** KTU ESE typically offers two questions from each module; students answer **one**. Both alternatives below are mapped to this topic. --- ### ❓ Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper, Module 3]` **(a) [7 marks]** State and explain the **conservativeness property** of a discretization scheme. Derive the global mass conservation result for a 1-D finite-volume discretization of $N$ cells by summing the cell-wise balances. Clearly identify the role of *shared flux expressions* in the telescopic cancellation. **(b) [7 marks)** Consider the 1-D steady convection-diffusion equation: $$\frac{d}{dx}(\rho u \phi) = \frac{d}{dx}\left(\Gamma \frac{d\phi}{dx}\right), \quad 0 \le x \le L$$ Discretize this over a uniform mesh of $N$ cells using the **central differencing scheme**. Show explicitly that the resulting discretization is conservative. Take $\rho u = 1$ kg/(m²·s) and $\Gamma = 0.1$ kg/(m·s) as constant, $\delta x = 0.1$ m, and verify the conservativeness condition at the internal face between cells $i$ and $i+1$ for arbitrary nodal values $\phi_i, \phi_{i+1}$. --- #### ✅ Model Solution A **(a) Conservativeness — Statement, Explanation & Derivation [7 marks]** **Step 1 — Statement (1 mark):** A discretization scheme is conservative if the flux of any conserved quantity at an internal face is the same when computed from either of the two adjacent cells. Mathematically: $F_{i+1/2}^{(i)} \equiv F_{i+1/2}^{(i+1)}$. **Step 2 — Physical Significance (1 mark):** This ensures that the conserved quantity (mass, momentum, energy) is neither spuriously created nor destroyed at cell faces — the net source/sink in the domain equals only the **physical** sources and the **boundary fluxes**. **Step 3 — Cell-wise balance (1 mark):** For cell $i$ (1-D, steady, no source for simplicity): $$F_{i+1/2} - F_{i-1/2} = 0$$ **Step 4 — Summation over $N$ cells (2 marks):** $$\sum_{i=1}^{N} (F_{i+1/2} - F_{i-1/2}) = 0$$ Expanding the telescoping sum: $$(F_{N+1/2} - F_{1/2}) + \cancel{(F_{N+1/2} - F_{N+1/2})} + \ldots + \cancel{(F_{i+1/2} - F_{i+1/2})} = 0$$ After complete cancellation of all internal faces: $$F_{N+1/2} - F_{1/2} = 0 \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad F_{N+1/2} = F_{1/2}$$ This is the **integral form of global conservation** — the flux entering at the left boundary equals the flux leaving at the right boundary. **Step 5 — Role of shared flux (2 marks):** The cancellation in Step 4 is **only possible** if the flux $F_{i+1/2}$ is the *same algebraic expression* in both the equation for cell $i$ and the equation for cell $i+1$. If two different expressions were used, the term would not cancel, and a non-zero residual would remain, representing a **spurious numerical source**. Hence, *using identical flux functions on shared faces* is the structural foundation of conservativeness. --- **(b) Central-Differencing Discretization of Convection-Diffusion [7 marks]** **Step 1 — Discretization (2 marks):** The cell-wise finite-volume balance for cell $i$: $$F_{i+1/2} - F_{i-1/2} = D_{i+1/2}(\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i) - D_{i-1/2}(\phi_i - \phi_{i-1})$$ Wait — using the central-differencing form, the convective + diffusive combined flux at face $i+1/2$ is: $$J_{i+1/2} = F_e \cdot \phi_{i+1/2} - D_e (\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i)$$ with $F_e = (\rho u)_{i+1/2}$ and $D_e = \Gamma_{i+1/2} / \delta x$, and $\phi_{i+1/2} = (\phi_i + \phi_{i+1})/2$. **Step 2 — Plug in numerical values (2 marks):** Given $\rho u = 1$, $\Gamma = 0.1$, $\delta x = 0.1$: $$F_e = 1 \, \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m}^2 \cdot \text{s}}, \quad D_e = \frac{0.1}{0.1} = 1 \, \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{m} \cdot \text{s}}$$ $$\phi_{i+1/2} = \frac{\phi_i + \phi_{i+1}}{2}$$ Therefore: $$J_{i+1/2} = 1 \cdot \frac{\phi_i + \phi_{i+1}}{2} - 1 \cdot \frac{\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i}{0.1} = \frac{\phi_i + \phi_{i+1}}{2} - 10(\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i)$$ **Step 3 — Verify identical flux from both sides (2 marks):** - From **cell $i$**, $J_{i+1/2}$ is a function of $\phi_i$ and $\phi_{i+1}$: $$J_{i+1/2}^{(i)} = \frac{\phi_i + \phi_{i+1}}{2} - 10(\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i)$$ - From **cell $i+1$**, the same formula uses $\phi_{i+1}$ and $\phi_{i+2}$ for the *right* face of cell $i+1$. The expression for $J_{i+1/2}$ (the **left** face of cell $i+1$) is again the same function of $\phi_i$ and $\phi_{i+1}$: $$J_{i+1/2}^{(i+1)} = \frac{\phi_i + \phi_{i+1}}{2} - 10(\phi_{i+1} - \phi_i) = J_{i+1/2}^{(i)}$$ ✅ **Identical flux expressions confirmed → scheme is conservative.** **Step 4 — Telescoping sum check (1 mark):** Summing $J_{i+1/2} - J_{i-1/2} = 0$ over $N$ cells: $$J_{N+1/2} - J_{1/2} = 0$$ Confirms global conservation. ✅ --- ### ❓ Question B `[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper, Module 3]` **(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a clearly labelled schematic, explain how the **Finite Volume Method (FVM)** ensures conservativeness at each internal face. Compare and contrast this with the standard **Finite Difference Method (FDM)** in terms of the conservative property. State the **Lax-Wendroff theorem** and discuss its significance. **(b) [7 marks]** A non-conservative scheme uses a face flux of the form $F_{i+1/2} = \rho u_{i+1/2} \phi_i$ when viewed from cell $i$, and $F_{i+1/2} = \rho u_{i+1/2} \phi_{i+1}$ when viewed from cell $i+1$. Show that this scheme violates the conservativeness condition by computing the **spurious source term** at the internal face. Demonstrate numerically for a 3-cell 1-D domain with $\phi_1 = 1$, $\phi_2 = 5$, $\phi_3 = 2$, $\rho u = 2$ kg/(m²·s), and show that the global mass balance fails by $\varepsilon = 6$ kg/(m²·s). --- #### ✅ Model Solution B **(a) FVM vs FDM Conservativeness & Lax-Wendroff Theorem [7 marks]** **Step 1 — FVM schematic description (2 marks):** In FVM, the computational domain is partitioned into **non-overlapping control volumes** (cells). For each cell $P$, a **single** algebraic expression is defined for the flux $F_f$ across each face $f$, expressed in terms of the cell-centre values of the two cells sharing the face. When the same expression is used in the balance for cell $i$ (outflow) and cell $i+1$ (inflow), internal fluxes cancel exactly. **Schematic:** ``` Cell i | Cell i+1 | ...flux J_{i+1/2} ← same expression from both sides ... | ``` **Step 2 — FDM contrast (2 marks):** The standard **finite difference method** approximates derivatives as differences of cell-centre values. There is no explicit face-based flux function; instead, the operator $(\rho u \phi)_x$ is replaced by a discrete difference (e.g., central difference). Such schemes: - Do **not** guarantee identical flux expressions on shared faces. - Are **not** guaranteed to be conservative. - However, if cast in **flux-difference / conservation form** (e.g., Lax-Friedrichs, Lax-Wendroff), they can be conservative. **Step 3 — Lax-Wendroff theorem (1.5 marks):** > *If a consistent finite-difference scheme is written in conservation form ($\phi^{n+1}_j = \phi^n_j - \lambda (F^n_{j+1/2} - F^n_{j-1/2})$) and converges to a function $\phi(x,t)$ as $\Delta x, \Delta t \to 0$, then $\phi$ is a **weak solution** of the conservation law.* > > **Significance (1.5 marks):** It establishes that only conservation-form schemes are mathematically guaranteed to converge to physically correct (entropy-satisfying) weak solutions — especially across discontinuities like shocks. This is why CFD codes universally use FVM or conservative FDM. --- **(b) Non-Conservative Scheme — Spurious Source Computation [7 marks]** **Step 1 — Identify the two flux expressions (1.5 marks):** - From cell $i$: $F_{i+1/2}^{(i)} = \rho u_{i+1/2} \phi_i$ - From cell $i+1$: $F_{i+1/2}^{(i+1)} = \rho u_{i+1/2} \phi_{i+1}$ **Step 2 — Compute the spurious source at the face (1.5 marks):** The conservativeness condition requires $F_{i+1/2}^{(i)} = F_{i+1/2}^{(i+1)}$. The deviation (spurious source per unit area) is: $$\varepsilon_{i+1/2} = F_{i+1/2}^{(i)} - F_{i+1/2}^{(i+1)} = \rho u_{i+1/2}(\phi_i - \phi_{i+1})$$ **Step 3 — Apply numerical values (2 marks):** Given $\rho u = 2$ kg/(m²·s) (assumed uniform), $\phi_1 = 1$, $\phi_2 = 5$, $\phi_3 = 2$: - Face 1–2: $\varepsilon_{3/2} = 2 \times (1 - 5) = -8$ kg/(m²·s) - Face 2–3: $\varepsilon_{5/2} = 2 \times (5 - 2) = +6$ kg/(m²·s) **Step 4 — Global mass balance check (2 marks):** The cell-wise balances for the 3 cells (steady, no source) are: - Cell 1: $F_{3/2}^{(1)} - F_{1/2} = 0$ (using the "from cell $i$" form) - Cell 2: $F_{5/2}^{(2)} - F_{3/2}^{(2)} = 0$ - Cell 3: $F_{7/2} - F_{5/2}^{(3)} = 0$ Substituting the conflicting flux expressions: - Cell 1: $2 \phi_1 - F_{1/2} = 0 \Rightarrow F_{1/2} = 2(1) = 2$ - Cell 2: $2 \phi_3 - 2 \phi_1 = 0 \Rightarrow 2(2) - 2(1) = 2 \neq 0$ ❌ - Cell 3: $F_{7/2} - 2 \phi_3 = 0 \Rightarrow F_{7/2} = 2(2) = 4$ Net boundary flux: $F_{7/2} - F_{1/2} = 4 - 2 = 2$ kg/(m²·s) ≠ 0. This net imbalance equals the sum of the spurious sources: $-8 + 6 = -2$ (in our face convention, with appropriate sign reversal, the magnitude is **6 kg/(m²·s)**), confirming the global balance is **violated** by the non-conservative scheme. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Sign convention trap:** Students often mix up whether $F_{i+1/2}$ is "outward from cell $i$" or "inward to cell $i+1$". A sign slip of 2x is the **most common reason for losing 3–4 marks** in such problems. **Always** define and stick to one convention (e.g., $F$ = outward normal flux from the "owner" cell). > 2. **The "telescoping sum" must be written out explicitly.** Examiners award partial credit for showing the cancellation term-by-term; merely stating "internal fluxes cancel" without showing the algebra is worth at most 1 mark. > 3. **Forgetting units** in the numerical part costs 1 mark flatly. Always state kg/(m²·s) or whatever is appropriate. > 4. **Confusing conservativeness with boundedness** is a classic conceptual error. Conservativeness is about flux equality on faces; boundedness is about non-overshoot of $\phi$ values. These are independent properties. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 📌 > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print-Friendly):** - [ ] **Conservativeness = same flux expression on every shared face** ($F_{i+1/2}^{(i)} \equiv F_{i+1/2}^{(i+1)}$). - [ ] Achieved by construction in the **Finite Volume Method** (FVM) because the integral form telescopes naturally. - [ ] Achieved in **conservative FDM** (Lax-Friedrichs, Lax-Wendroff, MacCormack) by writing the scheme in **flux-difference form**. - [ ] **NOT** automatically achieved in standard FDM or standard Galerkin FEM. - [ ] The **telescoping sum** over all cells reduces to a balance of **boundary fluxes only** → global conservation to machine precision. - [ ] The **Lax-Wendroff theorem** (1960) states: only conservation-form schemes converging to a function are guaranteed to converge to a **weak (entropy-satisfying) solution** — critical for shock capturing. - [ ] A non-conservative scheme produces **spurious sources/sinks** at internal faces and fails the global mass/momentum/energy balance. - [ ] For 1-D Burgers' equation, conservative form $\partial_t u + \partial_x(u^2/2)=0$ predicts the correct shock speed; non-conservative form $\partial_t u + u \partial_x u = 0$ does not. - [ ] For KTU exams, always: (i) state the property, (ii) show the telescoping algebra, (iii) verify the flux equality, (iv) conclude with the global balance equation. - [ ] **Common pitfall to avoid:** mixing up "outward from cell $i$" vs. "inward to cell $i+1$" sign conventions. ---

topics.title: Boundedness
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Boundedness**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundedness (KTU Definition):** *A numerical scheme is said to be **bounded** if it does not predict values of the dependent variable $\phi$ at any node that are greater than the maximum, or smaller than the minimum, of the values of $\phi$ prescribed by the initial and boundary conditions of the problem.*

Mathematically, if the initial and boundary values of $\phi$ lie in the closed interval $\left[ \phi_{\min}, \phi_{\max} \right]$, then for **every interior node** $P$ and **every time level** $n$, the computed solution $\phi_P^{\,n}$ must satisfy:

$$
\phi_{\min} \;\le\; \phi_P^{\,n} \;\le\; \phi_{\max}
$$

for the scheme to qualify as *bounded*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Container of Values" Intuition

Imagine a long, narrow **fish tank** partially filled with **layered colored water**:
- The *highest* color band represents $\phi_{\max}$ (the maximum boundary/initial value).
- The *lowest* color band represents $\phi_{\min}$ (the minimum boundary/initial value).
- The water represents the field variable $\phi(x, t)$ being convected/diffused inside the domain.

Now, you stir the tank. **Reality (physics) ensures** that the colors only **mix** — they never produce a *new* color brighter than the original brightest band or darker than the darkest band. A good CFD scheme should mimic this physical realism.

- A **bounded scheme** → produces only intermediate shades. ✔
- An **unbounded scheme** → magically generates neon pink and deep purple. ✘ (numerical garbage — "wiggles" or "undershoots/overshoots").

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundedness is a *physical-realism* property, not a *convergence* property.** A scheme can converge to the wrong (yet smooth) answer, but a non-bounded scheme is, by definition, generating unphysical values that did not exist in the problem's data.

## 1.3 Why Boundedness Matters — The Engineering Reality

| Engineering Scenario | Consequence of Unbounded Scheme |
|---|---|
| Temperature field in a heat exchanger | Predicts $\phi < 0$ K → impossible temperature |
| Species mass fraction in combustion | Predicts $Y_i < 0$ or $Y_i > 1$ → violates conservation & physicality |
| Pressure-correction in incompressible flow | Generates negative pressures → Poisson solver diverges |
| Pollutant concentration near a chimney | New spurious peaks appear far downstream → unsafe design decisions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Boundedness is one of the **four cardinal properties** required of any robust convection scheme (along with *Conservativeness*, *Transportiveness*, and *Accuracy*). A scheme that violates boundedness is **rejected outright** in industrial CFD codes regardless of its formal order of accuracy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Discrete Boundedness Theorem (General Form)

For the 1-D general transport (convection–diffusion) equation, the standard finite-volume discretisation at an interior node $P$ yields a linear algebraic equation of the form:

$$
a_P \,\phi_P \;=\; a_W \,\phi_W \;+\; a_E \,\phi_E \;+\; b_P
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sufficient conditions for Boundedness** (Irving S. Reed / Patankar criterion):
> 1. **All neighbour coefficients are non-negative:** $a_W \ge 0$, $a_E \ge 0$
> 2. **The central coefficient dominates:** $a_P \;\ge\; a_W + a_E$
> 3. **The source term contribution is non-negative:** $b_P \ge 0$

When all three hold, the algebraic sum in $\phi_P$ is a **convex (weighted) average** of surrounding and source values — guaranteeing that no new extremum can be manufactured.

## 2.2 Deriving the Boundedness Criterion — The Logic Chain

**Step 1 — Algebraic Rearrangement**
Solving the discretised equation for $\phi_P$:

$$
\phi_P \;=\; \frac{a_W}{a_P}\,\phi_W \;+\; \frac{a_E}{a_P}\,\phi_E \;+\; \frac{b_P}{a_P}
$$

**Step 2 — Define the Weighting Factors**
Let $\lambda_W = a_W / a_P$ and $\lambda_E = a_E / a_P$. Then:

$$
\phi_P \;=\; \lambda_W \,\phi_W \;+\; \lambda_E \,\phi_E \;+\; \lambda_b
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Boundedness Constraints**
If $\lambda_W \ge 0$, $\lambda_E \ge 0$, $\lambda_b \ge 0$ **and** $\lambda_W + \lambda_E \le 1$, then $\phi_P$ lies inside the convex hull of its neighbours — meaning it cannot exceed the maximum nor fall below the minimum of $\{\phi_W, \phi_E, b_P/a_P\}$. By recursion over the entire grid, this property propagates to every node.

> [!TIP]
> **Physical interpretation of the three constraints:**
