| **Linearity** | **Nonlinear** (because of $\mathbf{V} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{V}$). | Superposition principle fails. Closed-form analytical solutions exist only for a few idealized geometries. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for ESE.** It is the single most-tested content from Module 3.

| Concept | Equation | Remarks |
|---------|----------|---------|
| General Vector N-S | $\rho \left( \dfrac{D\mathbf{V}}{Dt} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \mathbf{V} + \rho \mathbf{g}$ | Material derivative formulation |
| Continuity (Incompressible) | $\dfrac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \dfrac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \dfrac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0$ | Constant density |
| Vorticity Vector | $\boldsymbol{\omega} = \nabla \times \mathbf{V}$ | Measures local rotation |
| Vorticity Transport Eq. | $\dfrac{D\boldsymbol{\omega}}{Dt} = (\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{V} + \nu \nabla^{2} \boldsymbol{\omega}$ | Vortex stretching + diffusion |
| Stream Function (2D) | $u = \dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial y}, \quad v = -\dfrac{\partial \psi}{\partial x}$ | Automatically satisfies continuity |
| Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho U L}{\mu} = \dfrac{U L}{\nu}$ | Ratio of inertial to viscous forces |
| Kinematic Viscosity | $\nu = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho}$ | Units: $m^{2}/s$ |
| Euler Number | $Eu = \dfrac{p}{\rho U^{2}}$ | Ratio of pressure to inertial forces |
| Froude Number | $Fr = \dfrac{U}{\sqrt{gL}}$ | Ratio of inertial to gravitational forces |
| Cauchy Momentum | $\rho \dfrac{D\mathbf{V}}{Dt} = \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma} + \rho \mathbf{g}$ | Most general form (any fluid) |
| Stokes Hypothesis | $\lambda = -\dfrac{2}{3}\mu$ | Bulk viscosity for monoatomic gas |

## 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

The N-S equations underpin virtually every fluid-related engineering discipline:

- **Aerospace:** Airfoil design, transonic/supersonic flow over aircraft, rocket nozzle flow.
- **Mechanical:** Internal combustion engine cooling, turbine blade heat transfer, lubrication theory.
- **Civil:** Wind loading on tall buildings, dam spillway hydraulics, river flow modeling.
- **Biomedical:** Blood flow through arteries (uses modified N-S for non-Newtonian biofluids), airflow in the human nasal passage.
- **Chemical:** Mixing in stirred-tank reactors, multiphase flow in pipelines.
- **Atmospheric & Oceanographic:** Weather prediction, ocean current modeling, pollutant dispersion.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Millennium Problem:** The Clay Mathematics Institute has offered a **\$1,000,000 prize** for a rigorous proof of the global existence and smoothness of solutions to the 3D incompressible N-S equations. This underscores the profound mathematical depth hidden within these equations.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Navier-Stokes Equations from a Differential Control Volume

The most rigorous route to the N-S equations (and the one most often asked in KTU 14-mark derivations) is the **Reynolds Transport Theorem** applied to a fluid continuum.

### Step 1: Starting Point — Newton's Second Law for a Fluid Particle

Apply Newton's 2nd law to a fluid parcel of mass $dm$:

$$
\sum \mathbf{F} = \frac{d}{dt}(m \mathbf{V})
$$

For a continuum element of fixed mass, this becomes:

$$
\sum \mathbf{F} = \rho \, \frac{D\mathbf{V}}{Dt} \, d\forall
$$

where $d\forall$ is the differential volume element, and $\dfrac{D\mathbf{V}}{Dt}$ is the **material derivative**:

$$
\frac{D\mathbf{V}}{Dt} = \frac{\partial \mathbf{V}}{\partial t} + u \frac{\partial \mathbf{V}}{\partial x} + v \frac{\partial \mathbf{V}}{\partial y} + w \frac{\partial \mathbf{V}}{\partial z}
$$

**Why a material derivative?** Because a fluid parcel is *moving* — we must track its velocity change both from local unsteadiness and from spatial transport.

### Step 2: Enumerate the Forces Acting on the Fluid Element

The total force $\sum \mathbf{F}$ has two fundamental classes:

1. **Surface Forces** (act on the control surface $dA$): These are the *normal* stresses (pressure) and *tangential* stresses (viscous shear).
2. **Body Forces** (act on the entire mass $dm$): Typically gravity, $\rho \mathbf{g}$.

The surface force per unit area is encoded in the **Cauchy stress tensor** $\boldsymbol{\sigma}$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\sigma} = \begin{pmatrix} \sigma_{xx} & \tau_{xy} & \tau_{xz} \\ \tau_{yx} & \sigma_{yy} & \tau_{yz} \\ \tau_{zx} & \tau_{zy} & \sigma_{zz} \end{pmatrix}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Cauchy's Equation of Motion

The net surface force on a differential element is the *divergence* of the stress tensor:

$$
\mathbf{F}_{\text{surface}} = \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma} \,\, d\forall
$$

Combining with body force $\rho \mathbf{g}$:

$$
\rho \frac{D\mathbf{V}}{Dt} = \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma} + \rho \mathbf{g}
$$

This is the **Cauchy momentum equation** — valid for *any* continuum, Newtonian or not.

### Step 4: Constitutive Relation for a Newtonian Fluid

A Newtonian fluid obeys the **Stokes' constitutive hypothesis** — the stress tensor is a linear function of the strain rate tensor $\dot{\boldsymbol{\epsilon}}$:

$$
\boldsymbol{\sigma} = -p \mathbf{I} + \boldsymbol{\tau}
$$

where the deviatoric (viscous) stress is:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mu \left( \nabla \mathbf{V} + (\nabla \mathbf{V})^{T} \right) + \lambda (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{V}) \mathbf{I}
$$

For **incompressible flow** ($\nabla \cdot \mathbf{V} = 0$), the second term vanishes, leaving:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mu \left( \nabla \mathbf{V} + (\nabla \mathbf{V})^{T} \right)
$$

### Step 5: Substitute and Simplify

Substituting back:

$$
\nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \mathbf{V}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\rho \left( \frac{\partial \mathbf{V}}{\partial t} + \mathbf{V} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \mathbf{V} + \rho \mathbf{g}
$$

This is the **incompressible Newtonian Navier-Stokes equation**. $\blacksquare$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Vorticity Transport Equation

The **vorticity equation** is a key alternative formulation because it eliminates pressure (for incompressible 2D flow) and reveals vortex dynamics.

### Step 1: Take the Curl of the N-S Momentum Equation

Apply $\nabla \times$ to both sides of:

$$
\rho \frac{D\mathbf{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \mathbf{V} + \rho \mathbf{g}
$$

### Step 2: Evaluate Term-by-Term

- **Curl of pressure gradient:** $\nabla \times (\nabla p) = 0$ (always, since curl of gradient is zero).
- **Curl of gravity (conservative body force):** $\nabla \times (\rho \mathbf{g}) = 0$ (since $\mathbf{g} = -\nabla \phi$).
- **Curl of material derivative:** $\nabla \times \left( \dfrac{D\mathbf{V}}{Dt} \right) = \dfrac{D\boldsymbol{\omega}}{Dt} - (\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{V} + \boldsymbol{\omega} (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{V})$
- **Curl of viscous term:** $\nabla \times (\nu \nabla^{2} \mathbf{V}) = \nu \nabla^{2} \boldsymbol{\omega}$ (since $\nabla \times \nabla^{2} \mathbf{V} = \nabla^{2} (\nabla \times \mathbf{V})$).

For incompressible flow ($\nabla \cdot \mathbf{V} = 0$):

$$
\frac{D\boldsymbol{\omega}}{Dt} = (\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{V} + \nu \nabla^{2} \boldsymbol{\omega}
$$

This is the **Vorticity Transport Equation** (VTE). $\blacksquare$

**Physical Interpretation:**

- The term $\nu \nabla^{2} \boldsymbol{\omega}$ is the **diffusion of vorticity** by viscosity (vorticity "leaks" out of the flow into walls).
- The term $(\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{V}$ is the **vortex stretching** term — a 3D phenomenon where a vortex line is stretched (e.g., by a velocity gradient), causing it to intensify (conservation of angular momentum).

## 3.3 Derivation of the Non-Dimensional Form & Reynolds Number

The non-dimensional form is the gateway to **dynamic similarity** in wind tunnel and scale-model testing.

### Step 1: Define Characteristic Scales

Let $L$ = characteristic length, $U$ = characteristic velocity, $T_{ref} = L/U$ = characteristic time, $p_{ref} = \rho U^{2}$ = characteristic pressure.

### Step 2: Substitute Dimensionless Variables

Introduce:

$$
\tilde{x} = \frac{x}{L}, \quad \tilde{y} = \frac{y}{L}, \quad \tilde{z} = \frac{z}{L}
$$

$$
\tilde{u} = \frac{u}{U}, \quad \tilde{v} = \frac{v}{U}, \quad \tilde{w} = \frac{w}{U}
$$

$$
\tilde{t} = \frac{t \, U}{L}, \quad \tilde{p} = \frac{p}{\rho U^{2}}
$$

### Step 3: Rewrite the Inertial Term

$$
\rho \frac{D u}{Dt} \to \rho \frac{U^{2}}{L} \frac{D \tilde{u}}{D \tilde{t}}
$$

### Step 4: Rewrite the Viscous Term

$$
\mu \nabla^{2} u \to \frac{\mu U}{L^{2}} \tilde{\nabla}^{2} \tilde{u}
$$

### Step 5: Form the Ratio — Reynolds Number

Dividing the inertial term by the viscous term:

$$
\frac{\rho U^{2}/L}{\mu U / L^{2}} = \frac{\rho U L}{\mu} \equiv Re
$$

### Step 6: Final Dimensionless Equation

$$
\frac{D \tilde{\mathbf{V}}}{D \tilde{t}} = -\nabla \tilde{p} + \frac{1}{Re} \tilde{\nabla}^{2} \tilde{\mathbf{V}} + \frac{1}{Fr^{2}} \tilde{\mathbf{g}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** Two geometrically similar flows are **dynamically similar** if and only if they have the same $Re$ (and $Fr$ if gravity matters). This is the **foundation of wind tunnel and ship model testing**.

## 3.4 Exact Analytical Solution 1: Plane Couette Flow

**Problem Setup:** Two infinite parallel plates separated by distance $h$. The lower plate is stationary; the upper plate moves at constant velocity $U_{0}$. The flow is steady, incompressible, fully developed, and unidirectional ($v = w = 0$).

### Step 1: Apply Continuity

$$
\frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} + \frac{\partial w}{\partial z} = 0 \implies v = 0 \text{ (assumed)}, \quad \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} = 0
$$

So $u = u(y)$ only.

### Step 2: Reduce the x-Momentum Equation

With $\partial u / \partial x = 0$, all inertial terms vanish:

$$
0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \frac{d^{2} u}{d y^{2}}
$$

### Step 3: Assume Zero Pressure Gradient (typical Couette setup)

$$
\frac{dp}{dx} = 0 \implies \frac{d^{2} u}{d y^{2}} = 0
$$

### Step 4: Integrate Twice and Apply Boundary Conditions

Integrating once: $\dfrac{du}{dy} = C_{1}$

Integrating again: $u = C_{1} y + C_{2}$

BC1: $u(0) = 0 \implies C_{2} = 0$

BC2: $u(h) = U_{0} \implies C_{1} = U_{0} / h$

### Step 5: Final Velocity Profile

$$
u(y) = U_{0} \frac{y}{h}
$$

The velocity profile is **linear** in $y$. The shear stress is uniform across the gap:

$$
\tau = \mu \frac{du}{dy} = \mu \frac{U_{0}}{h} = \text{constant}
$$

## 3.5 Exact Analytical Solution 2: Plane Poiseuille Flow

**Problem Setup:** Flow between two fixed parallel plates separated by distance $2h$ (or $h$, depending on convention), driven by an imposed pressure gradient $\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x} = -G$ (where $G > 0$).

### Step 1: Simplifications

Same as Couette: steady, incompressible, fully developed, unidirectional. So $\dfrac{\partial^{2} u}{\partial y^{2}} \neq 0$, and the reduced equation is:

$$
0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial x} + \mu \frac{d^{2} u}{d y^{2}}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute the Pressure Gradient

$$
\mu \frac{d^{2} u}{d y^{2}} = \frac{\partial p}{\partial x} = -G
$$

$$
\frac{d^{2} u}{d y^{2}} = -\frac{G}{\mu}
$$

### Step 3: Integrate Twice

$$
u(y) = -\frac{G}{2\mu} y^{2} + C_{1} y + C_{2}
$$

### Step 4: Apply No-Slip BCs

BC1: $u(-h) = 0$ and BC2: $u(+h) = 0$. By symmetry, $C_{1} = 0$.

From BC1:

$$
0 = -\frac{G}{2\mu} h^{2} + C_{2} \implies C_{2} = \frac{G h^{2}}{2\mu}
$$

### Step 5: Final Parabolic Profile

$$
u(y) = \frac{G}{2\mu} (h^{2} - y^{2})
$$

Maximum velocity at center ($y = 0$):

$$
u_{\max} = \frac{G h^{2}}{2\mu}
$$

This is a **parabolic** profile, characteristic of pressure-driven viscous flow. $\blacksquare$

## 3.6 Exact Analytical Solution 3: Hagen-Poiseuille Flow (Flow in a Pipe)

**Problem Setup:** Steady, laminar, incompressible, axisymmetric flow in a circular pipe of radius $R$ driven by pressure gradient.

### Step 1: Cylindrical Coordinates

By axisymmetry, $u_{\theta} = 0$, $\partial / \partial \theta = 0$. Assume $u_{z} = u_{z}(r)$.

### Step 2: Continuity

$$
\frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial (r u_{r})}{\partial r} + \frac{\partial u_{z}}{\partial z} = 0
$$

Since $u_{z}$ depends only on $r$, $\partial u_{z} / \partial z = 0$, hence $r u_{r} = \text{const}$. With $u_{r} = 0$ at the wall (no-slip), we have $u_{r} = 0$ everywhere.

### Step 3: z-Momentum Equation

$$
0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} + \mu \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{du_{z}}{dr} \right)
$$

Let $\dfrac{dp}{dz} = -G$ (constant):

$$
\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{du_{z}}{dr} \right) = -\frac{G}{\mu}
$$

### Step 4: Integrate

$$
r \frac{du_{z}}{dr} = -\frac{G r^{2}}{2\mu} + C_{1}
$$

$$
\frac{du_{z}}{dr} = -\frac{G r}{2\mu} + \frac{C_{1}}{r}
$$

The velocity must be finite at $r = 0$, so $C_{1} = 0$.

$$
u_{z} = -\frac{G r^{2}}{4\mu} + C_{2}
$$

### Step 5: Apply No-Slip at Wall

$u_{z}(R) = 0 \implies C_{2} = \dfrac{G R^{2}}{4\mu}$

### Step 6: Final Profile

$$
u_{z}(r) = \frac{G}{4\mu} (R^{2} - r^{2})
$$

Maximum velocity at centerline ($r = 0$):

$$
u_{\max} = \frac{G R^{2}}{4\mu}
$$

### Step 7: Volumetric Flow Rate

Integrate $u_{z}$ over the pipe cross-section:

$$
Q = \int_{0}^{R} u_{z}(r) \, (2 \pi r) \, dr = \frac{\pi G R^{4}}{8 \mu}
$$

This is the famous **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** — the volumetric flow rate is proportional to the **fourth power** of the radius. Halving the pipe diameter reduces flow to 1/16. This is why plumbers always prefer larger pipes for high-flow systems.

## 3.7 Stokes Flow (Creeping Flow) — $Re \ll 1$

When inertial forces are negligible compared to viscous forces ($Re \to 0$), the convective term drops out:

$$
0 = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \mathbf{V} + \rho \mathbf{g}
$$

This **linear** PDE admits the famous **Stokes' solution for a sphere** (e.g., a falling raindrop in still air at low $Re$):

$$
\mathbf{V}(r, \theta) = \frac{3 R U_{0}}{4 r} (1 - e^{-\lambda(r-R)}) \sin\theta \, \hat{\theta} - U_{0} \cos\theta \, \hat{r}
$$

The **drag force** on a sphere in Stokes flow is:

$$
F_{D} = 6 \pi \mu R U_{0}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Stokes flow is *the* classic example of an analytically tractable limit. Always mention the assumption $Re \ll 1$ and the resulting linearity, which is what makes the closed-form solution possible.

## 3.8 Why Closed-Form Solutions Are Rare — The Nonlinearity Bottleneck

The convective acceleration term $\mathbf{V} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{V}$ is the root cause of turbulence. In the **energy cascade** picture:

1. Large-scale eddies contain most of the kinetic energy.
2. The nonlinear term transfers energy to smaller and smaller eddies (Richardson cascade).
3. At the Kolmogorov scale $\eta = ( \nu^{3} / \varepsilon )^{1/4}$ (where $\varepsilon$ is dissipation rate), viscous diffusion $\nu \nabla^{2} \mathbf{V}$ finally dissipates the energy as heat.

This is why **no general analytical solution** to the full 3D N-S equations exists — and why CFD (numerical methods) is the only path forward for practical engineering problems.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification of Solution Strategies for the Navier-Stokes Equations

The following Mermaid diagram maps the entire ecosystem of methods used to solve the N-S equations. This is the "decision tree" a CFD engineer follows when tackling a new problem.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: Navier Stokes Equation] --> A1{Steady or Unsteady?}
    A1 -->|Steady| A2{Special Symmetry Available?}
    A1 -->|Unsteady| A3{Re Number Regime?}

    A2 -->|Yes 1D Unidirectional| B1[Analytical Closed Form]
    A2 -->|Yes Stokes Re less than 1| B2[Analytical Stokes Solution]
    A2 -->|No General Geometry| B3[Numerical Required]

    A3 -->|Re less than 1 Creeping| C1[Linear N S Solvable]
    A3 -->|Moderate Re Laminar| C2[Direct Numerical Simulation DNS]
    A3 -->|High Re Turbulent| C4{Compute Budget?}

    C4 -->|High| D1[DNS Full Resolution]
    C4 -->|Medium| D2[Large Eddy Simulation LES]
    C4 -->|Low| D3[RANS Turbulence Models]

    B3 --> E1{Discretization Scheme?}
    E1 -->|Finite Difference FDM| F1[Structured Grid Solver]
    E1 -->|Finite Volume FVM| F2[Conservative Cell Centered]
    E1 -->|Finite Element FEM| F3[Unstructured Mesh]

    D3 --> G1[Standard k epsilon]
    D3 --> G2[k omega SST]
    D3 --> G3[Spalart Allmaras]
    D3 --> G4[Reynolds Stress Model RSM]

    B1 --> H1[Couette Flow]
    B1 --> H2[Poiseuille Flow]
    B1 --> H3[Hagen Poiseuille Pipe]
    B1 --> H4[Stokes First Problem]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Start at the top. For any N-S problem, first determine if the flow is steady or unsteady, what the Reynolds number is, and what geometric symmetry exists. Only after exhausting analytical possibilities do you commit to a numerical method.

## 4.2 Mathematical Forms of the Navier-Stokes Equations — Transformation Map

The same physical equation can be cast in multiple equivalent mathematical forms, each suited to a different numerical method.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N0[Incompressible Newtonian N S] --> N1[Conservation Form]
    N0 --> N2[Convective Advective Form]
    N0 --> N3[Vorticity Stream Function 2D]
    N0 --> N4[Velocity Pressure Primitive]
    N0 --> N5[Dimensionless Form]

    N1 --> P1[Used in FVM for shock capturing]
    N2 --> P2[Used in FDM and FVM clear physics]
    N3 --> P3[Pressure eliminated 2D incompressible]
    N4 --> P4[Direct solve of u v w p]
    N5 --> P5[Similarity and Re scaling]

    P3 --> Q1[Combined 4th order biharmonic for psi]
    P5 --> Q2[Wind tunnel model testing]
```

## 4.3 Force Balance Schematic on an Infinitesimal Fluid Element

The following diagram illustrates the surface and body forces acting on a differential fluid cube — the geometric basis of the N-S derivation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    E0[Infinitesimal Fluid Cube dx dy dz] --> E1[Surface Forces]
    E0 --> E2[Body Forces]

    E1 --> E3[Normal Stresses sigma xx sigma yy sigma zz]
    E1 --> E4[Shear Stresses tau xy tau xz tau yz]

    E3 --> F1[Pressure Contribution minus grad p]
    E4 --> F2[Viscous Contribution mu Laplacian V]

    E2 --> F3[Gravity rho times g]
    E2 --> F4[Other Body Forces EM Centrifugal]

    F1 --> G0[Sum Equals rho times D V Dt]
    F2 --> G0
    F3 --> G0
    F4 --> G0

    G0 --> H0[Cauchy Momentum Equation]
    H0 --> I0[Substitute Newtonian Constitutive Law]
    I0 --> J0[Final Navier Stokes Equation]
```

## 4.4 Physical Significance of Reynolds Number Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[Reynolds Number Re] --> R1{Re less than 1}
    R0 --> R2{1 less than Re less than 2300}
    R0 --> R3{2300 less than Re less than 4000}
    R0 --> R4{Re greater than 4000}

    R1 --> S1[Stokes Creeping Flow]
    S1 --> S2[Smooth Streamline Motion]
    S2 --> S3[Linear N S Dominant Viscosity]

    R2 --> S4[Laminar Flow]
    S4 --> S5[Orderly Parallel Streamlines]
    S5 --> S6[Couette Poiseuille Solutions Valid]

    R3 --> S7[Transitional Regime]
    S7 --> S8[Intermittent Turbulent Bursts]
    S8 --> S9[Unpredictable Analytical methods fail]

    R4 --> S10[Fully Turbulent Flow]
    S10 --> S11[Chaotic 3D Eddies]
    S11 --> S12[RANS LES DNS Required]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Material Derivative:** Many students write $\partial u / \partial t$ instead of the full $D u / D t$ in the N-S equation. The convective term $\mathbf{V} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{V}$ **must** be present — it is the source of nonlinearity and is worth **at least 2 marks** in any derivation.
> 2. **Confusing the Stream Function and Velocity Potential:** Stream function $\psi$ applies to incompressible 2D flow. Velocity potential $\phi$ applies to *inviscid, irrotational* flow. Do not interchange them.
> 3. **Sign of Pressure Gradient:** A *positive* $dp/dx$ decelerates the flow (pushing backward). A *negative* $dp/dx$ accelerates it. The Couette/Poiseuille problems hinge on this sign.
> 4. **No-Slip vs. No-Penetration:** At a solid wall, BOTH $u_{wall} = u_{fluid}$ AND the normal component of velocity is zero. State both in BC answers.
> 5. **Reynolds Number Units:** $Re$ is **dimensionless**. Always quote the formula $Re = \rho U L / \mu$ and verify dimensional cancellation.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Bank]**

**State and explain the physical significance of each term in the Navier-Stokes equation for an incompressible Newtonian fluid.**

**Model Answer:**

The Navier-Stokes equation in vector form for an incompressible Newtonian fluid is:

$$
\rho \left( \frac{\partial \mathbf{V}}{\partial t} + \mathbf{V} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \mathbf{V} + \rho \mathbf{g}
$$

**Physical significance of each term:**

- **LHS — $\rho \dfrac{\partial \mathbf{V}}{\partial t}$:** Local (unsteady) acceleration. Represents the rate of change of momentum at a fixed point in space.

- **LHS — $\rho (\mathbf{V} \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{V}$:** Convective acceleration. Represents the transport of momentum by bulk fluid motion. This is the **nonlinear** term responsible for turbulence.

- **RHS — $-\nabla p$:** Pressure force per unit volume. Drives the fluid from high-pressure to low-pressure regions.

- **RHS — $\mu \nabla^{2} \mathbf{V}$:** Viscous force per unit volume. Represents momentum diffusion due to internal friction. Vanishes in inviscid (Euler) flow.

- **RHS — $\rho \mathbf{g}$:** Body force per unit volume (e.g., gravity). Acts on the fluid mass itself, not on its surface.

Collectively, the LHS represents the inertial (mass × acceleration) term, while the RHS represents the sum of surface and body forces. The equation is essentially **Newton's 2nd law applied to a fluid continuum**. $\blacksquare$

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**What is the Reynolds number? Derive the non-dimensional form of the Navier-Stokes equation and identify the dimensionless groups that emerge.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Reynolds number** is a dimensionless quantity that represents the ratio of inertial forces to viscous forces in a fluid flow:

$$
Re = \frac{\rho U L}{\mu} = \frac{U L}{\nu}
$$

where $U$ is a characteristic velocity, $L$ is a characteristic length, and $\nu$ is the kinematic viscosity.

**Derivation outline:**

Introduce dimensionless variables $\tilde{x} = x/L$, $\tilde{\mathbf{V}} = \mathbf{V}/U$, $\tilde{t} = tU/L$, $\tilde{p} = p / (\rho U^{2})$. Substituting into the N-S equation yields:

$$
\frac{D \tilde{\mathbf{V}}}{D \tilde{t}} = -\nabla \tilde{p} + \frac{1}{Re} \tilde{\nabla}^{2} \tilde{\mathbf{V}} + \frac{1}{Fr^{2}} \tilde{\mathbf{g}}
$$

**Dimensionless groups:**

- **Reynolds Number ($Re$):** Ratio of inertial to viscous forces.
- **Froude Number ($Fr = U / \sqrt{gL}$):** Ratio of inertial to gravitational forces (appears when $\mathbf{g}$ is non-negligible).
- **Euler Number ($Eu = p / (\rho U^{2})$):** Ratio of pressure forces to inertial forces.

Dynamic similarity between a model and prototype is guaranteed only if all relevant dimensionless groups match. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (Choice 1) — **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Q]**

**(a)** Starting from the Cauchy momentum equation, derive the Navier-Stokes equation for a Newtonian, incompressible fluid. State clearly all assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the vorticity transport equation from the Navier-Stokes equation. Explain the physical significance of each term. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a):

**Step 1: Cauchy Momentum Equation**

The general equation of motion for a continuum is:

$$
\rho \frac{D\mathbf{V}}{Dt} = \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma} + \rho \mathbf{g}
$$

where $\boldsymbol{\sigma}$ is the Cauchy stress tensor.

**Step 2: Decompose Stress into Pressure and Viscous Parts**

$$
\boldsymbol{\sigma} = -p \mathbf{I} + \boldsymbol{\tau}
$$

Substituting:

$$
\rho \frac{D\mathbf{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau} + \rho \mathbf{g}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the Newtonian Constitutive Relation**

For a Newtonian fluid:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mu \left[ (\nabla \mathbf{V} + (\nabla \mathbf{V})^{T}) - \frac{2}{3}(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{V}) \mathbf{I} \right]
$$

**Step 4: Incompressibility Assumption**

For an incompressible fluid, $\nabla \cdot \mathbf{V} = 0$, so the second term vanishes:

$$
\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mu (\nabla \mathbf{V} + (\nabla \mathbf{V})^{T})
$$

Taking the divergence:

$$
\nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\tau} = \mu \nabla^{2} \mathbf{V}
$$

**Step 5: Final N-S Equation**

$$
\rho \left( \frac{\partial \mathbf{V}}{\partial t} + \mathbf{V} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{V} \right) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \mathbf{V} + \rho \mathbf{g}
$$

**Assumptions stated:**

1. The fluid is a continuum.
2. The fluid is Newtonian (linear stress–strain-rate relationship).
3. The fluid is incompressible (constant $\rho$).
4. Viscosity $\mu$ is constant.
5. Body force is conservative (gravity only).

$\blacksquare$

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b):

**Step 1: Take the Curl**

Apply $\nabla \times$ to both sides of the N-S momentum equation:

$$
\nabla \times \left[ \rho \frac{D\mathbf{V}}{Dt} \right] = \nabla \times (-\nabla p) + \nabla \times (\mu \nabla^{2} \mathbf{V}) + \nabla \times (\rho \mathbf{g})
$$

**Step 2: Simplify Right-Hand Side**

- $\nabla \times (\nabla p) = 0$ (curl of gradient is identically zero).
- $\nabla \times (\mu \nabla^{2} \mathbf{V}) = \mu \nabla^{2} (\nabla \times \mathbf{V}) = \mu \nabla^{2} \boldsymbol{\omega}$ (because $\nabla \times$ and $\nabla^{2}$ commute for constant $\mu$).
- $\nabla \times (\rho \mathbf{g}) = 0$ (since gravity is a conservative field: $\mathbf{g} = -\nabla \phi$).

**Step 3: Expand the Left-Hand Side**

Using the vector identity $\nabla \times (\mathbf{V} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{V}) = (\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{V} - (\mathbf{V} \cdot \nabla) \boldsymbol{\omega} + \boldsymbol{\omega} (\nabla \cdot \mathbf{V}) - \mathbf{V} (\nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\omega})$:

For incompressible flow ($\nabla \cdot \mathbf{V} = 0$) and noting $\nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\omega} = 0$ (divergence of a curl is always zero):

$$
\nabla \times \left( \frac{D\mathbf{V}}{Dt} \right) = \frac{D\boldsymbol{\omega}}{Dt} - (\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{V}
$$

**Step 4: Final Vorticity Transport Equation**

$$
\frac{D\boldsymbol{\omega}}{Dt} = (\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{V} + \nu \nabla^{2} \boldsymbol{\omega}
$$

**Physical significance of each term:**

- $\dfrac{D\boldsymbol{\omega}}{Dt}$: Rate of change of vorticity following a fluid parcel.
- $(\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{V}$: **Vortex stretching** — a 3D effect in which velocity gradients along the vortex axis stretch and intensify the vortex (think of a spinning ice skater pulling in their arms).
- $\nu \nabla^{2} \boldsymbol{\omega}$: **Viscous diffusion of vorticity** — vorticity generated at walls diffuses into the flow. This is the only term that *destroys* vorticity.

$\blacksquare$

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** For steady, incompressible, laminar flow between two parallel plates separated by distance $h$ (lower plate at $y = 0$, upper plate at $y = h$), derive the velocity profile when (i) the upper plate moves at velocity $U_0$ with zero pressure gradient, and (ii) a constant pressure gradient $dp/dx = -G$ is applied with both plates stationary. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For steady, laminar, fully developed flow in a circular pipe of radius $R$, driven by a constant pressure gradient $dp/dz = -G$, derive the velocity profile and the volumetric flow rate. Show that the average velocity is half the maximum velocity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a):

**Assumptions for both sub-cases:**

- Steady: $\partial / \partial t = 0$
- Incompressible: $\rho = \text{const}$
- Laminar, fully developed: $\partial u / \partial x = 0$
- Unidirectional: $v = w = 0$, so $u = u(y)$ only

**Continuity equation reduces to:** $\partial u / \partial x = 0$ ✓ (consistent)

**Reduced x-momentum equation:**

$$
0 = -\frac{dp}{dx} + \mu \frac{d^{2} u}{d y^{2}}
$$

**Case (i) — Plane Couette Flow:**

Setting $dp/dx = 0$:

$$
\frac{d^{2} u}{d y^{2}} = 0 \implies u = C_1 y + C_2
$$

Apply no-slip BCs: $u(0) = 0 \implies C_2 = 0$; $u(h) = U_0 \implies C_1 = U_0 / h$.

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{u(y) = U_0 \frac{y}{h}} \quad \text{(Linear profile)}
$$

The wall shear stress is uniform: $\tau_w = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy} = \dfrac{\mu U_0}{h}$.

---

**Case (ii) — Plane Poiseuille Flow:**

Setting $dp/dx = -G$ (so $G > 0$ for flow in $+x$ direction):

$$
\frac{d^{2} u}{d y^{2}} = -\frac{G}{\mu} \implies u = -\frac{G}{2\mu} y^{2} + C_1 y + C_2
$$

Apply no-slip BCs: $u(0) = 0 \implies C_2 = 0$; $u(h) = 0 \implies C_1 = G h / (2\mu)$.

**Result:**

$$
\boxed{u(y) = \frac{G}{2\mu} (hy - y^{2}) = \frac{G}{2\mu} y (h - y)} \quad \text{(Parabolic profile)}
$$

Maximum velocity at $y = h/2$:

$$
u_{\max} = \frac{G h^{2}}{8 \mu}
$$

The shear stress is linear: $\tau(y) = \mu \dfrac{du}{dy} = \dfrac{G}{2} (h - 2y)$. $\blacksquare$

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b):

**Assumptions:**

- Steady, laminar, incompressible
- Fully developed: $\partial u_z / \partial z = 0$
- Axisymmetric: $\partial / \partial \theta = 0$, $u_\theta = 0$
- Unidirectional axial flow: $u_r = 0$, $u_z = u_z(r)$

**Continuity in cylindrical coordinates:**

$$
\frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial (r u_r)}{\partial r} + \frac{\partial u_z}{\partial z} = 0
$$

Since $u_z$ is independent of $z$ and $u_r = 0$, this is satisfied.

**z-Momentum equation in cylindrical coordinates:**

$$
0 = -\frac{\partial p}{\partial z} + \mu \frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{d u_z}{d r} \right)
$$

**Substitute $dp/dz = -G$:**

$$
\frac{1}{r} \frac{d}{dr} \left( r \frac{d u_z}{d r} \right) = -\frac{G}{\mu}
$$

**Integrate once:**

$$
r \frac{du_z}{dr} = -\frac{G r^{2}}{2\mu} + C_1
$$

$$
\frac{du_z}{dr} = -\frac{G r}{2\mu} + \frac{C_1}{r}
$$

**Regularity at $r = 0$ requires finite velocity** $\implies C_1 = 0$ (otherwise $du_z/dr \to \infty$).

**Integrate again:**

$$
u_z(r) = -\frac{G r^{2}}{4\mu} + C_2
$$

**Apply no-slip at the wall:** $u_z(R) = 0$:

$$
C_2 = \frac{G R^{2}}{4\mu}
$$

**Final velocity profile:**

$$
\boxed{u_z(r) = \frac{G}{4\mu} (R^{2} - r^{2})}
$$

**Maximum velocity at centerline ($r = 0$):**

$$
u_{\max} = \frac{G R^{2}}{4\mu}
$$

**Volumetric flow rate:**

$$
Q = \int_{0}^{R} u_z(r) \, (2\pi r) \, dr = \frac{\pi G}{2\mu} \int_{0}^{R} (R^{2} - r^{2}) r \, dr
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi G}{2\mu} \left[ \frac{R^{2} r^{2}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R} = \frac{\pi G}{2\mu} \cdot \frac{R^{4}}{4} = \frac{\pi G R^{4}}{8 \mu}
$$

**Average velocity:**

$$
u_{\text{avg}} = \frac{Q}{\pi R^{2}} = \frac{G R^{2}}{8\mu} = \frac{1}{2} u_{\max}
$$

Therefore, the average velocity is **exactly half** the maximum (centerline) velocity. $\blacksquare$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Module 3 — N-S Equations):**

- ✅ **N-S Equation (Vector Form):** $\rho (D\mathbf{V}/Dt) = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^{2} \mathbf{V} + \rho \mathbf{g}$
- ✅ **N-S is Newton's 2nd Law** applied to a fluid continuum.
- ✅ **LHS:** Inertial terms (local + convective). **RHS:** Pressure + viscous + body forces.
- ✅ **Nonlinear** because of $\mathbf{V} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{V}$ — no general closed-form solution exists for the full 3D equations.
- ✅ **Second-order** PDE — needs 2 boundary/initial conditions per variable.
- ✅ **Incompressible N-S** is **elliptic** (steady) or **parabolic** (unsteady).
- ✅ **Derivation Route:** Cauchy Momentum $\rightarrow$ Newtonian Constitutive Law $\rightarrow$ Incompressibility $\rightarrow$ N-S.
- ✅ **Cauchy Momentum:** $\rho (D\mathbf{V}/Dt) = \nabla \cdot \boldsymbol{\sigma} + \rho \mathbf{g}$ — most general, valid for *any* continuum.
- ✅ **Newtonian Constitutive Law:** $\boldsymbol{\tau} = \mu [(\nabla \mathbf{V} + (\nabla \mathbf{V})^{T}) - \frac{2}{3}(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{V})\mathbf{I}]$
- ✅ **Reynolds Number:** $Re = \rho U L / \mu = U L / \nu$ — governs the flow regime.
- ✅ **Vorticity Transport Equation:** $\dfrac{D\boldsymbol{\omega}}{Dt} = (\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \nabla) \mathbf{V} + \nu \nabla^{2} \boldsymbol{\omega}$ — vortex stretching + viscous diffusion.
- ✅ **Stream Function (2D):** $u = \partial \psi / \partial y$, $v = -\partial \psi / \partial x$ — automatically satisfies continuity.
- ✅ **Couette Flow:** $u(y) = U_0 (y/h)$ — **linear** profile, $\tau$ uniform.
- ✅ **Poiseuille Flow (Parallel Plates):** $u(y) = (G/2\mu)(hy - y^2)$ — **parabolic** profile.
- ✅ **Hagen-Poiseuille Flow (Pipe):** $u(r) = (G/4\mu)(R^2 - r^2)$, $Q = \pi G R^4 / 8\mu$, $u_{\text{avg}} = u_{\max} / 2$.
- ✅ **Stokes Flow ($Re \ll 1$):** Linear N-S; sphere drag $F_D = 6 \pi \mu R U_0$.
- ✅ **Dimensional Analysis Groups to Remember:** Reynolds ($Re$), Froude ($Fr$), Euler ($Eu$), Mach ($Ma$), Strouhal ($St$).
- ✅ **Common Mistakes:** Forgetting convective term, mixing stream function and velocity potential, sign errors in $dp/dx$, missing no-slip + no-penetration BCs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For any N-S derivation question, always (1) state your assumptions explicitly at the top, (2) reduce the equations using the simplifications, (3) integrate step-by-step, and (4) apply boundary conditions with clear labels. Examiners reward *clarity of logical flow* as much as the final answer.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Grid Arrangement - Collocated and Staggered Grid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Grid Arrangement - Collocated and Staggered Grid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Grid Arrangement in CFD?

In the **Finite Volume Method (FVM)**, the computational domain is divided into a finite number of discrete control volumes (cells). A **grid arrangement** specifies the **geometric location at which the flow variables** (velocity components $u, v, w$, pressure $p$, temperature $T$, and turbulence quantities) **are stored** during the numerical solution. The choice between a *collocated* and a *staggered* arrangement is one of the most fundamental decisions in CFD algorithm design, because it directly dictates how the **pressure–velocity coupling** is enforced.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** *Module 3* of Computational Fluid Dynamics explicitly mandates the study of both grid arrangements, the **pressure–velocity coupling problem** (often called the *checkerboard* or *zig-zag* pressure problem), and the **Rhie–Chow interpolation** technique used to cure this defect on collocated grids.

---

### 1.2 Collocated Grid (Co-located / Non-staggered Grid)

**Formal Definition:**
A *collocated grid* is a numerical arrangement in which **all flow variables** — the three velocity components $(u, v, w)$, pressure $(p)$, and any scalar $(\phi)$ — are **stored at the same spatial location**, typically the **geometric center of each control volume (CV)**.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Single Counter Analogy:**
Imagine a bank branch with **one single counter** where a single employee handles *all* tasks — account opening, cash withdrawal, loan enquiry, complaint registration, etc. While this is **simple to manage** (one queue, one infrastructure, one database), the employee is sometimes forced to *approximate* the answer to a query because the necessary data is not co-located on the same desk. In the same way, a collocated grid is **easy to program** (one node stores every variable), but the discretization of the pressure gradient acting on a face and the velocity divergence at the cell center are **decoupled**, leading to the infamous *checkerboard* pressure field.

> [!NOTE]
> In a collocated grid, to compute the mass flux through a face, the code must **interpolate** the velocity from the cell center to the face. The pressure gradient is also evaluated using *interpolated* values.

---

### 1.3 Staggered Grid

**Formal Definition:**
A *staggered grid* is a numerical arrangement in which the **velocity components are stored at the cell faces**, while the **pressure and all other scalar variables (temperature, turbulence quantities) are stored at the cell centers**. The face-normal velocity component on a particular face lies exactly on that face, while the tangential velocities lie on the *perpendicular* faces.

- $u$-velocity is stored on the **vertical (East/West)** faces of the cell.
- $v$-velocity is stored on the **horizontal (North/South)** faces of the cell.
- $w$-velocity (in 3-D) is stored on the **front/back** faces.
- $p, T, k, \varepsilon$ are stored at the **cell centers**.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Departmental Analogy:**
Now imagine a well-run government office where the **cashier sits at the cash window** (where money is *counted*), the **clerk sits at the file window** (where records are *maintained*), and the **manager sits at the central desk** (where decisions are *made*). Each person is positioned exactly where their work physically happens. This eliminates any need to *pass* the same file back and forth. In the same way, a staggered grid places each velocity component **on the face through which that very velocity component flows**, eliminating the need for interpolation and naturally satisfying the **mass–momentum coupling**.

> [!NOTE]
> The staggered grid was popularized by **Harlow and Welch (1965)** in the **Marker-and-Cell (MAC)** method and later formally adopted by **Patankar & Spalding (1972)** in the landmark **SIMPLE** algorithm.

---

### 1.4 Why Does the Choice Matter? The Pressure–Velocity Coupling Issue

The **Navier–Stokes equations** are a coupled system: the momentum equation requires the pressure gradient, while the continuity equation requires the velocity. On any grid, a wrong storage strategy produces **non-physical oscillations** in the pressure field — a pattern resembling a *chessboard*, where neighboring cells have wildly oscillating pressures that nevertheless satisfy the discretized equations. This is the famous **checkerboard (zig-zag) pressure problem**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** The most frequently asked definition in Module 3 is *“What is a checkerboard pressure field and how does a staggered grid prevent it?”* — A complete answer requires linking the **mathematical cause** (decoupled discretizations) with the **physical cure** (geometric staggering of velocities).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Discretized Equations on a Staggered Grid

Consider a 2-D control volume P (centered at node P), with neighbours N, S, E, W. The location of the variables is shown in Section 4 (Mermaid diagram).

The steady-state, incompressible **$u$-momentum** equation discretized over the control volume around the $u$-velocity node $u_e$ (located on the *East face of P*) is:

$$a_e u_e = a_w u_w + a_{nb} \sum u_{nb} + (p_P - p_E)\,A_e + S_u$$

Where:
- $a_e, a_w, a_{nb}$ are the convection–diffusion coefficients.
- $A_e = \Delta y \cdot 1$ is the area of the East face.
- **Crucial point:** The pressure drop $(p_P - p_E)$ acts **naturally** on the $u$-velocity $u_e$ that *sits exactly between* $p_P$ and $p_E$. No interpolation is needed.

Similarly, the $v$-momentum equation at the $v$-velocity node $v_n$ (located on the *North face of P*) contains $(p_P - p_N)\,A_n$ as the pressure force.

The **continuity equation** at the cell center (P) becomes:

$$\dot{m}_e - \dot{m}_w + \dot{m}_n - \dot{m}_s = 0$$

$$(\rho u_e A_e) - (\rho u_w A_w) + (\rho v_n A_n) - (\rho v_s A_s) = 0$$

Because each mass flux involves a velocity that *sits exactly on the face through which it flows*, the continuity equation is automatically coupled to the momentum equations, and the **checkerboard pressure field is structurally impossible**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why staggered grids avoid checkerboard pressure:**
> Suppose a *zig-zag* pressure field $p_{i,j} = (-1)^{i+j}$ exists. The pressure difference between two adjacent cells in the same row is $(p_P - p_E) = (-1)^{i+j} - (-1)^{i+j+1} = \pm 2$, which is **large**. The same large force will accelerate the velocity $u_e$ between them, *destroying* the zig-zag pattern within a few iterations. The physics itself eliminates the artefact.

---

### 2.2 Discretized Equations on a Collocated Grid

In a collocated grid, all variables are at the cell centers. The discretized $u$-momentum equation at node P reads:

$$a_P u_P = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb} - \left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right)_P \Delta V + S_u$$

The pressure gradient $(\partial p / \partial x)_P$ must be **approximated** using neighbouring cell-center pressures, typically:

$$\left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right)_P \approx \frac{p_E - p_W}{2\,\Delta x}$$

The continuity equation, when discretized at the cell center, must also use **interpolated face velocities**:

$$\dot{m}_e = \rho A_e \frac{u_P + u_E}{2}, \quad \dot{m}_w = \rho A_w \frac{u_W + u_P}{2}, \quad \text{etc.}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The Failure Mode on Collocated Grids:**
> If the pressure field zig-zags as $p_{i,j} = (-1)^{i+j}$, then $p_E - p_W = (-1)^{i+j+1} - (-1)^{i+j-1} = 0$. The pressure gradient evaluated at the cell center is **identically zero**, so the zig-zag pressure field produces **no driving force** on the velocity. The artificial field persists. This is the **mathematical root cause** of the checkerboard problem on collocated grids.

### 2.3 The Rhie–Chow Interpolation (The Collocated-Grid Cure)

To fix the defect on a collocated grid, **Rhie and Chow (1983)** proposed that the *face* mass flux used in the continuity equation be computed using a **momentum-weighted interpolation** of the pressure gradient, *not* a simple linear interpolation of $u$:

$$\dot{m}_e = \overline{\dot{m}}_e - D_e\,(p_E - p_P)$$

where $\overline{\dot{m}}_e$ is a *convection-interpolated* flux and $D_e$ is a coefficient derived from the momentum equation coefficients. This adds a *numerical* pressure–velocity coupling at the face, structurally mimicking the staggered grid.

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Equation | Expression | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Collocated $u$-momentum (cell P) | $a_P u_P = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb} - \left(\dfrac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right)_P \Delta V + S_u$ | Pressure gradient at cell center; needs interpolation. |
| 2 | Staggered $u$-momentum (node $u_e$) | $a_e u_e = a_w u_w + \sum a_{nb} u_{nb} + (p_P - p_E)A_e + S_u$ | No interpolation of $p$ required. |
| 3 | Staggered $v$-momentum (node $v_n$) | $a_n v_n = a_s v_s + \sum a_{nb} v_{nb} + (p_P - p_N)A_n + S_v$ | Pressure drop natural across face. |
| 4 | Continuity (staggered, P) | $\rho(u_e A_e - u_w A_w + v_n A_n - v_s A_s) = 0$ | Mass fluxes on faces directly. |
| 5 | Continuity (collocated, P) | $\rho\left(\dfrac{u_P+u_E}{2}A_e - \dfrac{u_W+u_P}{2}A_w + \ldots\right) = 0$ | Velocities must be interpolated to faces. |
| 6 | Rhie–Chow face flux | $\dot{m}_e = \overline{\dot{m}}_e - D_e (p_E - p_P)$ | Restores pressure–velocity coupling. |
| 7 | Checkerboard pressure trigger | $p_{i,j}=(-1)^{i+j}$ | Zero cell-centered $\partial p/\partial x$ on collocated grid. |
| 8 | SIMPLE algorithm | $p^* \rightarrow u^*,v^* \rightarrow p' \rightarrow p = p^* + p'$ | Works on both grids, but coupling different. |
| 9 | Pressure correction (staggered) | $a_P p'_P = a_E p'_E + a_W p'_W + a_N p'_N + a_S p'_S + b$ | $b$ is the **mass-source** from $u^*, v^*$. |
| 10 | Pressure correction (collocated, with Rhie–Chow) | Same form as (9), but face coefficients $D_e$ use Rhie–Chow. | Standard in OpenFOAM, ANSYS Fluent. |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Grid Choice Used | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| General-purpose CFD (OpenFOAM, Fluent) | **Collocated + Rhie–Chow** | Memory-efficient, easy to extend to complex geometries. |
| Classical incompressible flow solvers (Patankar's original SIMPLE) | **Staggered** | Guaranteed stability, no Rhie–Chow needed. |
| Compressible flow (high-Mach) | **Collocated** | Density-based algorithms avoid incompressible pressure-coupling issue. |
| Porous-media / multi-phase flows | **Collocated** | Many scalars; face-located interpolation trivial. |
| Teaching/research pedagogy | **Staggered** | Conceptually clean demonstration of coupling. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Staggered-Grid Pressure-Correction Equation

This derivation is the **backbone of the SIMPLE algorithm** and is a guaranteed high-yield derivation for KTU ESE Module 3.

**Step 1 — Start from the Incompressible Continuity Equation**

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = \frac{\partial u}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial v}{\partial y} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Integrate over a 2-D control volume P (the cell-center CV) and apply the Divergence Theorem**

$$\int_{CV} \nabla \cdot \vec{V}\, dV = \oint_{A} \vec{V} \cdot \hat{n}\, dA = 0$$

For a rectangular cell of width $\Delta x$ and height $\Delta y$ with East, West, North, South faces:

$$(u A)_e - (u A)_w + (v A)_n - (v A)_s = 0$$

Substituting face areas $A_e = A_w = \Delta y$ and $A_n = A_s = \Delta x$:

$$u_e \Delta y - u_w \Delta y + v_n \Delta x - v_s \Delta x = 0 \quad \text{...(Eq. A)}$$

**Step 3 — Split velocity and pressure into guessed (∗) and corrected (′) parts**

Assume a pressure field $p^*$ is guessed. Solving the discrete momentum equations gives velocities $u^*, v^*$. We *correct* these using a pressure correction $p'$:

$$p = p^* + p', \quad u = u^* + u', \quad v = v^* + v'$$

**Step 4 — Derive the velocity-correction formula from the discrete $u$-momentum equation**

The discrete $u$-momentum on the staggered grid is:

$$a_e u_e = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb} + (p_P - p_E) A_e + b$$

(The source $b$ contains the non-linear/extra terms.) Similarly for the *starred* (guessed) solution:

$$a_e u_e^* = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb}^* + (p_P^* - p_E^*) A_e + b$$

Subtracting:

$$a_e (u_e - u_e^*) = \sum a_{nb} (u_{nb} - u_{nb}^*) + \left[(p_P - p_E) - (p_P^* - p_E^*)\right] A_e$$

Defining $u_e' = u_e - u_e^*$ and $p' = p - p^*$:

$$a_e u_e' = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb}' + (p_P' - p_E') A_e$$

**Step 5 — Apply the *SIMPLE* simplification (drop $\sum a_{nb} u_{nb}'$)**

In the SIMPLE algorithm, the sum of the neighbour velocity-corrections is neglected, leaving:

$$a_e u_e' \approx (p_P' - p_E') A_e \quad \Rightarrow \quad u_e' = \frac{A_e}{a_e}(p_P' - p_E')$$

Defining $d_e = A_e / a_e$:

$$u_e' = d_e (p_P' - p_E') \quad \text{...(Eq. B)}$$

Similarly:

$$u_w' = d_w (p_W' - p_P'), \quad v_n' = d_n (p_P' - p_N'), \quad v_s' = d_s (p_S' - p_P')$$

**Step 6 — Substitute velocity-corrections into the Continuity Equation (Eq. A)**

The corrected velocity is $u_e = u_e^* + u_e'$. Substituting into Eq. A:

$$(u_e^* + d_e(p_P' - p_E'))\Delta y - (u_w^* + d_w(p_W' - p_P'))\Delta y + (v_n^* + d_n(p_P' - p_N'))\Delta x - (v_s^* + d_s(p_S' - p_P'))\Delta x = 0$$

**Step 7 — Group pressure-correction terms**

Rearranging the pressure terms (all on the left) and the mass-source (on the right):

$$(d_e + d_w)\Delta y\, p_P' + d_n \Delta x\, p_P' + d_s \Delta x\, p_P' = (u_w^* - u_e^*)\Delta y + (v_s^* - v_n^*)\Delta x + d_e \Delta y\, p_E' + d_w \Delta y\, p_W' + d_n \Delta x\, p_N' + d_s \Delta x\, p_S'$$

Defining the LHS coefficient $a_P$:

$$a_P = (d_e + d_w)\Delta y + (d_n + d_s)\Delta x$$

And the neighbour coefficients:

$$a_E = d_e \Delta y, \quad a_W = d_w \Delta y, \quad a_N = d_n \Delta x, \quad a_S = d_s \Delta x$$

The mass-source (right-hand side) is the **continuity residual**:

$$b = (u_w^* - u_e^*)\Delta y + (v_s^* - v_n^*)\Delta x$$

**Step 8 — The Final Pressure-Correction Equation**

$$\boxed{\,a_P\, p_P' = a_E\, p_E' + a_W\, p_W' + a_N\, p_N' + a_S\, p_S' + b\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **discretized Poisson equation for pressure correction** and is the *heart* of the SIMPLE family of algorithms. Once $p'$ is solved, the velocity corrections are obtained from Eq. B and the pressure is updated: $p = p^* + p'$.

---

### 3.2 The Collocated-Grid Continuation

On a collocated grid, the same derivation is followed **but** $d_e$ is no longer a single coefficient from a single momentum equation. The face velocity $u_e$ is built from the *interpolated* values of $u_P$ and $u_E$. When pressure-correction is applied, the standard linear interpolation produces the *deficient* coupling. Hence, the **Rhie–Chow interpolation** replaces the linear interpolation of $u$ with a **momentum-weighted interpolation** that *directly* includes the pressure jump $(p_E - p_P)$, effectively embedding the staggered-grid coupling into the face-flux expression.

The resulting pressure-correction equation has the **same form** as the staggered-grid version, but the coefficients $d_e$ are **assembled from Rhie–Chow fluxes** rather than directly from face-located momentum coefficients.

---

### 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix

| Criterion | Collocated Grid | Staggered Grid |
|---|---|---|
| Storage locations of $u, v, w$ | All at cell centers (P) | $u$ on vertical faces, $v$ on horizontal faces |
| Storage location of $p, T, k, \varepsilon$ | Cell centers (P) | Cell centers (P) |
| Continuity equation mass-flux | $\rho(u_P+u_E)/2 \cdot A_e$ | $\rho\, u_e A_e$ (direct) |
| Pressure gradient in $u$-equation | Interpolated, $\partial p/\partial x \approx (p_E - p_W)/(2\Delta x)$ | Direct, $(p_P - p_E)A_e$ on $u_e$ |
| Checkerboard pressure problem | **Yes**, without Rhie–Chow | **No**, structurally impossible |
| Cure for checkerboard | **Rhie–Chow interpolation** (1983) | Not required |
| Memory requirement | **Lower** (one set of nodes) | **Higher** (up to 3-4× storage for 3-D) |
| Code complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Extension to complex geometries (curvilinear, unstructured) | **Trivial** (same node for all variables) | **Difficult** (faces of polyhedra have varying normals) |
| Compatibility with SIMPLE/SIMPLEC/PISO | Yes (with Rhie–Chow) | Yes (naturally) |
| Modern adoption (OpenFOAM, Fluent, CFX) | **Standard** | Largely abandoned in industry; still used pedagogically |
| Numerical diffusion | Slightly higher (due to interpolation) | Lower (direct face values) |
| Robustness on distorted grids | Excellent | Marginal |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Collocated vs. Staggered Grid Variable Locations

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph COLLOC["COLLOCATED GRID (2-D cell)"]
        direction TB
        CP["Cell center P\n  u, v, p, T\n  ALL AT ONE POINT"]
        CE["E neighbour center:  u, v, p"]
        CW["W neighbour center:  u, v, p"]
        CN["N neighbour center:  u, v, p"]
        CS["S neighbour center:  u, v, p"]
    end
    subgraph STAGG["STAGGERED GRID (2-D cell)"]
        direction TB
        SP["Cell center P\n  p, T, k, eps\n  NO VELOCITY"]
        SUe["u_e on East face\n  u-component only"]
        SUw["u_w on West face\n  u-component only"]
        SVn["v_n on North face\n  v-component only"]
        SVs["v_s on South face\n  v-component only"]
        SPE["E neighbour center: p"]
        SPW["W neighbour centre: p"]
        SPN["N neighbour centre: p"]
        SPS["S neighbour centre: p"]
    end
    COLLOC -. "All variables share node" .- CP
    STAGG -. "Pressure nodes offset\nfrom velocity nodes" .- SP
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Pressure–Velocity Coupling Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Initialize p*, u*, v*"] --> B["Solve u-momentum\nget u*"]
    A --> C["Solve v-momentum\nget v*"]
    B --> D["Compute mass flux\non each face"]
    C --> D
    D --> E{"Grid Type?"}
    E -- Staggered --> F["Face flux uses\ndirect face velocity\nu_e, v_n, v_s, v_w"]
    E -- Collocated --> G["Face flux uses\ninterpolated u\nand Rhie-Chow\npressure gradient"]
    F --> H["Assemble pressure-\ncorrection equation"]
    G --> H
    H --> I["Solve p' (Poisson)"]
    I --> J["Correct velocities\nu = u* + d_e(p_P' - p_E')"]
    I --> K["Correct pressure\np = p* + p'"]
    J --> L{"Converged?"}
    K --> L
    L -- No --> A
    L -- Yes --> M["Output converged\nflow field"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Why the Checkerboard Pressure Persists on a Collocated Grid

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Z["Zig-zag pressure field\np_ij = (-1)^(i+j)"] --> Q["Cell-centred gradient\n∂p/∂x at P uses\np_E - p_W"]
    Q --> R["p_E = -p_W\n=> ∂p/∂x = 0"]
    R --> S["Zero driving force\non velocity u_P"]
    S --> T["Zig-zag persists\n(unchecked)"]
    Y["Zig-zag pressure on\nstaggered grid"] --> X["u_e is between p_P and p_E\n=> p_P - p_E = ±2 (non-zero)"]
    X --> W["Large driving force\non u_e"]
    W --> V["Zig-zag destroyed\nby physical force"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
*What is a collocated grid in CFD? Mention one major drawback of using it for incompressible flow simulation.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A collocated grid is a numerical grid arrangement in which all flow variables — the velocity components $(u, v, w)$, pressure $(p)$ and any scalar $\phi$ — are stored at the **same location**, typically the **cell centre**.
*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for cell centre detail)*

**Drawback:** On a collocated grid, the pressure gradient in the momentum equation and the velocity divergence in the continuity equation are evaluated using **interpolated** values. This decoupling can produce the **checkerboard (zig-zag) pressure field**, a non-physical oscillating pressure pattern that satisfies the discretized equations but contains no physical driving force on the velocity.
*(1 mark for drawback)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
*Explain how the staggered grid eliminates the checkerboard pressure problem.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In a staggered grid, the $u$-velocity is stored on the **vertical (East/West) cell faces**, the $v$-velocity on the **horizontal (North/South) cell faces**, while the pressure remains at the cell centre.
*(1 mark)*

When a hypothetical zig-zag pressure field $p_{i,j} = (-1)^{i+j}$ is assumed, the pressure difference across any face, e.g. $(p_P - p_E)$, becomes a **non-zero** quantity ($\pm 2$). This non-zero pressure jump **directly accelerates** the face-located velocity $u_e$ between $p_P$ and $p_E$, breaking the zig-zag pattern within a few iterations.
*(2 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** *Derive the pressure-correction equation for a 2-D staggered grid using the SIMPLE algorithm. Clearly state the assumptions made. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *With the help of a neat sketch, compare the storage locations of variables in a collocated and a staggered grid. List two advantages and two disadvantages of each arrangement. **(7 marks)***

---

**Solution to Q.A (a):**

**Step 1 — State the Governing Discrete Equations (1 mark)**
The discrete $u$-momentum equation at the $u_e$ node on the East face of cell P is:

$$a_e u_e = a_w u_w + a_{nb}\sum u_{nb} + (p_P - p_E)A_e + b$$

**Step 2 — Introduce Starred and Corrected Variables (1 mark)**

$$p = p^* + p', \quad u_e = u_e^* + u_e'$$

**Step 3 — Derive the Velocity-Correction Formula (2 marks)**
Subtracting the starred equation from the corrected equation and **applying the SIMPLE simplification** (dropping $\sum a_{nb} u_{nb}'$):

$$u_e' = d_e (p_P' - p_E'), \quad \text{where} \quad d_e = A_e / a_e$$

Similarly $u_w' = d_w(p_W' - p_P')$, $v_n' = d_n(p_P' - p_N')$, $v_s' = d_s(p_S' - p_P')$.

**Step 4 — Substitute into the Continuity Equation (2 marks)**

$$(u_e^* + d_e(p_P' - p_E'))\Delta y - (u_w^* + d_w(p_W' - p_P'))\Delta y + (v_n^* + d_n(p_P' - p_N'))\Delta x - (v_s^* + d_s(p_S' - p_P'))\Delta x = 0$$

**Step 5 — Group to obtain the Pressure-Correction Equation (1 mark)**

$$a_P p_P' = a_E p_E' + a_W p_W' + a_N p_N' + a_S p_S' + b$$

where $a_P = (d_e + d_w)\Delta y + (d_n + d_s)\Delta x$, $a_E = d_e \Delta y$, $a_W = d_w \Delta y$, $a_N = d_n \Delta x$, $a_S = d_s \Delta x$, and $b = (u_w^* - u_e^*)\Delta y + (v_s^* - v_n^*)\Delta x$.

---

**Solution to Q.A (b):**

**Sketch (3 marks):**

```
                STAGGERED GRID                   COLLOCATED GRID
       N                                N
       •--------v_n--------•              •--------•--------•
       |         |         |              |        |        |
       |         ↑         |              |        |        |
      u_w    P  (p,T)  u_e               u_W   P,u,v,p   u_E
       |         |         |              |        |        |
       |         ↓         |              |        |        |
       •--------v_s--------•              •--------•--------•
       S                                S
```

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**

| Aspect | Collocated | Staggered |
|---|---|---|
| Storage | All variables at cell center | $u$ on vertical faces, $v$ on horizontal faces, $p$ at center |
| Memory | Lower (1 set of nodes) | Higher (separate $u, v, p$ storage) |
| Code complexity | Simpler | More complex |
| Checkerboard pressure | Possible (need Rhie–Chow) | Eliminated naturally |
| Extension to 3-D / complex geometry | Easy | Difficult |
| Numerical diffusion | Slightly higher | Lower |

**Two advantages and disadvantages of each (already covered in the comparative table).**

---

#### **Question B — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 14 Marks]**

**(a)** *What is the Rhie–Chow interpolation? Why is it required on a collocated grid and not on a staggered grid? Derive the modified face mass-flux expression. **(7 marks)***

**(b)** *Explain the phenomenon of checkerboard pressure oscillation. Show mathematically why it is undetectable on a collocated grid and is eliminated on a staggered grid. **(7 marks)***

---

**Solution to Q.B (a):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
The Rhie–Chow interpolation (Rhie & Chow, 1983) is a face-flux interpolation technique used on collocated grids in which the **face velocity (mass flux)** is computed using a *momentum-weighted* combination of neighbouring cell velocities **plus the local pressure jump**, rather than a simple linear interpolation of the cell-centred velocities.

**Why it is needed (2 marks):**
On a collocated grid, the linear interpolation of $u$ to the East face of cell P is $\bar{u}_e = (u_P + u_E)/2$. The corresponding face mass flux $\dot{m}_e = \rho A_e \bar{u}_e$ contains *no explicit coupling* to the pressure jump $(p_E - p_P)$. As a result, a zig-zag pressure field $p_{i,j} = (-1)^{i+j}$ produces $\bar{u}_e$ values that are identical to those of a smooth pressure field, **hiding the non-physical oscillation**. Rhie–Chow re-introduces this coupling.

**Derivation of the Modified Face Mass Flux (4 marks):**
From the discrete $u$-momentum at cell P:

$$u_P = \frac{\sum a_{nb} u_{nb} - V_P (\partial p / \partial x)_P + b}{a_P} \quad \text{...(i)}$$

Define $H_P = (\sum a_{nb} u_{nb} + b)/a_P$ and $D_P = V_P / a_P$:

$$u_P = H_P - D_P (\partial p / \partial x)_P$$

Similarly $u_E = H_E - D_E (\partial p / \partial x)_E$. Linear interpolation of $u$ to the face gives:

$$\bar{u}_e = \frac{1}{2}(H_P + H_E) - \frac{1}{2}\left[D_P \left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right)_P + D_E \left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right)_E\right]$$

**Rhie–Chow's key idea:** replace the average of the cell-centered pressure gradients with the **central difference of cell-centred pressures**:

$$\bar{u}_e = \frac{1}{2}(H_P + H_E) - \overline{D}_e \frac{p_E - p_P}{\Delta x}$$

where $\overline{D}_e = (D_P + D_E)/2$. Therefore the face mass flux becomes:

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_e = \rho A_e \left[\frac{1}{2}(H_P + H_E)\right] - \rho A_e \overline{D}_e \frac{p_E - p_P}{\Delta x}}$$

The second term now *explicitly* contains the pressure jump across the face — restoring the staggered-grid-style coupling.

---

**Solution to Q.B (b):**

**Phenomenon (2 marks):**
The checkerboard pressure field is a **non-physical, high-frequency oscillation** in which adjacent cells have alternating high and low pressures (pattern resembling a chessboard). It satisfies the discretized momentum and continuity equations but represents no real physical pressure distribution. It is most commonly observed on collocated grids when the standard linear interpolation of velocities and pressure gradients is used.

**Mathematical Proof — Why it is *invisible* on a Collocated Grid (3 marks):**
Assume $p_{i,j} = (-1)^{i+j}$. At cell P (with index $i,j$):
- East neighbour E has $p_E = (-1)^{i+j+1} = -p_P$
- West neighbour W has $p_W = (-1)^{i+j-1} = -p_P$
- $p_E - p_W = -p_P - (-p_P) = 0$

The central-difference pressure gradient at P is therefore:

$$\left(\frac{\partial p}{\partial x}\right)_P = \frac{p_E - p_W}{2\Delta x} = 0$$

The momentum equation sees **zero pressure force** and hence leaves $u_P$ unchanged. The pressure oscillation **remains undetected** and persists in the solution.

**Mathematical Proof — Why it is *destroyed* on a Staggered Grid (2 marks):**
On a staggered grid, the $u$-velocity $u_e$ is located **on the East face** of P, *between* $p_P$ and $p_E$. The pressure difference used in the $u$-momentum equation is:

$$p_P - p_E = (-1)^{i+j} - (-1)^{i+j+1} = 2\,(-1)^{i+j} \neq 0$$

The discrete $u$-momentum equation at $u_e$ becomes:

$$a_e u_e = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb} + 2\,(-1)^{i+j} A_e + b$$

The non-zero pressure jump **drives a non-zero $u_e$**, which in turn modifies $v_n$ through the continuity equation. The zig-zag pattern is broken within a few iterations. Hence, the staggered grid is **structurally immune** to the checkerboard problem.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in this Topic (KTU ESE 2024 Pattern):**
> 1. **Defining "collocated grid" without mentioning the cell-centre location** — Examiner deducts 0.5 mark.
> 2. **Stating that the staggered grid "eliminates" the checkerboard problem without showing the mathematics** — Examiner expects a 1–2 line proof that $p_P - p_E = 2(-1)^{i+j} \neq 0$ for the zig-zag mode. Without this, answer is incomplete.
> 3. **In the SIMPLE pressure-correction derivation, students forget to state the "drop $\sum a_{nb} u_{nb}'$" simplification explicitly** — 1 mark lost in derivation.
> 4. **Confusing "Rhie–Chow" with "SIMPLE"** — Rhie–Chow is a *face-flux interpolation*; SIMPLE is a *pressure-velocity coupling algorithm*. They are distinct but complementary.
> 5. **Writing mass-flux $\dot{m}_e$ with a vertical pipe (absolute value notation) inside a table** — Examiner deducts 0.5 mark for unclear notation. Always use `\vert` in latex.
> 6. **Forgetting to mention that staggered grid has higher memory cost** — A 1-mark advantage/disadvantage question may be fully missed.
> 7. **Saying "Rhie–Chow eliminates the need for SIMPLE"** — **Wrong.** Rhie–Chow makes the collocated grid *compatible* with SIMPLE; the two are used together.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 — Grid Arrangement**

- A **collocated grid** stores $u, v, w, p, T$ at the **same cell-centre node**; simpler to code, lower memory.
- A **staggered grid** stores $u$ on vertical faces, $v$ on horizontal faces, $w$ on 3-D faces, and $p, T$ at the cell centre; eliminates checkerboard pressure.
- The **checkerboard (zig-zag) pressure problem** is a non-physical oscillation $p_{i,j} = (-1)^{i+j}$ that satisfies the discretized equations but represents no real pressure.
- On a **collocated grid**, the cell-centred pressure gradient $(\partial p/\partial x)_P = (p_E - p_W)/(2\Delta x)$ is **identically zero** for a zig-zag mode, so the artefact is **invisible** and persists.
- On a **staggered grid**, the face pressure jump $(p_P - p_E)$ is **non-zero** for a zig-zag mode, generating a real driving force that **destroys** the artefact.
- The **Rhie–Chow interpolation (1983)** is the standard cure on collocated grids — it embeds the staggered-grid-style pressure coupling into the face mass-flux.
- The face mass flux on a Rhie–Chow collocated grid is:
  $$\dot{m}_e = \rho A_e \left[\frac{H_P + H_E}{2}\right] - \rho A_e \,\overline{D}_e \,\frac{p_E - p_P}{\Delta x}$$
- The **SIMPLE algorithm** derives a **pressure-correction Poisson equation** of the form:
  $$a_P p_P' = a_E p_E' + a_W p_W' + a_N p_N' + a_S p_S' + b$$
  where $b$ is the **mass-source residual** from the guessed velocities.
- All modern general-purpose CFD codes (OpenFOAM, ANSYS Fluent, CFX) use **collocated + Rhie–Chow + SIMPLE/PISO**.
- **Patankar & Spalding (1972)** introduced the **SIMPLE algorithm** originally on a **staggered grid**.
- **Harlow & Welch (1965)** introduced the **Marker-and-Cell (MAC)** method — the precursor to the staggered grid.
- **Memory trade-off:** staggered grid uses up to **3–4× more storage** in 3-D because $u, v, w$ are stored on separate node sets.
- **Geometric trade-off:** staggered grid is **difficult to extend** to unstructured / curvilinear grids because face-normal directions vary arbitrarily.
- **Always state the SIMPLE simplification** (dropping $\sum a_{nb} u_{nb}'$) explicitly when deriving the velocity-correction equation.
- **Always mention the cell-centre location** when defining the collocated grid.
- **Always quote Rhie & Chow (1983)** as the source of the interpolation — examiners reward references to original literature.
- For incompressible flow, the choice of grid arrangement is **critical**; for compressible flow, the density-based algorithm avoids the issue, and collocated grids are routinely used.
- The keywords to remember: *collocated, staggered, checkerboard, zig-zag, Rhie–Chow, SIMPLE, pressure-correction, cell-centre, face-located, interpolation, momentum-weighted, mass-source, SIMPLE simplification, MAC, Patankar.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SIMPLE Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SIMPLE Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SIMPLE (Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure-Linked Equations)** is an iterative pressure–velocity coupling algorithm developed by **Patankar and Spalding (1972)** that resolves the fundamental difficulty of incompressible Navier–Stokes equations — namely, the absence of an explicit pressure equation. It uses a guessed pressure field to compute a velocity field, then applies a *pressure correction* derived from the continuity equation to enforce mass conservation at convergence.

In KTU 2024 syllabus terminology, the SIMPLE algorithm belongs to the family of **segregated pressure-based solvers**, as opposed to *coupled solvers* (e.g., density-based). It is the cornerstone of commercial CFD codes such as **ANSYS Fluent**, **STAR-CD**, and **OpenFOAM's `pimpleFoam`** family.

## 1.2 The Core Problem SIMPLE Solves

For an incompressible flow, the continuity equation is:

$$\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$$

But the momentum equation (Navier-Stokes) contains the pressure gradient $\nabla p$:

$$\rho \frac{D\vec{V}}{Dt} = -\nabla p + \mu \nabla^2 \vec{V} + \rho \vec{g}$$

There is **no transport equation for pressure**, unlike for $u$, $v$, $w$. Pressure appears only as a source term. SIMPLE bridges this gap via an ingenious iterative correction loop.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bath Tub Plumbing" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a bathroom with **3 water taps** (inlet taps A, B, C) feeding into a tub that drains through one outlet. You turn the taps to guess a flow configuration. You then *measure* whether the tub is flooding or emptying. If the water level rises, you **reduce** the inflow pressure (close the taps a bit); if it drops, you **increase** the inflow pressure (open them more). You keep tweaking the "pressure" until the inflow exactly equals the outflow — that's *mass conservation*.

In CFD terms:
- **Guessed pressure $p^*$** → your initial tap settings
- **Velocity field $\vec{V}^*$** computed from momentum → resulting water flow
- **Mass imbalance $\dot{m}^*_{in} - \dot{m}^*_{out}$** → whether tub floods or empties
- **Pressure correction $p'$** → the tweak applied to each tap
- **Convergence** → inflow = outflow at every node

## 1.4 Key Physical & Numerical Parameters (Bolded Constants)

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **$\alpha_p$** | Pressure under-relaxation factor | **0.3** (typical) |
| **$\alpha_u$** | Momentum under-relaxation factor | **0.7** (typical) |
| $p^*$ | Guessed / intermediate pressure field | Field variable |
| $p'$ | Pressure correction | $\text{Pa}$ |
| $u^*, v^*, w^*$ | Intermediate velocity components | $\text{m/s}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU examiners frequently ask the **typical range of under-relaxation factors**. Memorize: $\alpha_p \approx 0.2 - 0.3$ and $\alpha_u \approx 0.5 - 0.7$. These are *not arbitrary* — they prevent divergence when the pressure correction is large.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Discretized Momentum Equation — Starting Point

The finite-volume discretization of the steady momentum equation (in x-direction) for cell P with neighbour E yields:

$$a_P u_P = \sum_{nb} a_{nb} u_{nb} + (p_P - p_E) A_e + S_u$$

Where:
- $a_P, a_{nb}$ = central coefficients (from convection-diffusion discretization)
- $(p_P - p_E) A_e$ = pressure-force term
- $A_e$ = face area of east face
- $S_u$ = linearized source term

## 2.2 The SIMPLE Algorithm — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **operational core** of SIMPLE. Each step has a 'Why' explaining the physical/computational rationale.

**Step 1: Guess the pressure field $p^*$**
- *Why:* We need *some* pressure field to compute velocity via momentum equation. This is the bootstrapping step.

**Step 2: Solve the discretized momentum equations using $p^*$ to obtain $u^*, v^*, w^*$**
- *Why:* With a pressure guess, momentum becomes a closed linear system solvable for velocity.

**Step 3: Compute mass fluxes $ \dot{m}^*_e, \dot{m}^*_n, \dot{m}^*_t $ across cell faces**
- *Why:* These intermediate fluxes generally **do not** satisfy continuity, producing a "mass source" $\dot{m}^*$ imbalance per cell.

**Step 4: Solve the *pressure-correction equation* to obtain $p'$**
- *Why:* We derive $p'$ such that, when added to $p^*$, it produces velocities that conserve mass.

**Step 5: Correct pressure: $p^{new} = p^* + \alpha_p \, p'$**
- *Why:* Under-relaxation prevents divergence by limiting each iteration's correction.

**Step 6: Correct face mass fluxes and cell velocities using $p'$**
- *Why:* Ensures updated velocities are divergence-free *locally* at the cell.

**Step 7: Check convergence** (residuals of momentum, continuity, energy)
- *Why:* Iterate until $\sum_{cells} | \nabla \cdot \vec{V} | < \epsilon$.

**Step 8: Return to Step 2 with $p^{new}$ as new guess, repeat until converged**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — High-Yield Equation Table

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This table is the **single most important cheat sheet** for SIMPLE derivations. KTU board questions almost always derive from these exact forms.

| # | Equation | Description |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_P u_P = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb} + (p_P - p_E)A_e + S_u$ | Discretized x-momentum |
| 2 | $u_P^* = \frac{\sum a_{nb} u_{nb}^* + S_u + p_P^* A_e}{a_P} - \frac{A_e}{a_P}(p_P^* - p_E^*)$ | Intermediate velocity using $p^*$ |
| 3 | $u_P' = -\frac{A_e}{a_P}(p_P' - p_E') + d_P (p_{nb}' - p_P')$ | Velocity correction (with neighbour correction term) |
| 4 | $\dot{m}_e' = \rho A_e (u_e' + v_e' \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} + ...)$ | Mass flux correction (simplified for orthogonal) |
| 5 | $\sum_{nb} \left[ \rho A (u') \right]_{nb} = (\dot{m}^*_{nb} - \dot{m}^*_P)$ | Pressure-correction equation (mass conservation) |
| 6 | $a_P p_P' = \sum a_{nb} p_{nb}' + b$ | Final pressure-correction linear system |
| 7 | $a_P = \sum_{nb} \frac{\rho A^2}{a_{nb}}$ | Diagonal coefficient of $p'$ equation |
| 8 | $b = (\dot{m}^*_{nb} - \dot{m}^*_P)$ | Mass-imbalance source term |
| 9 | $p^{new} = p^* + \alpha_p \, p'$ | Pressure update with under-relaxation |
| 10 | $u^{new} = u^* + \alpha_u \, u'$ | Velocity update with under-relaxation |

**Notation note:** $d_P$ in Eq. 3 = $\sum (\frac{A}{a})_{nb}$ — this is the "neighbour pressure correction" term that **Patankar neglected** in the original SIMPLE. Its inclusion gave birth to **SIMPLEC**.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility — Why SIMPLE Matters

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Airfoil flow, transonic wing analysis, rocket nozzle flows |
| **Automotive** | Vehicle aerodynamics, under-hood thermal management, fuel injectors |
| **Biomedical** | Blood flow through arteries (with non-Newtonian corrections), respiratory airflow |
| **Process Industry** | Mixing tanks, heat exchangers, packed-bed reactors |
| **Civil/HVAC** | Wind loading on buildings, ventilation of tunnels and mines |

> [!NOTE]
> **SIMPLE vs. SIMPLEC vs. PISO:** SIMPLE neglects $d_P (p_{nb}' - p_P')$ (slow convergence). **SIMPLEC** retains it → faster convergence, stable with $\alpha_p = 1$. **PISO** adds a second corrector step (originally for transient flows) — 2nd and 3rd momentum corrections further enforce mass conservation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Discretized Momentum to Velocity-Correction Equation

### Step A — Decompose variables into "guess + correction"

$$p = p^* + p' \quad \Rightarrow \quad p' = p - p^*$$

$$u = u^* + u' \quad \Rightarrow \quad u' = u - u^*$$

> *Reason:* Since we don't know $p$ and $u$ directly, we work with the **corrections** $p'$ and $u'$ from the guesses.

### Step B — Write the exact momentum discretization (using true $p, u$)

$$a_P u_P = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb} + (p_P - p_E)A_e + S_u$$

### Step C — Write the *intermediate* momentum equation (using $p^*, u^*$)

$$a_P u_P^* = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb}^* + (p_P^* - p_E^*)A_e + S_u$$

> *Reason:* Same equation but with guessed values — note that the source $S_u$ and coefficients $a_P, a_{nb}$ are **not** split (this is the "semi-implicit" approximation).

### Step D — Subtract (C) from (B)

$$a_P (u_P - u_P^*) = \sum a_{nb}(u_{nb} - u_{nb}^*) + \left[(p_P - p_E) - (p_P^* - p_E^*)\right] A_e$$

$$a_P u_P' = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb}' + (p_P' - p_E') A_e$$

> *Reason:* Differences of pressure differences simplify to difference of pressure corrections. This is the **velocity-correction equation**.

### Step E — Patankar's Key Approximation (Origin of "Semi-Implicit")

$$\sum a_{nb} u_{nb}' \approx 0$$

> *Reason:* Patankar assumed the sum of neighbour corrections is much smaller than the diagonal term. This makes the velocity correction *local* (depends only on pressure gradient at cell P), enabling an **explicit** form:

$$\boxed{u_P' = -\frac{A_e}{a_P}(p_P' - p_E')}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **most-tested equation in KTU exams**. Memorize both the form and the *reason* behind the approximation. SIMPLEC removes this approximation.

## 3.2 Derivation: The Pressure-Correction Equation

### Step F — Apply Continuity on a Cell

For incompressible flow, the net mass flux through all faces of cell P must be zero:

$$\sum_{faces} (\rho u A)_{face} = 0$$

For an interior cell with 6 faces (3D), or 4 faces (2D):

$$(\rho u A)_e - (\rho u A)_w + (\rho v A)_n - (\rho v A)_s = 0$$

### Step G — Decompose fluxes into guess + correction

$$\dot{m}_e = \dot{m}_e^* + \dot{m}_e'$$

### Step H — Use velocity correction to express flux correction

At the east face, using the corrected velocity:

$$u_e = u_e^* + u_e' = u_e^* - \frac{A_e}{a_P}(p_P' - p_E') \quad \text{(1D orthogonal)}$$

Therefore:

$$\dot{m}_e' = \rho A_e \left[ -\frac{A_e}{a_{e,P}}(p_P' - p_E') \right]$$

For a general cell, this becomes a sum over all neighbours:

$$\dot{m}_{nb}' = -\rho \frac{A_{nb}^2}{a_{nb}}(p_P' - p_{nb}')$$

### Step I — Sum corrections to enforce continuity at iteration end

Since the **corrected** mass fluxes must sum to zero:

$$\sum_{nb} \dot{m}_{nb}^{new} = 0$$

$$\sum_{nb} (\dot{m}_{nb}^* + \dot{m}_{nb}') = 0$$

$$\sum_{nb} \dot{m}_{nb}' = -\sum_{nb} \dot{m}_{nb}^* = b$$

> *Reason:* The mass-imbalance of the guessed field ($b$) must be cancelled by the corrections.

### Step J — Assemble the pressure-correction linear system

$$\boxed{a_P p_P' = \sum a_{nb} p_{nb}' + b}$$

Where:

$$a_P = \sum_{nb} \frac{\rho A_{nb}^2}{a_{nb}}, \qquad a_{nb} = \frac{\rho A_{nb}^2}{a_{nb}}, \qquad b = \sum_{nb} \dot{m}_{nb}^*}$$

## 3.3 Under-Relaxation — Why It's Required

> [!NOTE]
> The pressure correction $p'$ is derived under the **neglected-neighbour-corrections** assumption, which **overestimates** $p'$. Applying $p^{new} = p^* + p'$ *fully* causes divergence in most practical problems.

The stabilized update is:

$$p^{new} = p^* + \alpha_p \, p'$$

$$\vec{V}^{new} = \vec{V}^* + \alpha_u \, \vec{V}'$$

For SIMPLE: $\alpha_p \approx 0.3$, $\alpha_u \approx 0.7$ (recommended by Patankar).
For SIMPLEC: $\alpha_p \approx 1.0$, $\alpha_u \approx 1.0$ is typically stable.

## 3.4 Complete Iterative Loop — Summary Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{Given: } \rho, \mu, \text{ geometry, boundary conditions} \\
&\text{Initialize: } p^* = p_0, \, u^* = v^* = w^* = 0 \\
&\text{Repeat until convergence:} \\
&\quad 1.\; \text{Solve } a_P u_P^* = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb}^* + (p_P^* - p_E^*)A_e + S_u \\
&\quad 2.\; \text{Compute } \dot{m}_{face}^* = (\rho \vec{V}^* \cdot \vec{A})_{face} \\
&\quad 3.\; \text{Compute source } b_P = \sum_{nb} \dot{m}_{nb}^* - \dot{m}_P^* \\
&\quad 4.\; \text{Solve } a_P p_P' = \sum a_{nb} p_{nb}' + b_P \\
&\quad 5.\; p^{new} = p^* + \alpha_p p' \\
&\quad 6.\; u^{new} = u^* - \frac{A_e}{a_P}\alpha_u(p_P' - p_E') \\
&\quad 7.\; \text{Compute residuals} \rightarrow \text{if converged, stop}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow Diagram of the SIMPLE Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Read Geometry, BCs, Fluid Properties]) --> init[Initialize Pressure Field p-star with Hydrostatic or Initial Guess]
    init --> calcCoeff[Compute Discretization Coefficients a_P, a_nb]
    calcCoeff --> solveMom[Solve 3 Momentum Equations using p-star to get u-star, v-star, w-star]
    solveMom --> calcFlux[Compute Face Mass Fluxes m-dot-star at All Faces]
    calcFlux --> calcSrc[Compute Mass Imbalance Source b_P = sum of m-dot-star across faces]
    calcSrc --> solvePprime[Solve Pressure Correction Equation a_P p-prime = sum a_nb p-prime-nb + b]
    solvePprime --> corrP[Correct Pressure: p-new = p-star plus alpha_p times p-prime]
    corrP --> corrV[Correct Velocities: V-new = V-star plus alpha_u times V-prime]
    corrV --> check{Residuals Less Than Tolerance epsilon}
    check -- No --> solveMom
    check -- Yes --> output[Output Converged Pressure, Velocity, and Derived Fields]
    output --> stop([End])
```
