<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the SIMPLE Algorithm. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Model Answer:**

The **SIMPLE (Semi-Implicit Method for Pressure-Linked Equations)** algorithm, introduced by **Patankar and Spalding (1972)**, is an iterative pressure-velocity coupling procedure used to solve the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations. It overcomes the absence of a pressure equation by:
1. Guessing a pressure field $p^*$
2. Solving momentum equations to obtain intermediate velocities $u^*, v^*, w^*$
3. Computing the mass imbalance per cell
4. Solving a pressure-correction equation for $p'$
5. Updating pressure as $p^{new} = p^* + \alpha_p p'$
6. Iterating until mass is conserved

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students forget to mention the role of **mass-conservation enforcement** — without this, the answer loses 1 mark. Always mention "pressure correction ensures mass conservation at convergence."

---

### Question 2: Why is under-relaxation applied in the SIMPLE algorithm? [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Model Answer:**

The pressure correction $p'$ is derived under the assumption that neighbour velocity corrections sum to zero (Patankar's simplification). This assumption **overestimates** $p'$, which if applied in full ($p^{new} = p^* + p'$) would cause divergence of the iterative process for most practical flows.

Hence, **under-relaxation factors** are introduced:
- Pressure: $p^{new} = p^* + \alpha_p \, p'$ with $\alpha_p \approx 0.3$
- Velocity: $V^{new} = V^* + \alpha_u \, V'$ with $\alpha_u \approx 0.7$

These factors **damp the correction**, ensuring stable convergence at the cost of more iterations.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (Choice 1): Full Derivation and Algorithm of SIMPLE** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Derive the velocity-correction equation starting from the discretized momentum equation.

**Step 1 — Discretized momentum equation for cell P:**

$$a_P u_P = \sum_{nb} a_{nb} u_{nb} + (p_P - p_E) A_e + S_u \quad \text{...(i)}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute guess + correction decomposition:**
Let $u = u^* + u'$, $p = p^* + p'$. Equation (i) becomes:

$$a_P (u_P^* + u_P') = \sum a_{nb} (u_{nb}^* + u_{nb}') + [(p_P^* + p_P') - (p_E^* + p_E')] A_e + S_u$$

**Step 3 — Write the same equation for the intermediate (guessed) field:**

$$a_P u_P^* = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb}^* + (p_P^* - p_E^*) A_e + S_u \quad \text{...(ii)}$$

**Step 4 — Subtract (ii) from the expanded equation:**

$$a_P u_P' = \sum a_{nb} u_{nb}' + (p_P' - p_E') A_e$$

**Step 5 — Apply Patankar's approximation:**

$$\sum a_{nb} u_{nb}' \approx 0$$

**Step 6 — Solve explicitly for $u_P'$:**

$$\boxed{u_P' = -\frac{A_e}{a_P}(p_P' - p_E')}$$

This is the **velocity-correction equation** in 1D orthogonal form.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Derive the pressure-correction equation.

**Step 1 — Apply continuity at cell P:**

$$\sum_{nb} \dot{m}_{nb} = 0$$

**Step 2 — Decompose flux as $\dot{m}_{nb} = \dot{m}_{nb}^* + \dot{m}_{nb}'$:**

$$\sum \dot{m}_{nb}^* + \sum \dot{m}_{nb}' = 0 \Rightarrow \sum \dot{m}_{nb}' = -\sum \dot{m}_{nb}^* = b_P$$

**Step 3 — Express flux correction using velocity correction:**

$$\dot{m}_e' = \rho A_e u_e' = -\frac{\rho A_e^2}{a_e}(p_P' - p_E')$$

For all neighbours:

$$\sum_{nb} \dot{m}_{nb}' = -\sum_{nb} \frac{\rho A_{nb}^2}{a_{nb}}(p_P' - p_{nb}')$$

**Step 4 — Equate and rearrange:**

$$-\sum_{nb} \frac{\rho A_{nb}^2}{a_{nb}}(p_P' - p_{nb}') = b_P$$

$$\boxed{a_P p_P' = \sum a_{nb} p_{nb}' + b_P}$$

With:
$$a_P = \sum_{nb} \frac{\rho A_{nb}^2}{a_{nb}}, \quad a_{nb} = \frac{\rho A_{nb}^2}{a_{nb}}, \quad b_P = \sum_{nb} \dot{m}_{nb}^*$$

**Step 5 — Pressure update:**

$$p^{new} = p^* + \alpha_p p', \quad \alpha_p \approx 0.3$$

---

### **Question B (Choice 2): Comparative Analysis of Pressure-Velocity Coupling Algorithms** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]: Explain the limitations of SIMPLE and how SIMPLEC addresses them.

**Limitations of SIMPLE:**
1. The assumption $\sum a_{nb} u_{nb}' \approx 0$ **neglects** the influence of neighbouring velocity corrections on cell P. This leads to **overestimation** of $p'$.
2. Therefore, **strong under-relaxation** ($\alpha_p \approx 0.3$) is needed, which slows convergence.
3. In flows with high pressure gradients (e.g., curved boundaries, high Reynolds number flows), convergence becomes slow or unstable.

**SIMPLEC — The Correction:**

In SIMPLEC, the velocity correction retains the neighbour term:

$$u_P' = -\frac{A_e}{a_P}(p_P' - p_E') + d_P (p_{nb}' - p_P')$$

where $d_P = \sum (A/a)_{nb}$. This makes $u_P'$ more accurate, so $p'$ is smaller, allowing $\alpha_p \to 1$ without divergence. **Result:** typically **50% fewer iterations** than SIMPLE.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]: Compare SIMPLE, SIMPLEC, and PISO. When is each preferred?

| Feature | SIMPLE | SIMPLEC | PISO |
|---|---|---|---|
| Neighbour correction included | No (neglected) | Yes | Yes |
| Corrector steps | 1 momentum | 1 momentum | 2–3 momentum |
| Under-relaxation ($\alpha_p$) | 0.2–0.3 | ~1.0 | ~1.0 |
| Convergence speed | Slowest | Fast | Fastest (steady) |
| Best for | General use | Moderate accuracy | High accuracy / transient |
| Stability | Conservative | Stable | Sensitive |

**Application rules:**
- **SIMPLE:** Robust default for beginners and ill-conditioned problems
- **SIMPLEC:** Industry default when iterations are expensive (large meshes)
- **PISO:** Preferred for transient (time-dependent) flows where additional corrector steps enhance per-timestep accuracy

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall on Choice Questions:** Many students write SIMPLE, SIMPLEC, PISO but **omit the specific rationale** for when to choose each. KTU's marking scheme awards 2 marks specifically for the "preferred for" column. **Always pair each algorithm with a domain (steady/transient, large/small mesh).**

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — SIMPLE Algorithm**

- ✅ SIMPLE = **S**emi-**I**mplicit **M**ethod for **P**ressure-**L**inked **E**quations (Patankar & Spalding, 1972)
- ✅ Solves the **pressure-velocity decoupling problem** for incompressible flows
- ✅ **Key equations to memorize:**
  - Velocity correction: $u_P' = -\frac{A_e}{a_P}(p_P' - p_E')$
  - Pressure correction: $a_P p_P' = \sum a_{nb} p_{nb}' + b_P$
  - Pressure update: $p^{new} = p^* + \alpha_p p'$
- ✅ **Patankar's approximation:** $\sum a_{nb} u_{nb}' \approx 0$ — this makes SIMPLE "semi-implicit" and necessitates under-relaxation
- ✅ **Under-relaxation factors:** $\alpha_p \approx 0.3$, $\alpha_u \approx 0.7$
- ✅ **Convergence criterion:** $\sum_{cells} |\nabla \cdot \vec{V}| < \epsilon$
- ✅ **Algorithm steps (in order):**
  1. Guess $p^*$
  2. Solve momentum → $u^*, v^*, w^*$
  3. Compute mass imbalance $b$
  4. Solve $p'$
  5. Correct $p$ and $\vec{V}$
  6. Iterate
- ✅ **SIMPLEC** retains the neighbour correction term → faster convergence, $\alpha_p \approx 1$
- ✅ **PISO** adds 2nd/3rd corrector step → best for transient flows
- ✅ **Coefficient of $p'$ equation:** $a_P = \sum \frac{\rho A^2}{a_{nb}}$ — note the **squared area** (commonly missed in derivations)
- ✅ **Source term of $p'$ equation:** $b_P = \sum \dot{m}_{nb}^*$ — the *mass imbalance*, not velocity divergence directly
- ✅ Used in **ANSYS Fluent, STAR-CD, OpenFOAM (`simpleFoam`, `pimpleFoam`)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 3 Mistakes KTU Students Make:**
> 1. Writing $\nabla \cdot \vec{V} = 0$ in discretized form without substituting $\dot{m} = \rho u A$ — leads to wrong source term $b$
> 2. Forgetting that the **intermediate momentum** uses $p^*$ (the guess), not $p$ — causes sign errors in $p'$
> 3. Setting $\alpha_p = 1$ for SIMPLE → divergence. Always state under-relaxation is *mandatory* for SIMPLE.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Need for Turbulence Modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Need for Turbulence Modelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Turbulence** is an irregular, chaotic, three-dimensional, rotational, and highly dissipative state of fluid motion characterized by strong non-linear interactions across a vast range of spatial and temporal scales. In the KTU 2024 Computational Fluid Dynamics framework, the **Need for Turbulence Modelling** arises because most practical engineering flows (aerospace, mechanical, civil, chemical) occur at high **Reynolds numbers (Re)** where the Navier-Stokes equations contain eddies spanning several orders of magnitude in size, making direct numerical resolution computationally impossible on existing hardware.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Snapshot**
> Turbulence modelling is the mathematical and physical approximation strategy used to close the **Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS)** equations by representing the effect of unresolved turbulent fluctuations on the mean flow without explicitly computing every eddy.

**The Core Engineering Dilemma:**
The instantaneous Navier-Stokes equations are exact for all fluid flows. However, they contain turbulent fluctuations at every length scale down to the Kolmogorov microscale. Resolving all of them is **Direct Numerical Simulation (DNS)**, which scales as $Re^{9/4}$ in memory and $Re^{3}$ per unit time-step in CPU cost. For a moderate $Re = 10^5$, this means approximately **$10^{11}$ grid points** — far beyond current supercomputer capacities for routine engineering use.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Whispering Gallery**
> Imagine standing in a large crowded airport terminal (the **macroscale mean flow**) and trying to hear one specific person (a **small turbulent eddy**). If you tried to record every individual voice, sneeze, footstep, and echo simultaneously (DNS), you would need millions of microphones. Instead, an engineer places a few calibrated microphones at key locations, listens for the "average noise level," and uses statistical formulas to describe how the background chatter influences what each person hears. That is exactly what a **turbulence model** does — it estimates the cumulative effect of the unresolved eddies on the mean flow.

**Plain-English Takeaway for First-Time Learners:**
- **DNS** = try to record every single conversation in the airport (perfect, but impossibly expensive).
- **LES (Large Eddy Simulation)** = record only the loud shouts; estimate the whispers statistically.
- **RANS** = only measure the average crowd noise level; use an empirical model for everything else.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Kinematic viscosity of air at 20 °C:** $\nu \approx 1.5 \times 10^{-5}\ m^2/s$
- **Reynolds Number threshold for transition:** $Re_{crit} \approx 5 \times 10^5$ (flat plate, external flow)
- **Inertial subrange slope in energy spectrum:** $-5/3$ (Kolmogorov, **1941**)
- **Computational cost scaling exponent:** $\sim Re^{3}$ for DNS

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physical Origin of the Modelling Need

The need for turbulence modelling stems from four interconnected engineering realities:

- **Multi-scale Nature of Turbulence:** Turbulent flows contain eddies from the integral length scale $L$ down to the Kolmogorov microscale $\eta$, spanning a ratio of $L/\eta \sim Re^{3/4}$. Each ratio of 10 in length requires a proportional grid resolution.
- **Three-Dimensionality and Unsteadiness:** Turbulent fluctuations are inherently 3-D, time-dependent, and rotational — meaning the full Navier-Stokes operator cannot be reduced to simpler 2-D or steady approximations without losing essential physics.
- **Wide Range of Time Scales:** The largest eddy turnover time is $\tau_L \sim L/u'$, while the smallest Kolmogorov time scale is $\tau_\eta \sim (\nu/\varepsilon)^{1/2}$. Their ratio scales as $\tau_L/\tau_\eta \sim Re^{1/2}$.
- **Computational Cost Explosion:** Without modelling, the required number of grid points scales as $N \sim Re^{9/4}$, and the total floating-point operations scale as $FLOPS \sim Re^{3}$. This makes DNS prohibitive beyond $Re \approx 10^4$ on current architectures.

### 2.2 The Closure Problem (Heart of the Modelling Issue)

When we apply **Reynolds Decomposition** to the Navier-Stokes equations, splitting instantaneous quantities into mean and fluctuating parts (e.g., $u_i = \overline{U}_i + u_i'$), the resulting **RANS equations** contain an additional unknown term — the **Reynolds stress tensor**:

$$\overline{u_i' u_j'}$$

This term has 6 independent components (in 3-D) but contributes 0 additional equations — hence the system is **unclosed**. Turbulence models provide mathematical closures (algebraic, one-equation, two-equation, or non-linear) to approximate this tensor in terms of known mean quantities.

### 2.3 Hierarchy of Turbulence Modelling Approaches

| Approach | What is Resolved | What is Modelled | Cost Scaling | Typical $Re_{max}$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DNS** (Direct Numerical Simulation) | All scales (large + small eddies) | Nothing — exact solution | $Re^{3}$ | $\sim 10^4$ |
| **LES** (Large Eddy Simulation) | Large energy-containing eddies | Sub-grid scale (SGS) stresses | $Re^{0.5\text{–}1}$ | $\sim 10^5$ – $10^6$ |
| **RANS** (Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes) | Only mean flow | All turbulent fluctuations | $Re^{0}$ (low) | $Re \to \infty$ (any) |
| **Hybrid RANS-LES** (e.g., DES, DDES) | RANS near walls, LES elsewhere | Blended regions | Intermediate | $\sim 10^6$ – $10^7$ |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 High-Yield. Memorize the dimensional homogeneity and the scaling exponents.**

| # | Equation | Symbolic Form | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho U L}{\mu} = \dfrac{U L}{\nu}$ | Ratio of inertial to viscous forces | dimensionless |
| 2 | Reynolds Decomposition | $u_i = \overline{U}_i + u_i'$ | Instantaneous = mean + fluctuation | m/s |
| 3 | Reynolds Stress Tensor | $\tau_{ij}^{R} = -\rho\,\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ | Momentum flux due to fluctuations | Pa |
| 4 | Kolmogorov Length Scale | $\eta = \left(\dfrac{\nu^{3}}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/4}$ | Smallest dissipative eddy size | m |
| 5 | Kolmogorov Time Scale | $\tau_\eta = \left(\dfrac{\nu}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/2}$ | Smallest eddy turnover time | s |
| 6 | Kolmogorov Velocity Scale | $u_\eta = \left(\nu\,\varepsilon\right)^{1/4}$ | Velocity at the smallest scale | m/s |
| 7 | Inertial Range Ratio | $L/\eta \sim Re^{3/4}$ | Scale separation between largest & smallest eddies | dimensionless |
| 8 | DNS Grid Point Count | $N \sim Re^{9/4}$ | Memory requirement for full resolution | points |
| 9 | DNS CPU Cost per Step | $C \sim Re^{3}$ | Floating point operations per time step | FLOP |
| 10 | Energy Cascade Rate | $\varepsilon \sim \dfrac{u'^{3}}{L}$ | Rate of kinetic energy transfer down the cascade | W/kg |
| 11 | Boussinesq Hypothesis | $-\overline{u_i' u_j'} = \nu_t\left(\dfrac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j} + \dfrac{\partial \overline{U}_j}{\partial x_i}\right) - \dfrac{2}{3}k\,\delta_{ij}$ | Eddy-viscosity closure | Pa |
| 12 | Eddy Viscosity Definition | $\nu_t = C_\mu \dfrac{k^{2}}{\varepsilon}$ | Spalart–Allmaras / Standard $k$-$\varepsilon$ relation | m²/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These scaling laws govern the **design choice** between DNS, LES, and RANS in any CFD project — from predicting aircraft wing stall, to designing chemical mixers, to forecasting pollutant dispersion in atmospheric flows.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) Closure Problem

We start from the incompressible **Navier-Stokes momentum equation** for an instantaneous velocity $u_i$:

$$\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial t} + u_j \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j}$$

**Step 1 — Apply Reynolds Decomposition.**
Substitute $u_i = \overline{U}_i + u_i'$ and $p = \overline{P} + p'$:

$$\frac{\partial (\overline{U}_i + u_i')}{\partial t} + (\overline{U}_j + u_j')\frac{\partial (\overline{U}_i + u_i')}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial (\overline{P} + p')}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 (\overline{U}_i + u_i')}{\partial x_j \partial x_j}$$

**Step 2 — Take the Time Average.**
Using Reynolds averaging rules $\overline{\overline{U}_i} = \overline{U}_i$, $\overline{u_i'} = 0$, and $\overline{\overline{U}_i\, u_j'} = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial t} + \overline{U}_j \frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j} + \overline{u_j' \frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j}} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \overline{P}}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j}$$

**Step 3 — Convert the Fluctuation Term Using Continuity.**
Because $\partial u_j'/\partial x_j = 0$, we use the product rule:

$$u_j' \frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j} = \frac{\partial (u_i' u_j')}{\partial x_j} - u_i' \underbrace{\frac{\partial u_j'}{\partial x_j}}_{= 0}$$

Therefore:

$$\overline{u_j' \frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j}} = \frac{\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'}}{\partial x_j}$$

**Step 4 — Write the Final RANS Equation.**

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial t} + \overline{U}_j \frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \overline{P}}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \frac{\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'}}{\partial x_j}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the Closure Problem.**
The last term $\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'}/\partial x_j$ is the **divergence of the Reynolds stress tensor**. It contains **6 unknown scalar quantities** (in 3-D), yet no additional governing equations exist for them. This is the **closure problem of turbulence modelling**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Kolmogorov Length-Scale Scaling from Dimensional Analysis

We seek the smallest eddy size $\eta$ in terms of kinematic viscosity $\nu$ and dissipation rate $\varepsilon$.

**Step 1 — Identify the Relevant Variables.**
At the smallest scale, viscosity dominates, so the controlling parameters are **$\nu$** (m²/s) and **$\varepsilon$** (m²/s³).

**Step 2 — Assume a Power-Law Form.**

$$\eta = C \cdot \nu^{a} \cdot \varepsilon^{b}$$

**Step 3 — Match Dimensions.**
Dimension of $\eta$ is $[L] = M^{0}\,L^{1}\,T^{0}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
[\nu^{a}] &= (L^{2}\,T^{-1})^{a} = L^{2a}\,T^{-a} \\
[\varepsilon^{b}] &= (L^{2}\,T^{-3})^{b} = L^{2b}\,T^{-3b}
\end{aligned}
$$

Equating exponents:
- Length: $1 = 2a + 2b$
- Time: $0 = -a - 3b$

**Step 4 — Solve the Linear System.**
From the second equation: $a = -3b$. Substituting into the first:

$$1 = 2(-3b) + 2b = -6b + 2b = -4b \quad\Rightarrow\quad b = -\frac{1}{4}$$

Therefore $a = -3(-1/4) = 3/4$.

**Step 5 — Final Kolmogorov Length Scale.**

$$\boxed{\;\eta = \left(\frac{\nu^{3}}{\varepsilon}\right)^{1/4}\;}$$

This derivation (historically attributed to **Kolmogorov, 1941**) shows that the smallest eddy size shrinks with increasing dissipation — explaining why high-$Re$ flows need exponentially finer grids.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — DNS Computational Cost Scaling $N \sim Re^{9/4}$

We estimate the memory requirement to resolve all scales from $L$ down to $\eta$.

**Step 1 — Domain Size.** The largest scale $L$ sets the domain size. In 3-D with $n$ points per direction, the total grid count is $N_{tot} = n^{3}$.

**Step 2 — Required Spacing.** The smallest eddy must be resolved, so grid spacing $\Delta x \sim \eta$.

**Step 3 — Points Per Direction.**

$$n = \frac{L}{\Delta x} \sim \frac{L}{\eta} \sim Re^{3/4}$$

(from $L/\eta \sim Re^{3/4}$).

**Step 4 — Total Grid Points.**

$$N_{tot} = n^{3} \sim \left(Re^{3/4}\right)^{3} = Re^{9/4}$$

**Step 5 — Floating-Point Operations.**
Each grid point update in Navier-Stokes requires $\mathcal{O}(1)$ operations per time step, and the time-step $\Delta t \sim \tau_\eta \sim Re^{-1/2}$ means we need $\mathcal{O}(Re^{1/2})$ steps per large-eddy turnover. Therefore:

$$\text{FLOPS} \sim N_{tot} \cdot \frac{1}{\Delta t} \sim Re^{9/4} \cdot Re^{1/2} = Re^{11/4}$$

However, for steady statistical turbulence, the widely quoted engineering scaling (used in KTU textbooks) is:

$$\boxed{\;N \sim Re^{9/4}, \quad \text{CPU} \sim Re^{3}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The $Re^{3}$ CPU scaling assumes we need $\mathcal{O}(Re^{3/4})$ time-steps per large-eddy turnover and $\mathcal{O}(Re^{3})$ operations per step.

**Numerical Illustration for KTU Exam-Style Insight:**
For $Re = 10^{4}$, DNS requires $N \approx 10^{9}$ points. For $Re = 10^{6}$ (typical aircraft wing), $N \approx 10^{13.5}$, exceeding the world's largest supercomputers for routine use.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Turbulence Modelling Hierarchy — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram captures the **decision logic** an engineer follows when selecting a turbulence modelling strategy for a CFD simulation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start CFD Problem Definition] --> B[Compute Reynolds Number Re]
    B --> C{Re less than 10000?}
    C -->|Yes| D[Use Direct Numerical Simulation DNS]
    C -->|No| E{Re between 10000 and 1000000?}
    E -->|Yes| F[Use Large Eddy Simulation LES]
    E -->|No| G[Use RANS or Hybrid RANS-LES]
    D --> H[Resolve all scales from L down to Kolmogorov eta]
    F --> I[Resolve large eddies only]
    G --> J[Resolve only mean flow]
    I --> K[Model sub-grid scale SGS stresses]
    J --> L[Model Reynolds stresses tau_ij_R]
    H --> M[Cost scales as Re cubed]
    K --> N[Cost scales as Re to the power 0.5 to 1]
    L --> O[Cost scales as Re to the power 0 constant]
    M --> P[Prohibitive for engineering use]
    N --> Q[Feasible for moderate Re applications]
    O --> R[Industry standard for high Re flows]
    P --> S[Justifies the NEED FOR TURBULENCE MODELLING]
    Q --> S
    R --> S
    S --> T[Closure Approximations: Algebraic, One Equation, Two Equation, Non Linear]
```

### 4.2 Energy Cascade and Kolmogorov Scale Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Integral Scale L with Energy E] -->|Inertial Transfer| B[Energy Cascade -5 by 3 slope]
    B --> C[Kolmogorov Scale eta]
    C -->|Viscous Dissipation| D[Heat epsilon W per kg]
    
    A1[Large Anisotropic Eddies] --> A
    A2[Energy Injection Region] --> A
    
    B1[Universal Equilibrium Range] --> B
    B2[Inertial Subrange] --> B
    
    C1[Smallest Universal Eddies] --> C
    C2[Isotropic Dissipation Scale] --> C
    
    D1[Kinetic Energy Converted to Heat] --> D
    D2[epsilon equals u prime cubed over L] --> D
```

### 4.3 Functional Flow: From Navier-Stokes to Closed RANS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Instantaneous Navier-Stokes] --> B[Reynolds Decomposition]
    B --> C[Time Average Operator]
    C --> D[RANS Equation with Unclosed Reynolds Stress]
    D --> E{Choose Closure Model}
    E -->|Algebraic| F[Zero Equation Models: Cebeci Smith, Baldwin Lomax]
    E -->|One Equation| G[Spalart Allmaras]
    E -->|Two Equation| H[Standard k epsilon, k omega, SST]
    E -->|Non Linear| I[Explicit Algebraic Stress Models]
    F --> J[Closed Mean Flow Equations]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Numerical Solution: FVM, FEM, FDM]
    K --> L[Engineering Predictions: Lift, Drag, Heat Transfer]
```

---

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define turbulence. Why is direct numerical simulation (DNS) of turbulent flows computationally prohibitive at high Reynolds numbers?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Turbulence is a chaotic, three-dimensional, rotational, and highly dissipative fluid motion characterized by fluctuations over a wide range of spatial and temporal scales. DNS is computationally prohibitive at high Reynolds numbers because the number of grid points required to resolve all eddies scales as $N \sim Re^{9/4}$, and the CPU cost per unit time scales as $\sim Re^{3}$. For a moderate $Re = 10^5$, DNS would need $\sim 10^{11}$ grid points — far exceeding current supercomputer capacities for routine engineering applications. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest Paper]**
*State the Kolmogorov hypotheses. What are the Kolmogorov length, velocity, and time scales?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
**Kolmogorov's First Hypothesis (1941):** At sufficiently high Reynolds numbers, the small-scale turbulent motions are statistically isotropic and universally similar.
**Second Hypothesis:** In the universal equilibrium range, the statistics of small scales depend only on $\nu$ and $\varepsilon$.
The Kolmogorov scales are:
- Length: $\eta = (\nu^{3}/\varepsilon)^{1/4}$
- Velocity: $u_\eta = (\nu\varepsilon)^{1/4}$
- Time: $\tau_\eta = (\nu/\varepsilon)^{1/2}$
**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024, ESE Module 4 Paper]**

**(a)** Derive the Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) equations starting from the instantaneous incompressible Navier-Stokes equations. Clearly identify the closure problem that arises. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of Kolmogorov's dimensional analysis, derive the expressions for the Kolmogorov length, velocity, and time scales. Discuss the engineering significance of these scales in selecting grid resolution for a turbulent CFD simulation. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation of RANS Equations (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* Write the instantaneous incompressible Navier-Stokes equation:

$$\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial t} + u_j \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j}$$

*Step 2 (1 mark):* Apply Reynolds decomposition: $u_i = \overline{U}_i + u_i'$, $p = \overline{P} + p'$.

*Step 3 (2 marks):* Take the time average using the linearity of the averaging operator, noting that $\overline{u_i'} = 0$ and $\overline{\overline{U}_i\, u_j'} = 0$.

*Step 4 (2 marks):* Simplify the nonlinear term using incompressibility ($\partial u_j'/\partial x_j = 0$) to obtain the Reynolds-stress form.

*Step 5 (1 mark):* Write the final closed-form RANS equation with the divergence of Reynolds stress term explicitly identified as the unclosed unknown — **this is the closure problem of turbulence modelling**.

$$\frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial t} + \overline{U}_j \frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \overline{P}}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \frac{\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'}}{\partial x_j}$$

---

**(b) Kolmogorov Scales Derivation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (2 marks):* Identify the relevant variables at the smallest scale — kinematic viscosity $\nu$ (m²/s) and dissipation rate $\varepsilon$ (m²/s³). Assume a power-law form: $\eta = C \nu^{a} \varepsilon^{b}$.

*Step 2 (2 marks):* Match dimensions to obtain the linear system:
- Length: $2a + 2b = 1$
- Time: $-a - 3b = 0$

*Step 3 (1 mark):* Solve to get $a = 3/4$, $b = -1/4$. Hence $\eta = (\nu^{3}/\varepsilon)^{1/4}$.

*Step 4 (1 mark):* By similar dimensional reasoning: $u_\eta = (\nu\varepsilon)^{1/4}$ and $\tau_\eta = (\nu/\varepsilon)^{1/2}$.

*Step 5 (1 mark):* **Engineering Significance:** For a typical aircraft wing at $Re = 10^{6}$, if $\nu = 1.5 \times 10^{-5}\ m^2/s$ and $L = 1\ m$, then $\eta \approx L \cdot Re^{-3/4} \approx 30\ \mu m$. This means the grid spacing must be $\sim 30\ \mu m$ near critical regions, requiring $\mathcal{O}(10^{10})$ grid points — justifying the **need for turbulence modelling** to avoid prohibitive DNS cost.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, ESE Module 4 Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the Kolmogorov energy cascade hypothesis. With neat derivations, show that the scale separation ratio $L/\eta \sim Re^{3/4}$ and the grid-point requirement for DNS scales as $N \sim Re^{9/4}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare Direct Numerical Simulation (DNS), Large Eddy Simulation (LES), and Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) approaches in terms of what is resolved, what is modelled, computational cost scaling, and practical applicability. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution Outline:

**(a) Energy Cascade & DNS Scaling (7 marks):**
- (2 marks) Describe Richardson's cascade concept: large eddies → inertial range → Kolmogorov dissipation.
- (2 marks) Derive $L/\eta \sim Re^{3/4}$ using $\varepsilon \sim u'^{3}/L$ and Kolmogorov scales.
- (2 marks) Derive $N \sim Re^{9/4}$ from $n = L/\eta$ in 3-D.
- (1 mark) Numerical example: $Re = 10^{6}$ requires $\sim 10^{13.5}$ grid points.

**(b) Comparison of Approaches (7 marks):**
A well-tabulated comparison covering: Resolved Scales, Modelled Quantities, Cost Scaling $Re^{n}$, Memory Requirements, Wall Treatment, Industrial Applicability. The student should clearly note that **RANS is the industry standard for high-Re engineering flows** because it decouples the cost from the Reynolds number.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps (Examiner-Verified):**
> 1. **Forgetting the incompressibility trick** ($u_j' \partial u_j'/\partial x_j = 0$) when deriving RANS — costs 1 to 2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $L/\eta$ with $L/\lambda$** where $\lambda$ is the Taylor microscale. The KTU expected answer is $Re^{3/4}$ (Kolmogorov), not $Re^{1/2}$.
> 3. **Mixing up Kolmogorov's three hypotheses** — examiners specifically test First (isotropy), Second (universal equilibrium), and Third (log-normal corrections).
> 4. **Stating RANS resolves all eddies** — this is FALSE. RANS only resolves the *mean* flow and models all fluctuations.
> 5. **Missing the dimensional analysis steps** in Kolmogorov scale derivations — KTU examiners require explicit exponent matching, not just the final formula.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Need for Turbulence Modelling)**

- ✅ Turbulence is **3-D, rotational, dissipative, multi-scale, and chaotic**.
- ✅ The need for modelling arises from **prohibitive DNS cost** at engineering Reynolds numbers.
- ✅ DNS cost: $N \sim Re^{9/4}$, $\text{FLOPS} \sim Re^{3}$.
- ✅ **Reynolds decomposition:** $u_i = \overline{U}_i + u_i'$ is the gateway to RANS.
- ✅ **Reynolds stress:** $\tau_{ij}^{R} = -\rho\,\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ is the unclosed term.
- ✅ **Closure problem:** 6 unknown Reynolds stress components, 0 extra equations.
- ✅ **Kolmogorov scales:** $\eta = (\nu^{3}/\varepsilon)^{1/4}$, $u_\eta = (\nu\varepsilon)^{1/4}$, $\tau_\eta = (\nu/\varepsilon)^{1/2}$.
- ✅ **Scale separation:** $L/\eta \sim Re^{3/4}$.
- ✅ **DNS, LES, RANS hierarchy** — know what each resolves and models.
- ✅ **Boussinesq hypothesis:** $-\overline{u_i' u_j'} = \nu_t(\partial \overline{U}_i/\partial x_j + \partial \overline{U}_j/\partial x_i) - \frac{2}{3}k\delta_{ij}$.
- ✅ **Industry preference:** RANS for routine design, LES for research-grade unsteady flows, DNS for fundamental studies only.
- ✅ **Energy cascade rate:** $\varepsilon \sim u'^{3}/L$.
- ✅ **Kolmogorov spectrum slope** in inertial subrange: $-5/3$ (universal constant).
- ✅ **RANS is the answer** to "Why we need turbulence modelling" — it is the practical bridge between the exact Navier-Stokes equations and engineering reality.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Closure Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Closure Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Closure Problem** in Computational Fluid Dynamics is the fundamental mathematical and physical obstacle that arises when attempting to solve the Reynolds-Averaged Navier–Stokes (RANS) equations (or any system of turbulence-averaged governing equations). When the instantaneous Navier–Stokes equations are time-averaged (Reynolds decomposition) or filtered (as in LES), new unknown terms appear — most importantly the **Reynolds Stress Tensor** $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ — that cannot be expressed solely in terms of the mean (or resolved) flow variables already contained in the original equation set. This leaves the system of governing equations **mathematically "open"** (under-determined). The **Closure Problem** is, therefore, the need to supply additional **constitutive relations**, **transport equations**, or **algebraic models** to render the system solvable, i.e., to "close" it.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Closure Problem sits at the heart of all turbulence modeling. Any turbulence model (algebraic, one-equation, two-equation, or full Reynolds Stress Model) is fundamentally a **proposed solution to the Closure Problem**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **financial auditor** checking a company's monthly books. You have three official ledgers (the three momentum equations + continuity) that record all the company's official income and outgo. But the company's operations also generate a large, hidden "off-the-books" economy — informal transactions, unrecorded cash flows, and unverified receipts. These hidden flows are the **Reynolds stresses** (the fluctuating/turbulent component of momentum flux). Your three official ledgers *cannot*, on their own, account for this hidden economy. To balance the books, you must make an **educated estimate** of the hidden activity based on observable clues (cash size, transaction patterns, growth rate). Each estimation method is a **turbulence model**, and the inability of the original ledgers to self-balance is the **Closure Problem**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Takeaway:** The Closure Problem simply means: *"Our averaged equations contain more unknowns than equations."* Turbulence models are the extra equations/assumptions we bolt on to make the count match.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants in Closure Theory

- **Turbulent Kinetic Energy** per unit mass: $k = \tfrac{1}{2}\overline{u_i' u_i'}$ with units **m²/s²**
- **Turbulent (Eddy) Viscosity**: $\mu_t$ with units **kg/(m·s)**
- **Kolmogorov length scale**: $\eta = \left(\nu^3/\varepsilon\right)^{1/4}$ in **m**
- **Kolmogorov time scale**: $\tau_\eta = \left(\nu/\varepsilon\right)^{1/2}$ in **s**
- **Kolmogorov velocity scale**: $v_\eta = \left(\nu\,\varepsilon\right)^{1/4}$ in **m/s**
- Standard model constants (e.g., $C_\mu = 0.09$, $C_{1\varepsilon}=1.44$, $C_{2\varepsilon}=1.92$, $\sigma_k=1.0$, $\sigma_\varepsilon=1.3$ for the standard $k$–$\varepsilon$ model).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Origin of the Closure Problem — Stepwise Logic

1. **Start with the instantaneous incompressible Navier–Stokes equation** (vector form):
   $$\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial t} + u_j \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j}$$

2. **Apply Reynolds Decomposition** — split every variable into a mean and a fluctuating part:
   $$u_i = \overline{U_i} + u_i', \quad p = \overline{P} + p'$$

3. **Substitute and time-average** (with $\overline{u_i'} = 0$, $\overline{\overline{U_i}} = \overline{U_i}$).

4. **Non-linear convective term** $u_j \partial u_i / \partial x_j$ produces the unknown product $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ (the **Reynolds stress**).

5. **The averaged momentum equation** now contains **10 new unknowns** (6 symmetric stresses + 3 turbulent heat fluxes + turbulent mass flux), but **no new equations** — hence the system is **not closed**.

6. **Closure** is the act of supplying extra equations, hypotheses, or models to relate these unknowns to the mean flow.

### 2.2 The Reynolds Stress Tensor

The unknown tensor in the momentum equation is:

$$\tau_{ij}^{(t)} = -\rho\,\overline{u_i' u_j'}$$

It is symmetric ($\tau_{ij}^{(t)} = \tau_{ji}^{(t)}$), giving **6 independent unknowns** in 3D.

> [!NOTE]
> The Reynolds stress tensor has two physical interpretations:
> (a) **Momentum flux** due to turbulent eddies transporting fluid parcels of different velocities.
> (b) **An additional effective stress** acting on the mean flow, analogous to viscous stress but far larger.

### 2.3 Hierarchy of Closure Approaches

| **Level** | **Closure Type** | **Extra Unknowns Introduced** | **Extra Equations/Assumptions Added** | **Examples** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | No closure (DNS) | None | None — solve all scales directly | Direct Numerical Simulation |
| 1 | Algebraic / Zero-equation | $\mu_t$ | Prandtl mixing-length hypothesis | Mixing length, Baldwin–Lomax |
| 2 | One-equation | $k$ or $\tilde{\nu}$ | One transport PDE | Spalart–Allmaras |
| 3 | Two-equation | $k$ and $\varepsilon$ (or $\omega$) | Two transport PDEs | Standard $k$–$\varepsilon$, $k$–$\omega$, SST |
| 4 | Algebraic Stress Model (ASM) | $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ | Algebraic equilibrium relations | Rodi ASM |
| 5 | Full Reynolds Stress Model (RSM) | $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ (6 components) | 6 transport PDEs + $\varepsilon$ equation | Launder–Reece–Rodi, SSG |
| 6 | LES (Filtered Navier–Stokes) | Sub-grid stress $\tau_{ij}^{SGS}$ | SGS model (Smagorinsky, Dynamic, WALE) | Smagorinsky, WALE |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Equation / Relation** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Typical Use** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $u_i = \overline{U_i} + u_i'$ | Reynolds decomposition | Foundation of RANS |
| 2 | $\tau_{ij}^{(t)} = -\rho\,\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ | Reynolds stress tensor | Extra unknown in RANS |
| 3 | $\overline{u_i' u_j'} = \tfrac{2}{3}k\delta_{ij} - \nu_t\left(\dfrac{\partial \overline{U_i}}{\partial x_j} + \dfrac{\partial \overline{U_j}}{\partial x_i}\right)$ | **Boussinesq Hypothesis (eddy-viscosity)** | Linear constitutive closure |
| 4 | $\mu_t = \rho\,C_\mu \dfrac{k^2}{\varepsilon}$ | Eddy viscosity (Std $k$–$\varepsilon$) | Two-equation closure |
| 5 | $k = \tfrac{1}{2}\overline{u_i' u_i'}$ | Turbulent kinetic energy | Energy of turbulence |
| 6 | $\dfrac{Dk}{Dt} = P_k - \varepsilon + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\!\left(\nu+\dfrac{\nu_t}{\sigma_k}\!\right)\dfrac{\partial k}{\partial x_j}\!\right]$ | $k$ transport equation | $k$ evolution |
| 7 | $\dfrac{D\varepsilon}{Dt} = C_{1\varepsilon}\dfrac{\varepsilon}{k}P_k - C_{2\varepsilon}\dfrac{\varepsilon^2}{k} + \dfrac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\!\left(\nu+\dfrac{\nu_t}{\sigma_\varepsilon}\!\right)\dfrac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial x_j}\!\right]$ | $\varepsilon$ transport equation | Dissipation rate evolution |
| 8 | $\ell_m = \kappa\,y$ (near wall) | Prandtl mixing length | Algebraic closure |
| 9 | $\tau_{ij}^{SGS} = -2\nu_t^{SGS}\overline{S}_{ij}$ | Sub-grid stress (LES) | Filtered Navier–Stokes |
| 10 | $\nu_t^{SGS} = (C_s\,\Delta)^2 \vert \overline{S} \vert$ | Smagorinsky SGS model | LES closure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Note that the Boussinesq hypothesis is the *cheapest* (and weakest) closure. The full RSM is the *most general* (and most expensive). Every turbulence model is a different **compromise between physical fidelity and computational cost**.

### 2.5 Engineering Utility

The Closure Problem and its solutions are central to virtually every engineering CFD application:

- **Aerospace:** Airfoil, wing, and full-aircraft RANS simulations rely on $k$–$\varepsilon$ or $k$–$\omega$ SST closures to predict lift, drag, and stall.
- **Automotive:** External aerodynamics, under-hood cooling, and HVAC in cabins use RSM or SST for separated flows.
- **Chemical / Process Engineering:** Stirred-tank reactors, mixing vessels — RSM captures swirl and anisotropy.
- **Civil / Environmental:** Wind loading on tall buildings, atmospheric dispersion — LES is increasingly used.
- **Turbomachinery:** Compressor and turbine blade rows — $k$–$\omega$ SST is the de-facto industry standard.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — From Instantaneous Navier–Stokes to the Closure Gap

We start with the **incompressible, constant-property** instantaneous Navier–Stokes momentum equation in index notation:

$$\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial t} + u_j \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} \quad (1)$$

along with continuity:

$$\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_i} = 0 \quad (2)$$

**Step 1 — Reynolds Decomposition.** Decompose every variable:

$$u_i(\mathbf{x},t) = \overline{U_i}(\mathbf{x},t) + u_i'(\mathbf{x},t)$$
$$p(\mathbf{x},t) = \overline{P}(\mathbf{x},t) + p'(\mathbf{x},t)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into (1).** Replace $u_i$, $p$:

$$\frac{\partial(\overline{U_i}+u_i')}{\partial t} + (\overline{U_j}+u_j')\frac{\partial(\overline{U_i}+u_i')}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial(\overline{P}+p')}{\partial x_i} + \nu\frac{\partial^2(\overline{U_i}+u_i')}{\partial x_j \partial x_j}$$

**Step 3 — Time-average the entire equation.** Using the Reynolds averaging rules

$$\overline{\overline{U_i}} = \overline{U_i}, \quad \overline{u_i'} = 0, \quad \overline{\overline{U_i}\,u_j'} = 0, \quad \overline{u_i'\,u_j'} \neq 0$$

the mean continuity equation drops out, and the mean momentum equation reduces to:

$$\frac{\partial \overline{U_i}}{\partial t} + \overline{U_j}\frac{\partial \overline{U_i}}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \overline{P}}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 \overline{U_i}}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \frac{\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'}}{\partial x_j} \quad (3)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **only** new term is the **divergence of the Reynolds stress** $-\partial(\overline{u_i' u_j'})/\partial x_j$. This is the term that **cannot be expressed** in terms of mean quantities alone — **the Closure Problem is born here.**

**Step 4 — Count the unknowns vs. equations.**

- Equations available: 3 (mean momentum) + 1 (mean continuity) = **4**
- Unknowns: $\overline{U_i}$ (3), $\overline{P}$ (1), $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ (6 symmetric) = **10**
- Deficit: **6 unknowns short** → the system is **not closed**.

### 3.2 Derivation — Boussinesq Eddy-Viscosity Hypothesis (a Closure)

Boussinesq (1877) proposed that turbulent momentum transfer behaves analogously to molecular viscosity, so that:

$$-\rho\,\overline{u_i' u_j'} = \mu_t\left(\frac{\partial \overline{U_i}}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial \overline{U_j}}{\partial x_i}\right) - \frac{2}{3}\rho k\,\delta_{ij} \quad (4)$$

**Derivation of the isotropic part.** In a homogeneous, isotropic turbulent field, the Reynolds stress tensor reduces to a diagonal form with equal normal stresses:

$$\overline{u_1'^2} = \overline{u_2'^2} = \overline{u_3'^2} = \tfrac{1}{3}\,\overline{u_i' u_i'} = \tfrac{2}{3}k$$

Multiplying by $-\rho\,\delta_{ij}$ (so that the trace is $-\rho \cdot \overline{u_i' u_i'} = -2\rho k$) and adding the deviatoric part proportional to mean strain, we recover (4).

> [!NOTE]
> The $-(2/3)\rho k \delta_{ij}$ term is the **isotropic normal-stress correction**. Many texts absorb it into a modified pressure $\overline{P} + (2/3)\rho k$.

### 3.3 Derivation — Transport Equation for $k$ (the $k$–$\varepsilon$ Foundation)

Begin with equation (3). Subtract (3) from the instantaneous (1) to obtain the fluctuating-momentum equation, multiply by $2u_i'$, and average. After lengthy but standard Reynolds-averaging manipulations (using continuity and the identity $\overline{u_i' u_j' \partial u_i'/\partial x_j} = -\varepsilon$):

$$\frac{Dk}{Dt} = \underbrace{-\overline{u_i' u_j'}\frac{\partial \overline{U_i}}{\partial x_j}}_{P_k} - \underbrace{\nu\overline{\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j}\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j}}}_{\varepsilon} + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[-\frac{1}{2}\overline{u_i' u_i' u_j'} - \frac{1}{\rho}\overline{p' u_i'} + \nu\frac{\partial k}{\partial x_j}\right] \quad (5)$$

Identifying the terms:

- $P_k$ = **production** of turbulent kinetic energy by mean shear.
- $\varepsilon$ = **dissipation** rate of $k$ at the Kolmogorov scale.
- The diffusion term is **closed** by **gradient-diffusion (gradient transport) hypothesis** (analogue of Fourier's law for heat):

$$-\frac{1}{2}\overline{u_i' u_i' u_j'} - \frac{1}{\rho}\overline{p' u_j'} = \frac{\nu_t}{\sigma_k}\frac{\partial k}{\partial x_j} \quad (6)$$

Using the Boussinesq relation, $P_k$ becomes:

$$P_k = \nu_t\left(\frac{\partial \overline{U_i}}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial \overline{U_j}}{\partial x_i}\right)\frac{\partial \overline{U_i}}{\partial x_j} \quad (7)$$

The final, **closed** $k$ equation is therefore:

$$\frac{Dk}{Dt} = P_k - \varepsilon + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\!\left(\nu + \frac{\nu_t}{\sigma_k}\right)\frac{\partial k}{\partial x_j}\right] \quad (8)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The chain of assumptions used — **Boussinesq + gradient-diffusion hypothesis** — is itself the closure. These are *not* derived from first principles; they are **modeled**.

### 3.4 Derivation — Dissipation Rate $\varepsilon$ Equation (Standard $k$–$\varepsilon$ Closure)

The exact $\varepsilon$ equation is too long and unclosed; the standard model *replaces* it with a dimensionally consistent, empirically tuned transport equation:

$$\frac{D\varepsilon}{Dt} = C_{1\varepsilon}\frac{\varepsilon}{k}P_k - C_{2\varepsilon}\frac{\varepsilon^2}{k} + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\!\left(\nu+\frac{\nu_t}{\sigma_\varepsilon}\right)\frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial x_j}\right] \quad (9)$$

- The $C_{1\varepsilon}\frac{\varepsilon}{k}P_k$ term models **production of $\varepsilon$** by mean-shear amplification of small scales.
- The $C_{2\varepsilon}\frac{\varepsilon^2}{k}$ term models **destruction of $\varepsilon$** by viscous breakdown at the Kolmogorov scale.
- The diffusion term uses the same gradient-diffusion closure as (8) with Prandtl number $\sigma_\varepsilon$.

Finally, the eddy viscosity closes the system:

$$\mu_t = \rho\,C_\mu\frac{k^2}{\varepsilon} \quad (10)$$

Equations (8), (9), (10) and (3) form a **closed set** of 6 equations for 6 unknowns ($\overline{U}_1,\overline{U}_2,\overline{U}_3,\overline{P},k,\varepsilon$).

### 3.5 Summary — Mathematical Matrix of the Closure

| **Step** | **Operation** | **Result** | **Closure Status** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Time-average Navier–Stokes | RANS eq. (3) with $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ | ❌ Open |
| 2 | Apply Boussinesq hypothesis | Replace $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ via (4) | ❌ Still open (needs $\mu_t$) |
| 3 | Add $k$ transport equation | 1 new PDE | ❌ Still open (needs $\varepsilon$) |
| 4 | Add $\varepsilon$ transport equation | 1 more PDE | ❌ Still open (needs $C_\mu$) |
| 5 | Specify $\mu_t = \rho C_\mu k^2/\varepsilon$ | Algebraic link | ✅ **Closed** |
| 6 | Specify $C_\mu, C_{1\varepsilon}, C_{2\varepsilon}, \sigma_k, \sigma_\varepsilon$ | Empirical constants | ✅ Solvable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Closure Problem — Conceptual Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Instantaneous Navier Stokes Equations] --> B[Apply Reynolds Decomposition]
    B --> C[Time Average]
    C --> D[RANS Equations]
    D --> E{Is System Closed?}
    E -- No --> F[Closure Problem: extra unknown Reynolds stresses]
    F --> G[Choose Closure Strategy]
    G --> H1[Algebraic: Mixing Length, Boussinesq]
    G --> H2[One Equation: Spalart Allmaras]
    G --> H3[Two Equation: k epsilon, k omega SST]
    G --> H4[Full RSM: 6 transport equations]
    G --> H5[LES: Smagorinsky, WALE, Dynamic]
    H1 --> I[Solved RANS System]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    H5 --> J[Solved Filtered Navier Stokes]
    E -- Yes --> I
```

### 4.2 Hierarchy of Turbulence Closure Models

```mermaid
graph TD
    DNS[Direct Numerical Simulation: NO closure, all scales resolved]
    LES[Large Eddy Simulation: Sub grid closure]
    RSM[Reynolds Stress Model RSM: 6 transport eqs for stresses]
    ASM[Algebraic Stress Model: algebraic eqs]
    TWO[k-omega SST, Realizable k-epsilon: Two equation]
    ONE[Spalart Allmaras: One equation]
    ZERO[Mixing length, Baldwin Lomax: Zero equation algebraic]

    DNS --> LES
    LES --> RSM
    RSM --> ASM
    ASM --> TWO
    TWO --> ONE
    ONE --> ZERO

    ZERO --> Z1[Cheapest, least general]
    DNS --> D1[Most expensive, most general]

    style DNS fill:#f9c,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style LES fill:#fc9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style RSM fill:#9cf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style TWO fill:#9fc,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style ZERO fill:#cf9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Closure-Problem Block Architecture (Functional Mapping)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Mean Quantities]
        M1[Mean velocity U i bar]
        M2[Mean pressure P bar]
        M3[Mean scalar fields]
    end

    subgraph CORE[Governing Equations]
        RANS[RANS Momentum Equation]
        CONT[Continuity Equation]
    end

    subgraph PROBLEM[The Closure Gap]
        RS[Reynolds Stress Tensor tau ij t = minus rho u i prime u j prime]
        UNK[6 additional unknowns no extra equations]
    end

    subgraph MODEL[Closure Model Layer]
        BH[Boussinesq Eddy Viscosity Hypothesis]
        VIS[mu t closure: k epsilon / k omega / mixing length]
        TR[k transport equation]
        EP[epsilon transport equation]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT[Closed Solvable System]
        SOL[6 equations, 6 unknowns solvable CFD]
    end

    M1 --> RANS
    M2 --> RANS
    M3 --> RANS
    RANS --> RS
    RS --> UNK
    UNK --> MODEL
    BH --> VIS
    TR --> EP
    EP --> VIS
    VIS --> RANS
    RANS --> SOL
    CONT --> SOL
```

> [!NOTE]
> **How to read these diagrams for KTU exams:** When asked to *"explain the closure problem"*, reproduce the logic of Section 4.1: instantaneous NS → averaging → RANS → identification of new unknowns → need for a model. When asked to *"classify turbulence models"*, reproduce the hierarchy in Section 4.2.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State the Closure Problem in the context of Reynolds-Averaged Navier–Stokes equations. Why does it arise?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Closure Problem is the inability of the time-averaged (RANS) momentum and continuity equations to form a determinate system. It arises because **Reynolds averaging** of the nonlinear convective term $u_j\partial u_i/\partial x_j$ produces a new unknown — the **Reynolds stress tensor** $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ — that cannot be expressed purely in terms of mean flow variables. The RANS system therefore contains 10 unknowns ($\overline{U_i}, \overline{P}, \overline{u_i' u_j'}$) with only 4 equations, leaving 6 unknowns undetermined. A **closure model** (algebraic, one-, or two-equation) is required to relate these Reynolds stresses to mean quantities, rendering the system solvable.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*What is the Boussinesq hypothesis? How does it serve as a closure?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Boussinesq hypothesis states that turbulent momentum transfer (Reynolds stresses) is proportional to the **mean rate of strain**, in direct analogy to molecular viscous stress. Mathematically:
$$-\rho\overline{u_i' u_j'} = \mu_t\!\left(\frac{\partial \overline{U_i}}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial \overline{U_j}}{\partial x_i}\right) - \tfrac{2}{3}\rho k\,\delta_{ij}$$
It acts as a closure by expressing the 6 unknown Reynolds stresses in terms of a **single scalar** — the **eddy (turbulent) viscosity** $\mu_t$. This reduces the 6-equation deficit of RANS to 1 unknown, which is then supplied by additional model equations (e.g., $k$–$\varepsilon$).

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Starting from the instantaneous incompressible Navier–Stokes equation, derive the **RANS momentum equation** and clearly identify the term that creates the **closure problem**. (7 marks)

**(b)** With the aid of a suitable diagram, **classify turbulence closure models** into algebraic, one-equation, and two-equation categories, giving **one example** and the **key transport equation** (if any) for each. (7 marks)

---

**Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. *Write the instantaneous equation (1 mark):*
   $$\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial t} + u_j\frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \nu\frac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j}$$

2. *Apply Reynolds decomposition* $u_i = \overline{U_i} + u_i'$, $p = \overline{P} + p'$ and substitute. (1 mark)

3. *Time-average* using $\overline{u_i'}=0$, $\overline{\overline{U_i}u_j'}=0$, $\overline{u_i'u_j'}\neq 0$. (1 mark)

4. *Obtain RANS equation:*
   $$\frac{\partial \overline{U_i}}{\partial t} + \overline{U_j}\frac{\partial \overline{U_i}}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \overline{P}}{\partial x_i} + \nu\frac{\partial^2 \overline{U_i}}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \frac{\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'}}{\partial x_j}$$ (2 marks)

5. *Identify closure problem:* The term $-\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'}/\partial x_j$ is the **divergence of the Reynolds stress tensor** and is unknown. RANS has 10 unknowns and 4 equations, leaving 6 independent unknowns unresolved. (2 marks)

---

**Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. *Algebraic (Zero-equation) closure* (2 marks):
   - Example: **Prandtl Mixing-Length Model**.
   - **No transport equation.** Closes via:
     $$\mu_t = \rho\,\ell_m^2\left|\frac{\partial \overline{U}}{\partial y}\right|,\quad \ell_m = \kappa y$$
   - Assumption: $\ell_m$ is a prescribed algebraic function of geometry.

2. *One-equation closure* (2 marks):
   - Example: **Spalart–Allmaras model**.
   - **One transport PDE** for the modified eddy viscosity $\tilde{\nu}$:
     $$\frac{D\tilde{\nu}}{Dt} = c_{b1}(1-f_{t2})\tilde{S}\tilde{\nu} - \left[c_{w1}f_w - \frac{c_{b1}\kappa^2}{f_{t2}}\right]\!\left(\frac{\tilde{\nu}}{d}\right)^{\!2} + \frac{1}{\sigma}\nabla\!\cdot\!\left[(\nu+\tilde{\nu})\nabla\tilde{\nu}\right] + c_{b2}\!\left(\frac{\partial \tilde{\nu}}{\partial x_k}\!\right)^{\!2}$$

3. *Two-equation closure* (3 marks):
   - Example: **Standard $k$–$\varepsilon$ model**.
   - **Two transport PDEs** for $k$ and $\varepsilon$:
     $$\frac{Dk}{Dt} = P_k - \varepsilon + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\!\left(\nu+\frac{\nu_t}{\sigma_k}\right)\frac{\partial k}{\partial x_j}\right]$$
     $$\frac{D\varepsilon}{Dt} = C_{1\varepsilon}\frac{\varepsilon}{k}P_k - C_{2\varepsilon}\frac{\varepsilon^2}{k} + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\!\left(\nu+\frac{\nu_t}{\sigma_\varepsilon}\right)\frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial x_j}\right]$$
   - Closes with: $\mu_t = \rho C_\mu k^2/\varepsilon$.

(Use Section 4.2 Mermaid diagram for the classification part.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**(a)** State the **Boussinesq eddy-viscosity hypothesis** and discuss its **physical meaning** and **limitations** as a closure. (7 marks)

**(b)** Derive the **transport equation for turbulent kinetic energy $k$** clearly identifying the **production term** $P_k$ and the **dissipation term** $\varepsilon$. Mention the closure assumption used for the diffusion term. (7 marks)

---

**Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. *Statement of hypothesis* (2 marks):
   $$-\rho\overline{u_i' u_j'} = \mu_t\!\left(\frac{\partial \overline{U_i}}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial \overline{U_j}}{\partial x_i}\right) - \tfrac{2}{3}\rho k\,\delta_{ij}$$
   The Reynolds stress is **proportional** to the mean rate of strain, with the **eddy viscosity** $\mu_t$ as the proportionality constant.

2. *Physical meaning* (2 marks):
   - Turbulent eddies transport momentum just as molecules transport momentum via viscosity.
   - $\mu_t$ represents the "mixing efficiency" of turbulence; it is **not a fluid property** but a flow-state property (depends on $k$, $\varepsilon$, geometry).
   - The term $-(2/3)\rho k\delta_{ij}$ is the isotropic normal stress correction.

3. *Limitations* (3 marks):
   - **Isotropy assumption** — fails in flows with strong streamline curvature, swirl, rotation, rapid distortion, or strong anisotropy (e.g., Rayleigh–Bénard convection, rotating channels, stratified flows).
   - **Single scalar $\mu_t$** cannot represent 6 independent Reynolds stresses.
   - Cannot capture **Reynolds-stress anisotropy-driven secondary flows** in non-circular ducts.
   - In **transitional flows**, Boussinesq fails since turbulence is not fully developed.

---

**Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. *Take fluctuating momentum equation*, multiply by $2u_i'$, time-average. (1 mark)

2. *Identify the production term* (2 marks):
   $$P_k = -\overline{u_i' u_j'}\frac{\partial \overline{U_i}}{\partial x_j} = \nu_t\left(\frac{\partial \overline{U_i}}{\partial x_j}+\frac{\partial \overline{U_j}}{\partial x_i}\right)\frac{\partial \overline{U_i}}{\partial x_j}$$
   Physical meaning: $P_k$ is the rate at which **mean-flow kinetic energy is transferred to fluctuating kinetic energy** by work of Reynolds stresses against the mean shear.

3. *Identify the dissipation term* (1 mark):
   $$\varepsilon = \nu\,\overline{\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j}\frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j}}$$
   Physical meaning: rate of turbulent kinetic energy converted into **heat** by viscosity at the Kolmogorov microscale.

4. *Write the closed $k$ equation* (2 marks):
   $$\frac{Dk}{Dt} = P_k - \varepsilon + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\!\left[\!\left(\nu + \frac{\nu_t}{\sigma_k}\right)\frac{\partial k}{\partial x_j}\right]$$
   Diffusion closure: **gradient-diffusion (gradient transport) hypothesis**, analogue of Fourier's law:
   $$-\frac{1}{2}\overline{u_i'u_i'u_j'} - \frac{1}{\rho}\overline{p'u_j'} = \frac{\nu_t}{\sigma_k}\frac{\partial k}{\partial x_j}$$
   (1 mark for explicitly naming the hypothesis).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks on Closure Problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting to identify the closure term explicitly.** It is *not* enough to derive the RANS equation — you must circle / underline / name the $-\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'}/\partial x_j$ term and count unknowns vs. equations.
> 2. **Confusing "Boussinesq hypothesis" with "turbulent viscosity".** Boussinesq is the *constitutive relation*; $\mu_t$ is the *scalar*; an *additional* model equation is required to close for $\mu_t$.
> 3. **Mixing up $P_k$ and $\varepsilon$.** $P_k$ comes from **mean shear × Reynolds stress** (energy *into* turbulence). $\varepsilon$ is **viscous destruction of $k$ at small scales** (energy *out of* turbulence).
> 4. **Forgetting the gradient-diffusion assumption** in the $k$ and $\varepsilon$ equations. The diffusion term is *not* exact — it is a modeled closure.
> 5. **Not stating model constants.** For $k$–$\varepsilon$, examiners expect $C_\mu \approx 0.09$, $C_{1\varepsilon} \approx 1.44$, $C_{2\varepsilon} \approx 1.92$.
> 6. **Confusing the RANS closure hierarchy with LES closure.** They are different paradigms: RANS models *all* turbulence; LES models only the *sub-grid* scales.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Closure Problem** = the RANS/filtered Navier–Stokes equations have **more unknowns than equations**; an extra modeling assumption is required.
- The closure term that appears in RANS is the **Reynolds stress tensor** $\tau_{ij}^{(t)} = -\rho\,\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ (6 independent components in 3D).
- **Boussinesq Hypothesis** is the simplest (and weakest) closure: relates Reynolds stresses to mean strain via a scalar **eddy viscosity** $\mu_t$.
- **Eddy-viscosity models** (zero-, one-, two-equation) supply an equation/relation for $\mu_t$. Examples: mixing length, Spalart–Allmaras, $k$–$\varepsilon$, $k$–$\omega$ SST.
- **Reynolds Stress Models (RSM)** solve 6 transport equations for the Reynolds stresses directly — no Boussinesq needed — capturing anisotropy.
- **LES** uses a different closure: the **sub-grid stress** $\tau_{ij}^{SGS}$, closed by Smagorinsky, WALE, or dynamic models.
- Standard $k$–$\varepsilon$ constants: $C_\mu=0.09$, $C_{1\varepsilon}=1.44$, $C_{2\varepsilon}=1.92$, $\sigma_k=1.0$, $\sigma_\varepsilon=1.3$.
- The $k$ equation contains **convection, production $P_k$, dissipation $\varepsilon$, and diffusion** terms.
- $\varepsilon$ equation is **modeled**, not derived exactly — uses empirical constants $C_{1\varepsilon}, C_{2\varepsilon}$.
- **Gradient-diffusion hypothesis** is the standard closure for the diffusion terms in the $k$ and $\varepsilon$ equations.
- As you move from algebraic → one-eq → two-eq → RSM → LES → DNS, **physical fidelity increases** but so does **computational cost**.
- Always **state the closure assumption explicitly** in your exam answer — that is the core of the Closure Problem discussion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reynolds Averaged Navier-Stokes Equation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECHT635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Reynolds Averaged Navier-Stokes Equation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Reynolds Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) equations** are the time-averaged governing equations of motion for a turbulent fluid flow. They are obtained by decomposing each instantaneous flow variable (velocity and pressure) into a **mean (time-averaged) component** and a **fluctuating component**, and subsequently time-averaging the instantaneous Navier-Stokes equations. In the standard Reynolds decomposition framework, a generic flow variable $\phi$ is written as: $$ \phi(\mathbf{x}, t) = \overline{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) + \phi'(\mathbf{x}, t) $$ where: - $\overline{\phi}(\mathbf{x})$ is the **time-averaged (mean) component**, formally defined as $\overline{\phi} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{t_0}^{t_0+T} \phi \, dt$ - $\phi'(\mathbf{x}, t)$ is the **fluctuating component**, satisfying $\overline{\phi'} = 0$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Emphasis:** The RANS equations introduce additional **unclosed terms** called the **Reynolds stresses** ($\overline{u_i' u_j'}$), which arise from the nonlinear convective acceleration term of the Navier-Stokes equations. These extra stresses make the system mathematically incomplete and require **turbulence models** for closure. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The River Photograph Imagine you are standing beside a fast-flowing, turbulent river carrying twigs and leaves. If you take a **short-exposure photograph** (say, 1/1000 of a second), you will freeze every tiny ripple, eddy, and chaotic swirl — this is analogous to solving the **instantaneous Navier-Stokes equations** (the basis of Direct Numerical Simulation, DNS). Now, if you use a **long-exposure camera** (say, 5 seconds), all the small chaotic fluctuations blur out and you are left with a **smooth average flow pattern** showing only the bulk current of the river. **This long-exposure photograph is what RANS computes.** RANS does not resolve individual turbulent eddies — it only captures their **statistical average effect** on the mean flow, which appears as an extra stress (the Reynolds stress) acting on the fluid. ## 1.3 Why RANS is the Workhorse of Engineering CFD > [!NOTE] > - **DNS cost** scales as $\mathcal{O}(Re^3)$ — completely impractical for industrial flows where $Re > 10^5$. > - **LES cost** scales as $\mathcal{O}(Re)$ to $\mathcal{O}(Re^{0.5})$ — manageable, but still expensive. > - **RANS cost** is essentially independent of $Re$ — making it the **standard for industrial and KTU-curriculum CFD** due to its computational economy and reasonable accuracy. Standard physical constants retained in the RANS formulation are: - $\rho$ — fluid density ($\mathbf{kg/m^3}$) - $\mu$ — dynamic viscosity ($\mathbf{kg/(m \cdot s)}$) - $\nu = \mu / \rho$ — kinematic viscosity ($\mathbf{m^2/s}$) # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Reynolds Decomposition — Structured Logic The mathematical procedure to obtain RANS proceeds in four logical stages: 1. **Decompose every flow variable** into a sum of a time-mean part and a fluctuating part: $u_i = \overline{U}_i + u_i'$, $p = \overline{P} + p'$. 2. **Substitute** the decomposed variables into the original instantaneous Navier-Stokes equations. 3. **Apply the time-averaging operator** (Reynolds Averaging Rules) to every term. 4. **Identify and isolate** the new unclosed correlation terms — the Reynolds stresses. ## 2.2 The Five Fundamental Reynolds Averaging Rules For two arbitrary variables $\phi$ and $\psi$, the time-averaging operator $\overline{(\cdot)}$ satisfies: $$ \overline{\phi + \psi} = \overline{\phi} + \overline{\psi} $$ $$ \overline{c \cdot \phi} = c \cdot \overline{\phi} \quad (\text{where } c = \text{constant}) $$ $$ \overline{\overline{\phi}} = \overline{\phi} $$ $$ \overline{\partial \phi / \partial x_i} = \partial \overline{\phi} / \partial x_i $$ $$ \overline{\overline{\phi} \, \psi'} = 0 \quad ; \quad \overline{\phi' \psi'} \neq 0 $$ The **last rule is the critical one** — it ensures that only the products of fluctuating components (e.g., $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$) survive and become the source of the closure problem. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Equation Name** | **Mathematical Form** | **Physical Meaning** | |---|---|---| | Reynolds Decomposition | $\phi = \overline{\phi} + \phi'$ | Splits flow into mean + fluctuation | | Time-Averaged Mean | $\overline{\phi} = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{t_0}^{t_0+T} \phi \, dt$ | Definition of ensemble/time mean | | Mean of Fluctuation | $\overline{\phi'} = 0$ | Fluctuations average to zero by definition | | RANS Continuity | $\dfrac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_i} = 0$ | Mass conservation of the mean flow | | RANS Momentum | $\dfrac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial t} + \overline{U}_j \dfrac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j} = -\dfrac{1}{\rho}\dfrac{\partial \overline{P}}{\partial x_i} + \nu \dfrac{\partial^2 \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \dfrac{\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'}}{\partial x_j}$ | Mean momentum balance | | Reynolds Stress Tensor | $\tau_{ij}^{t} = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'}$ | Extra stresses due to turbulence | | Turbulent Kinetic Energy | $k = \tfrac{1}{2} \overline{u_i' u_i'}$ | Energy contained in turbulence | | Boussinesq Hypothesis | $-\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'} = \mu_t \left( \dfrac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j} + \dfrac{\partial \overline{U}_j}{\partial x_i} \right) - \tfrac{2}{3} \rho k \, \delta_{ij}$ | Eddy-viscosity closure | | Total (Effective) Stress | $\tau_{ij}^{eff} = (\mu + \mu_t) \left( \dfrac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j} + \dfrac{\partial \overline{U}_j}{\partial x_i} \right)$ | Molecular + turbulent viscous stress | > [!NOTE] > The absolute magnitude notation $\vert \tau_{ij}^{t} \vert$ (for exam answer sheets) is written in handwriting as $\lvert \, \rvert$, but in the LaTeX-formatted cheat sheet above, $\mu_t$ represents **turbulent (eddy) viscosity** in units of $\mathbf{kg/(m \cdot s)}$. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility RANS equations are the **backbone of industrial CFD codes** such as ANSYS Fluent, OpenFOAM, and Star-CCM+. Applications span: - **Aerospace:** Airfoil lift/drag prediction, transonic wing flow, jet engine turbine stages. - **Automotive:** Vehicle aerodynamic drag coefficient ($C_D$) estimation, cabin ventilation. - **Civil Engineering:** Wind loading on tall buildings and long-span bridges, stack-gas dispersion. - **Process Engineering:** Mixing tanks, pipe networks, heat exchanger optimization. - **Marine:** Hull resistance and propeller cavitation studies. The primary engineering payoff is achieving **$\mathcal{O}(10^6)$ to $\mathcal{O}(10^9)$ cell simulations on a workstation in hours**, rather than weeks on a supercomputer. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Starting Point — Instantaneous Incompressible Navier-Stokes Equations For a Newtonian, incompressible fluid with constant viscosity, the conservation of mass and momentum are: $$ \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_i} = 0 $$ $$ \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial t} + u_j \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} $$ (Einstein summation convention is implied over repeated indices $i$ and $j$.) ## 3.2 Step 1 — Apply Reynolds Decomposition Substitute $u_i = \overline{U}_i + u_i'$ and $p = \overline{P} + p'$ into the momentum equation: $$ \frac{\partial (\overline{U}_i + u_i')}{\partial t} + (\overline{U}_j + u_j') \frac{\partial (\overline{U}_i + u_i')}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial (\overline{P} + p')}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 (\overline{U}_i + u_i')}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} $$ **Textual logic:** We substitute term-by-term. The convective term will expand into four products. ## 3.3 Step 2 — Expand the Nonlinear Convective Term The product $(\overline{U}_j + u_j')(\overline{U}_i + u_i')$ expands to: $$ (\overline{U}_j + u_j')\frac{\partial (\overline{U}_i + u_i')}{\partial x_j} = \overline{U}_j \frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j} + \overline{U}_j \frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j} + u_j' \frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j} + u_j' \frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j} $$ **Textual logic:** This is the critical step — the term $u_j' \frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j}$ is nonlinear in fluctuating quantities and is the **only term that produces a non-zero contribution when time-averaged**. ## 3.4 Step 3 — Apply the Time-Averaging Operator Term by Term Applying $\overline{(\cdot)}$ to every term using the five Reynolds rules: $$ \frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial t} + \overline{U}_j \frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j} + \overline{U}_j \frac{\partial \overline{u_i'}}{\partial x_j} + \overline{u_j' \frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j}} + \overline{u_j' \frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j}} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \overline{P}}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \overline{p'}}{\partial x_i} $$ **Textual logic:** Apply Rule 1 (linearity), Rule 4 (commutation of averaging and differentiation), and the fact that $\overline{\phi'} = 0$. ## 3.5 Step 4 — Simplify Using $\overline{\phi'} = 0$ The terms $\overline{u_i'} = 0$ and $\overline{p'} = 0$, so several terms vanish: $$ \overline{U}_j \frac{\partial \overline{u_i'}}{\partial x_j} = 0 $$ $$ \overline{u_j'} \frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j} = 0 \quad \text{(since } \overline{U}_i \text{ is independent of time)} $$ $$ -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \overline{p'}}{\partial x_i} = 0 $$ The **only surviving nonlinear term** is the critical Reynolds stress correlation: $$ \overline{u_j' \frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j}} = \frac{\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'}}{\partial x_j} \quad \text{(using commutation of averaging and differentiation)} $$ ## 3.6 Step 5 — Assemble the Final RANS Momentum Equation The simplified, time-averaged momentum equation is: $$ \boxed{\frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial t} + \overline{U}_j \frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \overline{P}}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \frac{\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'}}{\partial x_j}} $$ **Textual logic:** The first three terms on the right and left resemble the laminar Navier-Stokes equation. The **extra divergence term** of the Reynolds stress tensor is the price we pay for time-averaging. ## 3.7 Step 6 — Symmetric Reynolds Stress Tensor The Reynolds stress tensor is defined as: $$ \tau_{ij}^{t} = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'} = \begin{bmatrix} -\rho \overline{u'^2} & -\rho \overline{u'v'} & -\rho \overline{u'w'} \\ -\rho \overline{u'v'} & -\rho \overline{v'^2} & -\rho \overline{v'w'} \\ -\rho \overline{u'w'} & -\rho \overline{v'w'} & -\rho \overline{w'^2} \end{bmatrix} $$ **Textual logic:** The diagonal components $\overline{u_i'^2}$ represent **normal stresses** (energy of velocity fluctuations), and the off-diagonal $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ components represent **shear stresses** (momentum transfer by turbulent eddies). ## 3.8 Step 7 — The Closure Problem & Boussinesq's Hypothesis The RANS system has **4 unknowns** ($\overline{U}, \overline{V}, \overline{W}, \overline{P}$) but introduces **6 additional unknown Reynolds stresses** $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$. This is the **closure problem** of turbulence. **Boussinesq Hypothesis (1877)** closes the system by analogy with Newtonian viscosity: $$ -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'} = \mu_t \left( \frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial \overline{U}_j}{\partial x_i} \right) - \frac{2}{3} \rho k \, \delta_{ij} $$ **Textual logic:** $\mu_t$ is the **eddy (turbulent) viscosity**, $k = \tfrac{1}{2} \overline{u_i' u_i'}$ is the **turbulent kinetic energy**, and $\delta_{ij}$ is the Kronecker delta ($\delta_{ij} = 1$ if $i=j$, $0$ otherwise). Models such as $k$-$\varepsilon$, $k$-$\omega$, and Spalart-Allmaras compute $\mu_t$ from additional transport equations. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Hierarchy of Turbulence Modeling Approaches (Contextual Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[NS Equations
Incompressible Newtonian] --> B{Computational Strategy} B --> C[DNS
Direct Numerical Simulation] B --> D[LES
Large Eddy Simulation] B --> E[RANS
Reynolds Averaged NS] C --> C1[Resolves ALL eddies
No modeling required] C --> C2["Cost ~ Re cubed
Research only"] D --> D1[Resolves LARGE eddies] D --> D2[Models subgrid scales
SGS model] E --> E1[Time averages fluctuations] E1 --> E2[Introduces Reynolds stresses] E2 --> E3[Closure Problem] E3 --> E4[Algebraic Models
Mixing Length] E3 --> E5[One Equation Models
Spalart Allmaras] E3 --> E6[Two Equation Models
k epsilon and k omega] E6 --> F1[Standard k-epsilon
Industrial workhorse] E6 --> F2[RNG k-epsilon
Better for separation] E6 --> F3[Realizable k-epsilon] E6 --> F4[Standard k-omega
Near wall modeling] E6 --> F5[SST k-omega
Best of both worlds] style A fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style B fill:#3a5f8f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style C fill:#5f8f3a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style D fill:#8f5f3a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style E fill:#8f3a3a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style E3 fill:#a83232,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style F1 fill:#4a6b3a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style F2 fill:#4a6b3a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style F3 fill:#4a6b3a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style F4 fill:#6b5f3a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff style F5 fill:#6b5f3a,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:1px,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Functional Architecture of the RANS Derivation Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Step 1
Instantaneous NS] --> P2[Step 2
Reynolds Decomposition
phi equals mean + prime] P2 --> P3[Step 3
Expand Convective Term
Four product terms] P3 --> P4[Step 4
