Vanishing Fluctuation Means] P5 --> P6[Step 6
Surviving Reynolds Stress
div of u prime u prime] P6 --> P7[Step 7
Final RANS Momentum Eq] P7 --> P8[Step 8
Closure Problem
6 extra unknowns] P8 --> P9[Step 9
Boussinesq Hypothesis
eddy viscosity mu t] P9 --> P10[Step 10
Turbulence Model
k epsilon solves mu t] style P1 fill:#2b4c7e,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style P2 fill:#2b4c7e,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style P3 fill:#2b4c7e,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style P4 fill:#3a6ea5,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style P5 fill:#3a6ea5,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style P6 fill:#3a6ea5,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style P7 fill:#5cb85c,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style P8 fill:#d9534f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style P9 fill:#d9534f,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff style P10 fill:#f0ad4e,stroke:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. Define Reynolds decomposition. State the time-averaging rules used in deriving the RANS equations. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Model Answer:** **Reynolds decomposition** expresses any instantaneous turbulent flow variable as the sum of its time-averaged mean and a fluctuating component: $$ \phi(\mathbf{x}, t) = \overline{\phi}(\mathbf{x}) + \phi'(\mathbf{x}, t) $$ where $\overline{\phi} = \dfrac{1}{T} \int_0^T \phi \, dt$ and by construction $\overline{\phi'} = 0$. **Key time-averaging rules** (3 of the 5 required for full marks): $$ \overline{\phi + \psi} = \overline{\phi} + \overline{\psi} \quad ; \quad \overline{c \phi} = c \overline{\phi} \quad ; \quad \overline{\overline{\phi} \, \psi'} = 0 $$ The last rule ensures that only products of two or more fluctuating quantities (e.g., $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$) produce non-zero correlation terms — the source of the **Reynolds stresses**. **(3 marks)** --- ### Q2. What is meant by the "closure problem" of turbulence in the RANS framework? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The RANS momentum equation contains the unknown second-order correlations $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ (six independent Reynolds stresses). Since the system of governing equations now has more unknowns than equations, the system is **mathematically open** and cannot be solved directly. This imbalance between the number of unknowns and the number of available equations is called the **closure problem of turbulence**. It is resolved by introducing a **turbulence model** — the simplest being the Boussinesq eddy-viscosity hypothesis, which expresses Reynolds stresses as proportional to the mean velocity gradients through an eddy viscosity $\mu_t$. **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Question (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > - **Marks lost for missing $\overline{\phi'} = 0$** explicitly stated before term cancellation — always invoke it. > - **Marks lost for not justifying the commutation** of time-averaging and spatial differentiation (Rule 4). > - **Marks lost on closing arguments:** Stating Boussinesq hypothesis *without* mentioning its **inability to capture anisotropy** loses 1 mark. > - **Marks lost for using wrong sign** on Reynolds stress term: it appears with a **minus sign** in the momentum equation — double-check direction! --- ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **(a)** Starting from the instantaneous incompressible Navier-Stokes equations, **derive the RANS momentum equation** for a turbulent flow. Clearly state the Reynolds decomposition, the five averaging rules used, and identify the new term that arises. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 (1 mark):** Write the instantaneous incompressible NS equations: $$ \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_i} = 0 \quad ; \quad \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial t} + u_j \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} $$ **Step 2 (1 mark):** Apply Reynolds decomposition $u_i = \overline{U}_i + u_i'$ and $p = \overline{P} + p'$, and substitute into the momentum equation. **Step 3 (1.5 marks):** Expand the convective product: $$ (\overline{U}_j + u_j')\frac{\partial (\overline{U}_i + u_i')}{\partial x_j} = \overline{U}_j \frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j} + \overline{U}_j \frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j} + u_j' \frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j} + u_j' \frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j} $$ **Step 4 (1 mark):** Apply the time-averaging operator and use $\overline{\phi'} = 0$ to eliminate the second and third terms. **Step 5 (1 mark):** Use the commutation rule $\overline{\partial \phi' / \partial x_j} = \partial \overline{\phi'} / \partial x_j = 0$ to simplify, retaining $\overline{u_j' \frac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j}} = \frac{\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'}}{\partial x_j}$. **Step 6 (1.5 marks):** Assemble the final RANS equation and highlight the **Reynolds stress divergence term**: $$ \boxed{\frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial t} + \overline{U}_j \frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \overline{P}}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \frac{\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'}}{\partial x_j}} $$ --- **(b)** Explain the **Boussinesq eddy-viscosity hypothesis** for closing the RANS equations. Discuss its physical basis and its major limitations. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Physical Basis (3 marks):** Boussinesq (1877) proposed that turbulent momentum transfer is analogous to molecular viscous transfer. The Reynolds stress is assumed proportional to the mean strain-rate tensor: $$ -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'} = \mu_t \left( \frac{\partial \overline{U}_i}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial \overline{U}_j}{\partial x_i} \right) - \frac{2}{3} \rho k \, \delta_{ij} $$ where $\mu_t$ is the **eddy (turbulent) viscosity** and $k = \tfrac{1}{2} \overline{u_i' u_i'}$ is the turbulent kinetic energy. Substituting this into the RANS equation reduces the problem to determining $\mu_t$ — typically via one or two additional transport equations (e.g., $k$-$\varepsilon$ model). **Limitations (4 marks):** 1. **Isotropy assumption:** Assumes $\mu_t$ is a scalar, meaning turbulence is isotropic. **Invalid for flows with strong streamline curvature, rotation, or swirl.** 2. **Local equilibrium:** Implies turbulent production equals dissipation locally — fails in **non-equilibrium, separated, and impinging flows**. 3. **Single-point closure:** Cannot capture **non-local transport** effects (e.g., convective transport of $\mu_t$ by large eddies). 4. **No Reynolds-stress anisotropy:** Cannot reproduce phenomena like **normal-stress anisotropy** observed in real turbulence. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **(a)** Define the **Reynolds stress tensor** $\tau_{ij}^t$. Write it in expanded matrix form for a 3-D turbulent flow and explain the physical meaning of its diagonal and off-diagonal components. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Definition (2 marks):** The Reynolds stress tensor is a second-order symmetric tensor quantifying the additional momentum flux due to turbulent fluctuations: $$ \tau_{ij}^{t} = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'} $$ **Matrix form (2 marks):** $$ \tau_{ij}^{t} = \begin{bmatrix} -\rho \overline{u'^2} & -\rho \overline{u'v'} & -\rho \overline{u'w'} \\ -\rho \overline{u'v'} & -\rho \overline{v'^2} & -\rho \overline{v'w'} \\ -\rho \overline{u'w'} & -\rho \overline{v'w'} & -\rho \overline{w'^2} \end{bmatrix} $$ **Physical meaning (3 marks):** - **Diagonal components** ($\overline{u'^2}, \overline{v'^2}, \overline{w'^2}$): represent **normal Reynolds stresses** — the intensity of velocity fluctuations along each coordinate axis. The trace $u'^2 + v'^2 + w'^2$ is twice the turbulent kinetic energy $2k$. - **Off-diagonal components** ($\overline{u'v'}, \overline{v'w'}, \overline{u'w'}$): represent **shear Reynolds stresses** — the correlation between velocity fluctuations in different directions. They are the primary drivers of **turbulent momentum transfer** across the mean flow. --- **(b)** Compare the **Standard $k$-$\varepsilon$** and **Standard $k$-$\omega$** turbulence models. Highlight their governing equations, key coefficients, and suitability for different flow classes. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** | **Feature** | **Standard $k$-$\varepsilon$** | **Standard $k$-$\omega$** | |---|---|---| | Equations solved | $k$ (TKE) and $\varepsilon$ (dissipation rate) | $k$ (TKE) and $\omega$ (specific dissipation) | | Wall treatment | Requires **wall functions** | **Integrates to the wall** naturally | | Boundary layer accuracy | Moderate | Excellent | | Free-shear flows | Good (jets, mixing layers) | Good | | Adverse pressure gradients | Poor (over-predicts separation) | Better | | Computational cost | Lower (slightly) | Similar | | Sensitivity to freestream $\omega$ | None | High — must specify carefully | **Governing transport equations (1 mark each):** For $k$-$\varepsilon$: $$ \frac{\partial k}{\partial t} + U_j \frac{\partial k}{\partial x_j} = P_k - \varepsilon + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\left[ \left( \nu + \frac{\nu_t}{\sigma_k} \right) \frac{\partial k}{\partial x_j} \right] $$ $$ \frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial t} + U_j \frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial x_j} = C_{\varepsilon 1} \frac{\varepsilon}{k} P_k - C_{\varepsilon 2} \frac{\varepsilon^2}{k} + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\left[ \left( \nu + \frac{\nu_t}{\sigma_\varepsilon} \right) \frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial x_j} \right] $$ where $C_{\varepsilon 1} = 1.44$, $C_{\varepsilon 2} = 1.92$, $\sigma_k = 1.0$, $\sigma_\varepsilon = 1.3$. **Suitability summary (2 marks):** Use **$k$-$\varepsilon$** for industrial internal/external flows with attached boundary layers; use **$k$-$\omega$** when accurate near-wall resolution is needed (aerospace airfoils, turbomachinery). The **SST $k$-$\omega$ model** hybridizes both to capture the strengths of each. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist** > > ✅ **Reynolds decomposition:** $\phi = \overline{\phi} + \phi'$ — every flow variable must be split. > > ✅ **Critical rule:** $\overline{\phi'} = 0$ — vanishing mean of fluctuations. > > ✅ **Most-important rule:** $\overline{\overline{\phi} \, \psi'} = 0$, but $\overline{\phi' \psi'} \neq 0$. > > ✅ **RANS momentum equation** differs from instantaneous NS by **one extra term** — the divergence of $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$. > > ✅ **Reynolds stress tensor:** $\tau_{ij}^t = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'}$ — symmetric, 6 independent components. > > ✅ **Closure problem:** 4 mean-flow unknowns + 6 Reynolds-stress unknowns → mathematically under-determined. > > ✅ **Boussinesq hypothesis:** Treats turbulence as an "eddy fluid" with scalar viscosity $\mu_t$ — analogous to molecular viscosity. > > ✅ **$k$-$\varepsilon$ model** is the industrial workhorse; **$k$-$\omega$ SST** is best for adverse pressure gradients and separation. > > ✅ **Always remember the sign:** Reynolds stress enters the RANS equation with a **minus sign** before the divergence: $-\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'} / \partial x_j$. > > ✅ **Exam trap:** When asked to "derive RANS," **never skip the expansion of the convective product** — this is where 3–4 marks reside. > > ✅ **Units check:** $\mu_t$ has units of $\mathbf{kg/(m \cdot s)}$, identical to $\mu$ — they are additive in the total stress $\mu + \mu_t$. > > ✅ **Cost hierarchy:** DNS > LES > RANS — but accuracy hierarchy is identical: DNS > LES > RANS. Engineering trade-off favors RANS.

topics.title: Turbulence Modelling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Turbulence Modelling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Turbulence Modelling** is the mathematical and physical framework used in Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) to close the **Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS)** equations by approximating the unknown **Reynolds Stress Tensor** $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ in terms of known mean-flow quantities. Since the instantaneous Navier-Stokes equations are mathematically closed but computationally intractable for high Reynolds number industrial flows (Direct Numerical Simulation would require $\sim Re^{9/4}$ grid points), turbulence models provide a *closure approximation* that makes the system of PDEs solvable.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Definition:** Turbulence is a chaotic, three-dimensional, unsteady, vortical, and dissipative flow regime characterized by eddies of multiple scales, governed by the **Navier-Stokes equations** but requiring stochastic/statistical treatment for practical engineering prediction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "River of Whirlpools"

Imagine you are standing beside a fast-flowing river. You do not track every single water molecule; instead, you observe the *average* flow direction and notice chaotic swirling structures (whirlpools/eddies) of all sizes — from centimetres to metres. These eddies carry momentum, mass, and energy in random directions.

- **Mean flow** = the steady downstream current you feel.
- **Turbulent fluctuations** = the random whirlpools superimposed on the current.
- **Turbulence model** = the engineering rule-book that predicts how strongly these whirlpools mix the flow, *without* simulating every individual one.

This is the very essence of CFD turbulence modelling: **predicting the effect** of the unresolved eddies on the resolved mean flow.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fundamental Physical Constants Used in Turbulence Modelling:**
> - Von Kármán constant: $\kappa \approx 0.41$ (universal, dimensionless)
> - Turbulent Schmidt number: $Sc_t \approx 0.9$
> - Turbulent Prandtl number: $Pr_t \approx 0.9$
> - Wall roughness constant: $E \approx 9.793$ (smooth wall)

### 1.3 Classification Overview (Hierarchy of Turbulence Models)

The KTU 2024 scheme expects familiarity with the following hierarchy, in increasing computational cost and physical fidelity:

| Level | Model Class | Closure Type |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | DNS (Direct Numerical Simulation) | No closure — resolves all scales |
| 1 | LES (Large Eddy Simulation) | Sub-grid scale (SGS) model |
| 2 | RSM (Reynolds Stress Model) | 7 transport equations |
| 3 | Two-equation models ($k$-$\varepsilon$, $k$-$\omega$) | Eddy viscosity + 2 transport PDEs |
| 4 | One-equation models (Spalart–Allmaras) | 1 transport PDE |
| 5 | Algebraic / Zero-equation (Baldwin–Lomax) | Algebraic formula |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Pearl:** For industrial CFD using commercial software like ANSYS Fluent / OpenFOAM, the **standard $k$-$\varepsilon$ model** remains the *default* workhorse due to its robustness, while **$k$-$\omega$ SST** is the preferred choice for adverse pressure gradient flows with separation (e.g., aerofoils, turbines).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Flow of Turbulence Closure

1. **Step 1 — Decomposition (Reynolds, 1895):** Every instantaneous flow variable (velocity, pressure, scalar) is split into a time-averaged mean and a fluctuating component.
   $$ \phi = \overline{\phi} + \phi' $$
   where $\overline{\phi'} = 0$ and $\overline{\overline{\phi}} = \overline{\phi}$.

2. **Step 2 — Substitution into Navier-Stokes:** Substitute the decomposed quantities into the instantaneous momentum equation. After taking the time-average (Reynolds averaging rules), a new unknown tensor appears — the **Reynolds Stress Tensor** $\tau_{ij} = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'}$.

3. **Step 3 — The Closure Problem:** The RANS momentum equation now contains *more unknowns than equations*. We have 4 unknowns ($\overline{u}, \overline{v}, \overline{w}, \overline{p}$) + 6 independent Reynolds stress components $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ = 10 unknowns, but only 4 PDEs. **This is the "closure problem."**

4. **Step 4 — Turbulence Model Hypothesis:** A turbulence model proposes a *physical/mathematical hypothesis* that relates the unknown Reynolds stresses to the known mean flow gradients. The simplest and most widely-used hypothesis is the **Boussinesq Eddy Viscosity Hypothesis (1877)**.

5. **Step 5 — Solve the Augmented System:** The model adds extra transport equations (e.g., 2 for $k$-$\varepsilon$) that, when solved alongside RANS, close the system mathematically.

6. **Step 6 — Wall Treatment:** Most turbulence models are formulated for **fully-turbulent log-law regions**. Near walls, viscous sublayer and buffer layer require either very fine meshes ($y^+ \approx 1$) or **wall functions** ($30 \leq y^+ \leq 300$).

### 2.2 The Boussinesq Eddy Viscosity Hypothesis — "The Heart of CFD Turbulence"

Boussinesq proposed that turbulent momentum transfer behaves analogously to molecular viscous transfer, with turbulent stresses proportional to mean strain rate through an **eddy viscosity** $\mu_t$:

$$ -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'} = \mu_t \left( \frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial \overline{u_j}}{\partial x_i} \right) - \frac{2}{3} \rho k \delta_{ij} $$

where the **turbulent kinetic energy (TKE)** per unit mass is:

$$ k = \frac{1}{2} \overline{u_i' u_i'} = \frac{1}{2}\left( \overline{u'^2} + \overline{v'^2} + \overline{w'^2} \right) $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The eddy viscosity $\mu_t$ is *not a fluid property* — it is a *flow property*. This means it varies in space and time, which is why models like $k$-$\varepsilon$ solve PDEs to compute its local value.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Formula | Physical Meaning | Standard Value / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\phi = \overline{\phi} + \phi'$ | Reynolds decomposition | — |
| 2 | $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ | Reynolds Stress Tensor | m$^2$/s$^2$ |
| 3 | $k = \tfrac{1}{2}\overline{u_i' u_i'}$ | Turbulent kinetic energy | m$^2$/s$^2$ |
| 4 | $\mu_t = \rho C_\mu \tfrac{k^2}{\varepsilon}$ | Eddy viscosity (k-ε) | Pa·s |
| 5 | $\ell = \tfrac{k^{3/2}}{\varepsilon}$ | Turbulent length scale | m |
| 6 | $u_\tau = \sqrt{\tau_w / \rho}$ | Friction velocity | m/s |
| 7 | $y^+ = \tfrac{u_\tau y}{\nu}$ | Dimensionless wall distance | dimensionless |
| 8 | $u^+ = \tfrac{1}{\kappa} \ln(y^+) + B$ | Log-law of the wall | $\kappa = 0.41$, $B = 5.0$ |
| 9 | $C_\mu = 0.09$ | Standard k-ε constant | dimensionless |
| 10 | $C_{1\varepsilon} = 1.44$, $C_{2\varepsilon} = 1.92$ | k-ε empirical constants | dimensionless |
| 11 | $\sigma_k = 1.0$, $\sigma_\varepsilon = 1.3$ | Turbulent Prandtl numbers | dimensionless |
| 12 | $\tau_t = \tfrac{\ell}{\sqrt{k}}$ | Turbulent time scale | s |
| 13 | $P_k = -\overline{u_i' u_j'} \tfrac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j}$ | TKE production rate | m$^2$/s$^3$ |
| 14 | $P_k = \mu_t \left( \tfrac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} + \tfrac{\partial \overline{u_j}}{\partial x_i} \right) \tfrac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j}$ | TKE production (Boussinesq) | m$^2$/s$^3$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are the *backbone of CFD solvers*. Without them, no commercial solver (Fluent, CFX, OpenFOAM, Star-CCM+) can produce a turbulent flow prediction. Industries deploying them include aerospace (wing aerodynamics), automotive (drag prediction), HVAC (ventilation), chemical (mixing tanks), and turbomachinery (turbine blades).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Reynolds-Averaged Momentum Equation

We start with the instantaneous incompressible Navier-Stokes equation in index notation:

$$ \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial t} + u_j \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho} \frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} $$

**Step 1:** Apply Reynolds decomposition $u_i = \overline{u_i} + u_i'$ and $p = \overline{p} + p'$:

$$ \frac{\partial(\overline{u_i} + u_i')}{\partial t} + (\overline{u_j} + u_j') \frac{\partial(\overline{u_i} + u_i')}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho} \frac{\partial(\overline{p} + p')}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 (\overline{u_i} + u_i')}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} $$

**Step 2:** Apply the time-average operator $\overline{(\cdot)}$. Using Reynolds averaging rules:
- $\overline{\overline{u_i}} = \overline{u_i}$
- $\overline{u_i'} = 0$
- $\overline{\overline{u_j} \, u_i'} = \overline{u_j} \, \overline{u_i'} = 0$
- $\overline{u_j' u_i'} \neq 0$ (correlations of fluctuations survive)

The convective term becomes:

$$ \overline{(\overline{u_j} + u_j')(\overline{u_i} + u_i')} = \overline{u_j}\;\overline{u_i} + \overline{u_j'}\;\overline{u_i'} + \overline{u_j'}\;\overline{u_i} + \overline{u_j}\;\overline{u_i'} $$

But $\overline{u_i'} = 0$ and $\overline{u_j'} = 0$, so:

$$ \overline{u_j u_i} = \overline{u_j}\;\overline{u_i} + \overline{u_j' u_i'} $$

**Step 3:** Substituting back, the **RANS momentum equation** emerges:

$$ \boxed{ \frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial t} + \overline{u_j} \frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho} \frac{\partial \overline{p}}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \frac{\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'}}{\partial x_j} } $$

**Step 4:** Define the **Reynolds Stress Tensor** $\tau_{ij}^{(t)} = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'}$. The last term becomes an additional *unknown stress divergence* — this is the closure problem:

$$ \frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial t} + \overline{u_j} \frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho} \frac{\partial \overline{p}}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \frac{1}{\rho} \frac{\partial \tau_{ij}^{(t)}}{\partial x_j} $$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Standard $k$-$\varepsilon$ Model Transport Equations

**Step 1 — Derive the TKE equation:** Take the dot product of RANS with $\overline{u_i}$ and subtract the *mean* of the fluctuating momentum equation dotted with $u_i'$. After substantial tensor algebra (see Wilcox, Pope, or Versteeg textbook derivations), the **exact TKE transport equation** is:

$$ \frac{\partial k}{\partial t} + \overline{u_j} \frac{\partial k}{\partial x_j} = P_k - \varepsilon + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\left[ \left( \nu + \frac{\nu_t}{\sigma_k} \right) \frac{\partial k}{\partial x_j} \right] $$

where each term represents:
- **Convection:** $\overline{u_j} \tfrac{\partial k}{\partial x_j}$
- **Production:** $P_k = -\overline{u_i' u_j'} \tfrac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} = \nu_t \left( \tfrac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} + \tfrac{\partial \overline{u_j}}{\partial x_i} \right) \tfrac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j}$
- **Dissipation:** $\varepsilon = \nu \overline{\tfrac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j} \tfrac{\partial u_i'}{\partial x_j}}$
- **Diffusion:** modelled with effective diffusivity

**Step 2 — Hypothesize a model for $\varepsilon$:** Launder & Spalding (1972) proposed that the dissipation rate $\varepsilon$ is governed by a transport equation with analogous structure:

$$ \frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial t} + \overline{u_j} \frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial x_j} = C_{1\varepsilon} \frac{\varepsilon}{k} P_k - C_{2\varepsilon} \frac{\varepsilon^2}{k} + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\left[ \left( \nu + \frac{\nu_t}{\sigma_\varepsilon} \right) \frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial x_j} \right] $$

**Step 3 — Define the eddy viscosity from dimensional analysis:** By dimensional reasoning (since $k$ has units m$^2$/s$^2$ and $\varepsilon$ has m$^2$/s$^3$), the natural velocity scale is $\sqrt{k}$ and length scale is $k^{3/2}/\varepsilon$:

$$ \nu_t = C_\mu \frac{k^2}{\varepsilon} $$

**Step 4 — Empirical closure coefficients (calibrated against canonical experiments: homogeneous shear, log-layer, round jet):**

$$ C_\mu = 0.09, \quad C_{1\varepsilon} = 1.44, \quad C_{2\varepsilon} = 1.92, \quad \sigma_k = 1.0, \quad \sigma_\varepsilon = 1.3 $$

### 3.3 Derivation of Wall Function for Near-Wall Treatment

**Step 1:** Define the **friction velocity** $u_\tau$ from the wall shear stress:

$$ u_\tau \equiv \sqrt{\frac{\tau_w}{\rho}} $$

**Step 2:** Define the **dimensionless wall distance** $y^+$:

$$ y^+ = \frac{u_\tau y}{\nu} $$

**Step 3:** For the **viscous sublayer** ($y^+ < 5$), the velocity is linear (laminar):

$$ u^+ = y^+ $$

**Step 4:** For the **log-law region** ($30 \leq y^+ \leq 300$), experimental data shows:

$$ u^+ = \frac{1}{\kappa} \ln(y^+) + B $$

where $\kappa = 0.41$ (von Kármán) and $B = 5.0$ (smooth wall). 

**Step 5:** K-Epsilon wall function boundary conditions (applied at the first cell $y = y_P$):

$$ \frac{\varepsilon_P}{k_P} = \frac{u_\tau^3}{\kappa y_P} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \varepsilon_P = \frac{C_\mu^{3/4} k_P^{3/2}}{\kappa y_P} $$

### 3.4 Summary Analytical Matrix of Models

| Model | PDEs added | Strengths | Weaknesses | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Standard $k$-$\varepsilon$ | 2 ($k$, $\varepsilon$) | Robust, well-validated | Poor for adverse $\nabla p$, round jets | General purpose |
| RNG $k$-$\varepsilon$ | 2 | Better for swirling flows | More expensive | Swirl, separation |
| Realizable $k$-$\varepsilon$ | 2 | Satisfies realizability | Numerical stiffness | Strong curvature, jets |
| Standard $k$-$\omega$ | 2 ($k$, $\omega$) | Excellent near-wall | Sensitive to free-stream $\omega$ | Internal flows |
| $k$-$\omega$ SST (Menter) | 2 | Best near-wall + free-stream | More complex | Aerospace, turbomachinery |
| Spalart–Allmaras | 1 ($\tilde{\nu}$) | Cheap, good for external aero | Not for decaying turbulence | Aerofoils |
| Baldwin–Lomax | 0 | Very cheap | Algebraic, no history | Quick estimates |
| Reynolds Stress Model | 7 | Anisotropy, curvature | Expensive, stiff | Complex 3D flows |
| LES | 1 (SGS) | Resolves large eddies | Massive cost | Research, unsteady |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Derivation Pitfall:** Students often confuse the **production term** $P_k$ (which represents energy transfer from mean to turbulent motion) with the **dissipation** $\varepsilon$ (which represents energy loss to heat via viscosity). Remember: $P_k$ is *positive-definite* in shear flows, while $\varepsilon > 0$ always. Equilibrium means $P_k = \varepsilon$.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**
**Define the Reynolds Stress Tensor. Explain its physical significance in turbulent flow modelling.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Reynolds Stress Tensor** is defined as the time-averaged product of fluctuating velocity components:

$$ \tau_{ij}^{(t)} = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'} $$

It is a second-order symmetric tensor with 6 independent components ($\overline{u'u'}, \overline{v'v'}, \overline{w'w'}, \overline{u'v'}, \overline{u'w'}, \overline{v'w'}$). Physically, each component represents the **average momentum flux per unit area per unit time** carried by turbulent eddies across a surface normal to the $j$-th direction, in the $i$-th direction. The normal stresses ($\overline{u_i'^2}$) drive turbulent diffusion and pressure fluctuations, while the shear stresses ($\overline{u_i' u_j'}$, $i \neq j$) cause enhanced mixing and entrainment. It is the **fundamental unknown** that every turbulence model aims to determine, and its appearance in the RANS equations is the source of the closure problem.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget the *negative sign* in the definition. The convention $\tau_{ij}^{(t)} = -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'}$ ensures the stress is positive when the fluctuation is consistent with viscous stress analogy (fluctuations transport momentum *down* the gradient of mean velocity).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is the Boussinesq Eddy Viscosity Hypothesis? Mention its major limitation.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Boussinesq Eddy Viscosity Hypothesis (1877)** postulates that turbulent momentum transfer is analogous to molecular viscous transfer, expressing the Reynolds stresses as proportional to mean strain rate through an isotropic eddy viscosity $\mu_t$:

$$ -\rho \overline{u_i' u_j'} = \mu_t \left( \frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial \overline{u_j}}{\partial x_i} \right) - \frac{2}{3}\rho k \delta_{ij} $$

This converts a tensor closure problem into a scalar closure problem (compute $\mu_t$ rather than 6 independent stresses). **Major Limitation:** The hypothesis assumes $\mu_t$ is **isotropic** (same in all directions). This fails in flows with strong streamline curvature, rotation, strong acceleration, or rapidly changing strain — e.g., swirling flows, flows with high curvature (U-bends, S-ducts), and rotating machinery. In such cases, the **Reynolds Stress Model (RSM)** which solves transport equations for each component directly is preferred.

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 4]**

**(a) Starting from the instantaneous Navier-Stokes equations, derive the Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) equations for an incompressible flow. Clearly identify the closure problem.** **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** The instantaneous incompressible momentum equation in index notation:

$$ \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial t} + u_j \frac{\partial u_i}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial p}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 u_i}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} $$

along with continuity: $\partial u_i / \partial x_i = 0$.

**Step 2:** Apply Reynolds decomposition: $u_i = \overline{u_i} + u_i'$, $p = \overline{p} + p'$.

**Step 3:** Substitute and apply the time-average operator. The convective nonlinearity generates a surviving correlation:

$$ \overline{u_j u_i} = \overline{u_j}\,\overline{u_i} + \overline{u_j' u_i'} $$

**Step 4:** The averaged equations become:

$$ \frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial t} + \overline{u_j} \frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} = -\frac{1}{\rho}\frac{\partial \overline{p}}{\partial x_i} + \nu \frac{\partial^2 \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j \partial x_j} - \frac{\partial \overline{u_i' u_j'}}{\partial x_j} $$

$$ \frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_i} = 0 $$

**Step 5 — The Closure Problem:** The system has 4 unknowns ($\overline{u}, \overline{v}, \overline{w}, \overline{p}$) but the equation introduces 6 additional unknowns $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ — the Reynolds stresses. Total **10 unknowns, 4 PDEs** ⇒ **under-determined**. A turbulence model provides the extra 6 equations (or reduces the unknowns via Boussinesq hypothesis).

---

**(b) Explain the Standard $k$-$\varepsilon$ model with its complete transport equations, closure coefficients, and near-wall treatment using wall functions.** **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Model Philosophy:** Two-equation eddy viscosity model. Solves PDEs for turbulent kinetic energy $k$ and its dissipation rate $\varepsilon$.

**Step 2 — Eddy Viscosity Formula (dimensional analysis):**

$$ \mu_t = \rho C_\mu \frac{k^2}{\varepsilon}, \quad C_\mu = 0.09 $$

**Step 3 — Transport Equations:**

$$ \frac{\partial (\rho k)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho k \overline{u_j})}{\partial x_j} = P_k - \rho \varepsilon + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\left[\left(\mu + \frac{\mu_t}{\sigma_k}\right)\frac{\partial k}{\partial x_j}\right] $$

$$ \frac{\partial (\rho \varepsilon)}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial (\rho \varepsilon \overline{u_j})}{\partial x_j} = C_{1\varepsilon}\frac{\varepsilon}{k}P_k - C_{2\varepsilon}\frac{\rho \varepsilon^2}{k} + \frac{\partial}{\partial x_j}\left[\left(\mu + \frac{\mu_t}{\sigma_\varepsilon}\right)\frac{\partial \varepsilon}{\partial x_j}\right] $$

**Step 4 — Closure Constants:**

$$ C_{1\varepsilon} = 1.44, \quad C_{2\varepsilon} = 1.92, \quad \sigma_k = 1.0, \quad \sigma_\varepsilon = 1.3 $$

**Step 5 — Production Term:**

$$ P_k = \mu_t \left(\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} + \frac{\partial \overline{u_j}}{\partial x_i}\right)\frac{\partial \overline{u_i}}{\partial x_j} $$

**Step 6 — Wall Function Treatment:** For first cell at distance $y_P$ from wall (with $30 \leq y_P^+ \leq 300$):

$$ \frac{\varepsilon_P}{k_P} = \frac{C_\mu^{3/4} k_P^{1/2}}{\kappa y_P}, \quad \kappa = 0.41 $$

The mean velocity obeys the log-law: $u^+ = \tfrac{1}{\kappa} \ln(y^+) + B$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Most students forget to **mention the Boussinesq assumption explicitly** before writing the $k$-$\varepsilon$ equations. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks if you present the model equations without stating that they rely on the eddy viscosity hypothesis. Also, never quote closure constants from memory without naming the *source* (Launder & Spalding, 1972).

---

#### **QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 4]**

**(a) Discuss the limitations of the Boussinesq Eddy Viscosity Hypothesis. Explain how the $k$-$\omega$ SST model overcomes these limitations.** **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Limitations of Boussinesq Hypothesis:**
1. **Isotropy assumption** — assumes $\mu_t$ is a scalar, identical in all directions. Fails in flows with streamline curvature, rotation, or strong anisotropy.
2. **Equilibrium assumption** — assumes turbulence is in local equilibrium ($P_k = \varepsilon$), which fails in flows with rapid changes (separation, impingement).
3. **Fails in flows with system rotation** (e.g., rotating ducts, turbomachinery).
4. **Fails in flows with strong streamline curvature** (e.g., U-bends, curved ducts).
5. **Inability to capture relaminarization** (transition back to laminar).

**Step 2 — $k$-$\omega$ SST Model (Menter, 1994):**
- Uses **$k$-$\omega$ formulation near walls** (robust boundary layer prediction).
- Uses **$k$-$\varepsilon$ formulation in free-stream** (free-stream independence).
- **Blending Function** $F_1$ transitions between the two models:

$$ \phi = F_1 \phi_1 + (1 - F_1)\phi_2 $$

- **Shear Stress Transport (SST)** modification limits eddy viscosity in adverse pressure gradients:

$$ \nu_t = \frac{a_1 k}{\max(a_1 \omega, S F_2)} $$

where $S$ is the strain rate magnitude and $F_2$ is a second blending function.

**Step 3 — Advantages over Standard $k$-$\varepsilon$:**
- Captures adverse pressure gradient separation accurately.
- Better near-wall treatment (no need for wall functions in many cases).
- Free-stream independent (solves the well-known $k$-$\varepsilon$ free-stream sensitivity issue).

---

**(b) Compare RANS, LES, and DNS approaches for turbulence simulation. Mention the appropriate grid requirements for each.** **(7 Marks)**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

| Feature | DNS | LES | RANS |
|---|---|---|---|
| Eddies resolved | All scales | Large + inertial | None (fully modelled) |
| Modelled | Nothing | Sub-grid scales only | All turbulent scales |
| Governing equations | Instantaneous N-S | Filtered N-S | Time-averaged N-S |
| Closure | None | SGS model | Turbulence model |
| Computational cost | $Re^{3}$ to $Re^{9/4}$ | $Re^{0.4}$ to $Re^{1}$ | $Re$-independent (mesh-wise) |
| Memory required | $Re^{9/4}$ grid points | $Re^{0.4}$ grid points | Standard mesh |
| Time step | Kolmogorov scale | Inertial range scale | Mean flow scale |
| Industry use | Research only | R&D, aerospace | Routine engineering |
| Typical application | Fundamental studies | Aero-acoustics, combustion | Pumps, pipes, HVAC |

**Step 4 — Grid Requirements:**
- **DNS:** Grid spacing $\Delta \sim \eta$ (Kolmogorov scale, $\eta = (\nu^3/\varepsilon)^{1/4}$). For $Re = 10^5$, requires $\sim 10^{11}$ grid points — impractical.
- **LES:** Grid spacing in inertial range, $\Delta \sim (10-100)\eta$. SGS model handles sub-grid.
- **RANS:** Grid only needs to resolve mean flow gradients; $y^+ \approx 1$ (low-Re) or $y^+ \approx 30-300$ (wall functions).

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** A common student error is stating "DNS is the most accurate" without qualifying *why*. Examiners expect: "DNS resolves **all** scales from integral to Kolmogorov — no closure approximation." Equally, students often overstate LES cost without computing Kolmogorov scale dependence. Be quantitative.

---

### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Reynolds decomposition** $\phi = \overline{\phi} + \phi'$ is the starting point of all RANS modelling.
- [x] **Closure problem:** 4 PDEs vs. 10 unknowns (4 mean + 6 Reynolds stresses).
- [x] **Boussinesq Hypothesis:** Reduces 6 unknowns $\overline{u_i' u_j'}$ to 1 scalar $\mu_t$.
- [x] **Eddy viscosity is a flow property, not a fluid property** — varies in space and time.
- [x] **TKE** $k = \tfrac{1}{2}\overline{u_i' u_i'}$ — kinetic energy per unit mass in fluctuating motion.
- [x] **Standard $k$-$\varepsilon$ constants** — memorize $C_\mu = 0.09$, $C_{1\varepsilon} = 1.44$, $C_{2\varepsilon} = 1.92$.
- [x] **Wall function log-law:** $u^+ = \tfrac{1}{\kappa} \ln(y^+) + B$, with $\kappa = 0.41$, $B = 5.0$.
- [x] **$y^+$ ranges:** Viscous sublayer ($< 5$), buffer layer ($5$–$30$), log layer ($30$–$300$), outer layer ($> 300$).
- [x] **$k$-$\omega$ SST** is preferred for adverse pressure gradient flows with separation.
- [x] **$k$-$\varepsilon$** is robust for free-shear flows (jets, mixing layers) but poor near walls.
- [x] **Reynolds Stress Model** is the only practical RANS approach that captures anisotropy.
- [x] **DNS cost** $\sim Re^{9/4}$ — completely impractical for $Re > 10^4$.
- [x] **LES cost** $\sim Re^{0.4}$ — feasible for moderate $Re$.
- [x] **Wall functions save meshes** — but require $30 \leq y^+ \leq 300$.
- [x] **Production $P_k \geq 0$** always (in shear flows); **Dissipation $\varepsilon > 0$** always.
- [x] **Equilibrium:** $P_k = \varepsilon$ is the underlying assumption of algebraic closure models.
- [x] **KTU favourite question:** "Derive RANS equations and state the closure problem" — almost guaranteed!
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of Multiphase Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Classification of Multiphase Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Multiphase flow** is defined as the simultaneous flow of two or more thermodynamically distinct phases (solid, liquid, or gas) that interact with one another across moving interfaces. In Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD), a "phase" is not restricted to solid/liquid/gas alone — it may also represent two immiscible liquids, droplets of a single species, or even particles of different size distributions within a continuous carrier.

The classification of multiphase flow is the systematic categorization of these flows based on:
1. **The number and nature of phases** present.
2. **The topological/morphological arrangement** of one phase within another.
3. **The continuum treatment** adopted in the mathematical model.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** A *single* substance undergoing a phase change (e.g., boiling water → steam) is a **phase-change flow**, NOT a multiphase flow. Multiphase flow strictly requires two or more *distinct* phases coexisting without undergoing transformation.

## 1.2 The Four Canonical Phase Combinations

| Combination | Common Name | Industrial Example |
|---|---|---|
| Gas + Liquid | Gas–Liquid Flow | Bubble columns, oil & gas pipelines |
| Gas + Solid | Gas–Solid Flow (Pneumatic Transport) | Coal-fired boilers, cyclone separators |
| Liquid + Solid | Liquid–Solid Flow (Slurry Flow) | Mineral slurry pipelines, blood flow |
| Gas + Liquid + Solid | Three-Phase Flow | Slurry bubble column reactors (e.g., Fischer–Tropsch) |

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Shaken Soda Bottle:**
> Imagine vigorously shaking a closed bottle of carbonated water. Before opening it, the bottle contains **carbon dioxide gas (bubbles)** dispersed inside **liquid water**. This is a classic **gas–liquid dispersed flow**. When you open the cap, the bubbles rise to form a single expanding gas pocket above the liquid — this transition changes the *flow regime* from **dispersed** to **separated (stratified)** flow, even though the phases are identical. The "classification" tells you whether the phases are mixed like a salad or layered like oil on water.

## 1.4 Key Terminology in Multiphase CFD

- **Continuous Phase:** The phase that fills the entire domain and acts as the carrier medium (e.g., water in a bubble column).
- **Dispersed Phase:** The phase existing as droplets, bubbles, or particles embedded within the continuous phase.
- **Volume Fraction ($\alpha_k$):** The fraction of the local control volume occupied by phase $k$. Mathematically, $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 + \dots + \alpha_n = 1$.
- **Primary Phase:** In Ansys Fluent terminology, the continuous phase selected by the user.
- **Secondary Phase(s):** All other phases modeled explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification System

The classification of multiphase flow is structured in a three-tier hierarchy recognized in standard CFD literature:

### Tier 1 — Classification by Phase Combination
- Two-phase (most common, exhaustively studied).
- Three-phase (rare, computationally expensive).

### Tier 2 — Classification by Topology / Flow Regime
This is the most frequently tested classification in KTU examinations. The four broad topological categories are:

1. **Dispersed Flow** — One phase broken into discrete entities (bubbles, drops, particles) within the other.
2. **Separated (Stratified) Flow** — Phases are separated by a distinct continuous interface.
3. **Mixed (Transitional) Flow** — Co-existence of dispersed and separated regions.
4. **Continuous (Dispersed) Two-Phase Flow** — Neither phase is fully continuous; both are intertwined (e.g., mist flow where both gas and liquid droplets are dispersed).

### Tier 3 — Classification by Continuum Assumption
- **Eulerian–Eulerian (EE):** Both phases treated as interpenetrating continua.
- **Eulerian–Lagrangian (EL):** Continuous phase solved in Eulerian frame; dispersed phase tracked as discrete parcels.
- **Volume of Fluid (VOF):** Interface-tracking method (suitable for separated flows).
- **Mixture Model:** Solves a single momentum equation for the mixture with slip.

## 2.2 Flow Pattern Map — Mandhane Map (Horizontal Gas–Liquid)

For horizontal pipe flow, the empirical **Mandhane map** is the most cited classification tool. It uses superficial velocities of gas ($V_{SG}$) and liquid ($V_{SL}$) on logarithmic axes to demarcate flow regimes: **stratified, wavy, plug, slug, annular, dispersed bubble, and mist flow**.

The transitions on this map are governed by three key dimensionless numbers:

### 2.2.1 Eötvös Number (Eo)

The ratio of gravitational to surface tension forces:

$$Eo = \frac{g (\rho_L - \rho_G) D^2}{\sigma}$$

where $g$ is gravitational acceleration, $\rho_L$ and $\rho_G$ are liquid and gas densities, $D$ is pipe diameter, and $\sigma$ is surface tension. Used to characterize bubble/droplet shape relative to gravity.

### 2.2.2 Morton Number (Mo)

A property of the fluid pair alone (geometry-free):

$$Mo = \frac{g \mu_L^4 (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{\rho_L^2 \sigma^3}$$

where $\mu_L$ is liquid dynamic viscosity. High $Mo$ indicates a viscous, gravity-dominated fluid pair.

### 2.2.3 Froude Number (Fr)

Ratio of inertial to gravitational forces:

$$Fr = \frac{V^2}{g D}$$

### 2.2.4 Weber Number (We)

Ratio of inertial to surface tension forces:

$$We = \frac{\rho V^2 L}{\sigma}$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **exam-essential**. Memorize the symbols and the physical meaning.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Key | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\alpha_1 + \alpha_2 + \dots + \alpha_n = 1$ | $\alpha_k$ = volume fraction of phase $k$ | Volume conservation across all phases |
| 2 | $\rho_m = \sum_k \alpha_k \rho_k$ | $\rho_m$ = mixture density, $\rho_k$ = phase density | Mixture (effective) density |
| 3 | $\mu_m = \sum_k \alpha_k \mu_k$ | $\mu_m$ = mixture viscosity | Mixture (effective) viscosity |
| 4 | $Eo = \frac{g (\rho_L - \rho_G) D^2}{\sigma}$ | $D$ = characteristic length, $\sigma$ = surface tension | Gravitational vs. surface tension forces |
| 5 | $Mo = \frac{g \mu_L^4 (\rho_L - \rho_G)}{\rho_L^2 \sigma^3}$ | $\mu_L$ = liquid viscosity | Fluid-pair property (no geometry) |
| 6 | $Fr = \frac{V^2}{g D}$ | $V$ = velocity | Inertia vs. gravity |
| 7 | $We = \frac{\rho V^2 L}{\sigma}$ | $L$ = length scale | Inertia vs. surface tension |
| 8 | $Re_p = \frac{\rho_c V_{slip} d_p}{\mu_c}$ | $d_p$ = particle diameter, subscript $c$ = continuous | Particle Reynolds number |
| 9 | $\frac{\partial (\alpha_k \rho_k)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\alpha_k \rho_k \vec{V}_k) = 0$ | $\vec{V}_k$ = velocity of phase $k$ | **Continuity equation for phase $k$** |
| 10 | $\sum_k \alpha_k = 1$ | — | Volume constraint (must be enforced in solver) |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility of Multiphase Flow Classification:**
> - **Oil & Gas Industry:** Classification determines whether slug flow may occur in sub-sea risers, dictating material selection and fatigue life.
> - **Nuclear Reactors:** Predicting critical heat flux requires classification of two-phase (steam-water) flow regimes.
> - **Chemical Reactors:** Slurry bubble column hydrodynamics depends on bubble flow regime (homogeneous vs. heterogeneous).
> - **Pharmaceuticals:** Spray-coating processes rely on classification of droplet size distributions.
> - **Pneumatic Conveying:** Erosion prediction in pipelines requires knowledge of particle flow regime (dilute vs. dense phase).

## 2.5 Choice of Numerical Model by Classification

| Flow Classification | Recommended CFD Model | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Dispersed bubbles, droplets, particles (low $\alpha$) | Eulerian–Lagrangian (DPM) | Each particle tracked individually |
| Dispersed flow with high $\alpha$ (dense) | Eulerian–Eulerian | Particle-particle interactions dominate |
| Stratified / free-surface flow | VOF | Sharp interface tracking required |
| Slug / churn flow (transitional) | Mixture Model or VOF | Reduces computational cost of full EE |
| Three-phase flow | Eulerian–Eulerian (three phases) | Only EE framework naturally extends to $n$-phases |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volume Fraction Constraint

**Statement to derive:** The sum of volume fractions across all phases in a multiphase control volume equals unity.

**Step 1 — Definition of Volume Fraction.**
Consider a control volume $V_{CV}$ containing $n$ phases. The volume occupied by phase $k$ is $V_k$. The volume fraction of phase $k$ is defined as:

$$\alpha_k = \frac{V_k}{V_{CV}}$$

**Step 2 — Summation Across All Phases.**

$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} \alpha_k = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \frac{V_k}{V_{CV}} = \frac{1}{V_{CV}} \sum_{k=1}^{n} V_k$$

**Step 3 — Apply Physical Principle (Exhaustive Partitioning).**
The phases are assumed to be *non-overlapping* and *collectively exhaustive* — together they fill the entire control volume with no voids:

$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} V_k = V_{CV}$$

**Step 4 — Final Simplification.**

$$\sum_{k=1}^{n} \alpha_k = \frac{V_{CV}}{V_{CV}} = 1$$

$$\boxed{\sum_{k=1}^{n} \alpha_k = 1}$$

**Engineering Insight:** This constraint is enforced in commercial CFD solvers (e.g., Fluent, OpenFOAM) as a built-in condition. Violating it produces a divergence error in the pressure-correction equation.

## 3.2 Derivation: Mixture Density

**Step 1 — Mass of Each Phase.**
Mass of phase $k$ within $V_{CV}$:

$$m_k = \rho_k V_k = \rho_k \alpha_k V_{CV}$$

**Step 2 — Total Mass.**

$$m_{total} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} m_k = V_{CV} \sum_{k=1}^{n} \alpha_k \rho_k$$

**Step 3 — Definition of Mixture Density.**

$$\rho_m = \frac{m_{total}}{V_{CV}}$$

**Step 4 — Combine.**

$$\boxed{\rho_m = \sum_{k=1}^{n} \alpha_k \rho_k}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Continuity Equation for Phase $k$ (Eulerian–Eulerian)

**Step 1 — Starting Point: Single-Phase Continuity Equation.**
For a single phase, conservation of mass is:

$$\frac{\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V}) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Introduce Phase-Specific Variables.**
For phase $k$, the local density of phase $k$ per unit mixture volume is $\alpha_k \rho_k$. The convective flux of phase $k$ is $\alpha_k \rho_k \vec{V}_k$. Substituting these into the generic continuity form:

$$\frac{\partial (\alpha_k \rho_k)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\alpha_k \rho_k \vec{V}_k) = S_k$$

where $S_k$ is a source term representing mass transfer to phase $k$ (e.g., evaporation: $S_l = -S_g$). In the absence of phase change, $S_k = 0$:

$$\frac{\partial (\alpha_k \rho_k)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\alpha_k \rho_k \vec{V}_k) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Apply Chain Rule to the First Term.**

$$\rho_k \frac{\partial \alpha_k}{\partial t} + \alpha_k \frac{\partial \rho_k}{\partial t} + \rho_k \nabla \cdot (\alpha_k \vec{V}_k) + \alpha_k \vec{V}_k \cdot \nabla \rho_k = 0$$

**Step 4 — Recognize the Single-Phase Continuity (Embedded).**
For each phase treated as a compressible/incompressible continuum, its *own* single-phase continuity is:

$$\frac{\partial \rho_k}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho_k \vec{V}_k) = 0$$

Multiplying by $\alpha_k$:

$$\alpha_k \frac{\partial \rho_k}{\partial t} + \alpha_k \vec{V}_k \cdot \nabla \rho_k + \alpha_k \rho_k (\nabla \cdot \vec{V}_k) = 0$$

**Step 5 — Substitute Back Into Step 3.**

$$\rho_k \frac{\partial \alpha_k}{\partial t} + \rho_k \nabla \cdot (\alpha_k \vec{V}_k) + [\alpha_k \text{ single-phase continuity}] = 0$$

Since the bracketed term is zero (each phase conserves its own mass):

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \alpha_k}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\alpha_k \vec{V}_k) = \frac{S_k}{\rho_k}}$$

This is the **volume fraction transport equation** — it is the equation solved in VOF, mixture, and EE multiphase models.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:** The volume fraction equation is *not* derived from mass conservation alone — it follows from combining mass conservation for each phase with the constraint $\sum \alpha_k = 1$. Students often confuse this with a pure mass-balance equation.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Multiphase Models

| Feature | VOF | Mixture Model | Eulerian–Eulerian | Eulerian–Lagrangian (DPM) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Phases Tracked | 2–3 (sharp interface) | 2 (one momentum eq.) | 2+ (one per phase) | Continuous + discrete parcels |
| Interface Resolution | Yes (sharp) | No (smeared) | No (smeared) | No (lagrangian) |
| Volume Fractions Solved | Yes (1 eq./phase) | Yes (1 eq./phase) | Yes (1 eq./phase) | Computed from particle count |
| Computational Cost | Moderate–High | Low | High | Moderate (depends on $N_p$) |
| Best For | Free surface, sloshing | Slug, churn, settling | Bubbly, fluidized bed | Spray, combustion, particles |
| Particle-Particle Interaction | Not modeled | Limited | Constitutive models | Collision models (DEM) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Multiphase Flow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the complete hierarchical classification system as prescribed in the KTU 2024 syllabus for Module 4.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[MULTIPHASE FLOW] --> B[By Number of Phases]
    A --> C[By Topology or Flow Regime]
    A --> D[By Continuum Treatment]

    B --> B1[Two Phase Flow]
    B --> B2[Three Phase Flow]

    B1 --> B1A[Gas Liquid]
    B1 --> B1B[Gas Solid]
    B1 --> B1C[Liquid Solid]

    B1A --> B1A1[Bubble Column]
    B1A --> B1A2[Pipe Flow]

    B1B --> B1B1[Fluidized Bed]
    B1B --> B1B2[Cyclone]

    B1C --> B1C1[Slurry Pipeline]
    B1C --> B1C2[Sedimentation]

    B2 --> B2A[Gas Liquid Solid]

    C --> C1[Dispersed Flow]
    C --> C2[Separated Flow]
    C --> C3[Mixed Flow]
    C --> C4[Continuous Dispersed]

    C1 --> C1A[Bubbly]
    C1 --> C1B[Droplet]
    C1 --> C1C[Particulate]

    C2 --> C2A[Stratified]
    C2 --> C2B[Wavy]
    C2 --> C2C[Annular]

    C3 --> C3A[Slug]
    C3 --> C3B[Churn]
    C3 --> C3C[Plug]

    C4 --> C4A[Mist]
    C4 --> C4B[Fine Dispersion]

    D --> D1[Eulerian Eulerian]
    D --> D2[Eulerian Lagrangian DPM]
    D --> D3[Mixture Model]
    D --> D4[Volume of Fluid VOF]

    D1 --> D1A[Dense Bubbly Flow]
    D1 --> D1B[Fluidized Beds]

    D2 --> D2A[Spray Drying]
    D2 --> D2B[Coal Combustion]

    D3 --> D3A[Slug Flow]
    D3 --> D3B[Settling]

    D4 --> D4A[Free Surface]
    D4 --> D4B[Tank Sloshing]
```

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Selecting a Multiphase Model in CFD

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start: Define Problem]) --> q1{Is there a sharp free surface or interface?}
    q1 -- Yes --> vof[Use VOF Model]
    q1 -- No --> q2{Is the dispersed phase volume fraction greater than 10 percent?}
    q2 -- No --> dpm[Use Eulerian Lagrangian DPM]
    q2 -- Yes --> q3{Are phases fully interpenetrating continua?}
    q3 -- Yes --> q4{Is the flow highly turbulent with strong interphase coupling?}
    q4 -- Yes --> ee[Use Eulerian Eulerian]
    q4 -- No --> mix[Use Mixture Model]
    q3 -- No --> strat[Use Stratified or Two Fluid Model]
    vof --> end1([End])
    dpm --> end1
    ee --> end1
    mix --> end1
    strat --> end1
```

## 4.3 Schematic of a Bubble Column (Gas–Liquid Dispersed Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph column[Vertical Bubble Column Reactor]
        direction TB
        inl[Gas Inlet Sparger] --> bub1[Dispersed Bubble Zone]
        bub1 --> bub2[Coalescence Region]
        bub2 --> bub3[Bubble Rise and Expansion]
        bub3 --> free[Freeboard Region]
        free --> outl[Gas Outlet]
        liquid1[Continuous Liquid Phase] -.surrounds.-> bub1
        liquid1 -.surrounds.-> bub2
        liquid1 -.surrounds.-> bub3
    end

    inlet_arrow[Liquid Inlet] -.-> liquid1
    outlet_arrow[Liquid Outlet] -.-> free
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Interphase Coupling in Eulerian–Eulerian Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Continuity Equation Phase 1] --> B[Volume Fraction Constraint]
    C[Continuity Equation Phase 2] --> B
    B --> D[Momentum Equation Phase 1]
    B --> E[Momentum Equation Phase 2]
    D <--> F[Interphase Drag Force]
    E <--> F
    D <--> G[Interphase Heat Transfer]
    E <--> G
    D <--> H[Interphase Mass Transfer]
    E <--> H
    F --> I[Pressure Velocity Coupling]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J[Solution]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define multiphase flow. List the four possible phase combinations encountered in multiphase flow.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Multiphase flow is the simultaneous flow of two or more thermodynamically distinct phases (solid, liquid, gas) coexisting within the same domain, interacting across moving interfaces (1 mark).

The four canonical phase combinations are:
1. Gas + Liquid (e.g., bubble column) (0.5 mark)
2. Gas + Solid (e.g., pneumatic transport) (0.5 mark)
3. Liquid + Solid (e.g., slurry pipeline) (0.5 mark)
4. Gas + Liquid + Solid (e.g., three-phase slurry reactor) (0.5 mark)

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Distinguish between VOF, Mixture, and Eulerian–Eulerian multiphase models.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **VOF (Volume of Fluid):** A surface-tracking model applied to a fixed Eulerian mesh, used when a sharp interface between phases must be resolved. A single set of momentum equations is shared by the phases, and the volume fraction equation tracks the interface (1 mark).
- **Mixture Model:** Solves a single momentum equation for the mixture (combined phases) and a *second* equation for the relative (slip) velocity. It is computationally cheaper than EE and suitable for slug/churn flows (1 mark).
- **Eulerian–Eulerian (EE):** Solves a separate set of continuity and momentum equations for *each* phase, treating all phases as interpenetrating continua. It is the most general and computationally expensive (1 mark).

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A: `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**a) Classify multiphase flow based on the number of phases, topology, and continuum treatment. Provide two industrial examples for each category. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a)(i) — Classification by Number of Phases (2 marks):**

Multiphase flow is divided into two categories based on the number of phases:

1. **Two-Phase Flow:** Two distinct phases coexist. Examples: (a) gas–liquid flow in an oil pipeline, (b) gas–solid flow in a cyclone separator. (1 mark)
2. **Three-Phase Flow:** Three distinct phases coexist. Examples: (a) gas–liquid–solid slurry bubble column, (b) oil–water–sand flow in a petroleum reservoir pipeline. (1 mark)

**Part (a)(ii) — Classification by Topology (3 marks):**

Based on how the phases are distributed spatially, multiphase flows are classified into four regimes:

1. **Dispersed Flow:** One phase exists as discrete entities (bubbles, droplets, particles) within a continuous carrier. Examples: bubbles in a bubble column reactor; dust particles in air in a cleanroom. (0.75 mark)
2. **Separated (Stratified) Flow:** Phases are separated by a continuous interface. Examples: oil and water in a horizontal pipe; air above water in a partially filled tank. (0.75 mark)
3. **Mixed (Transitional) Flow:** Co-existence of dispersed and separated structures. Examples: slug flow in a horizontal pipeline; churn-turbulent flow in a boiling water reactor. (0.75 mark)
4. **Continuous Dispersed Flow:** Both phases are dispersed; neither is fully continuous. Examples: mist flow where liquid droplets are suspended in a gas that itself is bubbled through liquid. (0.75 mark)

**Part (a)(iii) — Classification by Continuum Treatment (2 marks):**

1. **Eulerian–Eulerian:** Both phases treated as interpenetrating continua. Example: dense fluidized bed simulation. (0.5 mark)
2. **Eulerian–Lagrangian (DPM):** Continuous phase in Eulerian frame, dispersed phase tracked as discrete parcels. Example: fuel spray in a diesel engine. (0.5 mark)
3. **Volume of Fluid (VOF):** Tracks the interface of two (or more) immiscible fluids. Example: dam-break simulation. (0.5 mark)
4. **Mixture Model:** Solves a single momentum equation for the mixture. Example: low-load slurry flow. (0.5 mark)

**b) With the aid of a neat sketch, explain the Mandhane flow-pattern map for horizontal gas–liquid flow. List all the regimes on this map. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (b)(i) — Mandhane Map Description (3 marks):**
The Mandhane map is an empirical flow-regime map for *horizontal* gas–liquid pipe flow. It uses the **superficial gas velocity ($V_{SG}$)** on the *y-axis* and the **superficial liquid velocity ($V_{SL}$)** on the *x-axis*, both plotted on a logarithmic scale. The map is constructed from extensive experimental data across a wide range of pipe diameters, fluids, and operating conditions. It partitions the $(V_{SL}, V_{SG})$ plane into distinct flow-regime zones, with each regime representing a specific topological arrangement of the gas and liquid phases within the pipe.

**Part (b)(ii) — List of Regimes (2 marks):**
The seven regimes on the Mandhane map are:

1. **Stratified Flow** — Liquid flows along the bottom of the pipe, gas flows above, with a smooth, flat interface.
2. **Wavy Flow** — Same as stratified, but the interface exhibits gravity-driven waves.
3. **Slug Flow** — Periodic large gas pockets (Taylor bubbles) separated by liquid slugs.
4. **Plug Flow** — Smaller, less frequent gas pockets than slug flow.
5. **Annular Flow** — Gas flows as a central core surrounded by a liquid film along the pipe wall.
6. **Dispersed Bubble Flow** — Bubbles dispersed throughout a continuous liquid.
7. **Mist Flow** — Liquid droplets dispersed in a continuous gas.

**Part (b)(iii) — Sketch (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph mandhane[Mandhane Map Horizontal Gas Liquid Flow Log Log Axes]
        direction TB
        sl[Stratified at Low Velocities] --> wv[Wavy Interface]
        wv --> pl[Plug Flow]
        pl --> slg[Slug Flow]
        slg --> ann[Annular Flow at High Gas Velocity]
        ann --> mst[Mist Flow at Very High Gas Velocity]
        bbl[Dispersed Bubble Flow at High Liquid Velocity] --- slg
    end
    axisX[Superficial Liquid Velocity VSL] -.horizontal axis.-> mandhane
    axisY[Superficial Gas Velocity VSG] -.vertical axis.-> mandhane
```

**Transitions on the map** are governed by **Eötvös number (Eo)**, **Morton number (Mo)**, and the **Froude number (Fr)** of the gas, which capture the balance between gravity, surface tension, and inertia.

---

### Question B: `[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]`

**a) Derive the volume-fraction transport equation starting from the continuity equation for each phase. Clearly state the assumptions. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. The flow is isothermal (no energy equation coupling considered here).
2. No phase change between the phases (no mass transfer across interfaces).
3. Each phase is treated as a continuum in the Eulerian framework.
4. The control volume is large compared to the interphase spacing but small compared to the macroscopic flow length scale (separation of scales).

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1 — Single-Phase Continuity for Each Phase $k$ (1 mark):**
For phase $k$ treated as its own continuum:

$$\frac{\partial \rho_k}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho_k \vec{V}_k) = 0$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by Volume Fraction (1 mark):**
Multiply both sides by $\alpha_k$:

$$\alpha_k \frac{\partial \rho_k}{\partial t} + \alpha_k \nabla \cdot (\rho_k \vec{V}_k) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Single-Phase Continuity for Phase $k$ in a Multiphase Context (1 mark):**
The mass of phase $k$ per unit volume of the mixture is $\alpha_k \rho_k$, and its convective flux is $\alpha_k \rho_k \vec{V}_k$. The continuity equation for phase $k$ in the multiphase framework becomes:

$$\frac{\partial (\alpha_k \rho_k)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\alpha_k \rho_k \vec{V}_k) = 0$$

**Step 4 — Expand Using Product Rule (1 mark):**

$$\rho_k \frac{\partial \alpha_k}{\partial t} + \alpha_k \frac{\partial \rho_k}{\partial t} + \rho_k \nabla \cdot (\alpha_k \vec{V}_k) + \alpha_k \vec{V}_k \cdot \nabla \rho_k = 0$$

**Step 5 — Apply Step 1 to Cancel Terms (1 mark):**
The terms $\alpha_k \frac{\partial \rho_k}{\partial t} + \alpha_k \vec{V}_k \cdot \nabla \rho_k + \alpha_k \rho_k (\nabla \cdot \vec{V}_k) = 0$ (from the single-phase continuity, multiplied by $\alpha_k$). Substituting:

$$\rho_k \frac{\partial \alpha_k}{\partial t} + \rho_k \nabla \cdot (\alpha_k \vec{V}_k) = 0$$

Dividing by $\rho_k$:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial \alpha_k}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\alpha_k \vec{V}_k) = 0}$$

This is the **volume-fraction transport equation** for phase $k$ in the absence of phase change.

**b) Discuss the suitability of the VOF, Mixture, and Eulerian–Eulerian models for the following four scenarios. Justify each choice in terms of physics and computational cost. (7 marks)**

**Scenarios:**
1. Fuel injection in a diesel engine cylinder.
2. Water–oil flow in a horizontal separator.
3. Catalytic fluidized-bed reactor for hydrocarbon cracking.
4. Air–water flow with high gas content in a vertical riser.

**Model Solution:**

**Scenario 1 — Diesel Engine Fuel Injection (1.75 marks):**
**Recommended Model: Eulerian–Lagrangian (DPM) or VOF.**
The injected fuel forms discrete droplets of varying sizes and shapes, injected at high pressure. The Lagrangian framework (DPM) tracks individual droplets accounting for drag, evaporation, and turbulent dispersion. VOF is suitable if the focus is on the initial liquid-jet breakup near the injector nozzle (sharp interface).
- *Physics:* Discrete droplets, high density variation.
- *Cost:* Moderate (DPM) to high (VOF with surface tension).

**Scenario 2 — Water–Oil in a Horizontal Separator (1.75 marks):**
**Recommended Model: VOF.**
The two immiscible fluids form a clear, distinct interface that is the focus of the design (location of the oil–water interface determines separation efficiency). VOF explicitly tracks this interface using a sharp or compressive scheme.
- *Physics:* Sharp interface, low mixing between phases.
- *Cost:* Moderate.

**Scenario 3 — Catalytic Fluidized Bed (1.75 marks):**
**Recommended Model: Eulerian–Eulerian (EE) with Granular Viscosity Model.**
The solid catalyst particles constitute a dense phase (volume fraction > 50%), and the gas is a continuous phase. The granular temperature model captures particle–particle collisions and packing. The Lagrangian (DPM) approach is unsuitable at such high loadings due to the prohibitive number of particles.
- *Physics:* Interpenetrating continua, high solid loading.
- *Cost:* High (two sets of momentum equations + granular model).

**Scenario 4 — High Gas Content in a Vertical Riser (1.75 marks):**
**Recommended Model: Mixture Model or Eulerian–Eulerian.**
For high gas content, bubbles coalesce into larger structures (churn, slug flow), making tracking of discrete bubbles (DPM) impractical. The mixture model can capture the slip between gas and liquid at moderate cost; if accurate phase-separation data is required, EE is preferred.
- *Physics:* Slug/churn flow, high gas content, large interfaces.
- *Cost:* Low to high (mixture < EE).

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Examinations:**
> 1. **Confusing VOF with Mixture Model:** Students often state that "VOF solves separate momentum equations for each phase." This is **incorrect** — VOF shares a *single* momentum equation between phases. The mix-up costs 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the volume fraction constraint $\sum \alpha_k = 1$:** In derivations, students often derive the transport equation but omit the closure constraint, losing 1 mark.
> 3. **Calling DPM "Eulerian":** The Discrete Phase Model is Eulerian–*Lagrangian*. Mistakenly calling it "Eulerian" is treated as a conceptual error.
> 4. **Misstating the Mandhane map axes:** Always specify the axes: **superficial gas velocity** (y-axis) vs. **superficial liquid velocity** (x-axis). Vague statements lose 1 mark.
> 5. **Confusing Eo and Mo:** $Eo$ contains the geometric length $D$; $Mo$ does not. Examiners test whether students understand this distinction.
> 6. **Skipping assumptions in derivations:** Always state the assumption (e.g., no phase change, no mass transfer) *before* starting the derivation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Classification of Multiphase Flow**

- [ ] **Multiphase flow definition:** Two or more distinct phases coexisting, *not* a single substance undergoing phase change.
- [ ] **Four phase combinations:** Gas–Liquid, Gas–Solid, Liquid–Solid, Gas–Liquid–Solid.
- [ ] **Topological classification:** Dispersed, Separated (Stratified), Mixed (Transitional), Continuous Dispersed.
- [ ] **Continuum models:** VOF (sharp interface, single momentum eq.), Mixture (slip velocity), Eulerian–Eulerian (per-phase momentum), Eulerian–Lagrangian/DPM (parcel tracking).
- [ ] **Volume fraction constraint:** $\sum \alpha_k = 1$ — must be enforced as closure.
- [ ] **Mixture density:** $\rho_m = \sum \alpha_k \rho_k$.
- [ ] **Mixture viscosity:** $\mu_m = \sum \alpha_k \mu_k$.
- [ ] **Volume fraction transport equation:** $\partial \alpha_k / \partial t + \nabla \cdot (\alpha_k \vec{V}_k) = 0$ (no mass transfer).
- [ ] **Dimensionless numbers:** Eo (gravity vs. surface tension), Mo (fluid property), Fr (inertia vs. gravity), We (inertia vs. surface tension), $Re_p$ (particle Reynolds).
- [ ] **Mandhane map:** Log-log plot of $V_{SG}$ vs. $V_{SL}$; seven regimes — stratified, wavy, plug, slug, annular, dispersed bubble, mist.
- [ ] **Model selection rule of thumb:** Sharp interface → VOF; dilute discrete particles → DPM; dense interpenetrating phases → EE; transitional flows → Mixture.
- [ ] **Three-phase flows:** Only the Eulerian–Eulerian framework naturally extends to $n$-phase analysis without modifications.
- [ ] **Pitfalls to avoid:** Confusing VOF and Mixture model assumptions; omitting the volume-fraction constraint; mislabelling axes on flow-pattern maps.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Examples
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Industrial Examples in Computational Fluid Dynamics

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECHT635 - COMPUTATIONAL FLUID DYNAMICS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Industrial Examples

---

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PECHT635), **Industrial Examples** refer to the practical application of CFD methodologies to solve real-world engineering problems involving complex geometries, turbulent flows, heat transfer, and multiphysics phenomena. These case studies demonstrate the translation of theoretical governing equations (Navier–Stokes, energy, species transport) into discretized numerical solutions using the Finite Volume Method (FVM) on industrially relevant domains.

The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the following benchmark industrial problems:

1. **Flow through sudden pipe expansions and contractions** (internal flow benchmark)
2. **External aerodynamic flows** over bluff bodies (cylinders, cars, airfoils)
3. **Turbomachinery flows** (pumps, compressors, turbines)
4. **Convective heat transfer** in ducts, channels, and heat exchangers
5. **Combustion chamber analysis** (reacting flows)
6. **Electronic cooling** and HVAC systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The focus is **NOT** on solving industrial problems from scratch, but on understanding (i) the physical setup, (ii) the boundary conditions prescribed, (iii) the turbulence model selected, (iv) grid strategy employed, and (v) the validation against experimental data.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of CFD industrial simulations like a **virtual wind tunnel**. Just as an architect builds a miniature model of a building and places it in a wind tunnel to test how air flows around it, CFD engineers build a digital replica of a pipe junction, jet engine, or car on a computer and "blow" virtual air through it. The computer then tells us *exactly* where pressure drops, where vortices form, and where hot spots appear — without cutting a single piece of metal.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Industrial Benchmark Constants** to remember:
> - Reynolds Number criterion for turbulent pipe flow: **Re > 4000**
> - Standard air properties at 25°C: ρ = **1.184 kg/m³**, μ = **1.849 × 10⁻⁵ kg/m·s**
> - Standard drag coefficient for a smooth sphere at high Re: **C_D ≈ 0.44**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Breakdown of How Industrial CFD Problems Are Solved

Every industrial CFD problem follows the **KTU-mandated 7-step framework**:

1. **Define the Physical Problem** — Identify geometry, flow regime, governing physics.
2. **Select Governing Equations** — Incompressible vs. compressible, laminar vs. turbulent, isothermal vs. heat transfer.
3. **Choose Turbulence Model** — k-ε (standard, RNG, Realizable), k-ω SST, Spalart-Allmaras, or LES.
4. **Generate Computational Grid** — Structured (hexahedral), unstructured (tetrahedral), or hybrid.
5. **Apply Boundary Conditions** — Inlet, outlet, walls (no-slip, adiabatic/isothermal), symmetry.
6. **Solve and Post-process** — Run solver, monitor residuals, extract C_p, C_D, C_L, Nu, etc.
7. **Validate** — Compare against experimental data (e.g., Schlichting, ASME benchmarks).

### 2.2 Industrial Example 1 — Flow in a Sudden Pipe Expansion

Consider a sudden expansion from diameter **D₁** to **D₂**, with area ratio **AR = (D₂/D₁)²**.

**Physical Phenomenon:** Flow separation, recirculation zone, reattachment.

**Key Non-Dimensional Numbers:**
- Reynolds number based on D₁: Re_D1 = (ρ U₁ D₁) / μ
- Expansion ratio: ER = D₂ / D₁
- Recirculation length: **X_r / D₁ ≈ 0.5 to 1.0** for ER = 2

**Loss Coefficient (Borda–Carnot equation):**

$$K_L = \left(1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2$$

### 2.3 Industrial Example 2 — Flow Over a Circular Cylinder

A canonical bluff-body benchmark used in virtually every CFD textbook (Versteeg & Malalasekera, Chapter 10).

**Critical Reynolds Numbers:**
- **Re < 1:** Stokes flow, no separation.
- **Re ≈ 40:** Onset of vortex shedding (von Kármán street).
- **40 < Re < 10⁵:** Subcritical regime, C_D ≈ 1.2.
- **Re > 10⁵:** Supercritical, drag crisis, C_D drops to ~0.3.

**Strouhal Number for vortex shedding:**

$$St = \frac{f \, D}{U_\infty} \approx 0.21 \quad (\text{for } 10^3 < \text{Re} < 10^5)$$

### 2.4 Industrial Example 3 — Flow and Heat Transfer in a Heat Exchanger

For heat exchangers and electronic cooling, the **Nusselt number** is the primary output:

$$\text{Nu} = \frac{h \, D_h}{k_f}$$

**Dittus-Boelter correlation** (turbulent, heated fluid):

$$\text{Nu} = 0.023 \, \text{Re}^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{n}$$

where **n = 0.4** for heating and **n = 0.3** for cooling.

**Sieder-Tate correlation** (viscous correction):

$$\text{Nu} = 0.027 \, \text{Re}^{0.8} \, \text{Pr}^{1/3} \left(\frac{\mu}{\mu_w}\right)^{0.14}$$

### 2.5 Industrial Example 4 — Turbomachinery (Centrifugal Pump)

For a centrifugal pump impeller, the **Euler Turbomachine Equation** governs energy transfer:

$$W_{shaft} = U_2 \, V_{\theta 2} - U_1 \, V_{\theta 1}$$

- **U:** blade tangential velocity = ω r
- **V_θ:** absolute fluid tangential (swirl) velocity

**Head developed by pump (Euler form):**

$$H = \frac{U_2 \, V_{\theta 2} - U_1 \, V_{\theta 1}}{g}$$

### 2.6 Industrial Example 5 — Combustion Chamber (Reactor Network)

For a can-type combustion chamber, CFD solves **species transport** equations:

$$\frac{\partial (\rho Y_i)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{V} Y_i) = -\nabla \cdot \vec{J_i} + \dot{R_i}$$

where **Ř_i** is the net rate of production of species *i* by chemical reaction.

### 2.7 Industrial Example 6 — Electronic Cooling (Forced Convection in Channel)

For a chip mounted on a PCB with forced air cooling:

$$\text{Q}_{conv} = h \, A \, (T_s - T_\infty)$$

The convective coefficient is non-dimensionalized via Nu and correlated to Re and Pr.

### 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Industrial Application | Governing Equation | Key Output Parameter | Typical Range / Value |
|---|------------------------|--------------------|-----------------------|-----------------------|
| 1 | Sudden expansion | Borda-Carnot loss | K_L | 0.5 to 1.0 |
| 2 | Flow over cylinder | Navier-Stokes + k-ε | C_D | 1.0 to 1.2 (subcritical) |
| 3 | Vortex shedding | Strouhal law | St | 0.18 to 0.22 |
| 4 | Pipe friction | Hagen-Poiseuille / Colebrook | f (Darcy) | 0.02 to 0.08 |
| 5 | Convective heat transfer | Energy equation | Nu | 4.36 (laminar, const T_s) |
| 6 | Dittus-Boelter | Nu = 0.023 Re^0.8 Pr^n | h | varies |
| 7 | Turbomachine | Euler turbomachine eq. | H (head) | 10 to 200 m |
| 8 | Combustion | Species transport | T_adiabatic_flame | 1800 to 2300 K |
| 9 | Electronic cooling | Q_conv = hAΔT | h | 10 to 200 W/m²K |
| 10 | Car aerodynamics | RANS / k-ω SST | C_D | 0.25 to 0.40 |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute values in equations above use parentheses or vertical spacing — never the pipe `|` character — to avoid breaking markdown table syntax. The pipe character `|` has been replaced with `\vert` style spacing in all rows.

### 2.9 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive industry** uses CFD to reduce drag coefficient C_D by 20–30%, saving millions of liters of fuel annually.
- **Aerospace** uses RANS/LES on wings to predict stall, reducing wind-tunnel testing costs by 70%.
- **HVAC** engineers simulate room ventilation to ensure COVID-safe air exchange rates.
- **Power generation** uses conjugate heat transfer (CHT) CFD to optimize steam turbine blade cooling.
- **Electronics** companies (Intel, AMD) use CFD to design chip heatsinks — a $5B market segment.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Borda–Carnot Loss Coefficient for Sudden Expansion

This is a **high-yield KTU derivation** — appears almost every year.

**Starting point:** Apply the **integral momentum equation** and **Bernoulli's equation** across the sudden expansion from area **A₁** to **A₂**.

**Step 1 — Mass conservation:**

$$ \rho \, U_1 \, A_1 = \rho \, U_2 \, A_2 $$

Therefore:

$$ U_2 = U_1 \, \frac{A_1}{A_2} $$

**Step 2 — Bernoulli between sections (1) just before and (2) far downstream where flow is reattached:**

$$ \frac{P_1}{\rho g} + \frac{U_1^2}{2g} = \frac{P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{U_2^2}{2g} + h_L $$

Solving for head loss **h_L**:

$$ h_L = \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} + \frac{U_1^2 - U_2^2}{2g} $$

**Step 3 — Apply momentum equation to the control volume between sections (1) and (2):**

$$ \sum F_x = \rho Q (U_2 - U_1) $$

The forces are pressure forces on sections (1) and (2):

$$ P_1 A_1 - P_2 A_2 = \rho U_2 A_2 (U_2 - U_1) $$

Using continuity, A₁ U₁ = A₂ U₂ = Q.

**Step 4 — Combine and simplify.**

Divide momentum equation by ρ g A₁, and substitute U₂ = U₁ (A₁/A₂):

$$ \frac{P_1 - P_2}{\rho g} = \frac{A_2}{A_1} \cdot \frac{U_2}{g} \left( U_2 - U_1 \right) + \frac{P_2 A_2}{\rho g A_1} $$

After algebraic manipulation (which KTU expects you to show step-by-step):

$$ h_L = \frac{(U_1 - U_2)^2}{2g} $$

**Step 5 — Substitute U₂ = U₁ (A₁/A₂):**

$$ h_L = \frac{U_1^2}{2g} \left( 1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2} \right)^2 $$

**Step 6 — Express as a loss coefficient (non-dimensionalizing by U₁²/2g):**

$$
\boxed{K_L = 1 - \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right)^2 = \left(1 - \frac{1}{ER^2}\right)^2}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Where ER = D₂/D₁** is the expansion ratio. For ER = 2 (doubled diameter): K_L = (1 − 1/4)² = 0.5625.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Strouhal Number for Vortex Shedding Behind a Cylinder

**Step 1 — Dimensionless analysis.** The shedding frequency **f** depends on: cylinder diameter **D**, free-stream velocity **U_∞**, fluid density **ρ**, and fluid viscosity **μ**.

This gives 5 variables with 2 fundamental dimensions (M, L, T) → **3 dimensionless groups**.

**Step 2 — Construct the groups:**
- Reynolds number: Re = (ρ U_∞ D) / μ
- Strouhal number: St = (f D) / U_∞
- (Third group is typically a pressure coefficient, not needed for frequency)

**Step 3 — Buckingham Pi result:**

$$ St = \phi(\text{Re}) $$

**Step 4 — For the subcritical range (10³ < Re < 10⁵), experiments show St is nearly constant:**

$$
\boxed{St = \frac{f \, D}{U_\infty} \approx 0.21}
$$

This is the famous **von Kármán vortex street** shedding frequency.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Dittus-Boelter from Buckingham Pi + Empirical Fit

**Step 1 — Variables and dimensions for Nu:**
